- ✅ **Limitation:** Cannot handle left-recursive grammars, needs left factoring, limited to 1-symbol lookahead.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Non-Recursive Predictive Parsing. Best of luck for your KTU exams! 🎓**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bottom-Up Parsing - Shift Reduce Parsing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bottom-Up Parsing - Shift Reduce Parsing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bottom-up parsing** is a syntax analysis technique that constructs the parse tree for an input string starting from the **leaves** (terminals/tokens) and working upwards toward the **root** (start symbol of the grammar). The construction effectively traces a **rightmost derivation in reverse**.

**Shift-Reduce Parsing** is the canonical and most widely implemented form of bottom-up parsing. It employs a **parse stack**, an **input buffer**, and a **parsing algorithm** that performs four fundamental actions:

1. **Shift** — Push the next input symbol onto the stack.
2. **Reduce** — Replace a sequence of symbols on top of the stack (a *handle*) with the corresponding non-terminal (LHS of a production).
3. **Accept** — Successful parse; the stack contains only the start symbol $S$ and the input buffer is empty.
4. **Error** — Invoke error-recovery routines; no valid action exists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Shift-Reduce parsing is the *foundation* for LR parsers (SLR, CLR, LALR). The KTU examiner expects students to trace parses manually and identify *handles* and *viable prefixes* explicitly.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Wall-Building Metaphor"

Imagine you are constructing a **brick wall** (the parse tree) from a pile of bricks (tokens) and a blueprint (the grammar).

- **Shift** = Pick up the next brick and place it on top of your partially built wall (stack).
- **Reduce** = When the topmost bricks exactly match a section shown in the blueprint (a *handle*), you replace them with one **larger pre-fabricated block** (the LHS non-terminal).
- **Accept** = The wall is complete and matches the blueprint from top to bottom.
- **Error** = No brick available and no blueprint section matches the top of the wall.

You keep stacking until the **top of the wall matches a complete blueprint section**, then you collapse (reduce) that section into a bigger unit — repeating until only the foundation block (Start Symbol $S$) remains.

### 1.3 The Parse Stack Data Structure

The shift-reduce parser uses a **stack** with the following property:

$$\text{Stack contents} = \alpha \quad \text{(a viable prefix of a right-sentential form)}$$

Combined with the remaining input $w$, the configuration $(\alpha, w)$ corresponds to the right-sentential form $\alpha w$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Configuration:** A pair $\left( \text{Stack}, \text{Input Buffer} \right)$ uniquely identifies the parser state at any instant. KTU questions frequently ask students to write down configurations step-by-step.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Parser Actions — Operational Logic

Let $S$ be the stack, $\$$ be the bottom-of-stack marker, and $a$ be the lookahead symbol.

**Action 1: Shift**

- **Trigger:** The top of $S$ together with the lookahead $a$ does **not** form a reducible handle, but $a$ can begin a valid continuation.
- **How:** Push $a$ onto $S$; advance the input pointer.
- **Effect:** $|S| \gets |S| + 1$.

**Action 2: Reduce**

- **Trigger:** The topmost $k$ symbols of $S$ (where $k \ge 1$) match the **RHS** of some production $A \to \alpha$, and this $\alpha$ is a valid **handle** of the current right-sentential form.
- **How:** Pop $k$ symbols; push the LHS $A$.
- **Effect:** $|S| \gets |S| - k + 1$.

**Action 3: Accept**

- **Trigger:** $S$ contains only $\$S$ (Start Symbol) and the input buffer is empty (only $\$ left).
- **Meaning:** Parsing succeeded; the input is a valid sentence of the grammar.

**Action 4: Error**

- **Trigger:** Neither shift nor reduce is applicable.
- **Meaning:** The input does not belong to the language; recovery routines are invoked (panic-mode, phrase-level, etc.).

### 2.2 Handles and Handle Pruning

**Definition (Aho, Sethi, Ullman — Dragon Book standard):**

A **handle** of a right-sentential form $\gamma$ is a substring $\alpha$ such that:

1. $\alpha$ matches the RHS of some production $A \to \alpha$.
2. Replacing $\alpha$ by $A$ in $\gamma$ yields the previous right-sentential form in the rightmost derivation of $\gamma$.

Formally, if $S \Rightarrow^{*}_{rm} \alpha A w \Rightarrow_{rm} \alpha \beta w$, then $\beta$ in the position following $\alpha$ is a handle of $\alpha \beta w$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Handle Pruning:** Bottom-up parsing is the process of repeatedly detecting a handle in the current right-sentential form and reducing it. Each reduction corresponds to **one reverse step** of a rightmost derivation.

### 2.3 Viable Prefixes

A **viable prefix** is a prefix of a right-sentential form that:

- Does **not** extend past the handle of that right-sentential form.
- Can appear on the stack during shift-reduce parsing without causing an error.

The set of all viable prefixes forms a **regular language** recognized by a **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)** — this is the theoretical foundation of LR(0) item construction.

### 2.4 Conflicts in Shift-Reduce Parsing

The parser's decision table (ACTION/GOTO) may have ambiguity:

| Conflict Type | Description | Typical Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Shift-Reduce Conflict** | On a given symbol, the parser cannot decide whether to *shift* or *reduce* | Classic example: *dangling-else* in `if-then-else` |
| **Reduce-Reduce Conflict** | On a given symbol, the parser cannot decide *which* production to reduce by | Common in languages with overlapping type casts or identifier declarations |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** If a grammar is **not LR(k)** for any fixed $k$, the shift-reduce parser will exhibit conflicts. The student must be able to *identify the conflict* and suggest grammar transformations to remove it.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $\$S$ | Bottom-of-stack marker | Initial stack content |
| $\text{Handle}$ | RHS to be reduced next | Always exists for valid right-sentential forms |
| $\text{Viable Prefix}$ | Stack content at any moment | Prefix of a right-sentential form ending before the handle |
| $\text{Reduction}$ | Replace handle $\beta$ by LHS $A$ | Inverse of a rightmost derivation step |
| $\text{ACTION}[s, a]$ | Shift / Reduce / Accept / Error | $s$ = state, $a$ = terminal |
| $\text{GOTO}[s, A]$ | Next state after reducing $A$ | $A$ = non-terminal |
| $\vert \text{RHS} \vert$ | Number of symbols on RHS | Number of symbols popped during reduction |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Shift-reduce parsing (and its derivatives SLR, LALR, CLR) powers virtually every modern compiler frontend:

- **GCC** and **Clang** use **LALR(1)** parsers generated by **GNU Bison** and **LLVM TableGen**.
- **JavaParser**, **ANTLR-tooled grammars**, and many **DSL engines** rely on shift-reduce mechanics.
- **Industrial use cases:** SQL engines, configuration-file validators, protocol compilers (Protobuf), hardware description language frontends (Verilog/VHDL).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Trace a Shift-Reduce Parse

**Grammar $G$:**

$$E \to E + T \mid T$$

$$T \to T \ast F \mid F$$

$$F \to (E) \mid \text{id}$$

**Input string:** $\text{id} \ast \text{id} + \text{id}$

**Rightmost derivation (forward):**

$$
E \Rightarrow E + T \Rightarrow E + F \Rightarrow E + \text{id}
\Rightarrow T + \text{id} \Rightarrow T \ast F + \text{id} \Rightarrow T \ast \text{id} + \text{id}
\Rightarrow F \ast \text{id} + \text{id} \Rightarrow \text{id} \ast \text{id} + \text{id}
$$

The shift-reduce parser must perform the **reverse** of the above.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Parsing Trace (Reverse Rightmost Derivation)

| Step | Stack (top → bottom right) | Input Buffer | Action | Production Used |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\$ $ | $\text{id} \ast \text{id} + \text{id} \,\$$ | **Shift** | — |
| 2 | $\$ \,\text{id}$ | $\ast \text{id} + \text{id} \,\$$ | **Reduce** | $F \to \text{id}$ |
| 3 | $\$ \,F$ | $\ast \text{id} + \text{id} \,\$$ | **Reduce** | $T \to F$ |
| 4 | $\$ \,T$ | $\ast \text{id} + \text{id} \,\$$ | **Shift** | — |
| 5 | $\$ \,T \ast$ | $\text{id} + \text{id} \,\$$ | **Shift** | — |
| 6 | $\$ \,T \ast \text{id}$ | $+ \text{id} \,\$$ | **Reduce** | $F \to \text{id}$ |
| 7 | $\$ \,T \ast F$ | $+ \text{id} \,\$$ | **Reduce** | $T \to T \ast F$ |
| 8 | $\$ \,T$ | $+ \text{id} \,\$$ | **Reduce** | $E \to T$ |
| 9 | $\$ \,E$ | $+ \text{id} \,\$$ | **Shift** | — |
| 10 | $\$ \,E +$ | $\text{id} \,\$$ | **Shift** | — |
| 11 | $\$ \,E + \text{id}$ | $\$ $ | **Reduce** | $F \to \text{id}$ |
| 12 | $\$ \,E + F$ | $\$ $ | **Reduce** | $T \to F$ |
| 13 | $\$ \,E + T$ | $\$ $ | **Reduce** | $E \to E + T$ |
| 14 | $\$ \,E$ | $\$ $ | **Accept** | — |

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Why This Sequence is Correct

Let us verify that Step 7's handle $T \ast F$ is indeed a valid handle of the right-sentential form $T \ast F + \text{id}$.

At Step 6, the configuration is:

$$
\text{Stack} = \$ \,T \ast \text{id}, \quad \text{Input} = + \text{id} \,\$$
$$

The right-sentential form being processed is $T \ast \text{id} + \text{id}$.

Now, applying the rightmost derivation in reverse:

$$
T \ast \text{id} + \text{id} \;\leftarrow\; T \ast F + \text{id}
$$

This corresponds to the production $F \to \text{id}$ applied in reverse. Therefore $\text{id}$ (at position after $T \ast$) is the **handle**.

Applying this:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stack} &= \$ \,T \ast \text{id} \\
\text{Pop 1 symbol} &\Rightarrow \$ \,T \ast \\
\text{Push } F &\Rightarrow \$ \,T \ast F
\end{aligned}
$$

Now Stack $= \$ \,T \ast F$, Input $= + \text{id} \,\$$, giving right-sentential form $T \ast F + \text{id}$.

Continuing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T \ast F + \text{id} &\;\xleftarrow{\text{Reduce } T \to T \ast F}\; T + \text{id} \\
&\;\xleftarrow{\text{Reduce } E \to T}\; E + \text{id} \\
&\;\xleftarrow{\text{Reduce } F \to \text{id}}\; E + F \\
&\;\xleftarrow{\text{Reduce } T \to F}\; E + T \\
&\;\xleftarrow{\text{Reduce } E \to E + T}\; E
\end{aligned}
$$

Each step corresponds to exactly **one** parser action in the table above. The inverse of the rightmost derivation is preserved one-for-one.

### 3.4 Shift-Reduce Conflict — Illustrative Derivation

Consider the classic *dangling-else* grammar:

$$
S \to \text{if } E \text{ then } S \mid \text{if } E \text{ then } S \text{ else } S \mid \text{other}
$$

When parsing:

$$
\text{if } E \text{ then if } E \text{ then } S \text{ else } S
$$

at the moment we see **else** with $S$ on top of the stack, the parser faces a choice:

- **Shift** the `else` (assume it binds to the innermost `if`).
- **Reduce** $S \to \text{if } E \text{ then } S$ (assume `else` binds to outer `if`).

Both are valid in this grammar — this is a **shift-reduce conflict** that can only be resolved by either (a) grammar rewriting to enforce associativity, or (b) using the standard "match `else` with nearest `then`" rule.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Shift-Reduce Parser Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Buffer\nid * id + id $] --> B[Lexical Analyzer\nalready tokenized]
    B --> C[Lookahead Symbol\na = current token]
    C --> D{Parser Engine\nACTION / GOTO}
    D -- Shift --> E[Push 'a' onto Stack]
    D -- Reduce --> F[Pop k symbols\nPush LHS]
    D -- Accept --> G[Parsing Successful\nEmit AST]
    D -- Error --> H[Error Handler\nRecovery Routine]
    E --> I[Parse Stack\nbottom-to-top: $ X Y Z]
    F --> I
    I --> D
    style D fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 State Transition Flow — Parser Decision Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialize
    Initialize --> ReadToken: Push $ on stack
    ReadToken --> Decide: Read lookahead a
    Decide --> ShiftAction: ACTION[s,a] = shift n
    Decide --> ReduceAction: ACTION[s,a] = reduce A -> alpha
    Decide --> AcceptState: ACTION[s,a] = accept
    Decide --> ErrorState: ACTION[s,a] = error
    ShiftAction --> ReadToken: Push a; goto state n
    ReduceAction --> Decide: Pop alpha; push A; goto GOTO[s,A]
    AcceptState --> [*]: Parse complete
    ErrorState --> Recovery: Invoke panic-mode
    Recovery --> ReadToken: Synchronize
```

### 4.3 Handle Pruning Concept Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Right-Sentential Form\ngamma = alpha beta w] --> Identify{Identify Handle\nbeta on top of stack}
    Identify -- Yes --> Reduce[Reduce by A -> beta\nReplace beta with A]
    Identify -- No --> Shift[Shift next symbol\nExtend stack]
    Shift --> Identify
    Reduce --> NewForm[New form: alpha A w\nCloser to start symbol S]
    NewForm --> Check{Stack = $S$ and\nInput empty?}
    Check -- No --> Identify
    Check -- Yes --> Done[Accept: Input is valid sentence]
    style Start fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064
    style Done fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style Reduce fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17
    style Shift fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

*Define a handle. Explain the term "handle pruning" with reference to bottom-up parsing.*

**Model Answer:**

A **handle** of a right-sentential form $\gamma = \alpha \beta w$ is a substring $\beta$ that:

(a) Matches the RHS of some production $A \to \beta$, **and**

(b) Replacing $\beta$ by $A$ in $\gamma$ yields the **previous** right-sentential form in the rightmost derivation.

$$
S \Rightarrow^{*}_{rm} \alpha A w \Rightarrow_{rm} \alpha \beta w
$$

**Handle pruning** is the process of repeatedly detecting and reducing handles until the start symbol $S$ is reached. Each handle reduction corresponds to one reverse step of the rightmost derivation, hence bottom-up parsing is the **mechanization of rightmost derivation in reverse**.

**Mark distribution:** Definition (2 marks) + Handle pruning explanation (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

*List the four actions performed by a shift-reduce parser. What is the role of the bottom-of-stack marker `$`?*

**Model Answer:**

The four actions are:

1. **Shift** — Push next input symbol onto the stack.
2. **Reduce** — Replace handle on top of stack with the corresponding non-terminal (LHS of a production).
3. **Accept** — Announce successful parse (stack contains $\$S$ and input is empty).
4. **Error** — Invoke error recovery (no valid shift/reduce action).

**Role of `$`:** The marker `$` serves as a **sentinel** at the bottom of the stack. It:

- Marks the bottom for stack operations (avoids underflow).
- Acts as a **left end-marker** for the input string, enabling the parser to detect end-of-input.
- Provides a uniform initial configuration so the parser can detect acceptance when only $\$S$ remains on the stack.

**Mark distribution:** List (1 mark) + Brief explanation of any two actions (1 mark) + Role of `$` (1 mark).

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**Q3 (a)** Consider the following grammar $G$:

$$
S \to aSbS \mid bSaS \mid \varepsilon
$$

Trace the **shift-reduce parsing** for the input string `a b a b`. Show the stack contents, input buffer, and parser action at every step. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The grammar generates balanced/semi-balanced strings of $a$'s and $b$'s. Note: this grammar is **ambiguous**; we will follow a leftmost-derivation-in-reverse trace using the recursive-descent convention.

| Step | Stack | Input | Action | Production |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | `$` | `a b a b $` | Shift | — |
| 2 | `$ a` | `b a b $` | Shift | — |
| 3 | `$ a b` | `a b $` | Reduce | $S \to \varepsilon$ |
| 4 | `$ a S` | `a b $` | Shift | — |
| 5 | `$ a S a` | `b $` | Shift | — |
| 6 | `$ a S a b` | `$` | Reduce | $S \to \varepsilon$ |
| 7 | `$ a S a S` | `$` | Reduce | $S \to aSbS$ |
| 8 | `$ S` | `$` | Reduce | $S \to aSbS$ |
| 9 | `$ S` | `$` | Accept | — |

**Explanation of key steps:**

- **Step 3:** Top of stack is `b` and lookahead is `a`; `b` does not begin a production RHS that can be reduced directly, so we treat `b` as derivable from $\varepsilon$ via $S \to \varepsilon$ (in reverse, this means we reduce $b$ to $S$ — but since the grammar is ambiguous, we use the standard convention of reducing single terminals via $\varepsilon$ when context permits).
- **Step 7:** Stack top `$ a S a S$` matches RHS of $S \to aSbS$; reduce.
- **Step 8:** Stack becomes `$ S`; input empty; accept.

**Q3 (b)** Explain **shift-reduce conflicts** and **reduce-reeduce conflicts** with one example each. How are they resolved? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Shift-Reduce Conflict:** Occurs when the parser cannot decide whether to **shift** the next symbol or **reduce** by a production on the current stack top.

**Example:** Dangling-else grammar

$$
S \to \text{if } E \text{ then } S \mid \text{if } E \text{ then } S \text{ else } S \mid \text{other}
$$

When parsing `if E then if E then S else S`, after reading the inner `S`, the parser sees `else` and faces a choice: shift `else` (bind to inner if) or reduce $S \to \text{if } E \text{ then } S$ (bind to outer if). Both actions are legal in the grammar.

**Resolution:** Apply the **"match else with nearest then"** convention; or rewrite the grammar to enforce the convention:

$$
S \to \text{if } E \text{ then } S' \mid M$$
$$
S' \to \text{if } E \text{ then } S' \mid \text{if } E \text{ then } S' \text{ else } S' \mid M$$
$$
M \to \text{other} \mid S
$$

**Reduce-Reduce Conflict:** Occurs when the parser cannot decide **which production to reduce by** on the current stack top with the same lookahead.

**Example:** Suppose the parser has stack top $... x$ and lookahead $y$, with two productions applicable:

$$
A \to x \quad \text{and} \quad B \to x
$$

The parser cannot determine whether $x$ reduces to $A$ or $B$.

**Resolution:** Grammar rewriting (eliminate common prefixes), use context information, or switch to a parser with greater lookahead (LR(1) instead of LR(0)).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q4 (a)** For the grammar:

$$
E \to E + E \mid E \ast E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}
$$

Trace the **shift-reduce parser** for the input `id + id * id`. Identify all handles and reductions. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Rightmost derivation:**

$$
E \Rightarrow E + E \Rightarrow E + E \ast E \Rightarrow E + E \ast \text{id}
\Rightarrow E + \text{id} \ast \text{id} \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} \ast \text{id}
$$

**Shift-reduce trace (reverse):**

| Step | Stack | Input | Action | Handle & Production |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | `$` | `id + id * id $` | Shift | — |
| 2 | `$ id` | `+ id * id $` | Reduce | $\text{id} \to E$ via $E \to \text{id}$ |
| 3 | `$ E` | `+ id * id $` | Shift | — |
| 4 | `$ E +` | `id * id $` | Shift | — |
| 5 | `$ E + id` | `* id $` | Reduce | $\text{id} \to E$ via $E \to \text{id}$ |
| 6 | `$ E + E` | `* id $` | Shift | — |
| 7 | `$ E + E *` | `id $` | Shift | — |
| 8 | `$ E + E * id` | `$` | Reduce | $\text{id} \to E$ via $E \to \text{id}$ |
| 9 | `$ E + E * E` | `$` | Reduce | $E \ast E \to E$ via $E \to E \ast E$ |
| 10 | `$ E + E` | `$` | Reduce | $E + E \to E$ via $E \to E + E$ |
| 11 | `$ E` | `$` | Accept | — |

**Note:** This grammar is ambiguous (no precedence between $+$ and $*$). For a deterministic shift-reduce parser, this grammar would cause **shift-reduce conflicts**. The trace above assumes the standard left-to-right operator precedence ($*$ binds tighter than $+$).

**Q4 (b)** Explain the concept of **viable prefixes**. How are they related to the construction of an LR(0) automaton? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A **viable prefix** is a prefix of a right-sentential form that does not extend beyond the **right end of the handle**. In other words, if $\gamma = \alpha \beta w$ is a right-sentential form with handle $\beta$, then any prefix of $\alpha \beta$ that ends **at or before** the rightmost symbol of $\beta$ is a viable prefix.

**Properties of viable prefixes:**

1. They are **precisely** the strings that can appear on the parser stack at any moment.
2. They form a **regular language** — recognizable by a DFA.
3. The DFA states correspond to sets of **LR(0) items** (productions with a dot indicating parse progress).

**Relation to LR(0) Automaton:**

- Each **state** of the LR(0) automaton represents a **set of LR(0) items**, where each item is a production with a dot at some position, e.g., $A \to X \cdot YZ$.
- The set of strings (stack contents) that lead to a particular state equals the set of viable prefixes recognized at that state.
- Transitions of the automaton correspond to **shift** (terminal symbol consumed) or **goto** (non-terminal after reduction).

**Construction outline:**

1. Augment grammar with $S' \to S$.
2. Compute the closure of the initial item $\{[S' \to \cdot S]\}$.
3. For each item $A \to \alpha \cdot X\beta$ and each grammar symbol $X$, define a GOTO transition.
4. Repeat until no new states are generated.

The resulting DFA's states form the columns of the LR parsing table.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `$` marker** in the initial and final stack configurations. Always show `$` at the bottom.
> 2. **Confusing shift and reduce** — Shift *pushes*; Reduce *replaces* handle with non-terminal.
> 3. **Wrong handle identification** — A handle is NOT just any RHS match; it must correspond to the *current* rightmost derivation step.
> 4. **Skipping the Accept step** — Parsing ends with explicit "Accept", not just an empty input.
> 5. **Not showing production number** in the reduce action (e.g., "$E \to E + E$ by production 1").
> 6. **Missing conflict identification** in ambiguous grammars — Examiner wants you to note that ambiguity leads to shift-reduce or reduce-reduce conflicts.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bottom-up parsing = reverse rightmost derivation.** Every reduction is the inverse of one derivation step.
- **Shift-Reduce Parser** uses a stack + input buffer; performs one of **4 actions** at each step.
- **Handle** = RHS matched at top of stack whose reduction moves toward the start symbol.
- **Handle Pruning** = iterative detection and reduction of handles until $S$ is reached.
- **Viable Prefix** = stack contents at any moment; always a prefix of a right-sentential form ending at/before the handle.
- **Conflicts:**
  - *Shift-Reduce*: parser unsure whether to shift or reduce (e.g., dangling-else).
  - *Reduce-Reduce*: parser unsure which production to reduce by.
- **Accept Configuration:** Stack $= \$S$, Input $= \$$.
- **Grammar Ambiguity** → produces conflicts in shift-reduce parsing; resolution via rewriting or precedence rules.
- **Real-world implementation:** GCC, Clang, Bison — all use **LALR(1)** shift-reduce parsers.
- **Standard exam trace:** Always include (1) Step number, (2) Stack content (with `$` at bottom), (3) Remaining input, (4) Action (Shift/Reduce/Accept), (5) Production used.
- **LR family hierarchy:** LR(0) ⊂ SLR(1) ⊂ LALR(1) ⊂ CLR(1) ⊂ LR(k) — all built atop shift-reduce mechanics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple LR Parser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Simple LR Parser # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **SLR (Simple LR) Parser:** A bottom-up, shift-reduce parsing technique that uses the **canonical collection of LR(0) items** (Finite State Machine of viable prefixes) combined with the **FOLLOW sets** of non-terminals to construct a deterministic parsing table. It is the simplest enhancement of the LR(0) parser, designed by **Franklin L. DeRemer (1971)**, which can successfully parse a strictly larger class of context-free grammars than LR(0), while remaining significantly simpler to construct than the canonical LR(1) parser. In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the SLR parser belongs to the family of **LR(k, t) parsers** — specifically **LR(0, 1)** — where: - **k = 0** ⇒ The parser uses *LR(0) items* (no lookahead is embedded in the items). - **t = 1** ⇒ A *single-token lookahead* is used **only at reduce-time** to consult the FOLLOW set of the left-hand side non-terminal. The SLR parsing engine has **four fundamental components**: 1. **Stack** — holds a sequence of states and grammar symbols. 2. **Input Buffer** — holds the remaining input string terminated by the **end-marker `$`** (whose ASCII value is **36**). 3. **Parsing Table** — a two-part table containing the **ACTION** function and the **GOTO** function. 4. **Output** — emits production numbers used for the derivation (for syntax-directed translation). > [!NOTE] > **Key Notation Used Throughout KTU 2024 Module 1:** > - `sₖ` → shift the input symbol and push state `k` onto the stack. > - `rⱼ` → reduce by production number `j`; pop `2 × |α|` symbols (where `α` is the RHS of production `j`). > - `acc` → accept (successful parse). > - *blank cell* → error. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Airport Security Conveyor Belt":** > Imagine a security officer at an airport. A suitcase (input token) arrives on a conveyor belt. The officer consults a **rulebook** that says: *"If the next item on the belt matches the **FOLLOW set** of a non-terminal 'Box', then seal the box (i.e., reduce)."* Without the FOLLOW set check, the officer would over-eagerly seal the box even when more items (that should belong *inside* the box) are still coming. The **FOLLOW set** essentially tells the parser: *"Only commit a reduction if the next token is one that is legally allowed to follow the non-terminal on the Left-Hand Side."* **Why SLR is "Simple":** - It **reuses** the LR(0) automaton (built without any lookahead in the items). - It **sneaks in** a single-token lookahead by leveraging the **pre-computed FOLLOW sets** at reduce actions. - This avoids the explosion in the number of states that one would get with full LR(1) items. ## 1.3 Hierarchy of LR Parsers (KTU 2024 High-Yield) | Parser Type | Items Used | Lookahead | State Count | Grammar Class | |---|---|---|---|---| | LR(0) | LR(0) items | 0 | Smallest | Smallest | | **SLR(1)** | **LR(0) items + FOLLOW** | **1 (at reduce)** | **Small** | **Larger** | | CLR(1) / LR(1) | LR(1) items | 1 (in items) | Largest | Largest | | LALR(1) | LR(1) items, merged | 1 (in items) | = SLR(1) | Between SLR & CLR | > [!WARNING] > **Common Misconception:** "SLR(1) is the same as LALR(1)." **False.** SLR(1) constructs its DFA from **LR(0) items** and resolves reduces via FOLLOW sets. LALR(1) constructs its DFA from **LR(1) items** and then *merges states with identical LR(0) cores*. They produce the **same size tables** but differ in the construction method and the class of grammars accepted. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Foundational Definitions ### 2.1.1 Grammar Augmentation Given a grammar `G = (V, T, P, S)`, the **augmented grammar `G'`** adds a new start production: $$S' \rightarrow S$$ where `S'` is a fresh non-terminal, and `S` is the original start symbol. **Purpose:** Provides a unique accept state in the automaton. ### 2.1.2 LR(0) Item An **LR(0) item** (or *viable prefix*) is a production of the grammar with a distinguished dot `·` placed somewhere on its right-hand side. $$[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta]$$ - `A → αβ` is a production. - The dot indicates how much of the RHS has been recognized so far. - If `β = ε` (item is `[A → α·]`), the item is called a **complete item** or **reducible item** (a candidate for reduction). ### 2.1.3 Augmented Item Types | Item Form | Meaning | |---|---| | `[A → α·Xβ]` where `X` is a grammar symbol | Parser expects to shift `X` next. | | `[A → α·]` (complete) | Parser may reduce by `A → α`. | | `[S' → S·]` | Accept the input. | ### 2.1.4 FOLLOW Set $$\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{ a \in T \cup \{\$\} \mid S \Rightarrow^* \cdots A\, a \cdots \}$$ i.e., the set of terminals that can immediately follow `A` in any sentential form derived from `S`. The end-marker `$` is included in `FOLLOW(S')`. ## 2.2 The SLR(1) Construction Algorithm (Six Steps) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 1 — Algorithm to Construct an SLR(1) Parsing Table:** > > **Input:** An augmented grammar `G'`. > **Output:** The SLR(1) parsing table (ACTION and GOTO functions). > > **Step 1 — Augment the grammar:** Introduce `S' → S`. > > **Step 2 — Construct the canonical collection `C = {I₀, I₁, …, Iₙ}` of LR(0) items** for `G'` using the two functions: > - **CLOSURE(I):** If `[A → α·Bβ] ∈ I` and `B → γ` is a production, then add `[B → ·γ]` to `I`. Repeat until no new items can be added. > - **GOTO(I, X):** If `[A → α·Xβ] ∈ I`, then `GOTO(I, X) = CLOSURE({[A → αX·β]})`. > > **Step 3 — Compute FOLLOW sets** for every non-terminal in `G'`. > > **Step 4 — Build the State Diagram:** Each `Iᵢ` is a state; draw an arc labeled `X` from `Iᵢ` to `Iⱼ` if `GOTO(Iᵢ, X) = Iⱼ`. > > **Step 5 — Construct the parsing table** using the rules in §2.3. > > **Step 6 — Conflict Check:** If any table cell receives **more than one entry**, the grammar is **NOT SLR(1)**. ## 2.3 SLR(1) Table-Filling Rules For every item `[A → α·Xβ]` in state `Iᵢ` and `GOTO(Iᵢ, X) = Iⱼ`: | Condition | ACTION / GOTO Entry | Justification | |---|---|---| | 1. `X` is a **terminal** `a`, and `a` is in the input | `ACTION[i, a] = shift j` | Shift `a` and push state `j`. | | 2. `A ≠ S'`, item is `[A → α·]`, and `a ∈ FOLLOW(A)` | `ACTION[i, a] = reduce by A → α` | Reduce on valid lookahead. | | 3. Item is `[S' → S·]` | `ACTION[i, $] = accept` | Parsing complete. | | 4. `X` is a **non-terminal** `A`, and `GOTO(Iᵢ, A) = Iⱼ` | `GOTO[i, A] = j` | Move to state `j` after a reduction to `A`. | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Rule:** The action `shift j` has **priority** over `reduce`. In any cell, if a shift and a reduce both apply, this is a **shift-reduce conflict** and the grammar fails SLR(1). ## 2.4 Conflicts in SLR(1) ### 2.4.1 Shift–Reduce Conflict Occurs in state `Iᵢ` for terminal `a` when: $$\text{ACTION}[i, a] = s_j \quad \wedge \quad \text{ACTION}[i, a] = r_k$$ That is, the parser cannot decide whether to **shift `a`** (because `[A → α·aβ] ∈ Iᵢ`) or **reduce by `A → α`** (because `[B → γ·] ∈ Iᵢ` and `a ∈ FOLLOW(B)`). ### 2.4.2 Reduce–Reduce Conflict Occurs in state `Iᵢ` for terminal `a` when: $$\text{ACTION}[i, a] = r_j \quad \wedge \quad \text{ACTION}[i, a] = r_k \quad (j \neq k)$$ The parser cannot decide **which production to reduce by** because two different completions both have `a` in their FOLLOW set. > [!NOTE] > **If the SLR(1) table has no conflicts, the grammar is SLR(1).** Otherwise, the grammar is *not* SLR(1) (though it may still be LR(1) or ambiguous). ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Used In | |---|---|---| | $C = \{I_0, I_1, \ldots, I_n\}$ | Canonical collection of sets of LR(0) items | DFA construction | | $\text{CLOSURE}(I)$ | Smallest set containing $I$ such that if $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B \beta] \in I$, then $[B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma] \in I$ for every production $B \rightarrow \gamma$ | Item-set expansion | | $\text{GOTO}(I, X)$ | $\text{CLOSURE}(\{[A \rightarrow \alpha X \cdot \beta] \mid [A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot X \beta] \in I\})$ | DFA transitions | | $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ | Set of terminals that begin strings derivable from $\alpha$ | FOLLOW computation | | $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ | $\{a \in T \cup \{\$\} \mid S \Rightarrow^* \alpha A a \beta\}$ | Reduce action validation | | $\text{Stack Size after reduce}$ | $\vert \text{Stack}\vert - 2 \cdot \vert \alpha \vert$ | Operational detail | | $s_k$ | Shift and push state $k$ | ACTION cell entry | | $r_j$ | Reduce by production $j$ (RHS length $= \vert \alpha_j \vert$) | ACTION cell entry | | $\text{acc}$ | Accept (parsing successful) | ACTION[state, \$] | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Example > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example Grammar (Aho/Sethi/Ullman — *Dragon Book* canonical example):** > Production (0)    $S' \rightarrow S$ > Production (1)    $S \rightarrow C\,C$ > Production (2)    $C \rightarrow c\,C$ > Production (3)    $C \rightarrow d$ > > **Input string to parse:** `c d c d` (terminated by `$`) --- ## 3.1 Step 1 — Augment the Grammar (Already done) $S'$ is the new start symbol; $S' \rightarrow S$ is production **(0)**. --- ## 3.2 Step 2 — Construct the Canonical Collection of LR(0) Items **CLOSURE(I₀):** Begin with the augmented item $[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]$. Applying CLOSURE repeatedly: $$ \begin{aligned} I_0 & = \text{CLOSURE}(\{[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]\}) \\ & = \{ [S' \rightarrow \cdot S],\; [S \rightarrow \cdot CC],\; [C \rightarrow \cdot cC],\; [C \rightarrow \cdot d] \} \end{aligned} $$ **GOTO Operations from I₀:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{GOTO}(I_0, S) &= \text{CLOSURE}(\{[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]\}) = I_1 \\ \text{GOTO}(I_0, C) &= \text{CLOSURE}(\{[S \rightarrow C \cdot C]\}) = I_2 \\ \text{GOTO}(I_0, c) &= \text{CLOSURE}(\{[C \rightarrow c \cdot C]\}) = I_3 \\ \text{GOTO}(I_0, d) &= \text{CLOSURE}(\{[C \rightarrow d \cdot]\}) = I_4 \end{aligned} $$ **Compute I₂, I₃, I₄ explicitly:** $$ \begin{aligned} I_2 & = \text{CLOSURE}(\{[S \rightarrow C \cdot C]\}) \\ & = \{ [S \rightarrow C \cdot C],\; [C \rightarrow \cdot cC],\; [C \rightarrow \cdot d] \} \\[4pt] I_3 & = \text{CLOSURE}(\{[C \rightarrow c \cdot C]\}) \\ & = \{ [C \rightarrow c \cdot C],\; [C \rightarrow \cdot cC],\; [C \rightarrow \cdot d] \} \\[4pt] I_4 & = \{ [C \rightarrow d \cdot] \} \end{aligned} $$ **GOTO from I₂:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{GOTO}(I_2, C) &= \text{CLOSURE}(\{[S \rightarrow CC \cdot]\}) = I_5 \\ \text{GOTO}(I_2, c) &= I_3 \quad \text{(same set already computed)} \\ \text{GOTO}(I_2, d) &= I_4 \end{aligned} $$ **GOTO from I₃:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{GOTO}(I_3, C) &= \text{CLOSURE}(\{[C \rightarrow cC \cdot]\}) = I_6 \\ \text{GOTO}(I_3, c) &= I_3 \\ \text{GOTO}(I_3, d) &= I_4 \end{aligned} $$ **GOTO from I₅, I₆:** No outgoing transitions (every item is complete). > [!TIP] > **The canonical collection is the *set* of all distinct item sets.** Because $I_3$ and $I_7$ (would-be) contain the same items, we keep only one. Final collection: $C = \{I_0, I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4, I_5, I_6\}$. --- ## 3.3 Step 3 — Compute FOLLOW Sets Using the standard rules for FOLLOW (end-marker is `$`): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FOLLOW}(S') &= \{\$\} \quad \text{(FOLLOW rule: $ ends the start derivation)} \\ \text{FOLLOW}(S) &= \text{FOLLOW}(S') = \{\$\} \quad \text{(from } S' \rightarrow S \text{)} \\ \text{FOLLOW}(C) &= \begin{cases} \text{FIRST}(C) = \{c, d\} \cup \{\$\} & \text{(from } S \rightarrow CC \text{, first } C \text{)} \\ \text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\} & \text{(from } S \rightarrow CC \text{, second } C \text{)} \end{cases} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{FOLLOW}(C) = \{c, d, \$\} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.4 Step 4 — Build the SLR(1) Parsing Table Apply the rules from §2.3: **State I₀:** items $[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]$, $[S \rightarrow \cdot CC]$, $[C \rightarrow \cdot cC]$, $[C \rightarrow \cdot d]$ - Shift `c` → I₃, Shift `d` → I₄. - GOTO on `S` → 1, on `C` → 2. **State I₁:** item $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]$ - `ACTION[1, $] = accept`. **State I₂:** items $[S \rightarrow C \cdot C]$, $[C \rightarrow \cdot cC]$, $[C \rightarrow \cdot d]$ - Shift `c` → I₃, Shift `d` → I₄. - GOTO on `C` → 5. **State I₃:** items $[C \rightarrow c \cdot C]$, $[C \rightarrow \cdot cC]$, $[C \rightarrow \cdot d]$ - Shift `c` → I₃, Shift `d` → I₄. - GOTO on `C` → 6. **State I₄:** item $[C \rightarrow d \cdot]$ - Reduce on every symbol in `FOLLOW(C) = {c, d, $}` by production **(3)** $C \rightarrow d$. **State I₅:** item $[S \rightarrow CC \cdot]$ - Reduce on every symbol in `FOLLOW(S) = {$}` by production **(1)** $S \rightarrow CC$. **State I₆:** item $[C \rightarrow cC \cdot]$ - Reduce on every symbol in `FOLLOW(C) = {c, d, $}` by production **(2)** $C \rightarrow cC$. ### Final SLR(1) Parsing Table | State | ACTION on `c` | ACTION on `d` | ACTION on `$` | GOTO on `S` | GOTO on `C` | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **0** | s3 | s4 | — | 1 | 2 | | **1** | — | — | **acc** | — | — | | **2** | s3 | s4 | — | — | 5 | | **3** | s3 | s4 | — | — | 6 | | **4** | r3 | r3 | r3 | — | — | | **5** | — | — | r1 | — | — | | **6** | r2 | r2 | r2 | — | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conflict Check:** Every cell contains **at most one** entry. Therefore, this grammar is **SLR(1)**. --- ## 3.5 Step 5 — Trace the Parse of Input `c d c d $` The stack holds pairs of the form `(symbol, state)`. The top state drives the next move. | Step | Stack (symbols) | Stack (states) | Remaining Input | Action | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | `$` | `0` | `c d c d $` | s3 (shift `c` to state 3) | | 2 | `$ c` | `0 3` | `d c d $` | s4 (shift `d` to state 4) | | 3 | `$ c d` | `0 3 4` | `c d $` | r3 (reduce $C \rightarrow d$) | | 4 | `$ c C` | `0 3 6` | `c d $` | r2 (reduce $C \rightarrow cC$) | | 5 | `$ C` | `0 2` | `c d $` | s3 (shift `c` to state 3) | | 6 | `$ C c` | `0 2 3` | `d $` | s4 (shift `d` to state 4) | | 7 | `$ C c d` | `0 2 3 4` | `$` | r3 (reduce $C \rightarrow d$) | | 8 | `$ C c C` | `0 2 3 6` | `$` | r2 (reduce $C \rightarrow cC$) | | 9 | `$ C C` | `0 2 5` | `$` | r1 (reduce $S \rightarrow CC$) | | 10 | `$ S` | `0 1` | `$` | **acc** (accept) | > [!TIP] > The trace ended in state **1** with `$` as lookahead, where `ACTION[1, $] = acc`. The string `c d c d` is a valid sentence of the grammar, derived as: > $$S \Rightarrow CC \Rightarrow cC\,C \Rightarrow c\,d\,C \Rightarrow c\,d\,cC \Rightarrow c\,d\,c\,d$$ --- ## 3.6 Example Showing an SLR(1) Conflict > [!WARNING] > **Counter-Example Grammar (NOT SLR(1)):** > (1) $S \rightarrow aAd \mid bBd \mid aBe \mid bAe$ > (2) $A \rightarrow c$ > (3) $B \rightarrow c$ > > - `FOLLOW(A) = {d, e}` (since $A$ appears before $d$ in $aAd$ and before $e$ in $bAe$). > - `FOLLOW(B) = {d, e}` (since $B$ appears before $d$ in $bBd$ and before $e$ in $aBe$). > > After building the LR(0) automaton, the state containing the items $\{[A \rightarrow c \cdot],\; [B \rightarrow c \cdot]\}$ will produce: > - `ACTION[i, d] = r₂` and `ACTION[i, d] = r₃` simultaneously → **reduce–reduce conflict**. > > The grammar is **not SLR(1)**, although it is **LR(1)**. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of the SLR Parser ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef io fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef proc fill:#2e8b57,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef storage fill:#b8860b,stroke:#5c3d00,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef out fill:#8b0000,stroke:#3a0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px INPUT[/Input Buffer c d c d dollar/]:::io STACK[(STACK
Symbols and States)]:::storage STACKTOP{{Top State s}}:::storage LRDAF[(Canonical LR0
Item DFA)]:::storage PARSETABLE[(SLR Parsing Table
ACTION and GOTO)]:::storage FOLLOWSET[(FOLLOW Set Table)]:::storage LOOKUP[Lookup Module]:::proc DRIVER[Parsing Driver
Shift or Reduce or Accept]:::proc OUTPUT[/Production Sequence/]:::out INPUT -->|next token a| LOOKUP STACK --> STACKTOP --> LOOKUP LRDAF -.->|defines states| PARSETABLE FOLLOWSET -.->|reduces use FOLLOW| PARSETABLE PARSETABLE -->|entry action or goto| LOOKUP LOOKUP --> DRIVER DRIVER -->|push or pop| STACK DRIVER -->|advance pointer| INPUT DRIVER -->|emit rule| OUTPUT ``` > [!NOTE] > The **Canonical LR(0) Item DFA** and the **FOLLOW Set Table** are *pre-computed* at parser construction time (offline). The **Parsing Driver** is the only online component that consults the table per input token. --- ## 4.2 State Diagram — LR(0) Automaton for the Worked Example ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef kernel fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef accept fill:#2e8b57,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef reduce fill:#8b0000,stroke:#3a0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef neutral fill:#5a4fcf,stroke:#2c2480,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px I0((I0)):::neutral I1((I1)):::accept I2((I2)):::neutral I3((I3)):::neutral I4((I4)):::reduce I5((I5)):::reduce I6((I6)):::reduce I0 -->|S| I1 I0 -->|C| I2 I0 -->|c| I3 I0 -->|d| I4 I2 -->|C| I5 I2 -->|c| I3 I2 -->|d| I4 I3 -->|C| I6 I3 -->|c| I3 I3 -->|d| I4 ``` **Legend of states (kernel items only):** | State | Kernel Item(s) | Role | |:---:|:---|:---| | I0 | $[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]$, $[S \rightarrow \cdot CC]$ | Start state | | I1 | $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]$ | **Accept state** | | I2 | $[S \rightarrow C \cdot C]$ | Expecting $C$ or shift $c$/$d$ | | I3 | $[C \rightarrow c \cdot C]$ | Expecting $C$ or shift $c$/$d$ | | I4 | $[C \rightarrow d \cdot]$ | Reduce by $C \rightarrow d$ | | I5 | $[S \rightarrow CC \cdot]$ | Reduce by $S \rightarrow CC$ | | I6 | $[C \rightarrow cC \cdot]$ | Reduce by $C \rightarrow cC$ | --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Construction Workflow (Offline Phase) ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef step fill:#0b6e4f,stroke:#053d2c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef decision fill:#b8860b,stroke:#5c3d00,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef out fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px A[Augmented Grammar]:::step --> B[Compute Canonical
Collection of LR0 Items]:::step B --> C[Compute FOLLOW Sets
for all Non-terminals]:::step C --> D[Build SLR Parsing Table
ACTION and GOTO]:::step D --> E{Conflict
Check}:::decision E -->|No conflicts| F[Valid SLR1 Parser]:::out E -->|Shift-Reduce
or Reduce-Reduce| G[Grammar Not SLR1
Try CLR1 or LALR1]:::out ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Pattern]** ### Q1. What is an LR(0) item? How is it used in SLR parsing? **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **LR(0) item** is a production of the grammar with a dot `·` placed at some position on its right-hand side, denoting how much of the production has been seen so far at a given point during parsing. For example, for the production $A \rightarrow XYZ$, the four possible LR(0) items are $[A \rightarrow \cdot XYZ]$, $[A \rightarrow X \cdot YZ]$, $[A \rightarrow XY \cdot Z]$, and $[A \rightarrow XYZ \cdot]$. In **SLR parsing**, LR(0) items are used to **(i)** construct the canonical collection of item sets (the underlying DFA of viable prefixes) via the **CLOSURE** and **GOTO** operations, and **(ii)** identify reduction candidates — any complete item $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot]$ is a potential reduction. The SLR parser resolves *which* reductions to actually perform on a given input symbol by consulting the **FOLLOW set** of $A$. **(1 mark each point: definition, two roles.)** --- ### Q2. Why is the FOLLOW set essential in SLR(1) but not in LR(0) parsing? **Model Answer (3 marks):** In **LR(0)** parsing, reductions are performed *unconditionally* — whenever a complete item $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot]$ is on top of the stack, the parser reduces regardless of the lookahead symbol. This often causes erroneous reductions and limits LR(0) to a very small class of grammars. In **SLR(1)**, the parser uses the **FOLLOW set** of the left-hand side non-terminal $A$ as a **filter on reductions**: a reduction by $A \rightarrow \alpha$ is performed **only** if the current lookahead symbol $a$ belongs to $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$. This single-token lookahead (applied at reduce-time only) eliminates many spurious reductions and broadens the class of grammars SLR can handle — at the cost of computing FOLLOW sets in advance. **(1.5 marks for the LR(0) issue, 1.5 marks for SLR's FOLLOW-based resolution.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – December 2023 Pattern, Module 1, 14-Mark Question]** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Consider the following augmented grammar:** $$ \begin{aligned} (0)\;& S' \rightarrow S \\ (1)\;& S \rightarrow a\,S\,b \\ (2)\;& S \rightarrow a\,b \end{aligned} $$ **(a) Construct the canonical collection of LR(0) items for the above grammar. (7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Augment.** Already done (production 0). **Step 2 — I₀ = CLOSURE({[S' → ·S]})** $$ I_0 = \left\{ [S' \rightarrow \cdot S],\; [S \rightarrow \cdot aSb],\; [S \rightarrow \cdot ab] \right\} $$ **Step 3 — GOTO operations from I₀:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{GOTO}(I_0, S) &= \text{CLOSURE}(\{[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]\}) = I_1 \\ \text{GOTO}(I_0, a) &= \text{CLOSURE}(\{[S \rightarrow a \cdot Sb],\; [S \rightarrow a \cdot b]\}) = I_2 \end{aligned} $$ $$ I_1 = \{[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]\}, \qquad I_2 = \{[S \rightarrow a \cdot Sb],\; [S \rightarrow a \cdot b],\; [S \rightarrow \cdot aSb],\; [S \rightarrow \cdot ab]\} $$ **Step 4 — GOTO operations from I₂:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{GOTO}(I_2, S) &= \text{CLOSURE}(\{[S \rightarrow aS \cdot b]\}) = I_3 \\ \text{GOTO}(I_2, b) &= \text{CLOSURE}(\{[S \rightarrow ab \cdot]\}) = I_4 \\ \text{GOTO}(I_2, a) &= I_2 \quad \text{(recursive: same kernel)} \end{aligned} $$ $$ I_3 = \{[S \rightarrow aS \cdot b],\; [S \rightarrow \cdot aSb],\; [S \rightarrow \cdot ab]\}, \qquad I_4 = \{[S \rightarrow ab \cdot]\} $$ **Step 5 — GOTO from I₃:** $$ \text{GOTO}(I_3, b) = \text{CLOSURE}(\{[S \rightarrow aSb \cdot]\}) = I_5, \qquad I_5 = \{[S \rightarrow aSb \cdot]\} $$ **Step 6 — GOTO from I₄, I₅:** No new items. Construction terminates. $$ \boxed{C = \{I_0, I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4, I_5\}} $$ **(Award 1 mark for I₀, 2 marks for I₁, I₂, I₃, 1 mark for I₄, I₅, 1 mark for completeness of transitions, 1 mark for proper closure application, 1 mark for the final canonical collection.)** --- **(b) Compute the FOLLOW sets and build the SLR(1) parsing table. Is the grammar SLR(1)? Show the parse of input `a a b b b $`. (7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — FOLLOW sets:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FOLLOW}(S') &= \{\$\} \\ \text{FOLLOW}(S) &= \begin{cases} \text{FOLLOW}(S') = \{\$\} & \text{(from } S' \rightarrow S\text{)} \\ \text{FIRST}(b) = \{b\} & \text{(from } S \rightarrow aSb \text{)} \end{cases} \\ \Rightarrow \text{FOLLOW}(S) &= \{b, \$\} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — SLR(1) Table:** | State | ACTION on `a` | ACTION on `b` | ACTION on `$` | GOTO on `S` | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **0** | s2 | — | — | 1 | | **1** | — | — | **acc** | — | | **2** | s2 | s4 | — | 3 | | **3** | — | s5 | — | — | | **4** | r2 | r2 | r2 | — | | **5** | r1 | r1 | r1 | — | **Why each entry?** - `ACTION[4, a] = r2`, `ACTION[4, b] = r2`, `ACTION[4, $] = r2` because $I_4$ contains $[S \rightarrow ab \cdot]$ and $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{b, \$\}$ (rule of SLR says reduce on FOLLOW; $a$ is included because in the DFA it would never be a lookahead at I₄ — but per strict rule, we set on $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$; however here $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{b, \$\}$ so action on $a$ is *blank/error*; **correction applied below**): > [!IMPORTANT] > **Correction (strict SLR rule):** Since $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{b, \$\}$: > `ACTION[4, a] = error` (blank), `ACTION[4, b] = r2`, `ACTION[4, $] = r2`. > Similarly `ACTION[5, a] = error`, `ACTION[5, b] = r1`, `ACTION[5, $] = r1`. **Corrected SLR(1) Table:** | State | ACTION on `a` | ACTION on `b` | ACTION on `$` | GOTO on `S` | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **0** | s2 | — | — | 1 | | **1** | — | — | **acc** | — | | **2** | s2 | s4 | — | 3 | | **3** | — | s5 | — | — | | **4** | — | r2 | r2 | — | | **5** | — | r1 | r1 | — | **No conflicts ⇒ grammar is SLR(1).** **Step 3 — Parse of `a a b b b $`:** | # | Stack | Input | Action | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | `0` | `a a b b b $` | s2 | | 2 | `0 a 2` | `a b b b $` | s2 | | 3 | `0 a 2 a 2` | `b b b $` | s4 | | 4 | `0 a 2 a 2 b 4` | `b b $` | r2 ($S \rightarrow ab$) | | 5 | `0 a 2 a 2 S 3` | `b b $` | s5 | | 6 | `0 a 2 a 2 S 3 b 5` | `b $` | r1 ($S \rightarrow aSb$) | | 7 | `0 a 2 S 3` | `b $` | s5 | | 8 | `0 a 2 S 3 b 5` | `$` | r1 ($S \rightarrow aSb$) | | 9 | `0 S 1` | `$` | **acc** | **(2 marks for FOLLOW, 2 marks for table, 1 mark conflict conclusion, 2 marks for trace.)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a) With a suitable example, explain shift-reduce and reduce-reduce conflicts. Why does SLR fail to resolve them? (7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Shift-Reduce Conflict (3 marks):** A *shift-reduce conflict* arises in state $I_i$ when both a shift and a reduce are valid for the same lookahead terminal $a$. Example grammar: $$ \begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow E \\ E &\rightarrow E + T \mid T \end{aligned} $$ In the canonical collection, the state containing $[E \rightarrow E \cdot + T]$ (an item that can shift `+`) **and** $[T \rightarrow T \cdot * F]$ or any other reducible complete item whose FOLLOW contains `+` will face a shift-reduce conflict. The parser cannot decide whether to **shift `+`** (to extend the right-hand side) or **reduce** (to commit a smaller construct). In SLR, this happens whenever $a$ is in $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ for some complete item $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot]$ **and** $[B \rightarrow \beta \cdot a \gamma]$ also appears in the same state. **Reduce-Reduce Conflict (3 marks):** A *reduce-reduce conflict* arises in state $I_i$ when two different complete items request a reduce on the same lookahead. Classical example: $$ \begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow aAd \mid bBd \mid aBe \mid bAe \\ A &\rightarrow c \\ B &\rightarrow c \end{aligned} $$ The state with $\{[A \rightarrow c \cdot],\; [B \rightarrow c \cdot]\}$ has $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \text{FOLLOW}(B) = \{d, e\}$. On input $d$ or $e$, the parser cannot choose between reducing by $A \rightarrow c$ or $B \rightarrow c$. **Why SLR Fails (1 mark):** SLR(1) uses **the same FOLLOW set for all reductions of the same non-terminal**, irrespective of the *parsing context*. It cannot distinguish "this $A$ came from a position expecting $d$" vs. "this $A$ came from a position expecting $e$". The full **canonical LR(1) parser** solves this by attaching the lookahead **directly to each item**, producing LR(1) items $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot, a]$ where $a$ is the *context-sensitive* lookahead — not the generic FOLLOW set. --- **(b) Construct the SLR(1) parsing table for the following grammar and verify whether it is SLR(1). (7 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} (0)\;& S' \rightarrow S \\ (1)\;& S \rightarrow A\,A \\ (2)\;& A \rightarrow a\,A \\ (3)\;& A \rightarrow b \end{aligned} $$ **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Canonical Collection:** $$ \begin{aligned} I_0 &= \{[S' \rightarrow \cdot S],\; [S \rightarrow \cdot AA],\; [A \rightarrow \cdot aA],\; [A \rightarrow \cdot b]\} \\ I_1 &= \text{GOTO}(I_0, S) = \{[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]\} \\ I_2 &= \text{GOTO}(I_0, A) = \{[S \rightarrow A \cdot A],\; [A \rightarrow \cdot aA],\; [A \rightarrow \cdot b]\} \\ I_3 &= \text{GOTO}(I_0, a) = \{[A \rightarrow a \cdot A],\; [A \rightarrow \cdot aA],\; [A \rightarrow \cdot b]\} \\ I_4 &= \text{GOTO}(I_0, b) = \{[A \rightarrow b \cdot]\} \\ I_5 &= \text{GOTO}(I_2, A) = \{[S \rightarrow AA \cdot]\} \\ I_6 &= \text{GOTO}(I_2, a) = I_3 \\ I_7 &= \text{GOTO}(I_2, b) = I_4 \\ I_8 &= \text{GOTO}(I_3, A) = \{[A \rightarrow aA \cdot]\} \\ I_9 &= \text{GOTO}(I_3, a) = I_3 \\ I_{10} &= \text{GOTO}(I_3, b) = I_4 \end{aligned} $$ **Distinct sets:** $C = \{I_0, I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4, I_5, I_8\}$. **Step 2 — FOLLOW sets:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FOLLOW}(S') &= \{\$\} \\ \text{FOLLOW}(S) &= \{\$\} \quad \text{(from } S' \rightarrow S\text{)} \\ \text{FOLLOW}(A) &= \text{FIRST}(A) \cup \text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{a, b\} \cup \{\$\} = \{a, b, \$\} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — SLR(1) Parsing Table:** | State | ACTION on `a` | ACTION on `b` | ACTION on `$` | GOTO on `S` | GOTO on `A` | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **0** | s3 | s4 | — | 1 | 2 | | **1** | — | — | **acc** | — | — | | **2** | s3 | s4 | — | — | 5 | | **3** | s3 | s4 | — | — | 8 | | **4** | r3 | r3 | r3 | — | — | | **5** | — | — | r1 | — | — | | **8** | r2 | r2 | r2 | — | — | **Conflict Check:** Each cell has at most one entry ⇒ **No conflict ⇒ Grammar is SLR(1).** **(2 marks for item sets, 2 marks for FOLLOW, 2 marks for table, 1 mark for conflict conclusion.)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks in SLR(1) questions (KTU 2024 valuation key observations):** > > 1. **Forgetting the augmented production $S' \rightarrow S$.** *(−1 mark)* — Without it, the parser has no unique accept state. > 2. **Incomplete CLOSURE computation.** *(−1 to −2 marks)* — Missing items where $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B \beta]$ requires adding $[B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma]$ for *every* production of $B$. > 3. **Using the wrong set for reductions.** A common error is reducing on **FIRST** instead of **FOLLOW**. The correct rule is: reduce $A \rightarrow \alpha$ on terminal $a$ only if $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$. > 4. **Ignoring the $GOTO$ table entries** — They must be filled for **every non-terminal**, not just terminals. > 5. **Not verifying the canonical collection terminates.** Students often miss GOTO operations on already-visited states, leading to an under-counted DFA. > 6. **Forgetting the end-marker `$`** in FOLLOW of the start symbol. `$` (ASCII **36**) must always be in $\text{FOLLOW}(S')$. > 7. **Misclassifying the grammar:** A grammar can be LR(0) ⊂ SLR(1) ⊂ LALR(1) ⊂ LR(1) ⊂ Unambiguous CFL. Always state the *narrowest* class to which the grammar belongs. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — SLR(1) Parser (Module 1):** > > - ☐ **SLR = LR(0) automaton + FOLLOW-based reductions.** Memorize this one-line summary. > - ☐ **Augment the grammar first**, then build the canonical LR(0) item collection using **CLOSURE** and **GOTO**. > - ☐ **CLOSURE** adds items of the form $[B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma]$ whenever $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B \beta]$ is present. > - ☐ **GOTO(I, X)** moves the dot *past* the symbol $X$. > - ☐ **FOLLOW($S'$) = $\{\$\}$** is the *seed* for all FOLLOW-set computation. > - ☐ **Table-filling rules:** shift on terminal after `·`; reduce on FOLLOW of LHS non-terminal for complete items; GOTO on non-terminals; accept on $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]$ at `$`. > - ☐ **Two conflict types:** shift-reduce (shift vs. reduce in same cell) and reduce-reduce (two different reduces in same cell). > - ☐ **State count hierarchy:** LR(0) ≤ SLR(1) = LALR(1) ≤ LR(1). Table size: **SLR has the same number of states as LR(0)** — only the entries in the ACTION table differ. > - ☐ **Order of preference in conflicts:** Shift > Reduce (the parser prefers to defer commitment). > - ☐ **Standard input convention:** End of input is denoted `$` (also called **EOS**, *end-of-stack marker* in some texts). > - ☐ **The parsing driver is a pushdown automaton** whose stack alternates between symbols and states; the *top state* (not the top symbol) drives the next move. > - ☐ **If a grammar is not SLR(1), try CLR(1) or LALR(1)** before concluding that the grammar is ambiguous. > - ☐ **Dr. Aho's classic example** $S \rightarrow CC$, $C \rightarrow cC \mid d$ is the *de facto* textbook SLR(1) example — practice its full construction until automatic. > - ☐ **Remember the formulae:** > - Number of states $\leq 2^{|P|}$ in the worst case (where $|P|$ is the number of productions), but typically far less. > - Stack grows by **2 entries per shift** (symbol + state) and shrinks by $2 \cdot |\alpha|$ on reduce by $A \rightarrow \alpha$. ---

topics.title: More Powerful LR Parsers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** More Powerful LR Parsers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining the Class of "More Powerful" LR Parsers

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** hierarchy of bottom-up shift-reduce parsers, the term **"More Powerful LR Parsers"** formally refers to the two advanced deterministic parsing techniques: **Canonical LR(1)** (also called **CLR(1)**) and **Look-Ahead LR(1)** (also called **LALR(1)**). These parsers extend the basic LR(0) and SLR(1) methods by incorporating **lookahead symbols** into the parsing items themselves, thereby eliminating shift-reduce and reduce-reduce conflicts that plague simpler parsers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Canonical LR(1) Item** is a pair `[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta, a]`, where:
> - `A \rightarrow \alpha \beta` is a production in the augmented grammar `G'`
> - `a` is a **lookahead terminal** (or `$`) that can legally follow the use of `A` in a sentential form.
> - The lookahead is placed in the item to indicate that the reduction `A \rightarrow \alpha` is valid **only** if the next input symbol is `a`.

The **LALR(1)** parser, on the other hand, is an optimized version of CLR(1) that merges LR(1) states with **identical LR(0) cores**, dramatically reducing memory requirements while preserving most of the parsing power.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Bouncer with a VIP List

Imagine three bouncers at the entrance of a club (a syntax-checking analogy for compilers):

1. **LR(0) Bouncer**: Lets anyone in based purely on **appearance** (the item's core, ignoring context). He makes mistakes — sometimes let-ins cause fights (conflicts).
2. **SLR(1) Bouncer**: Checks the **FOLLOW set** (the guest list of who's allowed to follow whom). Better, but still has trouble when two guests share the same last name (FOLLOW collisions).
3. **CLR(1) Bouncer**: Carries a **personalized VIP card** for each guest — knows exactly *which next word* will make a reduction valid. He never makes a wrong call, but his guest list is huge.
4. **LALR(1) Bouncer**: The *efficient cousin* of the CLR(1) bouncer. He noticed that several VIP cards have the *same body but different notes*. He merges them into one card with the **union of notes**. Almost as accurate, but uses far less paper.

This bouncer analogy maps perfectly to the parser hierarchy: **LR(0) ⊂ SLR(1) ⊂ LALR(1) ⊂ CLR(1)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Power (Strict Inclusion):**
> $$\text{LR}(0) \subset \text{SLR}(1) \subset \text{LALR}(1) \subset \text{CLR}(1)$$
> Every grammar accepted by LR(0) is also accepted by SLR(1), and so on up the chain. The trade-off is **memory vs. language coverage**.

## 1.3 Why We Need "More Powerful" Parsers

SLR(1) fails on grammars that are **not SLR** — those where the FOLLOW sets cause spurious conflicts. Consider the classical non-SLR grammar:

$$S \rightarrow L = R \quad \mid \quad R$$
$$L \rightarrow * R \quad \mid \quad \text{id}$$
$$R \rightarrow L$$

This grammar causes an **SLR conflict** because `R` appears in FOLLOW(`L`), and both `*` and `=` are in FOLLOW(`L`) — yet a *single* lookahead cannot distinguish the correct action. **LR(1) items solve this** by carrying lookaheads *with the items themselves*, not with the non-terminals globally.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of an LR(1) Item

An **LR(1) item** is the smallest unit of information a shift-reduce parser uses. It has **two components**:

| Component | Symbol | Meaning |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Core (LR(0) Kernel) | `A → α·β` | Where we are in a production |
| Lookahead | `a` (∈ T ∪ {`$`}) | The input symbol that justifies a reduce action |

The dot `·` indicates the parsing position. Items with the dot at the end (`A → α·`) are **complete items** — candidates for reduction.

## 2.2 The Two Core Operations on LR(1) Items

### A. CLOSURE Operation
Given a set of LR(1) items `I`:

$$\text{CLOSURE}(I) = I \cup \big\{ [B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma, b] \;\big|\; [A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B \beta, a] \in I, \; B \rightarrow \gamma \in P, \; b \in \text{FIRST}(\beta a) \big\}$$

**Intuition:** If we may need to derive a `B` next, then we may need to derive any production of `B`. The lookahead `b` is computed from what can legally follow `B` in the current context — namely, `FIRST(βa)`.

### B. GOTO Operation
Given item set `I` and grammar symbol `X`:

$$\text{GOTO}(I, X) = \text{CLOSURE}\big(\{ [A \rightarrow \alpha X \cdot \beta, a] \;|\; [A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot X \beta, a] \in I \}\big)$$

**Intuition:** Move the dot past the symbol `X` for every item that has `X` as the next symbol.

## 2.3 Lookahead Propagation: The Heart of CLR(1)

When a kernel item generates new items via GOTO, its **lookahead is inherited unchanged** by the closure items *only if the dot is followed by a terminal that the grammar determines*. The formal rule is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lookahead Propagation Rule:**
> If `[A → α·Bβ, a]` and `β` derives a string starting with terminal `b`, then any item `[B → ·γ, b]` is added to the closure of the GOTO state. This lookahead `b` may differ from the parent's lookahead `a`.

## 2.4 The LALR(1) Trick: Merging by Cores

The **LALR(1)** parsing tables are constructed from CLR(1) by the following procedure:

1. Build the full CLR(1) collection of item sets `C = {I₀, I₁, ..., Iₙ}`.
2. Define the **core** of an LR(1) item set `I` as the set of LR(0) kernels (ignoring lookahead).
3. **Merge** all item sets having the same core. If `Iᵢ` and `Iⱼ` have the same core, replace both with `Iᵢ ∪ Iⱼ` (union of all items, with lookaights united).
4. The resulting number of states equals the number of LR(0) states — **the same as SLR(1)!**

> [!WARNING]
> **LALR(1) can still exhibit reduce-reduce conflicts after merging** that were not present in CLR(1). It can **never introduce a shift-reduce conflict** (this is a famous theorem: GOTO of merged states preserves the shift actions, since the shift target's core is the same).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation |
|---------|---------------|----------|
| **LR(1) Item** | `[A → α·β, a]` | `A` is non-terminal, `α,β ∈ (N∪T)*`, `a ∈ T ∪ {$}` |
| **CLOSURE Addition** | Add `[B → ·γ, b]` where `b ∈ FIRST(βa)` | `β` follows `B` in parent item, `a` is parent lookahead |
| **GOTO Result** | Items with dot moved past `X` | Includes CLOSURE |
| **First Item Set** | `I₀ = CLOSURE({[S' → ·S, $]})` | `S'` is augmented start symbol |
| **Augmented Grammar** | `G'` adds `S' → S` | `S'` never appears on RHS |
| **LALR Merge** | `Iᵢ ∪ Iⱼ` if `core(Iᵢ) = core(Iⱼ)` | Union of LR(1) items with same core |
| **# of LALR States** | Equals # of LR(0) states | Always ≤ # of CLR(1) states |
| **Power Hierarchy** | `LR(0) ⊂ SLR(1) ⊂ LALR(1) ⊂ CLR(1)` | Strict subset relations |
| **Conflict Types** | Shift-Reduce (S/R) or Reduce-Reduce (R/R) | Detected in item sets |
| **LALR Safety** | Merging cannot create S/R conflicts | Proven by theorem on GOTO targets |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

**YACC (Yet Another Compiler-Compiler)** and its GNU successor **Bison** use **LALR(1)** as their default parsing method. This is a deliberate engineering choice:

- **Memory efficiency**: LALR(1) tables are the same size as SLR(1) tables (proportional to the number of LR(0) states), but handle far more grammars.
- **Predictive power**: LALR(1) successfully parses virtually all practical programming language grammars (C, Java, Pascal).
- **Industry standard**: GCC, Clang, and most production compilers use LALR(1) or its variants.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: CLR(1) Item Set Construction

Consider the following grammar (a classic KTU-style example):

$$S' \rightarrow S$$
$$S \rightarrow CC$$
$$C \rightarrow cC \;\mid\; d$$

We will construct the **Canonical LR(1)** collection of item sets. The augmented grammar has productions: `S' → S`, `S → CC`, `C → cC`, `C → d`.

### Step 1: Construct the Initial Item Set `I₀`

Start with the kernel item `[S' → ·S, $]`. Apply CLOSURE.

For item `[S' → ·S, $]`:
- `S → CC` is a production
- The dot is followed by `C` (the first symbol of the RHS)
- Compute `FIRST(ε$) = {$}` (since the rest of RHS after `C` is empty)
- Add `[S → ·CC, $]`

For item `[S → ·CC, $]`:
- `C → cC` is a production
- The dot is followed by `c` (a terminal)
- Compute `FIRST(c$) = {c}`
- Add `[C → ·cC, c]`
- `C → d` is a production
- Compute `FIRST(c$) = {c}` (same context)
- Add `[C → ·d, c]`

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_0 &= \big\{ [S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \$], \\
     &\quad [S \rightarrow \cdot CC, \$], \\
     &\quad [C \rightarrow \cdot cC, c], \\
     &\quad [C \rightarrow \cdot d, c] \big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute GOTO Transitions from `I₀`

**GOTO(I₀, S):** Items with `S` after the dot: `[S' → ·S, $]`. Move dot → `[S' → S·, $]`. No further closure.

$$
I_1 = \{ [S' \rightarrow S \cdot, \$] \}
$$

**GOTO(I₀, C):** Items with `C` after the dot: `[S → ·CC, $]`. Move dot → `[S → C·C, $]`. Apply CLOSURE: `C` is followed by `$` (end of RHS), so we add `[C → ·cC, $]` and `[C → ·d, $]`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_2 &= \big\{ [S \rightarrow C \cdot C, \$], \\
     &\quad [C \rightarrow \cdot cC, \$], \\
     &\quad [C \rightarrow \cdot d, \$] \big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**GOTO(I₀, c):** Items with `c` after the dot: `[C → ·cC, c]`. Move dot → `[C → c·C, c]`. Closure: `C` followed by `c`, so add `[C → ·cC, c]` and `[C → ·d, c]`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_3 &= \big\{ [C \rightarrow c \cdot C, c], \\
     &\quad [C \rightarrow \cdot cC, c], \\
     &\quad [C \rightarrow \cdot d, c] \big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**GOTO(I₀, d):** Items with `d` after the dot: `[C → ·d, c]`. Move dot → `[C → d·, c]`.

$$
I_4 = \{ [C \rightarrow d \cdot, c] \}
$$

### Step 3: Continue GOTO Computations

**GOTO(I₂, C):** Item `[S → C·C, $]` → `[S → CC·, $]`.

$$
I_5 = \{ [S \rightarrow CC \cdot, \$] \}
$$

**GOTO(I₂, c):** Item `[C → ·cC, $]` → `[C → c·C, $]`. Closure adds `[C → ·cC, $]` and `[C → ·d, $]`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_6 &= \big\{ [C \rightarrow c \cdot C, \$], \\
     &\quad [C \rightarrow \cdot cC, \$], \\
     &\quad [C \rightarrow \cdot d, \$] \big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**GOTO(I₂, d):** Item `[C → ·d, $]` → `[C → d·, $]`.

$$
I_7 = \{ [C \rightarrow d \cdot, \$] \}
$$

**GOTO(I₃, C):** Item `[C → c·C, c]` → `[C → cC·, c]`.

$$
I_8 = \{ [C \rightarrow cC \cdot, c] \}
$$

**GOTO(I₆, C):** Item `[C → c·C, $]` → `[C → cC·, $]`.

$$
I_9 = \{ [C \rightarrow cC \cdot, \$] \}
$$

### Step 4: Build the Parsing Table

For each state `Iᵢ`:
- **Shift** on terminal `a` if `GOTO(Iᵢ, a) = Iⱼ`: action[i, a] = `shift j`
- **Reduce** by `A → α` if `[A → α·, a] ∈ Iᵢ`: action[i, a] = `reduce A → α`
- **Accept** if `[S' → S·, $] ∈ Iᵢ`: action[i, $] = `accept`
- **GOTO** on non-terminal `A`: goto[i, A] = `j` if `GOTO(Iᵢ, A) = Iⱼ`

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Four Parser Variants

| Property | LR(0) | SLR(1) | LALR(1) | CLR(1) |
|----------|-------|--------|---------|--------|
| **Item Form** | `[A → α·β]` | Same as LR(0) | Merged LR(1) | `[A → α·β, a]` |
| **Lookahead Source** | None | FOLLOW set | Per-item, merged | Per-item |
| **# States** | `n` | `n` | `n` | `≥ n` (often much more) |
| **Reduces Use** | All items in state | FOLLOW-based | Item-lookahead | Item-lookahead |
| **Conflict Class** | Many | Fewer | Very few | Fewest |
| **Practical Tools** | Educational only | None popular | **YACC, Bison** | Protobuf, ANTLR-like |
| **Memory Cost** | Low | Low | Low | High |
| **Detection of S/R** | Direct | FOLLOW intersection | Item-lookahead overlap | Item-lookahead overlap |

## 3.3 Conflict Detection: The S/R Conflict Test

For an LR(1) state containing both:
- A **shift item** `[A → α·aβ, b]` (where `a` is a terminal), and
- A **reduce item** `[B → γ·, c]`

A **shift-reduce conflict** exists if `a = c` (the same terminal can both shift and reduce). To resolve, the parser table would have two entries in `action[state, a]`, which is illegal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Rule for KTU Board Exams:**
> If `FIRST(βb) ∩ {terminals} ≠ ∅` after a kernel item, the closure must include those terminal lookaheads. Neglecting this step is the **#1 reason students lose marks** in CLR(1) construction problems.

## 3.4 LALR Merging: Step-by-Step

Given CLR(1) states above, observe that `I₃` and `I₆` have the **same LR(0) core**:

$$
\text{core}(I_3) = \text{core}(I_6) = \{[C \rightarrow c\cdot C], [C \rightarrow \cdot cC], [C \rightarrow \cdot d]\}
$$

Similarly, `I₄` and `I₇`, and `I₈` and `I₉` have identical cores.

**Merged LALR(1) states:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{36} &= I_3 \cup I_6 = \big\{ [C \rightarrow c \cdot C, c/\$], \\
        &\quad [C \rightarrow \cdot cC, c/\$], \\
        &\quad [C \rightarrow \cdot d, c/\$] \big\} \\
I_{47} &= I_4 \cup I_7 = \big\{ [C \rightarrow d \cdot, c/\$] \big\} \\
I_{89} &= I_8 \cup I_9 = \big\{ [C \rightarrow cC \cdot, c/\$] \big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

The notation `c/$` means the lookahead is the **union** `{c, $}`. The number of LALR states = **7** (I₀, I₁, I₂, I₃₆, I₄₇, I₅, I₈₉), matching the LR(0) count exactly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: CLR(1) Item Set Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Augmented Grammar G'"] --> B["Initialize I0 = CLOSURE of S' to .S with lookahead dollar"]
    B --> C{"For each symbol X in N union T"}
    C --> D["Compute GOTO of I, X"]
    D --> E["Apply CLOSURE on shifted items"]
    E --> F{"New item set Ij?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Add Ij to canonical collection C"]
    F -- No --> H["Skip duplicate state"]
    G --> C
    H --> C
    C --> I["All symbols processed?"]
    I -- No --> C
    I -- Yes --> J["Canonical Collection C is Complete"]
    J --> K["Build CLR Parsing Tables: Action and Goto"]
    K --> L{"LALR Conversion?"}
    L -- Yes --> M["Group item sets by LR0 Core"]
    M --> N["Union items with same core to form merged states"]
    N --> O["Build LALR Parsing Tables"]
    L -- No --> P["Output CLR Tables"]
    O --> Q["End"]
    P --> Q
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: The LR(1) Parsing Engine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"]
        TOK["Token Stream from Lexer"]
    end

    subgraph Stack["Parsing Stack"]
        S0["State 0"]
        S1["State 1"]
        Sn["State n"]
    end

    subgraph Table["Parsing Tables (CLR1 or LALR1)"]
        ACT["Action Table: shift, reduce, accept, error"]
        GOT["Goto Table: state transitions on non-terminals"]
    end

    subgraph Logic["Parser Decision Logic"]
        TOP["Top of Stack: Current State si"]
        PEEK["Peek next input symbol ai"]
        LOOKUP["Lookup action si, ai in Action Table"]
        DECIDE{"Action Type?"}
    end

    TOK --> PEEK
    S0 --> TOP
    Sn --> TOP
    TOP --> LOOKUP
    PEEK --> LOOKUP
    LOOKUP --> DECIDE
    DECIDE -- "shift j" --> SHIFT["Push state j onto stack; advance input"]
    DECIDE -- "reduce A to alpha" --> REDUCE["Pop 2 times alpha length; push Goto top, A"]
    DECIDE -- "accept" --> DONE["Parsing Successful"]
    DECIDE -- "error" --> ERR["Invoke Error Recovery Routine"]
    SHIFT --> GOT
    REDUCE --> GOT
    GOT --> TOP
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Conflict Resolution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Encounter Terminal X in State Ii"] --> B{"Does Ii Contain Shift Item on X?"}
    B -- Yes --> C{"Does Ii Contain Reduce Item with Lookahead X?"}
    B -- No --> D{"Does Ii Contain Reduce Item with Lookahead X?"}
    C -- Yes --> E["SHIFT-REDUCE CONFLICT"]
    C -- No --> F["Perform SHIFT action"]
    D -- Yes --> G{"Any other Reduce Item with Lookahead X?"}
    D -- No --> H["Perform REDUCE action"]
    G -- Yes --> I["REDUCE-REDUCE CONFLICT"]
    G -- No --> J["Perform single REDUCE action"]
    E --> K["Use LALR or CLR1 instead of SLR1"]
    I --> K
    K --> L["If still conflict: Grammar is Not LR1"]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0["LR0 Parser: No Lookahead. Core only."] -->|subset| S1["SLR1 Parser: Adds FOLLOW-based lookahead."]
    S1 -->|subset| LA["LALR1 Parser: Merged LR1 items by core."]
    LA -->|subset| C1["CLR1 Parser: Full per-item lookaheads."]
    L0 --- M1["Memory: O n states"]
    S1 --- M1
    LA --- M1
    C1 --- M2["Memory: Often greater than n states"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between LR(0) item and LR(1) item. Why is the latter more powerful?**

**Model Answer:**

An **LR(0) item** is a production with a dot indicating parser progress, written as `A → α·β`, with no associated terminal. An **LR(1) item** extends this to `[A → α·β, a]`, where `a` is a **lookahead terminal** that legitimizes a reduce action only when the next input symbol is `a`.

The LR(1) item is more powerful because it carries **context-sensitive information** directly. This eliminates the spurious shift-reduce and reduce-reduce conflicts that arise in SLR(1) when the FOLLOW set of a non-terminal contains symbols that are **not** actually valid in specific contexts. By localizing the lookahead to each item, LR(1) refines when a reduction is legal, accepting grammars that SLR(1) rejects.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the relationship between CLR(1) and LALR(1) parsers in terms of number of states and parsing power?**

**Model Answer:**

CLR(1) and LALR(1) have the **same parsing power** for shift-reduce conflicts (LALR(1) never introduces new S/R conflicts upon merging), but LALR(1) can introduce reduce-reduce conflicts that did not exist in CLR(1). In terms of states, **CLR(1) typically has more states** because it distinguishes item sets by both core *and* lookahead, while **LALR(1) merges item sets with identical cores**, yielding exactly as many states as the corresponding LR(0) automaton. Thus, LALR(1) trades a small loss of precision (in rare R/R conflict cases) for substantial memory savings.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper]

**Q: (a)** Construct the **CLR(1) parsing table** for the following grammar:
`S → A A`, `A → a A | b`

**(b)** Explain the CLOSURE and GOTO operations for LR(1) item sets with a suitable example.

#### Solution to (a): CLR(1) Item Set Construction

**Step 1: Augment the grammar**
Add `S' → S`. Productions: `S' → S`, `S → AA`, `A → aA`, `A → b`.

**Step 2: Build I₀**
Start: `[S' → ·S, $]`. Closure on `S` adds `[S → ·AA, $]`. For each `A` in RHS, FIRST of remainder is `FIRST(ε$) = {$}`, so add `[A → ·aA, $]` and `[A → ·b, $]`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_0 &= \big\{ [S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \$], \\
     &\quad [S \rightarrow \cdot AA, \$], \\
     &\quad [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, \$], \\
     &\quad [A \rightarrow \cdot b, \$] \big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute GOTO transitions**

`GOTO(I₀, S) = I₁ = {[S' → S·, $]}` (Accept state)

`GOTO(I₀, A) = I₂ = {[S → A·A, $]} ∪ closure → {[A → ·aA, $], [A → ·b, $]}`

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_2 &= \big\{ [S \rightarrow A \cdot A, \$], \\
     &\quad [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, \$], \\
     &\quad [A \rightarrow \cdot b, \$] \big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

`GOTO(I₀, a) = I₃ = {[A → a·A, $]} ∪ closure → {[A → ·aA, $], [A → ·b, $]}`

$$
I_3 = \big\{ [A \rightarrow a \cdot A, \$], [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, \$], [A \rightarrow \cdot b, \$] \big\}
$$

`GOTO(I₀, b) = I₄ = {[A → b·, $]}` (Reduce by `A → b`)

**Step 4: Continue from I₂**

`GOTO(I₂, A) = I₅ = {[S → AA·, $]}` (Reduce by `S → AA`)

`GOTO(I₂, a) = I₆ = {[A → a·A, $]} ∪ closure` — Note this is the same items as `I₃` core-wise, but with the *same* lookaheads, so `I₆ = I₃` (merged or duplicated).

`GOTO(I₂, b) = I₇ = {[A → b·, $]}` = `I₄`

**Step 5: From I₃**

`GOTO(I₃, A) = I₈ = {[A → aA·, $]}` (Reduce by `A → aA`)

**Step 6: Construct the parsing table**

| State | a | b | $ | S | A |
|-------|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | s3 | s4 | — | 1 | 2 |
| 1 | — | — | acc | — | — |
| 2 | s3 | s4 | — | — | 5 |
| 3 | s3 | s4 | — | — | 8 |
| 4 | — | — | r(A→b) | — | — |
| 5 | — | — | r(S→AA) | — | — |
| 8 | — | — | r(A→aA) | — | — |

where `sn` = shift to state `n`, `r(X)` = reduce by production `X`.

#### Solution to (b): CLOSURE and GOTO

**CLOSURE(I):** Given a set of LR(1) items `I`, repeatedly add items of the form `[B → ·γ, b]` for every item `[A → α·Bβ, a] ∈ I` and every production `B → γ`, where `b ∈ FIRST(βa)`. The closure is the fixed point of this addition.

**GOTO(I, X):** Computes the next state when the parser reads symbol `X`. It first forms the set of items obtained by advancing the dot past `X`, then applies CLOSURE.

**Example:** Starting from `I₀ = {[S' → ·S, $]}`, CLOSURE adds `[S → ·AA, $]` (because `S` has a production and `FIRST(ε$) = {$}`). Then GOTO(I₀, A) advances the dot in `[S → ·AA, $]` to get `[S → A·A, $]`, and CLOSURE then adds items for `A`'s productions with lookaheads derived from `FIRST(ε$)`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q: (a)** Construct the **LALR(1) parsing table** for the grammar:
`S → A | b`, `A → a A | a`

**(b)** Compare LR(0), SLR(1), LALR(1), and CLR(1) parsers with respect to states, conflicts, and practical use.

#### Solution to (a): LALR(1) Construction

**Augment:** `S' → S`, `S → A`, `S → b`, `A → aA`, `A → a`.

**Build CLR(1) item sets:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_0 &= \{[S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \$], [S \rightarrow \cdot A, \$], [S \rightarrow \cdot b, \$], [A \rightarrow \cdot aA, a/\$], [A \rightarrow \cdot a, a/\$]\}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Note: `[A → ·aA, $]` from `A` after `S → ·A`, and `[A → ·a, $]` similarly; both `a` and `$` appear as lookaheads for the items generated from `S → ·A` because the next symbol following `A` in the RHS of `S → A` is empty, so the lookahead equals the parent's lookahead `$`.)

`GOTO(I₀, S) = I₁ = {[S' → S·, $]}`
`GOTO(I₀, A) = I₂ = {[S → A·, $]}`
`GOTO(I₀, b) = I₃ = {[S → b·, $]}`
`GOTO(I₀, a) = I₄ = {[A → a·A, a], [A → ·aA, a], [A → ·a, a]}`

**GOTO(I₄, A) = I₅ = {[A → aA·, a]}` (Reduce by `A → aA`)

**Merge by cores:** All item sets in this small grammar already have unique cores, so LALR(1) = CLR(1) here. The resulting parsing table has **6 states** (I₀ through I₅).

**Parsing table:**

| State | a | b | $ | S | A |
|-------|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | s4 | s3 | — | 1 | 2 |
| 1 | — | — | acc | — | — |
| 2 | — | — | r(S→A) | — | — |
| 3 | — | — | r(S→b) | — | — |
| 4 | s4 | — | — | — | 5 |
| 5 | r(A→aA) | — | — | — | — |

#### Solution to (b): Comparison

| Aspect | LR(0) | SLR(1) | LALR(1) | CLR(1) |
|--------|-------|--------|---------|--------|
| **# States** | `n` | `n` | `n` | ≥ n (often 2-3×) |
| **Memory** | Lowest | Lowest | Lowest | Highest |
