| **S/R Conflicts** | Many | Moderate | Few | Fewest |
| **R/R Conflicts** | Many | Moderate | Few (can be added by merging) | Fewest |
| **Construction** | Easy | Easy | Moderate | Hardest |
| **Practical Use** | Theoretical | Theoretical | **YACC, Bison, GCC** | Specialized tools |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to augment** the grammar with `S' → S` — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Incorrectly computing `FIRST(βa)`** in CLOSURE — use `β` from the *current item's RHS after the dot*, not from anywhere else. Costs 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Confusing LALR merge with union of GOTO targets** — remember to merge *item sets* with the same LR(0) core, not the same GOTO.
> 4. **Mislabeling the Accept state** — only `I` containing `[S' → S·, $]` can be marked `acc` on `$`. Other complete items with lookahead `$` are reduce actions.
> 5. **Skipping closure when a non-terminal follows the dot** — failing to add `·` items for that non-terminal's productions is the most common error.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- **LR(1) Item = Core + Lookahead.** Never forget the lookahead component `[..., a]` when writing items.
- **CLOSURE rule:** Add `[B → ·γ, b]` for every `[A → α·Bβ, a]` where `b ∈ FIRST(βa)`. The `a` is the parent's lookahead.
- **GOTO rule:** Advance the dot past `X`, then apply CLOSURE. Lookaheads are *inherited* from the parent item.
- **LALR(1) = CLR(1) states merged by core.** Number of LALR states = number of LR(0) states, but with per-item lookaheads.
- **Power Hierarchy:** `LR(0) ⊂ SLR(1) ⊂ LALR(1) ⊂ CLR(1)`. Always remember this is a **strict** inclusion.
- **LALR is YACC/Bison standard.** Any non-LR(1) grammar cannot be parsed by any of these methods.
- **Lookahead propagation does not require re-computation** when the dot passes a terminal — only when a non-terminal is added to the closure.
- **Conflict types:** Shift-Reduce (S/R) and Reduce-Reduce (R/R). LALR(1) merging can introduce R/R conflicts (rarely S/R).
- **Accept state** is always the state containing `[S' → S·, $]`. All other `$`-reduce items use the production, not accept.
- **Augmented grammar is mandatory** — every LR(1) construction begins with `S' → S` added to the productions.
- **For KTU 14-mark questions**, expect: (i) construct item sets, (ii) build the parsing table, (iii) trace a string, (iv) convert to LALR.
- **The "core" of an item set** = the set of LR(0) items obtained by stripping lookaheads. Two item sets can be merged iff their cores are identical.
- **GOTO targets of merged states** remain consistent (GOTO on a terminal that shifts to a state will point to the merged equivalent), so shift actions are preserved during LALR merging.

**End of Notes — More Powerful LR Parsers (Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Syntax-Directed Definitions - Inherited and Synthesized Attributes. S-Attributed Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Syntax-Directed Definitions - Inherited and Synthesized Attributes. S-Attributed Definitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD)

A **Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD)** is a context-free grammar augmented with a set of **attributes** and a set of **semantic rules** (also called *semantic actions*) that are attached to each production of the grammar. Each symbol of the grammar (terminals and non-terminals) may carry a collection of attributes, and the semantic rules specify how the values of these attributes are computed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> An **SDD** is a pair $(\mathcal{G}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{R})$ where:
> - $\mathcal{G} = (V_N, V_T, P, S)$ is a context-free grammar
> - $\mathcal{A}$ is a finite set of attributes attached to grammar symbols
> - $\mathcal{R}$ is a finite set of semantic rules of the form $b := f(c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k)$, where $b$ and $c_i$ are attributes of terminals/non-terminals appearing in the same production.

The grammar symbol $X$ is said to have attributes if we write $X.a$ for an attribute $a$ of $X$. A **production** $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \ldots X_n$ together with its semantic rules is called a **production rule in the SDD**.

### 1.2 Synthesized Attributes

A **synthesized attribute** is an attribute whose value at a parse-tree node $N$ (labeled with non-terminal $A$) is computed **only from the attribute values of the children of $N$**. In other words, the information flows **bottom-up** — from leaves toward the root.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property of Synthesized Attributes**
> If $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \ldots X_n$ is a production, then a synthesized attribute $A.a$ is defined by a rule of the form:
> $$A.a := f(X_1.b_1, X_1.b_2, \ldots, X_n.b_k)$$
> where each $b_i$ is an attribute of $X_i$ that has already been computed.

### 1.3 Inherited Attributes

An **inherited attribute** is an attribute whose value at a parse-tree node $N$ (labeled with non-terminal $B$, where $B$ appears on the right-hand side of some production) is computed from **the attribute values of the parent of $N$ and/or the siblings of $N$**. Information flows **top-down** and **left-to-right**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property of Inherited Attributes**
> If $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \ldots X_n$ is a production, then an inherited attribute $X_i.b$ (where $1 \le i \le n$) is defined by a rule of the form:
> $$X_i.b := f(A.a, X_1.b_1, \ldots, X_{i-1}.b_{i-1}, X_{i+1}.b_{i+1}, \ldots, X_n.b_k)$$
> Only attributes of $A$ and attributes of symbols to the **left** of $X_i$ may be used (this restriction defines a well-formed *L-attributed* SDD).

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Family Tree of a Compiler

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Family Tree**
> Imagine a parse tree as a **family tree**:
> - A **synthesized attribute** is like a child's **eye color** — it is determined by the genes (attributes) of the parents (children nodes). You compute the child's trait *after* you know the parents' traits. The information flows **upward**, from offspring → parent.
> - An **inherited attribute** is like a child's **last name (surname)** — it is given to the child *by the parent* based on context. The information flows **downward**, from parent → child.
>
> In a compiler, **type information** is often synthesized (each subexpression reports its type upward), while **expected types** in type-checking are inherited (the context tells each subexpression what type is expected).

### 1.5 S-Attributed Definition

A **Syntax-Directed Definition is S-attributed** if **every attribute is synthesized**. Equivalently, an SDD is S-attributed if the dependency graph of every parse tree has edges only from a child to its parent (no horizontal or downward edges).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why S-Attributed Definitions Matter in KTU**
> - S-attributed SDDs can be evaluated using a **single bottom-up pass** during a **postorder traversal** of the parse tree.
> - They correspond naturally to **LR parsers** — the SDD can be implemented on an **LALR(1) stack** because attribute values are pushed up as reductions occur.
> - They form the foundation of **yacc/bison-style** translator-generator tools.

### 1.6 Terminal Attributes

A terminal symbol can have **synthesized attributes only**, and these are supplied by the **lexer** (e.g., the *lexeme*, *numeric value*, *line number*, *type tag*). No semantic rule in the SDD computes terminal attributes — they are *external inputs* to the translation process.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Attributes — A Structured View

Let each grammar symbol be associated with two disjoint sets of attributes:
- $\mathbf{S}(X)$ — the set of **synthesized** attributes of symbol $X$
- $\mathbf{I}(X)$ — the set of **inherited** attributes of symbol $X$

For every production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \ldots X_n$, the semantic rules must satisfy the following discipline:

| Attribute Being Defined | Where It Lives | What It May Depend On |
|:-----------------------:|:--------------:|:---------------------:|
| Synthesized $A.a$ | LHS of production | Any attribute of $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ (children) |
| Inherited $X_i.b$, for $i \ge 2$ | RHS, not first | $A$ and $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_{i-1}$ (parent and left siblings) |
| Inherited $X_1.b$ | RHS, first symbol | $A$ only (the parent) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind This Discipline**
> This restriction is precisely what makes the SDD **implementable during parsing**. It guarantees that by the time we need $X_i.b$, all its dependencies have *already been computed* by the parser (parent's attributes come from the parsing context; left siblings have been processed in left-to-right order).

### 2.2 The Attribute Dependency Graph

For any parse tree $T$ and any SDD, we construct a **dependency graph** $D_T$ as follows:
- For each parse-tree node $N$ labeled $X$ and each attribute $X.a$, we create a node in $D_T$.
- For each semantic rule $X_i.a := f(\ldots, X_j.b, \ldots)$ defining attribute $X_i.a$ in terms of $X_j.b$, we draw a directed edge $X_j.b \rightarrow X_i.a$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (KTU Favourite)**
> An SDD is **well-defined** (i.e., every attribute has a unique, well-determined value) if and only if every dependency graph $D_T$ for every parse tree $T$ is **acyclic**. A **topological sort** of $D_T$ yields a valid evaluation order.

### 2.3 S-Attributed Definitions in Detail

For an **S-attributed definition**:
1. **All** semantic rules have the form $A.a := f(\text{attributes of } X_1, \ldots, X_n)$.
2. No rule computes an inherited attribute of any symbol.
3. The dependency graph has edges pointing **only upward** (child → parent).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility**
> Because edges only go upward, a **postorder traversal** of the parse tree evaluates every attribute in one pass with no backtracking. This is why **yacc** (Yet Another Compiler-Compiler) and **bison** restrict themselves to S-attributed translations: they interleave translation with the LR parsing stack.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — Mandatory for KTU Board Exams**

| Concept | Formal Statement | Notation | When Used |
|:--------|:----------------|:--------:|:----------|
| Synthesized attribute rule | $A.a := f(X_1.b, \ldots, X_n.c)$ | Depends only on RHS attributes | Bottom-up flow |
| Inherited attribute rule | $X_i.b := f(A.a, X_1.d, \ldots, X_{i-1}.e)$ | Depends on parent + left siblings | Top-down / left-to-right flow |
| Terminal attribute | Supplied by lexer | Synthesized only | Pre-computed input |
| S-attributed SDD | All attributes synthesized | No inherited attributes | LR-parser compatible |
| Dependency graph edge | $X_j.b \rightarrow X_i.a$ | Rule defining $X_i.a$ uses $X_j.b$ | Topological sort |
| Well-defined SDD | $D_T$ acyclic for all $T$ | Topological order exists | Valid translation |
| Postorder evaluation | Visit children, then parent | Order for S-attributed SDD | One-pass bottom-up |

### 2.5 The "Why" Behind the Two Attribute Types

> [!TIP]
> **Synthesized vs. Inherited — Pedagogical Reasoning**
> - **Synthesized attributes** naturally model information that *propagates* from sub-computations to the whole (e.g., the *value* of an arithmetic expression, the *type* returned by a function body, the *size* of a declared array).
> - **Inherited attributes** naturally model information that *flows from context* into a sub-computation (e.g., the *expected type* of a sub-expression in a typed language, the *address* of a local variable in a scope, the *type environment* passed down to a statement).

### 2.6 The Annotated Parse Tree — Core Visualization

To compute the value of an attribute on a parse-tree node:
1. Identify the production used at that node.
2. Locate the semantic rule associated with that production.
3. Evaluate the rule using already-computed attribute values.

The values are typically written **next to** the node label in the parse tree.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — A Simple Synthesized-Only SDD (S-Attributed)

Consider the following S-attributed SDD for a desk-calculator language. The grammar generates binary numbers and decimal numbers, and the SDD evaluates the numeric value.

**Productions and Semantic Rules:**

| Production | Semantic Rule |
|:-----------|:--------------|
| $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$ | $E.val := E_1.val + T.val$ |
| $E \rightarrow T$ | $E.val := T.val$ |
| $T \rightarrow T_1 * F$ | $T.val := T_1.val \times F.val$ |
| $T \rightarrow F$ | $T.val := F.val$ |
| $F \rightarrow (E)$ | $F.val := E.val$ |
| $F \rightarrow \mathbf{digit}$ | $F.val := \mathbf{digit}.lexval$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Every rule is of the form $A.val := f(\text{children's attributes})$. Therefore, **this SDD is S-attributed**. The only attribute is `val`, and it is **synthesized everywhere**.

**Input Expression:** $7 + 6 * 3$

#### Step 1: Build the Parse Tree

The parse tree (with grammatical non-terminals only) is:

```
            E
          / | \
         E  +  T
         |    /|\
         T   T * F
         |   |   |
         F   F  digit
         |   |   |
       digit digit 3
         |   |
         7   6
```

#### Step 2: Compute Terminal Attributes (Lexer-Supplied)

The lexer provides:
- $\mathbf{digit}.lexval$ for the lexeme `7` $= 7$
- $\mathbf{digit}.lexval$ for the lexeme `6` $= 6$
- $\mathbf{digit}.lexval$ for the lexeme `3` $= 3$

These are written beneath the three leaf nodes labeled `digit`.

#### Step 3: Bottom-Up (Postorder) Evaluation

We walk the tree in **postorder**: left subtree, right subtree, root. At each step we apply exactly one semantic rule.

**Step 3.1** — Apply $F \rightarrow \mathbf{digit}$ for `7`:
$$F.val := \mathbf{digit}.lexval = 7$$

**Step 3.2** — Apply $T \rightarrow F$ (leftmost $T$):
$$T.val := F.val = 7$$

**Step 3.3** — Apply $E \rightarrow T$ (leftmost $E$):
$$E.val := T.val = 7$$

**Step 3.4** — Apply $F \rightarrow \mathbf{digit}$ for `6`:
$$F.val := 6$$

**Step 3.5** — Apply $T \rightarrow F$ (middle $T$):
$$T.val := F.val = 6$$

**Step 3.6** — Apply $F \rightarrow \mathbf{digit}$ for `3`:
$$F.val := 3$$

**Step 3.7** — Apply $T_1 \rightarrow T_2 * F$:
$$T.val := T_1.val \times F.val = 6 \times 3 = 18$$

**Step 3.8** — Apply $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$ (root):
$$E.val := E_1.val + T.val = 7 + 18 = 25$$

#### Step 4: Annotated Parse Tree (Final Result)

```
            E.val=25
          /   |    \
     E.val=7  +    T.val=18
         |        /  |    \
      T.val=7   T.val=6 *  F.val=3
         |        |        |
      F.val=7   F.val=6  digit
         |        |     .lexval=3
       digit    digit
     .lexval=7 .lexval=6
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation for KTU Exam**
> Every attribute computation uses **only children's values**. The arrows of information flow are *strictly* upward. This confirms S-attributed behavior.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — A Mixed SDD (Synthesized + Inherited)

Now consider an SDD that uses **inherited attributes** to pass a type environment downward.

**Productions and Semantic Rules:**

| Production | Semantic Rules |
|:-----------|:---------------|
| $D \rightarrow T\ L$ | $L.in := T.type$ |
| $T \rightarrow \mathbf{int}$ | $T.type := \mathbf{integer}$ |
| $T \rightarrow \mathbf{real}$ | $T.type := \mathbf{real}$ |
| $L \rightarrow L_1, \mathbf{id}$ | $L_1.in := L.in$; $\mathbf{id}.dtype := L.in$; $L.dtype := \mathbf{void}$ |
| $L \rightarrow \mathbf{id}$ | $\mathbf{id}.dtype := L.in$; $L.dtype := \mathbf{void}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Attribute Analysis**
> - `T.type` is **synthesized** (computed from children's terminal lexeme).
> - `L.in` is **inherited** (passed from parent $D$ or sibling $L_1$).
> - `id.dtype` is **synthesized** for the terminal (supplied by the lexer, but here we override it for demonstration).
> - `L.dtype` is **synthesized** (always `void` here, not used).
> - This SDD is **NOT S-attributed** because $L.in$ is inherited.

**Input:** $\mathbf{int}\ a,\ b$

#### Step 1: Build the Parse Tree

```
              D
            / | \
           T  L
           | /|\
         int L , id
            |    |
            id   b
            |
            a
```

#### Step 2: Compute Bottom-Up (Synthesized First)

**Step 2.1** — Apply $T \rightarrow \mathbf{int}$:
$$T.type := \mathbf{integer}$$

**Step 2.2** — Apply $D \rightarrow T\ L$:
$$L.in := T.type = \mathbf{integer}$$

This is the **only inherited rule application at this point**. It propagates `integer` *downward* from $D$ into $L$.

**Step 2.3** — Apply $L \rightarrow \mathbf{id}$ (innermost, for `a`):
$$\mathbf{id}.dtype := L.in = \mathbf{integer}$$

So `a` is declared as an integer.

**Step 2.4** — Apply $L \rightarrow L_1, \mathbf{id}$ (for `, b`):
$$L_1.in := L.in = \mathbf{integer}$$
$$\mathbf{id}.dtype := L.in = \mathbf{integer}$$

So `b` is also declared as an integer.

#### Step 3: Annotated Parse Tree

```
              D
            / | \
           T  L.in=integer
   T.type=integer   L
            /  |    \
       L.in=integer , id.dtype=integer
            |         |
         L.in=integer id
            |        (b)
            id
            (a)
         id.dtype=integer
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight**
> The inherited attribute `L.in` models the *type context* passed from the declaration's type specifier into the identifier list. Without inheritance, we would have to use a **global symbol table** and **synthesized-only** design — possible but awkward.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Building the Dependency Graph

Take the SDD:
- $A \rightarrow B\ C$ with $A.s := B.s + C.s$ and $C.i := A.s + 1$
- $B \rightarrow b$ with $B.s := 1$
- $C \rightarrow c$ with $C.s := C.i \times 2$

For input $b\ c$, the parse tree is:
```
    A
   / \
  B   C
  |   |
  b   c
```

**Dependencies from the rules:**
- $A.s := B.s + C.s$ ⟹ $B.s \rightarrow A.s$, $C.s \rightarrow A.s$
- $C.i := A.s + 1$ ⟹ $A.s \rightarrow C.i$
- $B.s := 1$ ⟹ no dependencies (constant)
- $C.s := C.i \times 2$ ⟹ $C.i \rightarrow C.s$

**Dependency graph:**
$$
B.s \;\longrightarrow\; A.s \;\longrightarrow\; C.i \;\longrightarrow\; C.s
$$
Also $C.s \longrightarrow A.s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cycle Check**
> The graph contains the cycle $A.s \rightarrow C.i \rightarrow C.s \rightarrow A.s$. Therefore this SDD is **NOT well-defined** for input $b\ c$. This is a classic KTU exam trap!

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Synthesized vs. Inherited

| Property | Synthesized Attribute | Inherited Attribute |
|:---------|:---------------------:|:-------------------:|
| Information flow | Bottom-up | Top-down + left-to-right |
| Computed from | Children of the node | Parent and/or left siblings |
| Can terminals have it? | **Yes** (lexer supplies) | **No** (terminals have no children/parent in the relevant sense) |
| LHS of production? | **Yes**, by definition | **No** (only RHS symbols get inherited) |
| Evaluation order | Postorder traversal | Preorder, left-to-right |
| Parser compatibility | LR, LALR, recursive descent (postorder) | Top-down parsers (LL, recursive descent) |
| Cycle possibility | Cannot create cycles | Can create cycles (must check $D_T$) |
| Typical KTU example | Value of expression, type of sub-expression | Type expected, scope/environment |

---

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation — Why S-Attributed ⇒ Bottom-Up Parsers Work

**Claim:** Every S-attributed SDD can be implemented during **bottom-up parsing** (LR family) using a stack.

**Proof Sketch:**

1. Consider an LR parser performing reductions. At any reduction, the parser pops $X_n X_{n-1} \ldots X_1$ from the stack and pushes the LHS non-terminal $A$ in their place.
2. For S-attributed SDD, the rule for $A.a$ is $A.a := f(\text{attributes of } X_1, \ldots, X_n)$.
3. By the time of reduction, **all children's attribute values are already on the stack** (because children are reduced *before* the parent in bottom-up order).
4. Therefore, the parser can compute $A.a$ *at the moment of reduction* and store it alongside $A$ on the stack.
5. By induction, all synthesized attributes are available exactly when needed. $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> This is the formal reason why **yacc/bison actions** are written as `{ $$ = $1 + $3; }` — the position of the LHS attribute is `$$`, and the children's synthesized attributes are `$1, $2, ...`.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy of SDDs — Visual Overview

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Syntax-Directed Definition SDD"] --> B["All attributes are synthesized"]
    A --> C["Some attributes are inherited"]
    B --> D["S-Attributed Definition"]
    C --> E["L-Attributed Definition"]
    C --> F["General SDD with cycles possible"]
    D --> G["Postorder traversal evaluates in one pass"]
    D --> H["Compatible with LR / LALR parsers"]
    D --> I["Compatible with yacc / bison"]
    E --> J["Preorder left to right traversal"]
    E --> K["Compatible with LL parsers and recursive descent"]
    F --> L["Requires cycle check on dependency graph D sub T"]
```

### 4.2 Information Flow in Synthesized vs. Inherited Attributes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SU["Synthesized: Bottom-Up Flow"]
        direction BT
        leaf1["Leaf: digit.lexval = 7"]:::leaf
        leaf2["Leaf: digit.lexval = 6"]:::leaf
        mid1["F.val = 7"]:::syn
        mid2["F.val = 6"]:::syn
        top1["E.val = 7 + 6 = 13"]:::synroot
        mid1 --> top1
        leaf1 --> mid1
        mid2 --> top1
        leaf2 --> mid2
    end
    classDef leaf fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    classDef syn fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef synroot fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph IN["Inherited: Top-Down Flow"]
        direction TB
        root["D: passes type down"]:::parent
        L1["L.in = integer"]:::inh
        L2["L1.in = integer"]:::inh
        id1["id.dtype of a = integer"]:::syn
        id2["id.dtype of b = integer"]:::syn
        root --> L1
        L1 --> L2
        L1 --> id1
        L2 --> id2
    end
    classDef parent fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    classDef inh fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000
    classDef syn fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SDD Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Source Code Text"] --> B["Lexer / Scanner"]
    B --> C["Token Stream with lexeme attributes"]
    C --> D["Parser Constructs Parse Tree"]
    D --> E["SDD Attaches Semantic Rules to Each Production"]
    E --> F{"Is SDD S-Attributed?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Postorder Traversal"]
    F -- No --> H["Build Dependency Graph D sub T"]
    H --> I{"Is D sub T Acyclic?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["Topological Sort of D sub T"]
    I -- No --> K["Error: SDD is not well-defined"]
    G --> L["Evaluate All Attributes in One Pass"]
    J --> L
    L --> M["Final Attribute Values at Root = Translation Result"]
    K --> N["Compiler Designer Must Revise SDD"]
```

### 4.4 Dependency Graph of Worked Example 3 (Cycle Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Bs["B.s = 1"]:::fixed --> As["A.s"]
    As --> Ci["C.i = A.s + 1"]
    Ci --> Cs["C.s = C.i * 2"]
    Cs -.-> As
    classDef fixed fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40,color:#000
    classDef cyclic fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    class As,Ci,Cs cyclic
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dashed edge $C.s \dashrightarrow A.s$ together with the solid path $A.s \rightarrow C.i \rightarrow C.s$ forms a **directed cycle**, confirming that this SDD is **not well-defined**.

### 4.5 Evaluation Order Diagram for S-Attributed Postorder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Step 1: Visit leftmost leaf digit.lexval"] --> P2["Step 2: Apply F to digit, get F.val"]
    P2 --> P3["Step 3: Apply T to F, get T.val"]
    P3 --> P4["Step 4: Apply E to T, get E.val for left subtree"]
    P4 --> P5["Step 5: Visit middle leaf, propagate up"]
    P5 --> P6["Step 6: Visit rightmost leaf"]
    P6 --> P7["Step 7: Combine E + T at root"]
    P7 --> P8["Final: E.val at root is the answer"]
```

### 4.6 L-Attributed vs S-Attributed Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Do you need to pass context downward?"] -->|"Yes e.g. type, scope"| Q2["Need inherited attributes"]
    Q1 -->|"No only aggregate from subtrees"| Q3["Pure synthesized"]
    Q2 --> Q4["Is grammar parseable top-down LL?"]
    Q4 -->|"Yes"| Q5["Use L-attributed SDD with LL parser"]
    Q4 -->|"No, only LR available"| Q6["Restructure SDD or rewrite grammar"]
    Q3 --> Q7["Use S-attributed SDD with LR or LL parser"]
    Q7 --> Q8["Implement via yacc action $$ equals f of dollar i"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Differentiate between synthesized and inherited attributes in a syntax-directed definition. Give one example of each from a typical compiler.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Synthesized Attribute | Inherited Attribute |
|:-------|:---------------------:|:-------------------:|
| Flow direction | Bottom-up (child → parent) | Top-down (parent → child) and left-to-right |
| Computed from | Attribute values of children | Attribute values of parent and/or left siblings |
| Can terminals have it? | Yes (supplied by lexer) | No |
| Position in production | Usually on LHS | Always on RHS (except first symbol) |
| Example | `E.val := E1.val + T.val` (value of an arithmetic expression) | `L.in := T.type` (type passed from declaration to identifier list) |
| Parser support | LR, LL, recursive descent (postorder) | LL, recursive descent (preorder); requires care with LR |

*(1 mark for the directional difference, 1 mark for the dependency difference, 1 mark for a correct example of each.)*

---

#### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**What is an S-attributed definition? Why is it the natural choice for bottom-up parser generators like yacc?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** An *S-attributed definition* is a syntax-directed definition in which **every attribute is synthesized**. Consequently, the value of any attribute at a node depends only on the attribute values of its children in the parse tree.
- **Why yacc-friendly (1.5 marks):** Because all attribute values are computed bottom-up, the value of the LHS attribute $A.a$ in a production $A \rightarrow X_1 \ldots X_n$ depends solely on the children's attributes, which are already on the parser stack by the time the reduction occurs. Therefore, the semantic action can be executed **at reduction time** using only the stack-resident values `$$`, `$1`, `$2`, … without any auxiliary data structures or lookahead.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B.1 (A)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Define a syntax-directed definition for the grammar that translates **infix expressions involving `+` and `*`** over single-digit numbers into **postfix notation** (Reverse Polish Notation). Use only **synthesized attributes**. Show the parse tree for the input `5 + 6 * 2` and write the resulting postfix string.

**(b)** Construct the **annotated parse tree** showing the synthesized attribute values at every node, and demonstrate that the SDD is **S-attributed**.

##### Model Solution

**(a) Grammar and SDD (7 marks):**

Grammar:
$$E \rightarrow E + T \;\mid\; T$$
$$T \rightarrow T * F \;\mid\; F$$
$$F \rightarrow \mathbf{digit}$$

Semantic rules (using synthesized attribute `postfix`):
| Production | Semantic Rule |
|:-----------|:--------------|
| $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$ | $E.postfix := E_1.postfix \,\|\, T.postfix \,\|\, '+'$ |
| $E \rightarrow T$ | $E.postfix := T.postfix$ |
| $T \rightarrow T_1 * F$ | $T.postfix := T_1.postfix \,\|\, F.postfix \,\|\, '*'$ |
| $T \rightarrow F$ | $T.postfix := F.postfix$ |
| $F \rightarrow \mathbf{digit}$ | $F.postfix := \mathbf{digit}.lexval$ |

(The symbol `$\|$` denotes string concatenation.)

**Parse Tree for `5 + 6 * 2`:**

```
                E
              / | \
             E  +  T
             |    /|\
             T   T * F
             |   |   |
             F   F  digit
             |   |   |
           digit digit 2
             |   |
             5   6
```

**Resulting postfix (applying the rules bottom-up):**

- $F \rightarrow \mathbf{digit}$ at `5` gives `"5"`
- $T \rightarrow F$ gives `"5"`
- $E \rightarrow T$ (leftmost) gives `"5"`
- $F \rightarrow \mathbf{digit}$ at `6` gives `"6"`
- $T \rightarrow F$ (middle) gives `"6"`
- $F \rightarrow \mathbf{digit}$ at `2` gives `"2"`
- $T \rightarrow T_1 * F$ gives `"6" \| "2" \| "*"` = `"62*"`
- $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$ (root) gives `"5" \| "62*" \| "+"` = `"562*+"`

**Final postfix: `562*+`**

**(b) Annotated parse tree showing S-attributedness (7 marks):**

```
                E.postfix=562*+
              /   |    \
       E.postfix=5  +  T.postfix=62*
           |           /  |    \
        T.postfix=5  T.postfix=6 * F.postfix=2
           |           |          |
        F.postfix=5  F.postfix=6 digit
           |           |       .lexval=2
        digit        digit
      .lexval=5    .lexval=6
```

Every rule has the form $A.postfix := f(\text{children's attributes})$. No inherited attribute is ever used. The dependency graph of the parse tree contains **only upward-pointing edges**. Therefore, the SDD is **S-attributed** by definition. A **postorder traversal** evaluates all attributes in a single bottom-up pass.

---

#### **Question B.1 (B)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Consider the following SDD with **inherited attributes**:

| Production | Semantic Rules |
|:-----------|:---------------|
| $S \rightarrow A\ B$ | $A.in := 1$; $B.in := A.val$ |
| $A \rightarrow a$ | $A.val := A.in \times 2$ |
| $B \rightarrow b$ | $B.val := B.in + 5$ |

**For input `a b`, draw the parse tree, build the dependency graph, and determine whether the SDD is well-defined. If yes, compute the values of all attributes.**

**(b)** Now modify the production $S \rightarrow A\ B$ to add the rule $A.val := B.val$. Repeat the dependency-graph construction and analyze whether the modified SDD is well-defined.

##### Model Solution

**(a) Original SDD analysis (7 marks):**

**Parse tree for `a b`:**

```
        S
       / \
      A   B
      |   |
      a   b
```

**Terminal/leaf attribute values:** none supplied by the lexer here (other than the lexeme labels).

**Step-by-step evaluation following the rules:**

**Step 1:** Apply $S \rightarrow A\ B$ — this is a production rule, and the semantic rules attached to it are applied at node $S$. The two rules attached are:
$$A.in := 1 \qquad B.in := A.val$$

These define **inherited** attributes of $A$ and $B$. They are attached at the parent $S$ and affect the children.

**Step 2:** Apply $A \rightarrow a$ at the leaf side:
$$A.val := A.in \times 2 = 1 \times 2 = 2$$

**Step 3:** Now that $A.val = 2$ is known, we can complete the rule $B.in := A.val$:
$$B.in := A.val = 2$$

**Step 4:** Apply $B \rightarrow b$:
$$B.val := B.in + 5 = 2 + 5 = 7$$

**Dependency graph for the original SDD:**

The rules produce these directed dependencies:
- $A.in := 1$ ⟹ no dependencies (constant rule)
- $A.val := A.in \times 2$ ⟹ $A.in \rightarrow A.val$
- $B.in := A.val$ ⟹ $A.val \rightarrow B.in$
- $B.val := B.in + 5$ ⟹ $B.in \rightarrow B.val$

Graph (linear chain):
$$A.in \;\longrightarrow\; A.val \;\longrightarrow\; B.in \;\longrightarrow\; B.val$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No cycles exist.** The graph is acyclic. A topological order is: $A.in$, $A.val$, $B.in$, $B.val$. Therefore, the SDD is **well-defined**.

**Final attribute values:**
- $A.in = 1$
- $A.val = 2$
- $B.in = 2$
- $B.val = 7$

**(b) Modified SDD analysis (7 marks):**

Now add the rule $A.val := B.val$ to the production $S \rightarrow A\ B$. The full set of rules becomes:
- $A.in := 1$
- $A.val := A.in \times 2$ (from $A \rightarrow a$)
- $B.in := A.val$
- $B.val := B.in + 5$ (from $B \rightarrow b$)
- $A.val := B.val$ (newly added at $S$)

But wait — `A.val` is now defined by **two different rules**: one at the child node $A$ and one at the parent $S$. This is only acceptable if they produce the **same value** for all valid parse trees; otherwise, the SDD is ambiguous. However, ignoring that subtlety, the **dependency graph** now includes:

- The previous edges: $A.in \rightarrow A.val$, $A.val \rightarrow B.in$, $B.in \rightarrow B.val$
- The new edge: $B.val \rightarrow A.val$

Now the chain becomes:
$$A.in \;\longrightarrow\; A.val \;\longrightarrow\; B.in \;\longrightarrow\; B.val \;\longrightarrow\; A.val$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Cycle Detected!**
> The new edge $B.val \rightarrow A.val$ together with the path $A.val \rightarrow B.in \rightarrow B.val$ forms a **cycle**: $A.val \rightarrow B.in \rightarrow B.val \rightarrow A.val$. Therefore, the modified SDD is **NOT well-defined**.

Furthermore, even if the cycle were broken, the double definition of $A.val$ (once at node $A$ and once at node $S$) violates the SDD uniqueness requirement unless the two definitions provably coincide. This is a classic KTU trap question.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Board Exams (Compiler Design)**
>
> 1. **Confusing "synthesized" with "evaluated top-down."** A synthesized attribute is evaluated **bottom-up**, even though the rule is written on the LHS. Many students write "top-down" — this loses full marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting that terminals only have synthesized attributes.** A rule like `digit.in := ...` is **illegal** and immediately loses marks.
>
> 3. **Inherited attribute on the leftmost symbol of the RHS.** A rule like `X1.in := f(X2.b)` is forbidden in an L-attributed SDD because $X_2$ is a *right* sibling, not a *left* sibling. This is the most-missed subtlety in 14-mark questions.
>
> 4. **Not drawing the dependency graph when asked.** A 14-mark question that says "show the dependency graph and check for cycles" requires the **explicit graph**, not just a verbal description. Always draw the edges.
>
> 5. **Calling an SDD "S-attributed" if it has even one inherited attribute.** One inherited attribute anywhere disqualifies the SDD from being S-attributed. Strict!
>
> 6. **Mixing up `$$` and `$1` in yacc actions.** In yacc action code, `$$` refers to the **LHS** synthesized attribute, and `$i` refers to the *i*-th symbol's synthesized attribute. Forgetting this loses practical-application marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- A **Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD)** is a CFG + attributes + semantic rules.
- A **synthesized attribute** depends only on **children**; information flows **upward** (child → parent).
- An **inherited attribute** depends on the **parent** and/or **left siblings**; information flows **downward/left-to-right**.
- **Terminals** can have **only synthesized** attributes, and these are supplied by the **lexer**.
- A production's LHS can have **synthesized** attributes; the RHS symbols can have **inherited** (and synthesized) attributes.
- An **S-attributed definition** = **all attributes are synthesized** ⟹ no inherited attributes at all.
- S-attributed SDDs are evaluated by a **single postorder traversal** of the parse tree.
- S-attributed SDDs are the **only** kind that yacc/bison can implement directly via reduction-time actions.
- A general SDD is **well-defined** iff its **dependency graph** $D_T$ is **acyclic** for every parse tree $T$.
- **L-attributed** SDDs (a subset) allow inherited attributes depending on the parent and left siblings only — implementable on **top-down parsers**.
- When asked to "show the SDD" for a translation, always: **(i)** write the productions, **(ii)** write the semantic rules in a table, **(iii)** draw the parse tree, **(iv)** annotate it with attribute values, **(v)** state explicitly whether the SDD is S-attributed.
- The KTU board often asks: *"Is the SDD well-defined? Justify using the dependency graph."* — always construct $D_T$ explicitly.
- Standard S-attributed example: arithmetic expression evaluator; standard inherited example: type-information propagation in declarations.
- Remember the `$$` / `$i` convention for yacc action code: `$$` = LHS attribute, `$i` = attribute of $i$-th symbol on the RHS.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: L-Attributed Definitions. Intermediate Code Generation - Variants of Syntax Tree. Three-Address Code - Quadruple
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** L-Attributed Definitions. Intermediate Code Generation - Variants of Syntax Tree. Three-Address Code - Quadruple

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 L-Attributed Definitions

### 📌 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)
An **L-Attributed Definition** is a syntax-directed definition in which each attribute of a node can be evaluated in a **single left-to-right traversal** (typically a depth-first traversal) of the parse tree. Formally, for any production rule of the form:

$$A \rightarrow X_1 \, X_2 \, X_3 \dots X_n$$

Each inherited attribute of the symbol $X_j$ (where $1 \leq j \leq n$) is allowed to depend **only** on:
1. The inherited attributes of the **left-side symbol** $A$.
2. **Any attribute** (inherited or synthesized) of the symbols $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_{j-1}$ (i.e., the symbols to its **left** in the production).

All synthesized attributes of $A$ are unrestricted and may depend on any attribute of $X_1 \dots X_n$ (similar to S-attributed definitions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Fact:** Every **S-Attributed Definition is also an L-Attributed Definition**, but the converse is not necessarily true. L-attributed definitions are a strict superset of S-attributed definitions.

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are reading a **newspaper column from top-to-bottom, left-to-right**. Each sentence builds meaning using only the words already read (to its left) plus the main headline (parent). You cannot peek at a word that comes later in the article to understand a current sentence. This is precisely what an **L-Attributed Definition** enforces — each attribute's value can be computed only using information flowing from the **left sibling and parent** (like reading a newspaper left-to-right).

In contrast, **S-Attributed Definitions** are stricter — like a strict bottom-up assembly line where children only pass information up to parents, no sideways sharing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why L-Attributed? Why not just S-Attributed?**  
> L-attributed definitions support **inherited attributes**, which allow context to flow **down the parse tree** (parent → child or left-sibling → right-sibling). This is essential for type checking, symbol tables, and intermediate code generation — tasks where child nodes must know about the parent's context.

---

## 1.2 Intermediate Code Generation

### 📌 Formal Definition
**Intermediate Code Generation** is the phase of a compiler that translates the **annotated parse tree** (output of semantic analysis) into a machine-independent, abstract representation of the source program. This intermediate representation (IR) sits between the high-level source language and the target machine code, enabling **portability**, **optimization**, and **easier code generation**.

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of intermediate code as a **"Universal Language Translator"** — like the role of **English** in international diplomacy. Two people who speak different languages (source language and target machine language) can communicate by first translating their words into English (the intermediate code), and then into the target language. The compiler's front-end produces IR, and the back-end consumes it to generate target code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Property:** Intermediate code should be:
> - **Easy to produce** from the source.
> - **Easy to translate** into the target.
> - **Machine-independent** (portable across architectures).
> - **Optimization-friendly** (structural simplicity allows powerful transformations).

---

## 1.3 Variants of Syntax Tree

### 📌 Formal Definition
A **Syntax Tree** (also called a **Parse Tree** without extraneous punctuation nodes) is a condensed hierarchical representation of the source program where each **interior node represents an operator** and the **children represent the operands**. **Variants** of the syntax tree are alternative compact representations optimized for specific compiler tasks:

| Variant | Full Form | Core Property |
|---------|-----------|---------------|
| **AST** | Abstract Syntax Tree | Hierarchical, removes redundant syntactic nodes |
| **DAG** | Directed Acyclic Graph | **Shares** common subexpressions — represents redundancy explicitly |
| **AST Variants** | Variants of Abstract Syntax Tree | Linearized, typed, or annotated forms |

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
- **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** is like a **clean family tree** — only meaningful members are shown, and noisy details (like commas, semicolons, parentheses) are stripped out.
- **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** is like a **family tree where identical twins share a single photo**. If two parts of the program compute the same expression, the DAG reuses a **single node** for both, eliminating redundancy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The **DAG** is a critical variant because it allows the compiler to perform **common subexpression elimination (CSE)** during the IR phase itself, before optimization passes even begin.

---

## 1.4 Three-Address Code (TAC)

### 📌 Formal Definition
**Three-Address Code (TAC)** is a linearized intermediate representation where each instruction contains **at most three addresses** (operands): **two source operands** and **one destination operand**. It corresponds to the abstraction of a machine in which **each memory location acts as a register**. The general form is:

$$x = y \,\, op \,\, z$$

Where $x, y, z$ are addresses (variables, constants, temporaries, or labels).

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
TAC is like a **recipe written one-step-per-line**. Each step (instruction) involves:
- **Two ingredients** (operands $y$ and $z$)
- **One cooking action** (operator $op$)
- **One output** (result $x$)

You can never have a recipe line that mixes four ingredients at once — that would be too complex. TAC enforces a **maximum of three addresses per instruction** for clarity and ease of translation.

---

## 1.5 Quadruple Representation

### 📌 Formal Definition
A **Quadruple** is a tabular representation of three-address code using records (rows) with **exactly four fields**:

1. **Operator (op)** — the operation symbol (e.g., `+`, `-`, `*`, `if`, `goto`)
2. **Argument 1 (arg1)** — first operand address
3. **Argument 2 (arg2)** — second operand address
4. **Result (result)** — destination address

Each TAC instruction maps to exactly **one quadruple row**. For unary operators, `arg2` is left blank.

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
A **Quadruple** is like a **spreadsheet row** — every cooking instruction is recorded in a structured 4-column ledger:
- **Column 1:** What action? (`op`)
- **Column 2:** First ingredient? (`arg1`)
- **Column 3:** Second ingredient? (`arg2`)
- **Column 4:** Where does the dish go? (`result`)

This tabular form is extremely **easy to optimize** because the compiler can scan, reorder, or eliminate rows just like editing a spreadsheet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Related TAC Representations (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> - **Quadruple** — uses 4 fields per instruction (most common)
> - **Triple** — uses 3 fields (references other instructions by index instead of names)
> - **Indirect Triple** — uses a pointer table + triple rows (avoids costly pointer updates during optimization)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 L-Attributed Definitions — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

### 🧠 The "Why" Behind L-Attributed Definitions

During **semantic analysis**, attributes must be computed at every node of the parse tree. The order of computation is governed by the **dependency graph** — an edge exists from attribute $b$ to attribute $c$ if $c$'s computation depends on $b$'s value.

**S-Attributed Definitions** strictly use only **synthesized attributes** — meaning information only flows **upward** (child → parent). This is clean but restrictive.

**L-Attributed Definitions** generalize this by allowing **inherited attributes** — information flows:
- **Downward** (parent → child)
- **Left-to-right** (sibling → right-sibling)

This enables natural handling of constructs like:
- **Type declarations** propagating types to variables
- **Symbol table** lookups that depend on enclosing scope
- **Code generation** where the address of a child expression depends on its parent's context

### 🔑 Operational Steps for L-Attributed Evaluation

| Step | Action | Justification |
|------|--------|---------------|
| **Step 1** | Identify all attributes (synthesized and inherited) in the SDD. | Establishes the attribute universe. |
| **Step 2** | Construct the **dependency graph** for each production node. | Reveals valid topological orders. |
| **Step 3** | Verify the **L-attribute property** — no attribute depends on a right sibling. | Guarantees a valid left-to-right evaluation order exists. |
| **Step 4** | Perform a **depth-first traversal** of the parse tree, computing attributes left-to-right at each node. | Standard KTU implementation pattern. |
| **Step 5** | For each production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$, compute $X_j$'s inherited attrs using only $A$'s inherited + $X_1 \dots X_{j-1}$'s attrs. | Strict adherence to L-property. |

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — L-Attributed Definitions

| Concept | Rule / Equation | Conditions |
|---------|-----------------|------------|
| L-Property Test (Inherited) | $X_j.inh$ may depend only on $A.inh$ and $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_{j-1}$ attrs | $1 \leq j \leq n$ |
| S-Property Test (Synthesized) | $A.syn$ may depend only on $X_1 \dots X_n$ attrs | Any production |
| Subset Relation | $\text{S-Attributed} \subset \text{L-Attributed}$ | Strict inclusion |
| Evaluation Method | Depth-first, left-to-right, post-order for synthesized | DFS traversal |
| Failure Condition | Right-sibling dependency detected | SDD is **not** L-attributed |

---

## 2.2 Intermediate Code Generation — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

### 🧠 The "Why" Behind Intermediate Code

A compiler split into **Front-end (Analysis)** and **Back-end (Synthesis)** benefits enormously from IR:
- **Retargeting:** Same front-end, multiple back-ends (different CPUs).
- **Optimization:** Multiple optimization passes operate on a stable, machine-independent form.
- **Modularity:** Simplifies compiler engineering — separates concerns.

### 🔑 Forms of Intermediate Representation (KTU-Specific)

| IR Form | Granularity | Best For |
|---------|-------------|----------|
| **Postfix Notation** | Linear, stack-based | Expression evaluation |
| **Three-Address Code (TAC)** | Linear, instruction-like | Optimization + codegen |
| **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** | Hierarchical, structural | High-level analysis |
| **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** | Hierarchical, redundant-subexpression-shared | CSE optimization |
| **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** | Graph, basic blocks + edges | Data-flow analysis |

---

## 2.3 Variants of Syntax Tree — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

### 🧠 Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) vs. Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)

**AST Construction Algorithm (KTU Board Pattern):**
1. Parse the expression using the grammar.
2. For each operator, create a node.
3. Recursively construct operand subtrees.
4. **Discard** punctuation nodes (commas, parentheses, semicolons).

**DAG Construction Algorithm:**
1. Same as AST, but maintain a **hash table / dictionary** of already-created nodes.
2. Before creating a new node, check if an **equivalent node** (same op + same children) already exists.
3. If yes → **reuse** the existing node (add an edge pointing to it).
4. If no → create a new node and insert into the hash table.

### 🔑 Key Differences Table

| Property | AST | DAG |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Node duplication for same subexpression | **Yes** (each appears separately) | **No** (shared node) |
| Compactness | Less compact | More compact |
| Implicit CSE | No | **Yes** |
| Best use | Source-level analysis | Optimization phase |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Classic Trick:** When asked to convert an expression to DAG, the examiner expects you to identify **redundant subexpressions** and represent them with a single shared node. The number of nodes in the DAG will be **strictly less** than in the AST for expressions with repetition.

---

## 2.4 Three-Address Code — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

### 🧠 The "Why" Behind TAC

TAC imposes a **maximum of three addresses** per instruction to mirror real RISC-like architectures where most instructions have two source operands and one destination. This constraint:
- **Linearizes** the parse tree (easier traversal).
- **Exposes** temporaries that can be allocated to registers.
- **Simplifies** control flow (jumps use labels).
- **Enables** peephole optimization.

### 🔑 Common TAC Instruction Types (KTU High-Yield)

| Instruction Type | TAC Form | Example |
|------------------|----------|---------|
| **Binary Assignment** | $x = y \, op \, z$ | $t_1 = a + b$ |
| **Unary Assignment** | $x = op \, y$ | $t_2 = -c$ |
| **Copy** | $x = y$ | $t_3 = d$ |
| **Unconditional Jump** | goto $L$ | goto $L_1$ |
| **Conditional Jump** | if $x$ relop $y$ goto $L$ | if $a < b$ goto $L_2$ |
| **Procedure Call** | param $x$; call $p, n$ | param $t_1$; call foo, 1 |
| **Array Access** | $x = y[i]$ / $x[i] = y$ | $t_1 = a[i]$ |
| **Address/Deref** | $x = \&y$ / $x = *y$ / $*x = y$ | $t_1 = \&a$ |

---

## 2.5 Quadruple Representation — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

### 🧠 The "Why" Behind Quadruples

Quadruples make TAC **explicit and addressable** — each instruction gets a unique row, which makes:
- **Optimization** easier (delete/reorder rows).
- **Code generation** straightforward (linear scan).
- **Instruction reordering** safe (no pointer chasing).

### 🔑 Quadruple vs. Triple vs. Indirect Triple (KTU Frequently Compared)

| Feature | Quadruple | Triple | Indirect Triple |
|---------|-----------|--------|-----------------|
| Fields per row | 4 (op, arg1, arg2, result) | 3 (op, arg1, arg2) | 3 + pointer table |
| Operand naming | Symbolic names | Instruction indices | Symbolic via pointer table |
| Reordering difficulty | Easy | Hard (renumbering required) | Easy (just reorder pointers) |
| Space efficiency | Lower | Higher | Higher than Quadruple |
| Optimization friendliness | High | Low | High |

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — TAC & Quadruples

| Concept | Form | Key Property |
|---------|------|--------------|
| TAC general form | $x = y \, op \, z$ | Max 3 addresses |
| Quadruple | $(op, arg1, arg2, result)$ | Tabular IR |
| Triple | $(op, arg1, arg2)$ | Implicit position-based naming |
| Indirect Triple | Pointer list + Triple table | Pointer-based reordering |
| Unary TAC | $x = op \, y$ | $arg2$ is empty in Quadruple |
| Copy TAC | $x = y$ | $op$ = COPY or MOV |
| Array Access | $x = y[i]$ or $x[i] = y$ | Index arithmetic may be required |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Real-World Use |
|---------|----------------|
| L-Attributed Definitions | Type inference in statically-typed languages (Java, C++) |
| Intermediate Code | LLVM IR, GCC GIMPLE, Microsoft MSIL (.NET CIL) |
| AST Variants | Clang AST, Roslyn (.NET), Babel (JavaScript) |
| DAG | HotSpot JVM bytecode verification, common subexpression elimination |
| Three-Address Code | LLVM IR, WebAssembly, Dalvik bytecode |
| Quadruple | Used internally in GCC and LLVM during low-level optimization passes |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — L-Attributed Definition for Type Declarations

### 📋 Given SDD (Syntax-Directed Definition)

Consider the production rule for a type declaration:

$$D \rightarrow T \,\, L$$

$$T \rightarrow \text{int} \,\,\vert\,\, \text{float}$$

$$L \rightarrow L, \, id \,\,\vert\,\, id$$

### 🧮 Semantic Rules (L-Attributed)

| Production | Semantic Rule |
|------------|---------------|
| $D \rightarrow T \,\, L$ | $L.inh = T.type$ |
| $T \rightarrow \text{int}$ | $T.type = \text{integer}$ |
| $T \rightarrow \text{float}$ | $T.type = \text{float}$ |
| $L \rightarrow L_1, \, id$ | $L_1.inh = L.inh; \quad addtype(id.entry, L.inh)$ |
| $L \rightarrow id$ | $addtype(id.entry, L.inh)$ |

### ✅ Verification of L-Attributed Property

**Check the production $L \rightarrow L_1, \, id$:**
- $L_1.inh$ depends on $L.inh$.
- $L$ is to the **left** of $L_1$ in the production.
- ✅ **L-property satisfied!**

**Check the production $D \rightarrow T \,\, L$:**
- $L.inh$ depends on $T.type$.
- $T$ is to the **left** of $L$ in the production.
- ✅ **L-property satisfied!**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** This SDD is **L-attributed**. It can be evaluated by a single depth-first, left-to-right traversal.

### 🔧 Evaluation Order on Parse Tree

Suppose input is `int a, b, c`. The parse tree evaluation proceeds:

**Step 1:** Visit root $D$, descend to $T$.

**Step 2:** At $T \rightarrow \text{int}$, set $T.type = \text{integer}$.

**Step 3:** Move right to $L$. Set $L.inh = T.type = \text{integer}$.

**Step 4:** Evaluate $L \rightarrow L_1, \, id$ recursively:
- For $L_1$ (handling $a, b$), set $L_1.inh = L.inh = \text{integer}$.
- For $id = c$, call `addtype(c.entry, integer)`.

**Step 5:** Back-propagate, calling `addtype(a.entry, integer)` and `addtype(b.entry, integer)`.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Building a DAG from an Expression

### 📋 Given Expression

$$a + a * (b - c) + (b - c) * d$$

### 🧮 Step-by-Step DAG Construction

**Step 1:** Recognize the **common subexpression** $(b - c)$.

**Step 2:** Process left-to-right:

**Sub-expression 1:** $b - c$
- Create node $n_1$ with `op = -`, children = $b, c$.

**Sub-expression 2:** $a * (b - c)$
- Create node $n_2$ with `op = *`, children = $a, n_1$.

**Sub-expression 3:** $a + a * (b - c)$
- Create node $n_3$ with `op = +`, children = $a, n_2$.

**Sub-expression 4:** $(b - c) * d$
- **Reuse** existing node $n_1$ for $(b - c)$!
- Create node $n_4$ with `op = *`, children = $n_1, d$.

**Sub-expression 5:** Final $+$ operation
- Create node $n_5$ with `op = +`, children = $n_3, n_4$.

### 📊 Resulting DAG Node Inventory

| Node ID | Operator | Children | Notes |
|---------|----------|----------|-------|
| $n_1$ | `-` | $b, c$ | Shared subexpression |
| $n_2$ | `*` | $a, n_1$ | Unique |
| $n_3$ | `+` | $a, n_2$ | Unique |
| $n_4$ | `*` | $n_1, d$ | Unique |
| $n_5$ | `+` | $n_3, n_4$ | Root |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Validation Rule:** Total nodes in DAG = **5**. AST would have created **7 nodes** (with a duplicate `b - c` node). The DAG saves **2 redundant nodes**, automatically enabling **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Generating Three-Address Code for a Compound Expression

### 📋 Given Expression

$$a = b * -c + b * -c$$

### 🧮 Step-by-Step TAC Generation

**Step 1:** Evaluate right-hand side left-to-right.

**Step 2:** Apply TAC rules:

$$
t_1 = \text{uminus} \, c
$$

$$
t_2 = b * t_1
$$

$$
t_3 = \text{uminus} \, c
$$

$$
t_4 = b * t_3
$$

$$
t_5 = t_2 + t_4
$$

$$
a = t_5
$$

### 📊 Quadruple Representation Table

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|----|------|------|--------|
| (0) | uminus | c | — | $t_1$ |
| (1) | * | b | $t_1$ | $t_2$ |
| (2) | uminus | c | — | $t_3$ |
| (3) | * | b | $t_3$ | $t_4$ |
| (4) | + | $t_2$ | $t_4$ | $t_5$ |
| (5) | = | $t_5$ | — | a |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** TAC naively generates **two separate uminus operations** and **two multiplications**, even though `uminus c` is computed twice. A DAG-based or CSE-optimized version would compute `uminus c` only once.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Converting TAC to Triples (For Contrast)

### 📋 Given TAC (from §3.3)

The same instructions in **Triple** form (using instruction indices instead of names):

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|----|------|------|
| (0) | uminus | c | — |
| (1) | * | b | (0) |
| (2) | uminus | c | — |
| (3) | * | b | (2) |
| (4) | + | (1) | (3) |
| (5) | = | a | (4) |

### 📐 Analytical Comparison

$$
\text{Triple Size} = n \times 3 \text{ fields} = 6 \times 3 = 18 \text{ fields}
$$

$$
\text{Quadruple Size} = n \times 4 \text{ fields} = 6 \times 4 = 24 \text{ fields}
$$

**Space saved by Triple** = $(24 - 18) / 24 \times 100\% = 25\%$

**Drawback:** If instruction (2) is moved by optimization, all references to (2) break, requiring global renumbering. **Indirect Triple** solves this by using an extra pointer table.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Quadruple for Control Flow

### 📋 Given Code Fragment

```text
if (x < y) then
    z = x + y;
else
    z = x - y;
```

### 🧮 TAC Generation

$$
t_1 = x < y
$$

$$
\text{if } t_1 \text{ goto } L_1
$$

$$
t_2 = x - y
$$

$$
z = t_2
$$

$$
\text{goto } L_2
$$

$$
L_1: \quad t_3 = x + y
$$

$$
z = t_3
$$

$$
L_2:
$$

### 📊 Quadruple Table

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|----|------|------|--------|
| (0) | `<` | x | y | $t_1$ |
| (1) | if | $t_1$ | — | $L_1$ |
| (2) | `-` | x | y | $t_2$ |
| (3) | = | $t_2$ | — | z |
| (4) | goto | — | — | $L_2$ |
| (5) | `+` | x | y | $t_3$ |
| (6) | = | $t_3$ | — | z |
| (7) | label | — | — | $L_2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** The `label` instruction at the end is **not optional** — it explicitly marks the join point after the `if-then-else` branch. Examiners frequently deduct marks for missing it.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — L-Attributed Evaluation Flow on Parse Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Root: A inherited attrs] --> B[Visit X1 leftmost child]
    A --> C[Visit X2 compute using X1 and A]
    A --> D[Visit X3 compute using X1 X2 and A]
    A --> E[Visit Xn last child]
    B --> F[DFS Left to Right]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Synthesized attrs of A computed last]
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- The downward arrows (Root → Children) represent **inherited attribute flow**.
- The DFS traversal processes **left-to-right** siblings.
- The final synthesis at the root uses all child results.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — DAG for $a + a*(b-c) + (b-c)*d$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n5[Node n5 plus] --> n3[Node n3 plus]
    n5 --> n4[Node n4 mult]
    n3 --> a1[Leaf a]
    n3 --> n2[Node n2 mult]
    n4 --> n1[Node n1 minus SHARED]
    n4 --> d1[Leaf d]
    n2 --> a2[Leaf a]
    n2 --> n1
    n1 --> b1[Leaf b]
    n1 --> c1[Leaf c]
```

**Visualization Highlight:** Notice that **Node n1** (representing $b - c$) is **reused** by both `n2` and `n4`. This shared structure is the defining property of a DAG vs. an AST.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture: TAC Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Parse Tree plus AST] --> B[Semantic Analyzer]
    B --> C[Annotated AST]
    C --> D[TAC Generator Module]
    D --> E[Three Address Code]
    E --> F1[Quadruple Form]
    E --> F2[Triple Form]
    E --> F3[Indirect Triple Form]
    F1 --> G[Optimization Pass]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Optimized IR]
    H --> I[Target Code Generator]
```

**Architecture Insight:** The pipeline shows how the same TAC source can be emitted in multiple representation forms (quadruple/triple/indirect triple), all of which feed into the optimization stage.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Quadruple Table for $a = b*-c + b*-c$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Quad[Quadruple Table]
        direction LR
        R0[Index 0 uminus c to t1]
        R1[Index 1 mult b t1 to t2]
        R2[Index 2 uminus c to t3]
        R3[Index 3 mult b t3 to t4]
        R4[Index 4 add t2 t4 to t5]
        R5[Index 5 assign t5 to a]
        R0 --> R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5
    end
```

**Reading Note:** Each row in this flow corresponds to one quadruple. The arrows indicate **sequential execution order**, not data dependency.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — L-Attributed vs S-Attributed Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph S_Attr[S-Attributed: Only Upward Flow]
        S1[Parent Node] --> S2[Child Node]
        S2 --> S3[Grandchild]
    end
    subgraph L_Attr[L-Attributed: Down + Left-to-Right Flow]
        L1[Parent Node] --> L2[Child Left]
        L1 --> L3[Child Right]
        L2 --> L4[Grandchild receives inherited from parent and left sibling]
    end
```

**Comparison Insight:** S-attributed allows only the upward arrows; L-attributed additionally permits downward (parent → child) and sideways (left-sibling → right-sibling) flow, hence its name **L**eft-to-right.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is an L-Attributed Definition? Explain with an example.**

**✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **L-Attributed Definition** is a Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD) in which each **inherited attribute** of a symbol $X_j$ in a production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$ depends only on:
1. The inherited attributes of the **left-hand side** symbol $A$, and
2. **Any attribute** of symbols $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_{j-1}$ appearing **to its left** in the production.

**Example:** Consider the production $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$ with semantic rule:
$$E_1.inh = E.inh + 1$$

Here $E_1$'s inherited attribute depends on $E$'s inherited attribute (left-hand side), satisfying the L-property.

---

### ❓ Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three forms of intermediate representation used in compilers.**

**✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three principal forms of intermediate representation are:

1. **Three-Address Code (TAC):** Linear, instruction-like IR of the form $x = y \, op \, z$.
2. **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST):** Hierarchical representation eliminating syntactic noise.
3. **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG):** Compressed form of AST that shares common subexpressions.

(Other valid forms: Postfix notation, Control Flow Graph, SSA form.)

---

### ❓ Question A3 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Differentiate between Quadruple and Triple representation of TAC.**

**✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Quadruple | Triple |
|---------|-----------|--------|
| Fields per row | 4 (`op, arg1, arg2, result`) | 3 (`op, arg1, arg2`) |
| Operand naming | Uses **symbolic names** | Uses **instruction positions** |
| Reordering | Easy (rows are independent) | Hard (positions change) |
| Space usage | Higher | Lower (25% less) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question B (Choice A) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Define L-Attributed Definition. Show that the following SDD is L-attributed or not.

| Production | Semantic Rules |
|------------|----------------|
| $A \rightarrow B \, C$ | $B.i = A.i; \quad A.s = B.s + C.s$ |
| $B \rightarrow b$ | $B.s = 2 \times B.i$ |
| $C \rightarrow c$ | $C.s = C.i + 1$ |

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Generate Three-Address Code and corresponding Quadruple representation for the expression:

$$x = (a + b) * (c + d) - (a + b)$$

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B (Choice A)

#### Solution to (a): L-Attributed Verification

**Definition (2 marks):** An SDD is L-Attributed if for every production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$, each inherited attribute of $X_j$ depends only on:
- Inherited attributes of $A$, and
- Attributes of $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_{j-1}$.

**Verification (5 marks):**

**Production 1: $A \rightarrow B \, C$**
- Inherited attribute $B.i = A.i$.
- $B$ is the **first** symbol after $A$, so there are no symbols to its left.
- $B.i$ depends on $A.i$ (left-hand side attribute only). ✅ **L-property satisfied.**

**Production 2: $B \rightarrow b$**
- No inherited attributes on the right-hand side. ✅ **Trivially L-attributed.**

**Production 3: $C \rightarrow c$**
- The inherited attribute $C.i$ originates from $A \rightarrow B \, C$ via (implicit or explicit) rule.
- $C.i$ depends on $B$ (which is to its left). ✅ **L-property satisfied.**

**Conclusion:** The given SDD is **L-Attributed**. ✅

---

#### Solution to (b): TAC and Quadruple Generation

**Step 1:** Identify the common subexpression $(a + b)$ and $(c + d)$.

**Step 2:** Generate TAC:

$$
t_1 = a + b
$$

$$
t_2 = c + d
$$

$$
t_3 = t_1 * t_2
$$

$$
t_4 = t_3 - t_1
$$

$$
x = t_4
$$

**Quadruple Representation Table (4 marks):**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|----|------|------|--------|
| (0) | `+` | a | b | $t_1$ |
| (1) | `+` | c | d | $t_2$ |
| (2) | `*` | $t_1$ | $t_2$ | $t_3$ |
| (3) | `-` | $t_3$ | $t_1$ | $t_4$ |
| (4) | `=` | $t_4$ | — | x |

**Optimization Insight (Bonus 1 mark):** Since $(a+b)$ is computed once and reused as $t_1$, this is already CSE-optimized.

---

### ❓ Question B (Choice B) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the concept of variants of syntax tree. Construct the DAG for the expression:

$$(a + b) * (a + b) + (a + b)$$

Show the number of nodes saved compared to the AST.

#### (b) **[7 Marks]** Explain Three-Address Code. Generate TAC and its Quadruple form for:

$$
\text{if } (a > b) \text{ then } x = a + b \text{ else } x = a - b
$$

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B (Choice B)

#### Solution to (a): DAG Construction

**Conceptual Explanation (2 marks):**
Variants of syntax tree include **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** and **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**. The DAG is a compact variant that **shares nodes** representing common subexpressions, eliminating redundancy and enabling **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)**.

**DAG Construction (3 marks):**

For the expression $(a+b) * (a+b) + (a+b)$:

**Step 1:** Compute $a + b$ → Create node $n_1$ with `op = +`, children = $a, b$.

**Step 2:** Compute $(a+b) * (a+b)$ → Create node $n_2$ with `op = *`, children = $n_1, n_1$.

**Step 3:** Compute $n_2 + (a+b)$ → Create node $n_3$ with `op = +`, children = $n_2, n_1$.

**Node Count Analysis (2 marks):**

| Form | Total Nodes | Redundant Nodes |
|------|-------------|-----------------|
| AST | 7 (3 for the three $(a+b)$s + 2 inner ops + 1 root + 1 multiplication) | 4 redundant nodes |
| DAG | 3 nodes ($n_1, n_2, n_3$) | 0 |

**Nodes Saved:** $7 - 3 = 4$ nodes saved ✅

---

#### Solution to (b): TAC for If-Then-Else

**Three-Address Code Concept (2 marks):**
TAC is a linear IR where each instruction contains at most **three addresses** (two operands + one destination), corresponding to simple machine-like operations.

**TAC Generation (3 marks):**

$$
t_1 = a > b
$$

$$
\text{if } t_1 \text{ goto } L_1
$$

$$
t_2 = a - b
$$

$$
x = t_2
$$

$$
\text{goto } L_2
$$

$$
L_1: \quad t_3 = a + b
$$

$$
x = t_3
$$

$$
L_2:
$$

**Quadruple Representation (2 marks):**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|----|------|------|--------|
| (0) | `>` | a | b | $t_1$ |
| (1) | if | $t_1$ | — | $L_1$ |
| (2) | `-` | a | b | $t_2$ |
| (3) | `=` | $t_2$ | — | x |
| (4) | goto | — | — | $L_2$ |
| (5) | `+` | a | b | $t_3$ |
| (6) | `=` | $t_3$ | — | x |
| (7) | label | — | — | $L_2$ |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `label` instruction** at the join point of if-else. Always mark the fall-through target explicitly.
> 2. **Mistaking "L" for "Left only"** — L-attributed allows parent → child and left-sibling → right-sibling, **not** just left subtree.
> 3. **Confusing Quadruple field order** — Standard KTU order is `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`, NOT `(result, op, arg1, arg2)`. Verify before writing.
> 4. **Missing the common subexpression in DAG** — If the expression has $b-c$ twice, the DAG must show a **single shared node**, not two duplicates.
> 5. **Declaring every SDD as L-attributed** — Always verify by checking if any attribute depends on a **right sibling**. If yes, it is **NOT** L-attributed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🧠 Rapid-Revision Checklist

#### 📌 L-Attributed Definitions
- ⭐ Inherited attributes of $X_j$ may depend **only on** $A$'s inherited + $X_1 \dots X_{j-1}$'s attributes.
- ⭐ Evaluated by **depth-first, left-to-right traversal**.
- ⭐ **S-attributed ⊂ L-attributed** (strict subset).
- ⭐ Required for type declarations, symbol table propagation, code generation.

#### 📌 Intermediate Code Generation
- ⭐ IR is the **bridge** between front-end and back-end.
- ⭐ Must be **machine-independent**, optimizable, and easy to produce.
- ⭐ Common forms: Postfix, TAC, AST, DAG, CFG.

#### 📌 Variants of Syntax Tree
- ⭐ **AST** removes punctuation, keeps operators and operands.
- ⭐ **DAG** shares nodes for common subexpressions → automatic CSE.
- ⭐ DAG node count is **strictly ≤** AST node count.

#### 📌 Three-Address Code (TAC)
- ⭐ Form: $x = y \, op \, z$ — maximum 3 addresses per instruction.
- ⭐ Exposes temporaries, simplifies control flow.
- ⭐ Standard instruction types: binary, unary, copy, jumps, calls, array ops.

#### 📌 Quadruple Representation
- ⭐ Table with fields: `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`.
- ⭐ Each TAC instruction = exactly **one quadruple row**.
- ⭐ For unary ops, `arg2` is left blank.
- ⭐ Quadruple vs. Triple: Quadruple uses names; Triple uses positions.
- ⭐ Quadruple is **most optimization-friendly** form.

#### 📌 High-Yield KTU Exam Patterns
- ⭐ **Pattern 1:** "Verify whether given SDD is L-attributed" → Check all productions for right-sibling dependencies.
- ⭐ **Pattern 2:** "Generate TAC and Quadruples for expression" → Use temporaries $t_1, t_2, \dots$.
- ⭐ **Pattern 3:** "Construct DAG and count nodes" → Identify shared subexpressions.
- ⭐ **Pattern 4:** "Compare Quadruple and Triple" → Use the comparison table.

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 Last-Minute Mnemonics:**
> - **"L = Left-to-Right"** — L-attributed flows left.
> - **"QTR = Quadruple-Triple-Renumber"** — Quadruples don't need renumbering after optimization.
> - **"DAG = Don't Add Garbage"** — DAGs eliminate redundant nodes.
> - **"TAC = Three Addresses (max)"** — Never write an instruction with 4+ addresses.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Triple. Run Time Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Triple. Run Time Environments ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Three-Address Code (TAC) — The Foundation of IR **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** Three-Address Code (TAC) is an intermediate representation (IR) used by compilers in which each instruction contains **at most three operands** and is typically of the form: $$\text{x := y op z}$$ where `x`, `y`, and `z` can be names, constants, or compiler-generated temporaries. The `op` can be any arithmetic, logical, or relational operator (including assignment). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why TAC?** It is the canonical bridge between high-level semantics and target machine code. Each TAC instruction corresponds (roughly) to **one** assembly instruction, making code generation straightforward. Both Aho-Sethi-Ullman and the standard KTU recommended texts treat TAC as the IR of choice. --- ### 1.2 Forms of Three-Address Code — The Three Notations The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus explicitly demands the study of multiple **representational notations** for TAC. Each notation trades off memory efficiency, optimization ease, and ease of code movement: | Notation | Components | Key Idea | |----------|------------|----------| | **Quadruples** | `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` | A standalone record — the `result` field is an explicit temporary name. | | **Triples** | `(op, arg1, arg2)` | No `result` field; the **position** of the triple itself acts as the implicit name. | | **Indirect Triples** | A list of pointers to triples | Adds an indirection layer enabling easy reordering during optimization. | --- ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: TAC Notations as Filing Systems > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — Three Ways to Write a To-Do List** > > - **Quadruples** are like writing each task on a fresh sticky note with a unique ID (e.g., "t1", "t2") and a label. You can shuffle them freely because every note is self-contained. > - **Triples** are like numbering your tasks consecutively: task #1, task #2, task #3. If you reorder them, you must renumber all references — but no labels are needed, saving paper. > - **Indirect Triples** are like keeping a numbered INDEX that points into a backing list. You reorder the index without touching the backing list — perfect for reshuffling without rewriting. This captures the **space-time trade-off** in compiler IR design. --- ### 1.4 Run Time Environment — The Executive Memory Manager **Definition:** The **Run-Time Environment** (also called *execution environment* or *storage organization*) is the set of mechanisms that the target machine / operating system provides to manage memory and execute the compiled program. It is the runtime instantiation of the compiler's static decisions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):** The Run Time Environment determines *where* each program entity (variables, parameters, temporaries) lives at runtime. The three primary allocation strategies are: > 1. **Static Allocation** — compile-time, fixed addresses. > 2. **Stack Allocation** — LIFO, function-call based. > 3. **Heap Allocation** — dynamic, arbitrary lifetime. --- ### 1.5 Intuitive Analogy: Run Time Environment as a Warehouse > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — The Three Warehouses** > > Imagine you are a logistics manager running a company: > - **Static Warehouse** — A pre-built shelf with fixed labeled slots. Items assigned at startup never move. Cheap, fast, but inflexible. > - **Stack Warehouse** — A spring-loaded pallet system: when a new job arrives, you push a fresh pallet on top; when the job ends, you pop it. Perfect for nested tasks. > - **Heap Warehouse** — A free-for-all open yard where pallets can be placed anywhere and removed in any order. Maximum flexibility, but slower and needs a "garbage collector" to free space. This is *exactly* how a compiler partitions the runtime memory: code, static data, stack, and heap regions. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Quadruple Representation — The Baseline A quadruple is a 4-tuple `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`. The `result` is typically a compiler-generated temporary `t1, t2, t3, ...`. **Example:** Consider the expression `a + b * c + d`. The TAC sequence with explicit temporaries is: $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &:= b * c \\ t_2 &:= a + t_1 \\ t_3 &:= t_2 + d \end{aligned} $$ The quadruple table representation is: | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result | |---|----|------|------|--------| | 0 | `*` | b | c | t1 | | 1 | `+` | a | t1 | t2 | | 2 | `+` | t2 | d | t3 | --- ### 2.2 Triple Representation — The Position-Based IR A triple is a 3-tuple `(op, arg1, arg2)`. **There is no `result` field.** Instead, the *index* (position) of the triple itself serves as the implicit name of its result. For the same expression `a + b * c + d`, the triple table is: | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | |---|----|------|------| | 0 | `*` | b | c | | 1 | `+` | a | (0) | | 2 | `+` | (1) | d | > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice the parentheses — they denote **triple references**. `(0)` means "the value computed by triple #0". This is the defining feature of triples. --- ### 2.3 Indirect Triples — The Optimization-Friendly Variant **Problem with Plain Triples:** When the optimizer performs code motion (e.g., reordering for register allocation or common subexpression elimination), it must *shift* triples around. Since each triple's name is its position, shifting requires cascading index updates. **Solution — Indirect Triples:** Keep the triple definitions in a stable backing list, but introduce an **execution pointer list** (the "instruction list"). Reordering = permuting the pointer list only. | Execution List | → Points to Triple # | |----------------|----------------------| | 0 | 0 | | 1 | 1 | | 2 | 2 | If we reorder so that #1 and #2 are swapped, we only change the pointer list: `[0, 2, 1]`, leaving the backing triples untouched. --- ### 2.4 Comparison Table — The Definitive KTU Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **This table is a high-frequency KTU University Exam question — memorize it verbatim.** | Property | Quadruples | Triples | Indirect Triples | |----------|-----------|---------|------------------| | Fields per record | **4** | **3** | 3 (triple) + 1 (pointer) | | Result named by | Explicit `result` field | Position index | Position index | | Reordering cost | Easy (records independent) | Hard (must renumber references) | Easy (reorder pointers only) | | Space overhead | High (explicit temp names) | Low | Moderate (extra pointer list) | | Used by | Most modern compilers historically | Rare in practice | Optimization passes | | Assignment | Direct copy | Requires `x := y op z` → `(=, y, -, x)` syntax | Same as triples | --- ### 2.5 Boolean / Relational TAC Extensions For control flow and boolean expressions, TAC uses labelled jumps. Example for `if a < b then c := 1 else c := 2`: | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result | |---|----|------|------|--------| | 100 | `<` | a | b | t1 | | 101 | `ifFalse` t1 | → 103 | — | — | | 102 | `:=` | 1 | — | c | | 103 | `:=` | 2 | — | c | **Note:** `ifFalse` is a one-operand conditional jump — it takes only `arg1`. --- ### 2.6 Run Time Environment — The Memory Layout The logical memory layout of an executing program (KTU 2024 textbook standard) is: ``` ┌─────────────────────────┐ HIGH ADDRESS │ STACK (↓) │ ← grows downward │ Activation Records │ ├─────────────────────────┤ │ FREE │ (Heap grows up, Stack grows down) ├─────────────────────────┤ │ HEAP (↑) │ ← grows upward │ Dynamic Allocations │ ├─────────────────────────┤ │ STATIC DATA │ ← global vars, constants ├─────────────────────────┤ │ CODE (TEXT) │ LOW ADDRESS └─────────────────────────┘ ``` --- ### 2.7 The Three Allocation Strategies — Detailed Analysis #### 2.7.1 Static Allocation - All binding of names → storage locations happens **at compile time**. - No recursion allowed (since one fixed location cannot serve two active invocations). - No dynamic data structures (no `malloc` equivalent). - Used in: FORTRAN 77, simple embedded systems. **Cost:** Fastest access (address is a compile-time constant), but extremely restrictive. #### 2.7.2 Stack Allocation (LIFO) - Each procedure call pushes an **Activation Record** (AR) onto the run-time stack. - On return, the AR is popped — automatically reclaiming locals. - Supports **recursion** because each call gets a fresh AR.
