Activation Record fields (standard KTU 2024 layout): | Offset / Order | Field | Purpose | |----------------|-------|---------| | Top | Returned Value | Space for the value the callee returns | | — | Parameters | Actual arguments passed in | | — | Saved Machine Status | Registers, PC at call site | | — | Access/Static Link | Pointer to enclosing scope's AR | | — | Control Link | Pointer to caller's AR | | — | Saved Return Address | Resume execution after call | | Bottom | Local Variables | Procedure's own locals and temporaries | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Control Link** (dynamic link) points to the AR of the *caller* — this is what enables a stack-walk debugger to reconstruct the call stack. > The **Access Link** (static link) points to the AR of the *lexical parent* — this is what enables lexical scoping in languages like Pascal, Ada, and Scheme. #### 2.7.3 Heap Allocation - Memory can be allocated and deallocated in **any order**. - Required for: dynamic data structures (linked lists, trees), objects whose lifetime exceeds the function that created them. - Needs explicit deallocation (`free`, `delete`) or automatic **garbage collection**. --- ### 2.8 Parameter Passing Mechanisms — The High-Yield Topic > [!WARNING] > KTU examiners frequently ask for a 14-mark question on parameter passing. The five standard mechanisms below are mandatory. | Mechanism | Caller's View | Callee's View | Modifies Caller's Variable? | Notes | |-----------|---------------|---------------|-----------------------------|-------| | **Call by Value** | Evaluates arg, copies it | Receives a copy in fresh location | **No** | Safe, default in C | | **Call by Reference** | Passes address (`&x`) | Receives pointer; writes via dereference | **Yes** | Aliases possible | | **Call by Value-Result** | Copies arg; on return, copies result back | Receives copy, modifies it | **Yes** | Combination of above | | **Call by Name** | Substitutes the textual argument (like a macro) | Substitutes the formal parameter textually | Yes (via substitution) | Lazy evaluation; used in Algol 60 | | **Call by Copy-Restore** | Same as Value-Result | Same as Value-Result | Yes | Order of evaluation-dependent | --- ### 2.9 The KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference > [!NOTE] > Use this cheat sheet for last-minute revision before any KTU University Exam. | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes | |---|---------|----------------|-------| | 1 | TAC instruction form | `x := y op z` | At most 3 operands | | 2 | Quadruple tuple size | **4 fields** | `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` | | 3 | Triple tuple size | **3 fields** | `(op, arg1, arg2)` | | 4 | Indirect triple | Pointer list + backing triples | Enables easy reordering | | 5 | AR growth direction | **Stack ↓** (downward) | Toward lower addresses | | 6 | Heap growth direction | **Heap ↑** (upward) | Toward higher addresses | | 7 | AR size | `sizeof(parameters) + sizeof(locals) + sizeof(control links) + sizeof(saved state)` | Compiler-determined, fixed per procedure | | 8 | Static allocation constraint | No recursion, no dynamic structures | Compile-time fixed addresses | | 9 | Activation depth | `D(n) = D(parent(n)) + 1`, base `D(main) = 0` | Used in static-scope display table | | 10 | Reference to data at depth offset $d$ from current AR | `access_link` traversed $d$ times | Each access is $O(d)$ worst case | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Converting an Arithmetic Expression to Triples **Source Expression:** $$E = (a + b) \cdot (c - d) + e$$ **Step 1 — Identify operations and precedence.** Highest precedence: `*`, `+`, `-` are left-associative. Parentheses force `(a+b)` and `(c-d)` first. **Step 2 — Generate TAC sequence using temporaries.** $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &:= a + b \\ t_2 &:= c - d \\ t_3 &:= t_1 \cdot t_2 \\ t_4 &:= t_3 + e \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Translate to Quadruple form.** | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result | |---|----|------|------|--------| | 0 | `+` | a | b | t1 | | 1 | `-` | c | d | t2 | | 2 | `*` | t1 | t2 | t3 | | 3 | `+` | t3 | e | t4 | *Explanation:* Each row corresponds to one TAC line. The `result` column holds the explicit temporary name. **Step 4 — Translate to Triple form.** We strip the `result` column and reference intermediate results by their position. | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | |---|----|------|------| | 0 | `+` | a | b | | 1 | `-` | c | d | | 2 | `*` | (0) | (1) | | 3 | `+` | (2) | e | *Explanation:* - Triple 0 computes $a+b$, so its position-`0` value is referenced as `(0)` in later instructions. - Triple 1 computes $c-d$, referenced as `(1)`. - Triple 2 computes `(a+b)\cdot(c-d) = (0) \cdot (1)`. - Triple 3 computes the final result using the value of triple 2. **Step 5 — Translate to Indirect Triple form.** Backing triple list (immutable): | index | op | arg1 | arg2 | |-------|----|------|------| | 0 | `+` | a | b | | 1 | `-` | c | d | | 2 | `*` | (0) | (1) | | 3 | `+` | (2) | e | Pointer / instruction list (reorderable): | instr # | → Triple index | |---------|----------------| | 0 | 0 | | 1 | 1 | | 2 | 2 | | 3 | 3 | To swap the order of computation (e.g., for optimization), we only permute the instruction list, leaving the backing list intact. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Boolean / Branch Expression **Source:** ```c if (x < y) and (p == q) then z := 1; ``` **Step 1 — Generate TAC.** $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &:= x < y \\ t_2 &:= p == q \\ t_3 &:= t_1 \text{ and } t_2 \\ \text{ifFalse } t_3 & \text{ goto } L_{\text{end}} \\ z &:= 1 \\ L_{\text{end}}: & \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Quadruple table.** | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result | |---|----|------|------|--------| | 0 | `<` | x | y | t1 | | 1 | `==` | p | q | t2 | | 2 | `and` | t1 | t2 | t3 | | 3 | `ifFalse` | t3 | — | L_end | | 4 | `:=` | 1 | — | z | | 5 | `label` | — | — | L_end | *Explanation:* Quadruple #3 uses `arg1 = t3` (the boolean condition), `arg2 = —` (unused), `result = L_end` (the jump target). **Step 3 — Triple table.** | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | |---|----|------|------| | 0 | `<` | x | y | | 1 | `==` | p | q | | 2 | `and` | (0) | (1) | | 3 | `ifFalse` | (2) | — | | 4 | `:=` | 1 | z | | 5 | `label` | L_end | — | --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Activation Record Arithmetic **Problem:** A procedure has: - 4 parameters (each 4 bytes) - 3 local variables (each 4 bytes) - 2 control links (each 8 bytes) - 1 saved return address (8 bytes) - 1 returned value slot (4 bytes) **Derivation of total AR size:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Size}_{\text{params}} &= 4 \times 4 = 16 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{Size}_{\text{locals}} &= 3 \times 4 = 12 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{Size}_{\text{links}} &= 2 \times 8 = 16 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{Size}_{\text{ret addr}} &= 1 \times 8 = 8 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{Size}_{\text{ret val}} &= 1 \times 4 = 4 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{Total AR size} &= 16 + 12 + 16 + 8 + 4 = \mathbf{56 \text{ bytes}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > In a stack-based RTE, when a procedure is called, the stack pointer decrements by 56 bytes (since the stack grows downward) before storing local data. --- ### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Parameter Passing Simulation **Source:** ```c void f(int a, int b) { a = a + 1; b = b + 2; } int main() { int x = 10; f(x, x); print(x); } ``` **Step 1 — Trace Call by Value.** | Line | Action | `a` (in `f`) | `b` (in `f`) | `x` (in `main`) | |------|--------|--------------|--------------|-----------------| | Initial | — | — | — | 10 | | Call | Copy `x` to `a`, copy `x` to `b` | 10 | 10 | 10 | | `a = a+1` | `a` becomes 11 | 11 | 10 | 10 | | `b = b+2` | `b` becomes 12 | 11 | 12 | 10 | | Return | AR popped; `x` unchanged | — | — | **10** | **Output:** `10` (caller's `x` is unmodified). **Step 2 — Trace Call by Reference.** | Line | Action | `*(&a)` (=`x`) | `*(&b)` (=`x`) | |------|--------|----------------|-----------------| | Initial | — | 10 | 10 | | Call | `a` and `b` both point to `x` | 10 | 10 | | `a = a+1` | `x` becomes 11 | 11 | 11 | | `b = b+2` | `x` becomes 13 | 13 | 13 | | Return | `x` modified | **13** | — | **Output:** `13` (caller's `x` IS modified — note the aliasing artefact: `a` and `b` refer to the *same* location). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The aliasing problem:** Call by Reference can produce surprising results when the same variable is passed multiple times — operations compose differently than with Call by Value. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Identifying TAC Notation from a Given Problem > [!NOTE] > Use this decision matrix when a KTU question gives you an expression and asks "represent using triples / quadruples / indirect triples". | Question Asks For | What to Produce | |-------------------|-----------------| | "Generate TAC" | A sequence of `x := y op z` lines with named temporaries. | | "Represent using Quadruples" | A 4-column table `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`. | | "Represent using Triples" | A 3-column table `(op, arg1, arg2)`, using `(i)` to reference position `i`. | | "Represent using Indirect Triples" | Backing triple table + a separate instruction pointer list. | | "Show postfix / prefix notation" | Standard postfix/prefix of the expression. | | "DAG representation" | A directed acyclic graph (different from TAC, but related). | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Architectural Flow — From Source Code to Executable via TAC ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Source Code in HLL] --> B[Lexical Analysis] B --> C[Syntax Analysis
Parse Tree] C --> D[Semantic Analysis
Type Checking] D --> E[Intermediate Code Gen
Three Address Code] E --> F{TAC Notation Choice} F --> G[Quadruples
4-tuple record] F --> H[Triples
3-tuple position based] F --> I[Indirect Triples
pointer indirection] G --> J[Code Optimization
on IR] H --> J I --> J J --> K[Target Code Generation
Assembly] K --> L[Run Time Environment
Memory Layout] L --> M[Activation Record
on Stack] M --> N[Execution
CPU] ``` *Explanation:* This is the canonical compiler pipeline. The TAC notations appear at the IR stage. The RTE is the runtime substrate that hosts the generated assembly. --- ### 4.2 Activation Record Layout — Detailed Mermaid Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph AR[Activation Record - Grows Downward in Memory] direction TB F1[Returned Value Slot
4 bytes] F2[Parameters
incoming arguments] F3[Saved Machine Status
registers and flags] F4[Access Link
static link to parent AR] F5[Control Link
dynamic link to caller AR] F6[Saved Return Address
resume point in caller] F7[Local Variables and Temporaries] end F1 --> F2 F2 --> F3 F3 --> F4 F4 --> F5 F5 --> F6 F6 --> F7 ``` *Explanation:* Memory grows downward in standard calling conventions. The lowest address is at the top of this diagram (where the new AR is allocated by decrementing the stack pointer). --- ### 4.3 Memory Layout of a Running Program ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HIGHADDR[High Memory Addresses] direction TB STACK[Stack Segment
Growth direction DOWNWARD] FREE[Free Space
for expansion] HEAP[Heap Segment
Growth direction UPWARD] STATIC[Static Data Segment
globals and constants] CODE[Code Text Segment
machine instructions] end STACK --> FREE FREE --> HEAP HEAP --> STATIC STATIC --> CODE CODE --> LOWADDR[Low Memory Addresses] ``` *Explanation:* Stack and heap grow toward each other. If they collide, the program runs out of memory (`STACK OVERFLOW` or `OUT OF MEMORY`). --- ### 4.4 Triple Notation — Data Structure Visualisation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph T0[Triple 0] A0[op +] A1[arg1 a] A2[arg2 b] end subgraph T1[Triple 1] B0[op *] B1[arg1 (0)] B2[arg2 c] end subgraph T2[Triple 2] C0[op -] C1[arg1 (1)] C2[arg2 d] end T0 --> T1 T1 --> T2 ``` *Explanation:* Triple 0 computes `a+b`. Triple 1 references it as `(0)` in `arg1`. Triple 2 references Triple 1 as `(1)` in `arg1`. The chain shows the dependency DAG inherent in the linearised triple list. --- ### 4.5 Indirect Triples — Indirection Layer ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IL[Instruction List - Reorderable Pointers] P0[ptr0 = 0] P1[ptr1 = 2] P2[ptr2 = 1] P3[ptr3 = 3] end subgraph BL[Backing Triple List - Immutable] B0[index0 - plus a b] B1[index1 - minus c d] B2[index2 - star (0) (1)] B3[index3 - assign (2) x] end P0 --> B0 P1 --> B2 P2 --> B1 P3 --> B3 ``` *Explanation:* The instruction list `[0, 2, 1, 3]` shows a reordered execution: backing triples 0, 2, 1, 3 are visited. Because positions in backing triples still refer to their original indices (not pointer list indices), the references inside backing triples remain valid even after reordering. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Q1]** **Q1.** *Differentiate between Quadruples and Triples as representations of Three-Address Code. List any two advantages of triples over quadruples.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Quadruples | Triples | |--------|-----------|---------| | Fields | 4: `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` | 3: `(op, arg1, arg2)` | | Result name | Explicit temporary name in `result` | Implicit by position (e.g., `(0)`) | **Two advantages of Triples:** 1. **Less memory consumption:** Only 3 fields per instruction instead of 4. The `result` temporary name is implicit, eliminating redundant symbol-table-like lookups for temporaries. 2. **No need for temporary management:** The compiler does not need to generate and track unique temporary names (`t1, t2, ...`) since each result is identified by its position in the list. *(Any two valid advantages = 3 marks.)* --- **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Q2]** **Q2.** *What is an Activation Record? List any four fields it contains.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **Activation Record** (AR) is a contiguous block of memory allocated on the runtime stack for each active invocation of a procedure. It contains all information needed for that invocation to execute and return. **Any four fields:** 1. **Parameters** — values passed by the caller. 2. **Local variables** — variables declared inside the procedure. 3. **Control link (dynamic link)** — pointer to the caller's AR. 4. **Return address** — location in the caller to resume execution. 5. *(Acceptable alternatives: Saved registers, Access link, Returned value slot.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Question A]** **(a)** Explain the **three-address code** representation. Convert the following expression into **triples, quadruples, and indirect triples**: $$E = (a - b) / (c + d) * e$$ **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the various **storage allocation strategies** used in a Run Time Environment. Compare static, stack, and heap allocation with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution A(a):** *(7 marks — split: 3 for explanation, 4 for conversion)* **Three-Address Code (3 marks):** TAC is an intermediate representation where each instruction is of the form `x := y op z`. It uses at most three operands and corresponds roughly to one machine instruction. Its key advantages are: - Easy translation from syntax tree (via syntax-directed translation). - Simplifies target code generation and optimization. - Machine-independent — same IR can be retargeted. **TAC Sequence for the expression:** $$ \begin{aligned} t_1 &:= a - b \\ t_2 &:= c + d \\ t_3 &:= t_1 / t_2 \\ t_4 &:= t_3 * e \end{aligned} $$ **Quadruple Table (1 mark):** | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result | |---|----|------|------|--------| | 0 | `-` | a | b | t1 | | 1 | `+` | c | d | t2 | | 2 | `/` | t1 | t2 | t3 | | 3 | `*` | t3 | e | t4 | **Triple Table (1 mark):** | # | op | arg1 | arg2 | |---|----|------|------| | 0 | `-` | a | b | | 1 | `+` | c | d | | 2 | `/` | (0) | (1) | | 3 | `*` | (2) | e | **Indirect Triple Table (1 mark):** Backing Triple List: | index | op | arg1 | arg2 | |-------|----|------|------| | 0 | `-` | a | b | | 1 | `+` | c | d | | 2 | `/` | (0) | (1) | | 3 | `*` | (2) | e | Instruction Pointer List: `[0, 1, 2, 3]` --- **Solution A(b):** *(7 marks)* **Storage Allocation Strategies:** **1. Static Allocation (2 marks)** - All binding of names to storage locations happens at **compile time**. - The compiler fixes the absolute address of every variable. - **Restrictions:** No recursion (cannot reuse space for re-entrant calls); no dynamic data structures. - **Example:** A simple program with global variables `int x = 5;` — the address of `x` is fixed in the `.data` segment. **2. Stack Allocation (3 marks)** - Each procedure call **pushes** an Activation Record onto the runtime stack. - On return, the AR is **popped** and memory is automatically reclaimed. - Supports **recursion** — each recursive call gets its own AR. - **Example:** A recursive factorial function `fact(n)` — calling `fact(3)` pushes 3 ARs; they unwind as calls return. **Comparison Table (2 marks):** | Property | Static | Stack | Heap | |----------|--------|-------|------| | Binding time | Compile | Run-time (per call) | Run-time (explicit alloc) | | Recursion support | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | Dynamic structures | ❌ | Limited | ✅ | | Access speed | Fastest | Fast | Slower | | Deallocation | Automatic (program exit) | Automatic (on return) | Manual or GC | | Used by | Embedded / Fortran | C local vars | `malloc`, `new`, closures | --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Question B]** **(a)** What are the different **parameter passing mechanisms** supported by programming languages? Explain any three in detail with examples and the effect on the caller. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain in detail the **Activation Record** structure. With a neat diagram, describe the layout of an Activation Record and explain the role of access links and control links. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution B(a):** *(7 marks — ~2.3 marks per mechanism)* **Parameter Passing Mechanisms:** The five standard mechanisms are: Call by Value, Call by Reference, Call by Value-Result, Call by Name, and Call by Copy-Restore. We explain three in detail: **1. Call by Value (2.3 marks)** - The actual argument is **evaluated** and its value is **copied** into the formal parameter's location. - Modifications inside the callee **do not affect** the caller's variable. - **Example:** In C, all scalar arguments are passed by value: ```c void f(int a) { a = a + 5; } int main() { int x = 10; f(x); /* x remains 10 */ } ``` - **Effect:** Safe and predictable; caller-side variable is unchanged. **2. Call by Reference (2.3 marks)** - The **address** of the actual argument is passed to the callee. - The callee accesses the variable through the address (dereferencing). - Modifications **do affect** the caller's variable. - **Example:** Pascal's `var` parameter or C++'s reference: ```cpp void f(int &a) { a = a + 5; } int main() { int x = 10; f(x); /* x becomes 15 */ } ``` - **Effect:** Aliasing — multiple parameters may refer to the same variable, producing surprising results. **3. Call by Value-Result (2.4 marks)** - Combines call-by-value and call-by-reference: the value is copied **in** at call time, and the final value is copied **back out** at return. - **Example (Algol W):** ```pascal procedure swap(val x, val y: integer); var t: integer; begin t := x; x := y; y := t end; ``` After call: variables are swapped at the caller's site. - **Effect:** Caller's variables may change; useful for "out" parameters without using pointers. --- **Solution B(b):** *(7 marks)* **Activation Record Structure:** An Activation Record (AR) is a contiguous block of memory on the runtime stack that holds all the state of one procedure invocation. **Layout (from top to bottom, high address → low address):** ``` ┌──────────────────────────┐ ← Top of AR (HIGH ADDRESS) │ Returned Value Slot │ ├──────────────────────────┤ │ Parameters │ ├──────────────────────────┤ │ Saved Machine Status │ │ (registers, condition │ │ codes, frame pointer) │ ├──────────────────────────┤ │ Access Link │ │ (static link) │ ├──────────────────────────┤ │ Control Link │ │ (dynamic link) │ ├──────────────────────────┤ │ Saved Return Address │ ├──────────────────────────┤ │ Local Variables & │ │ Compiler-generated │ │ Temporaries │ ← Bottom of AR (LOW ADDRESS) └──────────────────────────┘ ``` **Field Descriptions:** 1. **Returned Value Slot (1 mark)** — Space to hold the value the callee returns (especially useful for large return types or when the return value doesn't fit in a register). 2. **Parameters (1 mark)** — The actual arguments passed by the caller. Order and layout are determined by the calling convention. 3. **Saved Machine Status (1 mark)** — Contents of CPU registers (including the program counter of the caller) preserved so they can be restored on return. 4. **Access Link (Static Link) (1.5 marks)** — A pointer to the AR of the **lexically enclosing** procedure. Used to access non-local variables under **static (lexical) scoping**. In Pascal/Ada/Scheme, when procedure `P` accesses a variable from an outer scope, the compiler chains access links until it finds the AR holding that variable. 5. **Control Link (Dynamic Link) (1.5 marks)** — A pointer to the AR of the **caller** (the procedure that invoked the current one). This is what enables a stack-walk to reconstruct the dynamic call chain — used by debuggers, exception handlers, and profilers. 6. **Saved Return Address (1 mark)** — The address in the caller's code to resume execution once the callee returns. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** The *control link* traces the *dynamic* (runtime) call chain. The *access link* traces the *static* (lexical) nesting chain. They are usually different pointers. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:** > 1. **Triple references without parentheses:** Students often write `0` instead of `(0)` when referencing the result of triple #0. The parentheses are **mandatory** to distinguish a literal constant `0` from a triple reference. KTU examiners deduct marks for ambiguity. > 2. **Confusing Access Link with Control Link:** These are two different pointers with two different purposes. Writing "access link points to caller" is a 2-mark deduction in a typical valuation. > 3. **Forgetting the `arg2` field in `ifFalse` instructions:** Many students omit the second operand. Always include it (write `—` if unused) to show completeness. > 4. **Mixing up the three storage strategies:** Static ≠ Stack. Static = compile-time fixed; Stack = per-call push/pop. Heap = dynamic with arbitrary lifetime. Examiners grade strictly on these distinctions. > 5. **Indirect Triples confused with Triples:** Indirect triples have TWO data structures (the pointer list AND the backing list). Drawing only one table = 1-mark loss. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Triple and Run Time Environments** - [x] **Three-Address Code (TAC)** = IR where each instruction has form `x := y op z`. - [x] **Quadruples** = 4-tuple `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`; explicit temp names. - [x] **Triples** = 3-tuple `(op, arg1, arg2)`; results referenced by **position** using `(i)` syntax. - [x] **Indirect Triples** = backing triple list + reorderable instruction pointer list. - [x] **Quadruples vs Triples**: Quads use more memory but are easier to reorder; Triples save memory but require index updates on reordering. - [x] **Run Time Environment** manages memory layout: code, static data, heap, stack. - [x] **Stack grows downward, heap grows upward.** - [x] **Static allocation** = compile-time, no recursion allowed. - [x] **Stack allocation** = per-call AR, supports recursion. - [x] **Heap allocation** = dynamic lifetime, explicit deallocation or GC. - [x] **Activation Record** contains: Returned Value, Parameters, Saved Status, Access Link, Control Link, Return Address, Locals/Temporaries. - [x] **Access Link** = static (lexical) parent AR pointer. - [x] **Control Link** = dynamic (caller) AR pointer. - [x] **Parameter passing mechanisms**: Call by Value, Reference, Value-Result, Name, Copy-Restore. - [x] **Call by Reference** can produce aliasing artefacts. - [x] **One TAC instruction ≈ one machine instruction.** - [x] **Indirect triples** are preferred when code reordering (optimization) is needed. - [x] **Common KTU pitfall:** never omit parentheses around triple references. - [x] **Memory size formula for AR:** sum of all fields in bytes. - [x] **Activation depth formula:** $D(n) = D(\text{parent}(n)) + 1$.

topics.title: Local Optimizations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Local Optimizations

# 🧠 Local Optimizations — Compiler Design (KTU 2024 Scheme)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Local Optimization** is a class of code transformation techniques applied by the compiler **within a single basic block** (a maximal sequence of consecutive three-address instructions with single entry and single exit) without altering the program's observable behavior. The objective is to generate faster, smaller, and more efficient machine code by exploiting algebraic identities, constant values, and redundant computations confined to a single block.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this falls under the **Intermediate Code Generation & Optimization** phase, specifically *Machine-Independent Optimization* (a **Phase 2** transformation in the classical compiler pipeline).

---

### 🍕 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe Editor"

Imagine a chef writing a recipe (a basic block of instructions). Local optimization is like an editor reviewing **only one paragraph (basic block)** at a time:

- If the recipe says *"use 2 cups of flour"* and then later says *"the flour amount is 2 cups"*, the editor **folds the constant** — instead of writing it twice.
- If the recipe says *"add zero salt"* — the editor **deletes** that step (dead code).
- If it says *"multiply by 2"* — the editor **rewrites it as a left shift** (strength reduction).

The chef never has to look at other recipes (other basic blocks) — only this single paragraph. That's *local* optimization.

---

### 🎯 Scope Boundary: Local vs Global

| Aspect | Local Optimization | Global Optimization |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Single basic block | Across multiple blocks |
| **Data Structure** | DAG of a single block | Flow Graph (CFG) |
| **Complexity** | Low (O(n²) on block size) | High (data-flow analysis) |
| **KTU Marks Weight** | High (asked frequently in ESE) | Moderate |
| **Examples** | Constant folding, peephole, CSE | Loop-invariant code motion, LICM |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** A basic block is a *straight-line sequence of code* with **one entry point (the first instruction)** and **one exit point (the last instruction or branch)**. No branch enters except at the top, and no branch exits except at the bottom.

---

### 📐 Fundamental Transformations (The "Big Five" of Local Optimization)

The KTU syllabus emphasizes the following five transformations:

1. **Constant Folding** — Evaluate constant expressions at compile time.
2. **Constant Propagation** — Replace variables with their known constant values.
3. **Algebraic Simplification** — Apply mathematical identities (e.g., $x + 0 = x$).
4. **Strength Reduction** — Replace expensive ops with cheaper ones (e.g., $a \times 2 \to a \ll 1$).
5. **Dead Code Elimination** — Remove instructions whose results are never used.

A sixth, the **DAG-based Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)**, is also central to the KTU module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔬 The Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) — The Heart of Local Optimization

The **DAG** is the primary data structure used to perform local optimization. It serves three simultaneous purposes:

1. **Eliminates common subexpressions** automatically (shared nodes = shared subexpressions).
2. **Identifies which computations are dead** (leaves with no uses on the live-variable list).
3. **Determines the order in which instructions must be recomputed** for the optimized block.

#### Construction Rule (Aho/Sethi/Ullman Algorithm)

For each three-address instruction $a = b \ \text{op} \ c$:

1. Find a node $N$ (or create one) labeled $b$.  
2. Find a node $M$ (or create one) labeled $c$.  
3. Check if there is an existing node with operator `op` whose left child is $N$ and right child is $M$.  
4. **If yes** → reuse that node; do not create a new one. This is **CSE**.  
5. **If no** → create a new node with label `op` and children $N, M$.  
6. The variable $a$ is added to the **identifier list** of that node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Identifier List (Def-List):** Each leaf/internal node in the DAG maintains a list of variable names currently holding the value computed at that node. The *most recent* definition is used during code emission; older ones can be deleted if dead.

---

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet & Transformation Cheat-Sheet

| Transformation | Algebraic Identity | Example | Net Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant Folding** | $c_1 \ \text{op}\ c_2 \to c_3$ (compile-time eval) | `x = 3 + 5` → `x = 8` | Removes run-time arithmetic |
| **Constant Propagation** | If $b = c$ and later use $b$ | Replace uses of $b$ with $c$ | Enables further folding |
| **Algebraic Identity** | $a + 0 = a,\ a \times 1 = a$ | `x = y + 0` → `x = y` | Removes instruction |
| **Strength Reduction** | $a \times 2^k \to a \ll k$ | `x = y * 4` → `x = y << 2` | Replaces MUL with SHL |
| **Strength Reduction (Div)** | $a / 2^k \to a \gg k$ | `x = y / 8` → `x = y >> 3` | Replaces DIV with SHR |
| **Dead Code Elim.** | Result of stmt never used | `t = a*b` (if `t` unused) | Removes instruction |
| **Copy Propagation** | $a = c$ then use $a$ | Replace $a$ with $c$ | Shortens live ranges |
| **CSE (DAG-based)** | Reuse identical subexprs | `t1 = a+b; t2 = a+b` → share | Reduces redundant compute |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cost Ratio (RISC pipelines):** An integer multiplication typically costs **3–5 cycles**, while a logical shift costs **1 cycle**. Strength reduction on the hot path can yield 2×–4× speedup in tight loops.

---

### ⚙️ Algorithmic Workflow — The 5-Phase Local Optimizer

The compiler performs local optimization in this strict sequence:

1. **Partition** the three-address code (TAC) into basic blocks.
2. **Build** a DAG for each block.
3. **Perform** local value numbering / common subexpression elimination via the DAG.
4. **Emit** an optimized TAC list by traversing the DAG in reverse postorder (leaves first).
5. **Apply peephole optimization** on the emitted list (sliding window of $n$ instructions, typically $n=2$ or $3$).

### 🔭 Engineering Utility

- **Embedded systems / IoT firmware:** Reduces code size for flash-constrained MCUs.
- **DSP kernels (audio/video codecs):** Strength reduction inside FFT inner loops.
- **JIT compilers (JVM, V8, HotSpot):** Constant folding happens on every method entry.
- **GPU shader compilers (NVIDIA NVCC, glslang):** Pre-bake uniforms via constant propagation.

---

### 🧠 KTU Theoretical Depth — DAG Node Algebra

Each DAG node $n$ has the following tuple:

$$ n = \langle \text{label}(n), \ \text{left}(n), \ \text{right}(n), \ \text{id\_list}(n) \rangle $$

The **code emission** algorithm processes nodes bottom-up. For node $n$ with children $l, r$:

$$
\text{emit}(n) = \begin{cases}
\text{use operand from } l \text{ or } r \text{ directly} & \text{if leaf} \\
\text{emit left, emit right, then emit op} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

This is **reverse postorder**, which respects the partial order imposed by data dependencies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🧪 Worked Example 1 — DAG Construction (Classic KTU Problem)

**Given Three-Address Code (TAC):**

```
1.  t1 = 4 * i
2.  t2 = a [ t1 ]
3.  t3 = 4 * i
4.  t4 = b [ t1 ]      ← wait, re-derive with proper indexing
5.  t5 = t2 * t4
6.  a  [ t1 ] = t5
7.  t6 = 4 * i
8.  t7 = 4 * i
9.  t8 = b [ t7 ]
10. t9 = t2 * t8
11. b [ t6 ] = t9
12. i = i + 1
13. if i <= 20 goto (1)
```

> This is the **standard Aho example** used across KTU 2019/2022/2023 papers.

#### Step 1: Initialize DAG with leaf nodes

We track the *identifier list* of each node.

#### Step 2: Process instructions sequentially

**Stmt 1:** `t1 = 4 * i`
- Create leaf `n0: 4`, leaf `n1: i`
- No existing `*` node with children `(n0, n1)`.
- Create `n2: (*, n0, n1)`, id_list = `{t1}`

**Stmt 2:** `t2 = a [ t1 ]`
- Find node for `t1` → `n2`
- Create `n3: (=[], a, n2)`, id_list = `{t2}`  
- (Here we treat array base `a` as a leaf reference)

**Stmt 3:** `t3 = 4 * i`
- Check for existing `(*, n0, n1)` → **Found: n2** ✅
- id_list(n2) append → `n2: id_list = {t1, t3}`

**Stmt 4:** `t4 = b [ t3 ]` → re-indexed as `t4 = b [ t1 ]` (since `t3 ≡ t1`)
- Create `n4: (=[], b, n2)`, id_list = `{t4}`

**Stmt 5:** `t5 = t2 * t4`
- Operand nodes: `n3` (holds `t2`), `n4` (holds `t4`)
- Create `n5: (*, n3, n4)`, id_list = `{t5}`

**Stmt 6:** `a [ t1 ] = t5`
- `t1` → `n2`, `t5` → `n5`
- Create `n6: ([]=, a, n2, n5)`
- Mark `n3` (which holds `t2`) as **killed** because `a[]` is being written — any future read of `a[anything]` may be invalidated. For local optimization, we just record the assignment.

**Stmt 7:** `t6 = 4 * i`
- Existing `(*, n0, n1)` → `n2`. id_list(n2) += `t6` → `{t1, t3, t6}`

**Stmt 8:** `t7 = 4 * i`
- Same node `n2`. id_list(n2) += `t7` → `{t1, t3, t6, t7}`

**Stmt 9:** `t8 = b [ t7 ]`
- Operand `t7` → `n2` (using the alias since `t7 ≡ t1` algebraically).
- ⚠️ **But!** Statement 6 wrote to `a[]`, not `b[]`. Read of `b[]` is safe.
- Look for existing `(=[], b, n2)`. We have `n4` from stmt 4!
- id_list(n4) += `t8` → `{t4, t8}`

**Stmt 10:** `t9 = t2 * t8`
- `t2` → `n3`, `t8` → `n4` (via shared node).
- Look for existing `(*, n3, n4)`. We have `n5` from stmt 5!
- id_list(n5) += `t9` → `{t5, t9}`

**Stmt 11:** `b [ t6 ] = t9`
- Operand `t6` → `n2`, `t9` → `n5`
- Create `n7: ([]=, b, n2, n5)`

**Stmt 12:** `i = i + 1`
- Operand `i` → `n1`. Create `n8: (+, n1, 1)`, id_list = `{i}`.
- **Remove** `i` from id_list of `n1`.

#### Final Optimized Block Emitted (reverse postorder):

```
t1  = 4 * i
t2  = a[t1]
t4  = b[t1]
t5  = t2 * t4
a[t1] = t5
t8  = b[t1]      ← CSE: reuses n4 via t8
t9  = t2 * t8    ← CSE: reuses n5
b[t1] = t9
i   = i + 1
if i <= 20 goto 1
```

$$
\boxed{\text{Original: 13 instructions} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Optimized: 10 instructions}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{3 redundant } 4 \times i \text{ computations + 1 redundant load + 1 redundant multiply ELIMINATED}}
$$

---

### 🧪 Worked Example 2 — Constant Folding + Propagation Chain

**Source:**
```c
int x = 5;
int y = x + 3;
int z = y * 2;
return z - x;
```

#### Phase 1 — Constant Folding
- `x = 5` is a leaf constant.
- `y = x + 3` → `5 + 3 = 8` → **fold** → `y = 8`.
- `z = y * 2` → `8 * 2 = 16` → **fold** → `z = 16`.
- `return z - x` → `16 - 5 = 11` → **fold** → `return 11`.

**Result:** All four statements reduced to a single `return 11;`.

#### Phase 2 — Propagation-only (if folding is disabled)
- Propagate $x=5$ into `y = x + 3` → `y = 5 + 3`.
- Propagate $y=5+3$ into `z = y * 2` → `z = (5+3) * 2`.
- Propagate into `return` → `return ((5+3) * 2) - 5`.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "explain the difference between folding and propagation," emphasize that **propagation** moves values symbolically, while **folding** collapses arithmetic at compile time. Folding without propagation is possible but weaker; propagation enables further folding.

---

### 🧪 Worked Example 3 — Strength Reduction in a Loop

**Original loop:**
```c
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    A[i*4] = B[i*4] + 1;
}
```

**After strength reduction** ($i \times 4 \to i \ll 2$):
```c
int addrA = 0, addrB = 0;
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    A[addrA] = B[addrB] + 1;
    addrA += 4;     // strength-reduce the increment
    addrB += 4;
}
```

This eliminates one **multiply** per iteration × two operands = **2 multiplies eliminated per loop iteration**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget that strength reduction in a local context only applies to a *single basic block*. If the multiply is inside a loop, you need **loop optimization (LICM + IVT)** which is a *global* technique. The KTU module-2 scope is **local only** — so the strength-reduced form must remain a *single-block* transformation.

---

### 🧪 Worked Example 4 — Peephole Optimization (Sliding Window)

**Sequence:**
```
MOV R1, R2
ADD R3, R1
MOV R4, R3
```

**Peephole with window size 2:**

- Slide 1: `MOV R1, R2` + `ADD R3, R1` → no obvious pattern, keep.
- Slide 2: `ADD R3, R1` + `MOV R4, R3` → no obvious pattern, keep.

**However, with copy propagation applied locally:**
- `R1 = R2`, so replace uses of `R1` with `R2`:
  ```
  MOV R1, R2
  ADD R3, R2      ← substituted
  MOV R4, R3
  ```
- Now `ADD R3, R2` + `MOV R4, R3` → `R4 = R3 = R3 + R2` is alive. Redundant? No.
- But the **first `MOV R1, R2`** becomes dead if `R1` is not used elsewhere → **delete**.

**Optimized:**
```
ADD R3, R2
MOV R4, R3
```

---

### 📋 Analytical Matrix — Transformation Interactions

| Order of Application | Folding | Propagation | Strength | Dead-Code | CSE |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1st** | ✅ direct | needs folds | needs consts | needs DAG | needs DAG |
| **2nd** | — | ✅ | enabled | enabled | enabled |
| **3rd** | — | — | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **4th** | — | — | — | ✅ final sweep | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** Local optimization passes are applied until **fixed point** (no more changes occur). For a single basic block, this usually converges in 2–3 passes.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, May 2024 Pattern]**

#### **Q1. Define local optimization. List any four transformations performed under it.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Local optimization is a machine-independent code transformation technique that improves the efficiency of a program by performing transformations **within a single basic block** without altering the program's observable behavior.

Four transformations:

1. **Constant Folding** — e.g., `x = 3 * 4` → `x = 12`.
2. **Constant Propagation** — replacing variable uses with their known constant values.
3. **Algebraic Simplification** — e.g., `x = y + 0` → `x = y`.
4. **Dead Code Elimination** — removing instructions whose results are unused.

> [!TIP]
> For full 3 marks, students *must* give a one-line definition + list four distinct transformations with examples.

---

#### **Q2. What is a DAG? How does it help in local optimization?** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** is a graph-based intermediate representation of a basic block in which:

- **Leaves** represent identifiers or constants.
- **Internal nodes** represent operators.
- **Children** of an operator node are the operands.

**Role in local optimization:**

- **Common subexpression elimination (CSE):** When a repeated subexpression is detected, a single node is reused, eliminating redundant computation.
- **Dead code identification:** Identifiers in a node's list with no further uses are removed.
- **Order of recomputation:** Topological order gives a valid evaluation order during code emission.

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — ESE Pattern, Module 2]**

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — DAG Construction & Optimization** *(14 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** Construct the DAG for the following basic block of three-address code. Identify common subexpressions and dead code. *(7 marks)*

```
D1:  t1 = a + b
D2:  t2 = a + b
D3:  t3 = t1 - t2
D4:  t4 = t1 * c
D5:  t5 = t4 + t1
D6:  t6 = a + b
D7:  t7 = t1 * c
D8:  t8 = t6 - t7
```

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Process D1:** `t1 = a + b`
- Create `n0(a)`, `n1(b)`. No existing `+` node. Create `n2: (+, n0, n1)`, id_list = {t1}.

**Step 2 — Process D2:** `t2 = a + b`
- Look for existing `(+, n0, n1)` → **Found: n2**.
- id_list(n2) += t2 → **{t1, t2}** (CSE detected ✓).

**Step 3 — Process D3:** `t3 = t1 - t2`
- Operand nodes: `t1`→`n2`, `t2`→`n2` (same).
- Create `n3: (-, n2, n2)`, id_list = {t3}.

**Step 4 — Process D4:** `t4 = t1 * c`
- Operands: `t1`→`n2`, `c`→new leaf `n4(c)`.
- Create `n5: (*, n2, n4)`, id_list = {t4}.

**Step 5 — Process D5:** `t5 = t4 + t1`
- Operands: `t4`→`n5`, `t1`→`n2`.
- Create `n6: (+, n5, n2)`, id_list = {t5}.

**Step 6 — Process D6:** `t6 = a + b`
- Look for `(+, n0, n1)` → **n2 exists**.
- id_list(n2) += t6 → **{t1, t2, t6}** (another CSE).

**Step 7 — Process D7:** `t7 = t1 * c`
- Operands: `t1`→`n2`, `c`→`n4`.
- Look for `(*, n2, n4)` → **n5 exists**.
- id_list(n5) += t7 → **{t4, t7}** (CSE ✓).

**Step 8 — Process D8:** `t8 = t6 - t7`
- Operands: `t6`→`n2`, `t7`→`n5`.
- Create `n7: (-, n2, n5)`, id_list = {t8}.

**Final DAG structure:**

```
n2: (+) ← {t1, t2, t6}
  ├─ n0: a
  └─ n1: b
n3: (-) ← {t3}
  └─ n2
  └─ n2
n5: (*) ← {t4, t7}
  ├─ n2
  └─ n4: c
n6: (+) ← {t5}
  ├─ n5
  └─ n2
n7: (-) ← {t8}
  ├─ n2
  └─ n5
```

**Dead Code Identification:**

- `t3` is computed at `n3` but is `t3` used later? **No.** → `n3` is **DEAD**. ❌
- All other temps (`t1, t2, t4, t5, t6, t7, t8`) appear in later uses or are live-out.

**Optimized Block (after deleting dead code):**

```
t1  = a + b         ← used by n5, n6
t4  = t1 * c        ← used by n6
t5  = t4 + t1       ← (no further use? check)
t6  = a + b         ← CSE with t1
t7  = t1 * c        ← CSE with t4
t8  = t6 - t7       ← live-out
```

If only `t8` is live-out, then `t5` is also dead; final form reduces to:

```
t1 = a + b
t4 = t1 * c
t8 = t1 - t4
```

$$
\boxed{8 \text{ instructions} \longrightarrow 3 \text{ instructions (62.5\% reduction)}}
$$

---

**Q. (b)** Explain **peephole optimization** with any three suitable transformations. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Peephole optimization is a simple yet effective local optimization technique that examines a short sequence (a *"peephole"*) of target instructions and replaces them with an equivalent but faster sequence. The peephole is typically a sliding window of **2 or 3** consecutive instructions.

**Transformation 1 — Redundant Load/Store Elimination**

```
(1) MOV R1, A    ; load A into R1
(2) MOV A, R1    ; store R1 back to A   ← REDUNDANT
```

Optimized:

```
MOV R1, A
```

**Transformation 2 — Constant Folding in Target Code**

```
(1) MOV R1, 3
(2) ADD R2, R1, 5      ← operand R1 known
```

Optimized (after propagation + folding):

```
MOV R1, 3
MOV R2, 8              ← 3 + 5 precomputed
```

**Transformation 3 — Strength Reduction**

```
MUL R1, R2, 2     ← expensive multiply
```

Optimized:

```
SHL R1, R2, 1     ← single-cycle shift
```

**Transformation 4 — Unreachable Code Elimination**

```
GOTO L1
...
L1:  <unreachable code>
```

If no branch jumps to the dead label, the code between can be removed.

**Transformation 5 — Algebraic Identity**

```
MUL R1, R2, 1     ;  MUL by 1 is identity
```

→

```
MOV R1, R2
```

> [!TIP]
> For full 7 marks, write the **definition (1 mark)**, **three transformations with before/after code (4 marks)**, and a **concluding statement on peephole as a compiler phase (2 marks)**.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — Strength Reduction + Algebraic Transformations** *(14 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Q. (a)** Apply **strength reduction** to the following TAC block. Show before and after code. *(7 marks)*

```
L1:  t1 = i * 8
L2:  t2 = A [ t1 ]
L3:  t3 = i * 8
L4:  B [ t3 ] = t2
L5:  i = i + 1
L6:  if i < 100 goto L1
```

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the expensive pattern:** `i * 8` is a **multiply by power-of-two** ($8 = 2^3$).

**Step 2 — Apply the identity:** $a \times 2^k \equiv a \ll k$.

**Step 3 — Merge identical computations:** `t1` and `t3` compute the same value → use a single temporary `t_addr`.

**Optimized Block:**

```
L1:  t_addr = i << 3       ; strength-reduced shift
L2:  t_val   = A [ t_addr ]
L4:  B [ t_addr ] = t_val
L5:  i = i + 1
L6:  if i < 100 goto L1
```

**Performance Gain:**

- Original: 2 × `MUL` per iteration + 1 × `ADD` (for `i+1`).
- Optimized: 2 × `SHL` per iteration (1 cycle each) + 1 × `ADD`.
- **Speedup per iteration:** $\frac{2 \times 4 + 1}{2 \times 1 + 1} = \frac{9}{3} = 3\times$ (assuming MUL = 4 cycles, SHL = 1 cycle).

> [!NOTE]
> For loop-level strength reduction, you would also **hoist the shift** outside the loop (LICM) — but that is a *global* optimization and **outside Module 2 scope**.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain **constant propagation** and **constant folding** with an example. Differentiate them clearly. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Constant Folding:**  
A compile-time evaluation of constant expressions in which an arithmetic operation on literal constants is replaced by the resulting constant.

Example:

```
x = 3 + 5        →  x = 8        (folded)
y = 2 * 4 + 1    →  y = 9        (folded recursively)
```

**Constant Propagation:**  
A substitution technique where, if a variable is assigned a constant value and the variable is not redefined before its use, the constant is substituted for the variable in subsequent expressions.

Example:

```
x = 5            ; x is assigned 5
y = x + 3        ; after propagation → y = 5 + 3
z = y * 2        ; (if x is not reassigned)
```

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Constant Folding | Constant Propagation |
|---|---|---|
| **Operation** | Evaluates `c1 op c2` to `c3` | Replaces variable `v` with its known constant |
| **Input form** | Two or more constants | A variable bound to a constant |
| **Output form** | Single constant | Constant(s) embedded in an expression |
| **Order** | Usually applied *after* propagation | Usually applied *before* folding |
| **Side effect** | Enables further folding | Enables further folding downstream |
| **Risk** | None (mathematically safe) | Must verify no redefinition of `v` |

**Combined Example:**

```c
int a = 10;
int b = a + 5;     // propagate a=10 → b = 10 + 5
int c = b * 2;     // propagate b=15 → c = 15 * 2
                    // fold → c = 30
```

After **propagation + folding**, all three statements become:

```c
int a = 10;
int b = 15;
int c = 30;
```

And if none of `a, b, c` are used externally, the entire block is **dead code** → eliminated entirely.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔥 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Common places where students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CSE with copy propagation.** CSE eliminates identical *subexpressions*; copy propagation replaces one *variable* with another. KTU answer sheets frequently mark these as the same concept — they are NOT.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the identifier list.** When drawing a DAG, students often write variable names as nodes instead of attaching them to a node's *id_list*. **Always** show: node = `(*, n_x, n_y)` and a separate `id_list` annotation.
>
> 3. **Misclassifying dead code.** A temporary is dead only if it has *no live-out use*. Check whether the variable appears after the block (in the next block's `use` set). Local dead-code analysis is per-block, not per-instruction.
>
> 4. **Strength reduction on non-power-of-2.** You cannot reduce `x * 3` to shifts; only $x \times 2^k$. KTU questions may trick you with `* 6` (which is `* (4+2)` and needs 2 ops, not 1).
>
> 5. **Peephole window size.** Don't claim peephole looks at the "whole function" — it operates on a fixed-size sliding window (typically 2–3 instructions).
>
> 6. **Skipping the array-kill rule in DAG.** When you write to `a[i]`, you must kill *all prior reads* of `a[j]` for any `j` (since we don't know if `i == j`). KTU Module 2 problems may not emphasize this, but full-marks answers do.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Local Optimizations (Module 2)**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- **Local optimization** works within **one basic block** only.
- A **basic block** has one entry (leader) and one exit.
- A **DAG** is the primary IR for local optimization.

### ✅ The Big-5 Transformations
- **Constant Folding:** `c1 op c2 → c3`
- **Constant Propagation:** replace uses of `v` with its known constant.
- **Algebraic Simplification:** `$x+0, x*1, x-0, x/1$`
- **Strength Reduction:** `$x * 2^k → x << k$`, `$x / 2^k → x >> k$`
- **Dead Code Elimination:** remove instructions whose results are unused.

### ✅ DAG Construction Rules
- **Leaves** = identifiers/constants.
- **Internal node** = operator with two children.
- **Shared node** = CSE detected.
- **Identifier list** = all names currently bound to that node's value.

### ✅ Peephole Optimization
- **Window size:** 2–3 instructions.
- **Eliminations:** redundant loads, unreachable code, identity ops.
- **Strength-reduces** machine-level multiplies → shifts.

### ✅ KTU-Specific Watch-Points
- DAG problems always come with a "construct & optimize" sub-question.
- Peephole transformations are a guaranteed Part A question.
- Constant folding vs. propagation is a high-yield **definition question**.
- Strength reduction on powers of 2 only.

### ✅ Order of Application (for full-mark answers)
1. Partition into basic blocks.
2. Build DAG.
3. Apply CSE (automatic via DAG).
4. Apply algebraic identities.
5. Apply strength reduction.
6. Emit code in reverse postorder.
7. Run peephole optimizer.
8. Repeat until **fixed point**.

### ✅ Performance Numbers to Memorize
- Integer **MUL**: ~3–5 cycles.
- Integer **SHL/SHR**: ~1 cycle.
- Integer **ADD**: 1 cycle.
- **DAG construction** is $O(n^2)$ worst-case, $O(n)$ with hashing.

### ✅ Cross-References in KTU Syllabus
- **Module 2 → Module 3 bridge:** Global optimization (data-flow analysis) extends local techniques across blocks.
- **Module 2 → Module 1 bridge:** TAC (three-address code) is the IR on which DAG operates.
- **Module 2 → Module 4 bridge:** Target code generation benefits from peephole output.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Local Optimizations** ✨
> *Strictly aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech PERUT638 — Compiler Design and Operating Systems.*

topics.title: Principal Sources of Optimizations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principal Sources of Optimizations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Compiler Design, **Principal Sources of Optimizations** refer to the fundamental, recurring categories of program transformations that a compiler applies to intermediate code (typically **Three-Address Code / TAC**) in order to improve the program's runtime efficiency, memory footprint, and energy consumption — while strictly preserving the program's observable behavior (i.e., the transformation must be a *semantics-preserving* or *function-preserving* transformation).

According to standard compiler theory, these optimizations are classified into two broad families:

1. **Machine-Independent Optimizations** – performed on the intermediate representation (IR), independent of the target machine. Examples: constant folding, common subexpression elimination, dead-code elimination, loop optimizations, strength reduction.
2. **Machine-Dependent Optimizations** – exploit specific architectural features (registers, pipelines, caches). Examples: register allocation, instruction scheduling, peephole optimization, branch prediction hints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 2, the principal sources are anchored on the *basic blocks*, *flow graphs*, and *loop optimizations* trio. The KTU examiner typically expects a clear understanding of **why** a particular source applies, **what transformation** it produces, and **how** it improves the code (via DAGs, data-flow equations, or loop transformations).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Editor Polishing a Newspaper Article"**
> 
> Imagine a journalist writes a raw draft (this is your **intermediate code / 3AC**). Before the article reaches print, an experienced editor (the **optimizing compiler**) goes through it and:
> - Removes sentences that say the *same thing twice* (**Common Subexpression Elimination**)
> - Removes sentences that say *nothing meaningful* (**Dead Code Elimination**)
> - Pre-computes facts that don't change (e.g., "2024 is the current year" written as a **constant**) (**Constant Folding / Propagation**)
> - Pulls out a sentence that just declares a fact once, instead of repeating it inside every paragraph (**Loop-Invariant Code Motion**)
> - Replaces "multiply by 1024" with a faster "shift left by 10" (**Strength Reduction**)
> 
> The article (your program) says the **exact same thing** to the reader, but it's **shorter, faster, and cheaper to print**. That is the entire purpose of optimization.

### Categorization of Principal Sources

The **principal sources** can be enumerated as follows:

| # | Source of Optimization | Type |
|---|------------------------|------|
| 1 | Constant Folding | Compile-time evaluation |
| 2 | Constant Propagation | Symbolic substitution |
| 3 | Algebraic Simplification / Identity Elimination | Algebraic identities |
| 4 | Copy Propagation | Symbolic substitution |
| 5 | Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE) | Value numbering / DAG-based |
| 6 | Dead Code Elimination (DCE) | Reachability analysis |
| 7 | Unreachable Code Elimination | Reachability analysis |
| 8 | Reduction in Strength | Operator replacement |
| 9 | Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM) | Loop optimization |
| 10 | Induction Variable Elimination | Loop optimization |
| 11 | Function Inlining / Procedure Integration | Inter-procedural |
| 12 | Tail Call Optimization | Call-site optimization |
| 13 | Register Allocation | Machine-dependent |
| 14 | Peephole Optimization | Local, machine-aware |
| 15 | Instruction Scheduling | Pipeline-aware |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition – Function-Preserving Transformation:** A transformation $T$ on an intermediate program $P$ is *function-preserving* if for every legal input $x$, the output of $P(x)$ equals the output of $T(P)(x)$. In other words, the program computes the **same mathematical function** before and after optimization. KTU questions frequently test this property.

---

### Why Optimization Matters — The Performance Triad

The three metrics a compiler optimizer targets (in order of KTU exam importance) are:

1. **Time / Speed** — Reduce the number of executed instructions and the critical path.
2. **Space / Memory** — Reduce the code size and the data footprint.
3. **Energy / Power** — Reduced instructions $\Rightarrow$ reduced dynamic power dissipation (critical in mobile/embedded targets).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** A 10%–30% speedup is typical from classical optimizations (measured in Dhrystone/MHz benchmarks), and modern compilers (GCC `-O2`, LLVM `-O2`) routinely achieve **2×–5×** speedups on scientific codes. The KTU examiner loves the qualitative question: *"Why is optimization essential for embedded and high-performance systems?"* — answer with the **speed-space-power triad**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Three Pillars of Optimization Theory

Every principal source of optimization rests on **three pillars**:

1. **Identification** — *How does the compiler know the transformation is legal?*
   - This is the job of **Data-Flow Analysis (DFA)**: available expressions, reaching definitions, live variables, very-busy expressions, etc.
2. **Transformation** — *What does the compiler do to the IR?*
   - The IR is rewritten, replacing old constructs with equivalent but cheaper ones.
3. **Preservation** — *Why is the program still semantically equivalent?*
   - Based on algebraic identities, loop dominance frontiers, or value-range proofs.

### 2.1 Constant Folding

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A binary operation where **both operands are compile-time constants** is evaluated at compile time, and the result replaces the entire expression.

**Example:**
$$x = 3 + 4 \times 2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; x = 11$$

This requires that the constant values are *known* to the compiler. If the expression is in a loop body, the constant propagation rules still apply since the result is loop-invariant.

### 2.2 Constant Propagation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** If a variable `v` is assigned a constant `c` and `v` is not redefined before its next use, then the use of `v` is replaced by `c`.

**Example:**
$$a = 5; \;\; b = a + 3; \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; b = 5 + 3 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; b = 8$$

### 2.3 Algebraic Simplification / Identity Elimination

Standard identities the compiler recognizes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x + 0 &= x \\
x - 0 &= x \\
x \times 1 &= x \\
x \times 0 &= 0 \\
x / 1 &= x \\
x \;\;\text{xor}\;\; 0 &= x \\
x \;\;\text{and}\;\; x &= x \\
x \;\;\text{or}\;\; x &= x
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4 Copy Propagation

If `a = b` is a copy statement, every use of `a` may be replaced with `b`, *provided* `b` is not redefined on any path between the copy and the use.

**Example:**
$$a = b; \;\; c = a \times 2; \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; c = b \times 2$$

### 2.5 Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** If the same expression is computed multiple times with the *same operand values*, the redundant computations are eliminated, and the result is reused from a single computation.

**Example:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a + b \\
c &= t_1 \times 2 \\
t_2 &= a + b \quad\quad \text{(redundant)} \\
d &= t_2 + 5
\end{aligned}
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a + b \\
c &= t_1 \times 2 \\
d &= t_1 + 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pre-condition:** Between the two computations of $a+b$, neither $a$ nor $b$ may be redefined. This is established by the *available expressions* data-flow framework.

### 2.6 Dead Code Elimination (DCE)

A statement is **dead** if the value it computes is never used (i.e., its result is not live). It is a *function-preserving* transformation to remove it.

**Example:**
$$x = a + b; \quad y = c; \quad \text{return } y; \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; y = c; \quad \text{return } y;$$
(The statement `x = a + b` is dead and is removed.)

### 2.7 Strength Reduction

Replace an expensive operation by an equivalent cheaper one:

| Original Operation | Replaced With |
|--------------------|---------------|
| $x \times 2^k$ | $x \ll k$ (left shift) |
| $x / 2^k$ | $x \gg k$ (right shift) |
| $x^2$ | $x \times x$ (sometimes) |
| $a \times b + a \times c$ | $a \times (b + c)$ (factoring) |
| $\text{pow}(x, n)$ | repeated multiplication by $x$ |

### 2.8 Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A statement inside a loop is **loop-invariant** if it computes the same value on every iteration (i.e., it does not depend on variables that are modified within the loop). Such statements may be *moved* (hoisted) out of the loop, to a *pre-header* block, and executed only once.

**Example:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{for } (i=0; i<100; i++) \{ \\
&\quad a = x + y; \quad\quad\quad \text{(loop-invariant)} \\
&\quad A[i] = a \times i; \\
&\}
\end{aligned}
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
\begin{aligned}
&a = x + y; \quad\quad \text{(moved out)} \\
&\text{for } (i=0; i<100; i++) \{ \\
&\quad A[i] = a \times i; \\
&\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.9 Induction Variable Elimination

A variable that is modified in a loop by adding/subtracting a constant is an **induction variable**. The classic case is replacing a strength-reduced variable with a derived one.

**Example:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{for } (i=0; i<100; i++) \{ \\
&\quad j = 4 \times i; \quad\quad \text{(i is induction var)} \\
&\quad A[j] = i; \\
&\}
\end{aligned}
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
\begin{aligned}
&j = 0; \\
&\text{for } (i=0; i<100; i++) \{ \\
&\quad A[j] = i; \\
&\quad j = j + 4; \quad\quad \text{(no multiply in loop)} \\
&\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.10 Inlining / Procedure Integration

Replacing a procedure call with the body of the procedure. Eliminates call overhead, enables further optimizations (CSE across the inlined body), but increases code size — a classic *time–space* trade-off.

### 2.11 Peephole Optimization

A small sliding window (the "peephole") of 2–5 instructions is examined; redundant or inefficient instruction sequences are replaced. Examples:

- `MUL R1, R2, 2` $\rightarrow$ `SHL R1, R2, 1`
- Redundant `MOV` elimination
- Jump-to-jump: `JMP L1; …; L1: JMP L2` $\rightarrow$ `JMP L2`

---

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Optimization | Key Identifying Property | Time Complexity Impact | Data-Flow Used |
|---|--------------|--------------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Constant Folding | All operands literal | $O(1)$ per expression | Constant table |
| 2 | Constant Propagation | Reaching defs is constant | $O(N)$ per pass | Reaching Defs |
| 3 | Algebraic Identity | Matches identity pattern | $O(1)$ | None |
| 4 | Copy Propagation | Only one reaching def | $O(N)$ per pass | Reaching Defs |
| 5 | CSE | Expression is *available* | $O(N^2)$ worst | Available Expr |
| 6 | Dead Code Elimination | Variable not *live* | $O(N)$ per pass | Live Variable |
| 7 | Strength Reduction | Multiply by power of 2 | $O(1)$ per site | Induction Var |
| 8 | LICM | Operand not redefined in loop | $O(N)$ per loop | Reaching Defs + Dominators |
| 9 | Inlining | Call frequency & body size | Trade-off | Call-Graph Analysis |
| 10 | Peephole | Local 2–5 inst. window | $O(1)$ per window | None |

**Important Data-Flow Equations (KTU favorites):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Available Expressions:}\quad &\text{OUT}[B] = \bigcap_{P \in pred[B]} \text{IN}[P] \\
&\text{IN}[B] = \text{USE}[B] \cup (\text{OUT}[B] - \text{DEF}[B]) \\
\text{Reaching Definitions:}\quad &\text{OUT}[B] = \text{gen}[B] \cup (\text{IN}[B] - \text{kill}[B]) \\
&\text{IN}[B] = \bigcup_{P \in pred[B]} \text{OUT}[P] \\
\text{Live Variable Analysis:}\quad &\text{IN}[B] = \text{USE}[B] \cup (\text{OUT}[B] - \text{DEF}[B]) \\
&\text{OUT}[B] = \bigcup_{S \in succ[B]} \text{IN}[S]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These optimizations are the bedrock of every production compiler — GCC, LLVM, MSVC, ICC, Java HotSpot JIT. They are also critical in **embedded compilers** (e.g., for ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V) where code size and energy dominate. The LICM and strength-reduction pair alone can yield **3×–5×** speedups on tight numerical kernels (matrix multiply, FIR filters).

---

### The Flow-Graph View of Optimizations

The compiler converts the **intermediate code** into a **Control-Flow Graph (CFG)**, where:
- **Nodes** = basic blocks (maximal straight-line code sequences)
- **Edges** = control transfers between blocks

Each principal source operates on this CFG:
- **Local (intra-block)** optimizations: peephole, constant folding — work on a single block.
- **Global (inter-block)** optimizations: CSE, copy propagation, DCE — work across blocks.
- **Loop optimizations**: LICM, strength reduction — work on natural loops of the CFG.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Worked Example: Complete Optimization of a TAC Snippet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical KTU 14-mark question** — a TAC segment is given, and the student must apply **all principal optimizations** in sequence, showing the IR after each step.

**Source TAC (before optimization):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&(1)\;\; a = 5 \\
&(2)\;\; b = 2 \\
&(3)\;\; t_1 = a \times b \\
&(4)\;\; t_2 = t_1 + 10 \\
&(5)\;\; c = a \\
&(6)\;\; d = c + t_2 \\
&(7)\;\; \text{if } d > 20 \text{ goto } L_1 \\
&(8)\;\; \text{return } 0 \\
&(9)\;\; L_1: \text{ return } 1
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 1 — Constant Folding

Lines (1), (2), (3) become a single line because all operands of `a * b` are known constants.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(1')\;\; t_1 = 5 \times 2 = 10 \\
&(2')\;\; t_2 = t_1 + 10 = 20 \\
&(3')\;\; c = 5 \quad (\text{from } a=5) \\
&(4')\;\; d = c + t_2 = 5 + 20 = 25 \\
&(5')\;\; \text{if } 25 > 20 \text{ goto } L_1 \\
&(6')\;\; \text{return } 0 \\
&(7')\;\; L_1: \text{ return } 1
\end{aligned}
$$

The condition `25 > 20` is true at compile time.

---

### Step 2 — Constant Propagation + Folding on the Branch

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(1'')\;\; \text{if true goto } L_1 \\
&(2'')\;\; \text{return } 0 \\
&(3'')\;\; L_1: \text{return } 1
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Unreachable Code Elimination

Since the branch is now unconditional, line `(2'') : return 0` is **unreachable** and is removed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(1''')\;\; \text{goto } L_1 \\
&(2''')\;\; L_1: \text{return } 1
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Dead Code / Jump-to-Jump Peephole

The `goto L1` immediately followed by `L1:` can be collapsed to a single statement.

$$
\text{return } 1
$$

The program now consists of a single statement: `return 1`. This is the fully optimized form.

---

### Analytical Matrix: Detailed Comparison

| Stage | Lines | Instruction Count | Memory Reads | Speedup Factor |
|-------|-------|-------------------|---------------|----------------|
| Original | 9 | 9 | 6 (a, b, t1, t2, c, d) | 1.0× |
| After Constant Folding | 7 | 7 | 3 (a, b, c) | 1.3× |
| After Const. Propagation | 5 | 5 | 0 | 1.8× |
| After Unreachable Elim. | 3 | 3 | 0 | 3.0× |
| After Peephole | 1 | 1 | 0 | 9.0× |

> [!NOTE]
> The intermediate counts above are illustrative. KTU accepts the qualitative argument that *each step strictly reduces the number of executed instructions while preserving semantics*.

---

### Worked Example: Loop Optimizations (LICM + Strength Reduction)

**Source TAC:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&i = 0 \\
&L: \;\; t_1 = 100 \times i \quad\quad (1) \\
&\quad\;\; A[t_1] = i \quad\quad\quad\;\;(2) \\
&\quad\;\; i = i + 1 \quad\quad\;\;\;(3) \\
&\quad\;\; \text{if } i < n \text{ goto } L \quad(4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Identifying Induction Variable:** `i` is modified by a constant increment $\Rightarrow$ it is an induction variable.
**Identifying Strength-Reduction Candidate:** $t_1 = 100 \times i$ is an expensive multiplication that can be strength-reduced to an addition.

**Step 1 — Strength Reduction of $t_1$:**

Replace $t_1 = 100 \times i$ by $t_1 = t_1 + 100$ (an addition), initialized once before the loop.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&i = 0;\; t_1 = 0 \\
&L: \;\; A[t_1] = i \\
&\quad\;\; i = i + 1 \\
&\quad\;\; t_1 = t_1 + 100 \\
&\quad\;\; \text{if } i < n \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Induction Variable Elimination:**

The variable `i` is now used only in the comparison `i < n` and the increment `i = i + 1`. We can express `i` in terms of `t_1` as $i = t_1 / 100$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&t_1 = 0 \\
&L: \;\; A[t_1] = t_1 / 100 \\
&\quad\;\; t_1 = t_1 + 100 \\
&\quad\;\; \text{if } t_1 < 100 \times n \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Loop-Invariant Code Motion:**

`100 * n` inside the loop condition is loop-invariant. Hoist it:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&t_1 = 0;\; LIMIT = 100 \times n \\
&L: \;\; A[t_1] = t_1 / 100 \\
&\quad\;\; t_1 = t_1 + 100 \\
&\quad\;\; \text{if } t_1 < LIMIT \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Division Strength Reduction:**

$t_1 / 100$ where $t_1$ is always a multiple of 100 is a *constant* operation. Replace it with the index `i` retrieved via a counter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&i = 0;\; t_1 = 0;\; LIMIT = 100 \times n \\
&L: \;\; A[t_1] = i \\
&\quad\;\; i = i + 1 \\
&\quad\;\; t_1 = t_1 + 100 \\
&\quad\;\; \text{if } t_1 < LIMIT \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The expensive multiplication `100 * i` is gone, and the only loop operations are additions and an indexed store — the classic **strength-reduced + induction-var-eliminated** inner loop.

---

### Mathematical Justification: Why LICM is Semantics-Preserving

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Gold Standard Question:** *"Prove that LICM preserves program semantics."*

**Proof Sketch:**

Let $S$ be a statement inside a natural loop $L$ that is **loop-invariant**: every operand of $S$ either is a constant or has the *same reaching definition* on entry to $L$ from every predecessor of the loop's header.

1. **Pre-condition:** Since the operands of $S$ are not modified inside $L$, the value of $S$ is the same on every iteration.
2. **Move step:** Place $S$ in the pre-header $H$ (the unique predecessor of the loop header that is not in the loop).
3. **Dominance argument:** $H$ dominates the loop header, and the loop header dominates every block in $L$. Hence $S$ is evaluated on every path that reaches $L$ exactly once, before the loop body executes.
4. **Result:** The number of evaluations of $S$ drops from $N$ (number of iterations) to $1$. The output state of the program is unchanged.

This is the **legal basis** for LICM. Similar arguments apply to CSE (via *available expressions*), DCE (via *live variables*), and copy propagation (via *reaching definitions*).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```

The trade-off triangle highlights the inherent tension: inlining reduces time but increases space, while LICM reduces both.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** *Define the term "principal source of optimization" in the context of a compiler. List any four such sources. State the property that all of them must satisfy.*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

A **principal source of optimization** is a recurring, identifiable pattern in the intermediate code (typically Three-Address Code) of a program that can be transformed by the compiler into an *equivalent but more efficient* form.

**Four principal sources:**
1. **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)**
2. **Dead Code Elimination (DCE)**
3. **Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)**
4. **Strength Reduction**

**Mandatory property:** Every optimization must be a **function-preserving transformation**, i.e., the program must produce the *same output* for every legal input both before and after the transformation.

> [!NOTE]
> Marks: Definition 1 + Four sources 1.5 + Property 0.5.

---

**Q2.** *Explain the difference between "Constant Folding" and "Constant Propagation" with suitable examples.*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Constant Folding | Constant Propagation |
|--------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Definition** | Evaluating a constant expression at compile time and replacing it with the result. | Substituting the value of a variable known to be a constant into all its uses. |
| **Trigger** | Both operands are literals. | A variable is assigned a literal and not redefined before use. |
| **Mechanism** | Direct arithmetic on literals. | Data-flow analysis (reaching definitions). |
| **Example** | $a = 3 + 4 \Rightarrow a = 7$ | $a = 5; b = a + 3 \Rightarrow a = 5; b = 5 + 3 \Rightarrow b = 8$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Both are usually applied in conjunction: propagation first, then folding.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (as per KTU 2024 ESE pattern):** *Answer either Question A or Question B.*

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Explain the three principal sources of optimization — Common Subexpression Elimination, Dead Code Elimination, and Loop-Invariant Code Motion — with the data-flow analysis that justifies each. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Consider the following Three-Address Code segment. Apply ALL applicable principal optimizations in sequence. Show the intermediate code after each step and justify the legality of each transformation. (7 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(1)\;\; a = 2 \\
&(2)\;\; b = 4 \\
&(3)\;\; t_1 = a \times b \\
&(4)\;\; t_2 = t_1 + a \\
&(5)\;\; c = t_2 \\
&(6)\;\; d = c \times 2 \\
&(7)\;\; t_3 = a \times b \quad\quad \text{(redundant)} \\
&(8)\;\; e = t_3 + 5 \\
&(9)\;\; \text{if } d > e \text{ goto } L_1 \\
&(10)\;\; \text{return } 0 \\
&(11)\;\; L_1: \text{ return } 1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE) — 2 marks**

CSE removes redundant computations of expressions whose operands are unchanged. It is justified by the **Available Expressions** data-flow framework. An expression $e$ is *available* at a program point $p$ if every path from the entry to $p$ computes $e$ last, and no operand of $e$ has been redefined after that computation.

**Equations:**
$$
\text{OUT}[B] = \bigcap_{P \in pred[B]} \text{IN}[P]; \quad
\text{IN}[B] = \text{USE}_B \cup (\text{OUT}[B] - \text{DEF}_B)
$$

**Transformation:** The first computation of $e$ is kept; subsequent ones are replaced with a reference to its target variable.

**2. Dead Code Elimination (DCE) — 2 marks**

A statement is *dead* if the variable it defines is **not live** at that point. Liveness is established by the **Live Variable** data-flow framework (a backward flow analysis):

$$
\text{IN}[B] = \text{USE}[B] \cup (\text{OUT}[B] - \text{DEF}[B]); \quad
\text{OUT}[B] = \bigcup_{S \in succ[B]} \text{IN}[S]
$$

**Transformation:** Delete any statement whose LHS is not in $\text{IN}[B]$.

**3. Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM) — 3 marks**

A statement $S$ inside a natural loop $L$ is **loop-invariant** if every operand of $S$ is either a constant or has a *reaching definition outside $L$*. Such a statement is hoisted to the **pre-header** of the loop. The legality rests on the **dominance** property of the pre-header over the loop body and the fact that invariant operands are not redefined inside $L$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** LICM is applied AFTER CSE and DCE; this is the canonical ordering.

---

### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Constant Folding on lines (1), (2), (3), (4), (6):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&(1)\;\; t_1 = 2 \times 4 = 8 \\
&(2)\;\; t_2 = 8 + 2 = 10 \\
&(3)\;\; d = 10 \times 2 = 20 \\
&(4)\;\; e = 8 + 5 = 13 \quad (\text{via CSE then fold}) \\
&(5)\;\; \text{if } 20 > 13 \text{ goto } L_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — CSE on line (7):** `t3 = a * b` was already computed as `t1 = 8` on line (3). Replace with `t1`.

**Step 3 — Copy Propagation on line (5):** `c = t2` then `d = c * 2` becomes `d = t2 * 2`.

**Step 4 — Dead Code Elimination:**
- Line (3) in original `c = t2` becomes dead because `c` is never used after the propagation. **Remove.**
- Line `if 20 > 13 goto L1` becomes `if true goto L1`.
- `return 0` is now **unreachable**. **Remove.**

**Step 5 — Peephole / Jump-to-Jump:** `goto L1; L1: return 1` collapses to `return 1`.

**Final Optimized Code:**
$$
\text{return } 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The student must show the **intermediate** state after each of the 5 steps and explicitly justify each transformation. Award 1 mark per step + 2 marks for the final consolidated code.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *Discuss "Strength Reduction" and "Induction Variable Elimination" as principal sources of loop optimization. Use a worked example. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *For the following loop body in TAC, apply LICM, Strength Reduction, and Induction Variable Elimination. Show the transformed code and quantify the speedup. (7 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
&i = 0 \\
&L: \;\; t_1 = 4 \times i \\
&\quad\;\; A[t_1] = i \\
&\quad\;\; i = i + 1 \\
&\quad\;\; \text{if } i < 100 \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 Marks

**Strength Reduction (3 marks):** Replaces an expensive operation (e.g., multiplication) by an equivalent cheaper one (e.g., addition or shift). In loops, the canonical form is: if $t = a \times i$ where $i$ is an induction variable, replace the multiplication with a running addition initialized to $a$ and updated by $a$ each iteration.

**Induction Variable Elimination (4 marks):** An **induction variable** is one whose value on each iteration is a linear function of the iteration count: $x = x_0 + c \cdot k$. Multiple induction variables in the same family (linear combinations of one base variable) are redundant. The optimization removes all but one from the family, replacing uses with the base variable scaled appropriately.

**Worked example** (covered in detail in Section 3 of these notes). Final transformed loop:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&t_1 = 0;\; i = 0 \\
&L: \;\; A[t_1] = i \\
&\quad\;\; t_1 = t_1 + 4;\; i = i + 1 \\
&\quad\;\; \text{if } i < 100 \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Quantification:** Original loop executes one multiplication + one addition per iteration. Transformed loop executes two additions per iteration. On a typical 5-stage pipeline where multiplication takes **3–4 cycles** and addition takes **1 cycle**, the speedup per iteration is from ~5 cycles to ~2 cycles $\Rightarrow$ **~2.5× speedup** in the loop body.

### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 Marks

The example is identical to Q-B(a) but the student is required to show each step:

1. **Identify `i` as the base induction variable, `t1` as a derived induction variable** (related by $t_1 = 4i$).
2. **Strength-reduce `t1 = 4 * i`** to `t1 = t1 + 4` initialized before the loop.
3. **LICM:** No loop-invariant statement inside the loop body (the constant 4 is literal; the bound 100 is loop-invariant — hoist to a `LIMIT` variable for clarity).
4. **Induction variable elimination:** `t1` is the only necessary induction variable; `i` is used only in the comparison and the increment. If we keep `t1` and the bound `LIMIT = 4 * 100 = 400` is loop-invariant (hoisted), we can eliminate `i` entirely.

**Final optimized loop:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&t_1 = 0;\; LIMIT = 400 \\
&L: \;\; A[t_1] = t_1 / 4 \\
&\quad\;\; t_1 = t_1 + 4 \\
&\quad\;\; \text{if } t_1 < LIMIT \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

If a small loop-unroll + reciprocal multiply is used for `t1 / 4`, this can be further reduced to a shift `t_1 >> 2`, giving an overall **~3× speedup**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** 2 marks for identifying induction variables, 2 marks for LICM application, 2 marks for strength reduction, 1 mark for the final code and speedup estimate.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Examiner's Notes):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the "function-preserving" condition.** Many students apply optimizations without stating the legality condition. *Always* write: *"This transformation preserves the program semantics because ..."* (1 mark reserved).
> 2. **Confusing CSE with copy propagation.** CSE eliminates *expression* redundancy; copy propagation eliminates *variable* redundancy. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Applying LICM without verifying the loop-invariance pre-condition.** If an operand is redefined inside the loop, hoisting is illegal. State the pre-condition explicitly.
> 4. **Order of optimizations matters.** The canonical order is: Constant Folding → Constant Propagation → CSE → Copy Propagation → DCE → LICM → Strength Reduction → IVE. Reversing this order (e.g., LICM before CSE) misses opportunities.
> 5. **Confusing "dead code" with "unreachable code."** Dead = result not used. Unreachable = block never executed. Both are removed, but by different analyses.
> 6. **Not showing intermediate IR.** Showing only the final code costs 2–3 marks. Always show **each** step.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Principal Sources of Optimizations (KTU 2024)**
> 
> - **Definition:** A *principal source* is a recurring IR pattern enabling a semantics-preserving efficiency improvement.
