> - **Function-preservation is mandatory** — every optimization must be justified.
> - **Machine-independent sources** are: constant folding/propagation, algebraic simplification, CSE, copy propagation, DCE, LICM, strength reduction, IVE, inlining.
> - **Machine-dependent sources** are: register allocation, instruction scheduling, peephole optimization.
> - **Data-flow analyses** (Available Expressions, Reaching Definitions, Live Variables) are the *theoretical backbone* of CSE, DCE, and Copy Propagation.
> - **Loop optimizations** (LICM, Strength Reduction, IVE) dominate runtime gains for numerical code.
> - **Canonical order of optimization passes:** Folding → Propagation → CSE → CopyProp → DCE → LICM → Strength → IVE.
> - **Trade-offs:** Inlining (time↑, space↓), LICM (time↓, space↓), strength reduction (time↓, code size neutral).
> - **KTU favorite exam tip:** Be ready to optimize a 5–10 line TAC segment showing **all 5 stages** with justification.
> - **The optimization triangle:** time, space, energy — every pass must be understood in this triad.
> - **The dominance concept** underpins LICM's legality.
> - **Peephole optimizations** are local, simple, and powerful — they catch what global analyses miss.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process States

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process State Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> A **Process State** is the current operational condition of a process at any given instant during its lifetime in the CPU scheduler's perspective. The OS kernel maintains a finite set of discrete states through which every process transitions as it moves from creation to termination. These states represent the status of a process with respect to system resources such as the **CPU**, **main memory**, and **I/O devices**.

According to the **KTU 2024 Operating Systems Module**, a process traverses through a well-defined **Process State Diagram (PSD)** that captures the dynamic lifecycle of program execution under multiprogramming environments.

### 1.2 The Five Canonical Process States (Standard Two-State Model Extended)

| State Name | Symbol | Operational Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **New** | `N` | Process is being created |
| **Ready** | `R` | Process is waiting to be assigned to a processor |
| **Running** | `X` | Instructions are being executed by the CPU |
| **Blocked / Waiting** | `B` | Process is waiting for some event (I/O completion) |
| **Exit / Terminated** | `E` | Process has finished execution |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Teller Counter

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Bank Branch Analogy**
> Imagine you walk into a bank with three counters (analogous to a **single-core CPU**):
> - **New**: You just entered the bank and are filling the entry form (process creation in progress).
> - **Ready**: Your form is accepted; you are standing in the queue waiting for a free counter (waiting in the **Ready Queue**).
> - **Running**: You are at Counter 1 getting your transaction processed (CPU actively executing your process).
> - **Blocked**: While at the counter, the manager asks you to fetch a signature from the back office. You step aside and wait (waiting for **I/O or event completion**).
> - **Exit**: Your transaction is complete; you walk out of the bank (process termination).

Just as customers move between these conditions, the OS scheduler moves processes between states. The fundamental invariant is: **At any instant, exactly ONE process (on a single-core system) can be in the Running state**.

### 1.4 Key High-Yield Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know KTU Glossary**
> - **PCB (Process Control Block)**: Kernel data structure holding the state, registers, memory map, and accounting info.
> - **Dispatcher**: The module giving CPU control to the process selected by the short-term scheduler.
> - **Long-term Scheduler (Job Scheduler)**: Decides which jobs enter the Ready queue from the New state.
> - **Short-term Scheduler (CPU Scheduler)**: Decides which Ready process gets the CPU next.
> - **State Transition**: A directed edge in the PSD, triggered by an event (I/O, interrupt, scheduler dispatch).
> - **Voluntary Release**: When a process gives up the CPU (e.g., for I/O).
> - **Involuntary Preemption**: When the scheduler forcibly removes a process from the CPU (time quantum expiry).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-State Model (Foundational Baseline)

The simplest model uses only **Running** and **Not Running** states.

```
                    Dispatch
   [Not Running]  ──────────►  [Running]
         ▲                          │
         │                          │
         └────── Exit / I/O ────────┘
```

**Limitations of the Two-State Model**:
1. Cannot distinguish between a process waiting for the CPU vs. waiting for I/O.
2. Inefficient scheduler design — wakeups of I/O-blocked processes wrongly wake non-existent CPU-bound candidates.

### 2.2 The Five-State Process Model (KTU Standard)

This is the model emphasized in KTU 2024 examinations. The transitions are:

**Step 1 — New → Ready**
- Trigger: Long-term scheduler admits the process.
- Action: PCB is initialized, memory allocated, process placed in Ready queue.

**Step 2 — Ready → Running**
- Trigger: Short-term scheduler dispatches the process via the dispatcher.
- Action: CPU registers loaded from PCB; program counter (PC) and stack pointer (SP) restored.

**Step 3 — Running → Ready**
- Trigger: Time quantum expiry (preemptive) or higher-priority process arrival.
- Action: CPU context saved back into PCB; process re-queued.

**Step 4 — Running → Blocked**
- Trigger: Process issues a system call for I/O, or waits for an event/signal.
- Action: Process is moved to the appropriate wait queue; CPU is relinquished voluntarily.

**Step 5 — Blocked → Ready**
- Trigger: I/O completion interrupt fires; the OS moves the process back to the Ready queue.
- Action: PCB state field updated; process becomes eligible for scheduling.

**Step 6 — Running → Exit**
- Trigger: Process executes final statement or calls `exit()` system call, OR abnormally terminated (segfault).
- Action: Resources deallocated, PCB marked as zombie, parent notified via `wait()`.

### 2.3 The Seven-State Model (Extended with Suspended States)

When memory pressure occurs, the OS may **suspend** a blocked process to secondary storage:

| New State | Reason for Existence |
|---|---|
| **Ready-Suspended** | Process was in Ready but swapped out to free main memory |
| **Blocked-Suspended** | Process was Blocked and additionally swapped out |

> [!NOTE]
> **Suspension Mechanics**
> Suspended processes reside on disk. The OS uses a **medium-term scheduler** to manage swap-in/swap-out operations. This is critical in systems with constrained RAM (embedded systems, early Unix).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Process State Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU Utilization** | $U_{CPU} = 1 - P_{idle}$ | Fraction of time CPU is busy | % |
| **Throughput** | $T_p = \dfrac{N_{completed}}{\Delta t}$ | Processes completed per unit time | processes/sec |
| **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $T_{TAT} = T_{completion} - T_{arrival}$ | Total time in system | seconds |
| **Waiting Time (WT)** | $T_{WT} = T_{TAT} - T_{burst}$ | Time spent in Ready queue | seconds |
| **Response Time (RT)** | $T_{RT} = T_{first\_run} - T_{arrival}$ | Time to first CPU access | seconds |
| **Context Switch Overhead** | $T_{CS} = T_{save} + T_{load}$ | Time to swap processes on CPU | microseconds |
| **Degree of Multiprogramming** | $D_M = \vert Q_{ready} \vert + \vert Q_{running} \vert$ | Active processes in memory | dimensionless count |
| **Quantum Size Rule** | $q > T_{CS}$ | Time slice must exceed switch cost | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition (KTU Favorite)**
> For a system to be efficient, the time quantum $q$ must satisfy: $q \gg T_{CS}$. If $q$ approaches $T_{CS}$, the system wastes most of its time context-switching rather than executing.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Linux Kernel**: Uses states like `TASK_RUNNING`, `TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE`, `TASK_UNINTERRUPTIBLE`, `TASK_STOPPED`, `TASK_TRACED`.
- **RTOS (FreeRTOS, VxWorks)**: Uses simplified state machines like `Ready`, `Running`, `Blocked`, `Suspended`.
- **Android Process States**: `Foreground`, `Visible`, `Service`, `Background`, `Cached` — directly mapped from OS process states.
- **Cloud/Container Orchestration**: Kubernetes uses similar lifecycle states for pod scheduling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Waiting Time for Given Process States

Consider $n$ processes $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ with arrival times $a_i$ and CPU bursts $b_i$.

**Step 1 — Total Turnaround Time Equation**

$$
T_{TAT}^{(i)} = T_{waiting}^{(i)} + b_i + T_{I/O}^{(i)}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the Waiting Time Term**

The waiting time itself decomposes into Ready-queue wait and re-entry waits:

$$
T_{waiting}^{(i)} = \sum_{j=1}^{k_i} T_{ready\_j}^{(i)}
$$

where $k_i$ is the number of times process $i$ entered the Ready queue.

**Step 3 — Aggregate Across All Processes**

$$
\overline{T_{WT}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{waiting}^{(i)}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Bound (KTU Standard Form)**

For a non-preemptive FCFS scheduler with no I/O:

$$
\overline{T_{WT}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} b_j \right) = \frac{(n-1) \cdot \overline{b}}{2}
$$

where $\overline{b}$ is the mean CPU burst length.

**Interpretation**: On average, a process waits for half the total CPU work done before it, demonstrating why shorter jobs suffer in FCFS.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: State Transitions vs. Causes

| Transition | Direction | Trigger Event | Scheduler Role | Volition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{New} \rightarrow \text{Ready}$ | Forward | Admission | Long-term | System |
| $\text{Ready} \rightarrow \text{Running}$ | Forward | Dispatch | Short-term | System |
| $\text{Running} \rightarrow \text{Ready}$ | Backward | Preemption / Yield | Short-term | Mixed |
| $\text{Running} \rightarrow \text{Blocked}$ | Side | I/O request / Wait | None | Voluntary |
| $\text{Blocked} \rightarrow \text{Ready}$ | Side | I/O complete interrupt | None | System |
| $\text{Running} \rightarrow \text{Exit}$ | Terminal | `exit()` / fault | None | Voluntary/Forced |
| $\text{Blocked} \rightarrow \text{Blocked-Suspended}$ | Side (out) | Memory pressure | Medium-term | System |

### 3.3 Worked Example: State Transition Trace

**Problem**: Three processes $P_1, P_2, P_3$ arrive at times $t = 0, 1, 2$ with CPU bursts $5, 3, 2$ ms respectively under **Non-Preemptive SJF** scheduling. Compute average waiting time.

**Step 1 — List Remaining Bursts Over Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t = 0 &: P_1 \text{ enters Ready, dispatched (only one)} \\
t = 5 &: P_1 \text{ exits. Available: } P_2 \text{ (burst 3)}, P_3 \text{ (burst 2)} \\
t = 5 &: \text{SJF picks } P_3 \text{ (shorter)} \\
t = 7 &: P_3 \text{ exits. } P_2 \text{ runs} \\
t = 10 &: P_2 \text{ exits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Per-Process Waiting Times**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{WT}(P_1) &= 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ ms} \\
T_{WT}(P_2) &= 5 - 1 = 4 \text{ ms} \\
T_{WT}(P_3) &= 5 - 2 = 3 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Average**

$$
\overline{T_{WT}} = \frac{0 + 4 + 3}{3} = \frac{7}{3} \approx 2.33 \text{ ms}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Context Switch Cost Bound

Given $n$ processes each with burst $b$ and quantum $q$, the number of context switches is approximately:

$$
N_{CS} \approx \frac{n \cdot b}{q}
$$

Total time lost to context switching:

$$
T_{lost} = N_{CS} \cdot T_{CS} = \frac{n \cdot b \cdot T_{CS}}{q}
$$

Fraction of CPU wasted:

$$
F_{wasted} = \frac{T_{lost}}{T_{total}} = \frac{T_{CS}}{q}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight**: To keep $F_{wasted} < 1\%$, the scheduler must enforce $q \geq 100 \cdot T_{CS}$. For $T_{CS} = 10\,\mu s$, this gives $q \geq 1\,ms$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Five-State Process State Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["NEW"] -->|Long Term Scheduler Admits| B["READY"]
    B -->|Short Term Scheduler Dispatches| C["RUNNING"]
    C -->|Time Quantum Expires or Preempted| B
    C -->|Issues I/O or Waits for Event| D["BLOCKED WAITING"]
    D -->|I/O Completion Interrupt| B
    C -->|Exit System Call or Fault| E["EXIT TERMINATED"]
    B -.->|Memory Pressure Medium Term Swap Out| F["READY SUSPENDED"]
    D -.->|Memory Pressure Medium Term Swap Out| G["BLOCKED SUSPENDED"]
    F -.->|Swapped In| B
    G -.->|I/O Done and Swapped In| D
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of State Management

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["PROCESS P1 PCB"] -->|State Field| Q1["READY QUEUE"]
    P2["PROCESS P2 PCB"] -->|State Field| Q1
    P3["PROCESS P3 PCB"] -->|State Field| Q2["WAIT QUEUE I/O"]
    P4["PROCESS P4 PCB"] -->|State Field| Q3["SUSPENDED QUEUE"]
    LTS["LONG TERM SCHEDULER"] -->|Admits| Q1
    STS["SHORT TERM SCHEDULER"] -->|Selects| CP["CPU CORE"]
    MTS["MEDIUM TERM SCHEDULER"] -->|Swap In Out| Q3
    CP -->|Preempted| Q1
    CP -->|I/O Request| Q2
    CP -->|Exit| TP["TERMINATION POOL"]
    Q2 -->|Interrupt| Q1
```

### 4.3 State Transition Trigger Matrix (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Triggers["TRANSITION TRIGGERS"]
        T1["Admission by LTS"]
        T2["Dispatch by STS"]
        T3["Quantum Expiry"]
        T4["I/O System Call"]
        T5["I/O Completion Interrupt"]
        T6["Process Exit"]
        T7["Swap Out by MTS"]
        T8["Swap In by MTS"]
    end
    Triggers --> Engine["STATE TRANSITION ENGINE"]
    Engine --> Update["UPDATE PCB STATE FIELD"]
    Update --> Requeue["MOVE PCB TO NEW QUEUE"]
```

### 4.4 Process Lifecycle Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant OS as Operating System
    participant LTS as Long Term Scheduler
    participant STS as Short Term Scheduler
    participant CPU as CPU Core
    participant IO as I/O Device
    U->>OS: fork()
    OS->>OS: Create PCB
    OS->>LTS: Submit for admission
    LTS->>OS: Admit to Ready
    OS->>STS: Ready queue updated
    STS->>CPU: Dispatch process
    CPU->>OS: I/O request
    OS->>IO: Issue I/O
    OS->>STS: Move to Blocked queue
    IO->>OS: I/O complete
    OS->>STS: Move to Ready queue
    STS->>CPU: Dispatch again
    CPU->>OS: exit()
    OS->>U: Process terminated
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define a process state. List the various states of a process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A process state is the current condition of a process that indicates its status with respect to the system resources, particularly the CPU. The various states of a process in the five-state model are: **New, Ready, Running, Blocked, and Exit**. Each state represents a specific phase in the lifecycle of the process, from creation through execution to termination.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Differentiate between Ready state and Blocked state of a process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Ready State | Blocked State |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU Status** | Process is waiting for CPU | Process is waiting for I/O or event |
| **Queue** | Resides in Ready Queue | Resides in Event/Wait Queue |
| **Trigger to move** | Dispatch by scheduler | I/O completion interrupt |
| **Eligibility for CPU** | Immediately eligible | Not eligible until event occurs |
| **Example** | A process that just got preempted | A process waiting for disk read |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. Drawing state diagrams without labeling transitions clearly with the event/trigger.
> 2. Confusing the **Blocked** state with the **Suspended** state (they are distinct concepts).
> 3. Forgetting to mention which scheduler triggers each transition (LTS vs STS vs MTS).
> 4. Missing the difference between **preemptive** and **non-preemptive** transitions.
> 5. Failing to relate state transitions to PCB updates.
> Always show both the diagram AND the trigger conditions for full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the five-state process model with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of long-term, short-term, and medium-term schedulers in process state transitions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The five-state process model describes the lifecycle of a process using five distinct states: New, Ready, Running, Blocked, and Exit.

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N["NEW"] -->|Long Term Scheduler| R["READY"]
    R -->|Short Term Scheduler Dispatch| X["RUNNING"]
    X -->|Preemption or Yield| R
    X -->|I/O or Wait| B["BLOCKED"]
    B -->|I/O Complete| R
    X -->|Exit| E["EXIT"]
```

**Scheduler Roles (3 marks):**
- **Long-Term Scheduler (LTS)**: Controls the degree of multiprogramming by selecting jobs from New → Ready. Runs less frequently; optimizes the mix of I/O-bound and CPU-bound processes.
- **Short-Term Scheduler (STS)**: Selects the next process from Ready → Running. Runs very frequently (milliseconds); minimizes CPU idle time.
- **Medium-Term Scheduler (MTS)**: Handles swapping — moves processes between main memory and secondary storage to ease memory pressure.

---

**(b)** A system has 4 processes with arrival times and burst times as follows. Compute the **average waiting time** and **average turnaround time** using **SJF (Non-Preemptive)** scheduling. Show the Gantt chart and state transitions of each process. **(7 Marks)**

| Process | Arrival Time (ms) | Burst Time (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 7 |
| P2 | 2 | 4 |
| P3 | 4 | 1 |
| P4 | 5 | 4 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Build Gantt Chart**

At $t = 0$, only P1 is in Ready → P1 runs from 0 to 7.
At $t = 7$, all processes have arrived. Remaining bursts: P2=4, P3=1, P4=4.
SJF picks P3 (burst=1) → runs from 7 to 8.
Then P2 and P4 tie at burst=4. By FCFS tiebreak, P2 → runs 8 to 12. P4 → 12 to 16.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gantt Chart: } &\vert\,P1\,\vert\,\vert\,P3\,\vert\,\vert\,P2\,\vert\,\vert\,P4\,\vert \\
& 0 \quad 7 \quad 8 \quad 12 \quad 16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Compute Completion, TAT, WT**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CT}(P1) &= 7, \quad T_{TAT}(P1) = 7 - 0 = 7, \quad T_{WT}(P1) = 7 - 7 = 0 \\
T_{CT}(P2) &= 12, \quad T_{TAT}(P2) = 12 - 2 = 10, \quad T_{WT}(P2) = 10 - 4 = 6 \\
T_{CT}(P3) &= 8, \quad T_{TAT}(P3) = 8 - 4 = 4, \quad T_{WT}(P3) = 4 - 1 = 3 \\
T_{CT}(P4) &= 16, \quad T_{TAT}(P4) = 16 - 5 = 11, \quad T_{WT}(P4) = 11 - 4 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Compute Averages**

$$
\overline{T_{TAT}} = \frac{7 + 10 + 4 + 11}{4} = \frac{32}{4} = 8 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\overline{T_{WT}} = \frac{0 + 6 + 3 + 7}{4} = \frac{16}{4} = 4 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks): State Transitions Narrative**
- P1: New (t=0) → Ready (t=0) → Running (t=0) → Exit (t=7)
- P2: New (t=2) → Ready (t=2) → Running (t=8) → Exit (t=12)
- P3: New (t=4) → Ready (t=4) → Running (t=7) → Exit (t=8)
- P4: New (t=5) → Ready (t=5) → Running (t=12) → Exit (t=16)

**Final Answer:** $\overline{T_{WT}} = 4$ ms, $\overline{T_{TAT}} = 8$ ms.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **seven-state process model** including suspended states. Why is suspension needed in modern operating systems? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The seven-state model extends the five-state model by splitting the Ready and Blocked states into In-Memory and Suspended (swapped-out) variants.

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N["NEW"] -->|LTS Admits| RS["READY"]
    RS -->|STS Dispatch| X["RUNNING"]
    X -->|Preempt| RS
    X -->|I/O Wait| BS["BLOCKED"]
    BS -->|I/O Done| RS
    X -->|Exit| E["EXIT"]
    RS -.->|MTS Swap Out| RSS["READY SUSPENDED"]
    BS -.->|MTS Swap Out| BSS["BLOCKED SUSPENDED"]
    RSS -.->|MTS Swap In| RS
    BSS -.->|I/O Done then Swap In| BS
    BSS -.->|MTS Swap In First| RSS
```

**Need for Suspension (3 marks):**
1. **Memory Relief**: When main memory is saturated, less-active processes are moved to disk to free RAM for active processes.
2. **Power Efficiency**: Suspended processes consume no CPU and minimal power.
3. **System Responsiveness**: Brings in new processes faster by ensuring Ready queue is properly sized.
4. **Crash Recovery**: Suspended state preserves process context for later resumption after system crashes.

---

**(b)** Consider the following trace of process state transitions. Identify which scheduler is responsible for each transition and justify whether the transition is **preemptive** or **non-preemptive**. **(7 Marks)**

| Time (ms) | Process | Transition |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | P1 | New → Ready |
| 1 | P1 | Ready → Running |
| 5 | P1 | Running → Blocked |
| 6 | P2 | New → Ready |
| 7 | P1 | Blocked → Ready |
| 8 | P1 | Ready → Running |
| 12 | P1 | Running → Exit |

**Model Solution:**

**Analysis (1 mark per row, total 7 marks with reasoning):**

| Time | Transition | Scheduler | Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | New → Ready | Long-Term | Non-preemptive | Admission to system is voluntary/system-driven, not preemptive |
| 1 | Ready → Running | Short-Term | Non-preemptive | Initial dispatch (no running process to preempt) |
| 5 | Running → Blocked | None (Process action) | Non-preemptive | Voluntary I/O request — process gives up CPU |
| 6 | New → Ready | Long-Term | Non-preemptive | P2 admitted while P1 is blocked |
| 7 | Blocked → Ready | None (I/O subsystem) | Non-preemptive | Hardware interrupt triggers transition |
| 8 | Ready → Running | Short-Term | Non-preemptive | STS picks P1 from Ready; P2 also in Ready but P1 was first |
| 12 | Running → Exit | None (Process action) | N/A | Process calls `exit()` |

**Key Insight**: In this trace, ALL transitions are **non-preemptive** because the system does not show any time-quantum based preemption (Running → Ready without voluntary action).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**
> - **Five canonical states**: New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Exit (this is the KTU standard).
> - **Running state is unique** — only ONE process per CPU core can be in Running at a time.
> - **Ready ≠ Blocked**: Ready processes want the CPU; Blocked processes want an event.
> - **Three schedulers**: LTS (admission), STS (CPU selection), MTS (swapping).
> - **Suspended states** exist ONLY in systems with medium-term scheduling.
> - **PCB state field** is updated on EVERY transition — this is the OS's source of truth.
> - **Preemptive** transition: Running → Ready forced by scheduler.
> - **Voluntary** transition: Running → Blocked initiated by process.
> - **State diagram must be labeled with triggers** for full marks in KTU exams.
> - **Formula to remember**: $F_{wasted} = \dfrac{T_{CS}}{q}$, so keep $q \gg T_{CS}$.
> - **Context switch** is not a state — it is the MECHANISM enabling Ready ↔ Running transitions.
> - **Turnaround Time** = Waiting Time + Burst Time (ignoring I/O for simplicity).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Control Block
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process Control Block

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Process Control Block (PCB) — Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Control Block (PCB)** — also called a *Task Control Block* — is a kernel-level data structure maintained by the Operating System for **every active process** in the system. It acts as a *repository of all information required to manage, schedule, and resume* a process, including its current execution state, CPU context, memory map, I/O status, and accounting data.

The PCB is the **single source of truth** for a process. When a process is *preempted* or *context-switched out*, the OS saves the current state of the CPU into the PCB. When the process is later scheduled back, the OS reloads this state from the PCB — effectively restoring the process exactly where it left off.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Process Passport

Imagine a **hospital patient file**:

- When a patient (process) is admitted, the hospital creates a thick folder (PCB).
- The folder tracks everything: name, room number, current treatment, doctor notes, billing, previous test reports, allergies, next-of-kin (open files), and the medication schedule (CPU registers).
- If a doctor is suddenly called to an emergency, she notes the patient's current state in the folder, locks it, and moves to the next patient. Later, she reopens the folder and resumes treatment **exactly** where she stopped.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The PCB is **not** the process itself — it is the *metadata/identity card* of the process. Just as a passport does not contain the traveller, the PCB does not contain the executing program code; it contains *information needed to manage* the program.

## 1.3 Why is PCB Critical?

- **Context Switching**: Without PCB, the OS cannot remember what a process was doing.
- **Process Scheduling**: Scheduler decides which process runs next using fields inside the PCB.
- **Resource Management**: OS tracks file handles, memory limits, and I/O state via the PCB.
- **Process Termination & Cleanup**: When a process ends, its PCB is deallocated and all resources reclaimed.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Standard Metric:** A typical UNIX PCB occupies between **1 KB to 4 KB** of kernel memory, depending on the OS variant. Linux `task_struct` is roughly **8–16 KB** because it consolidates even more metadata.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown — The 10 Logical Categories of a PCB

The PCB is logically grouped into **ten standard information blocks**, as prescribed by classical OS textbooks (Silberschatz, Galvin, Gagne) and reflected in KTU 2024 syllabus expectations.

### Block 1 — Process Identification

- **Process ID (PID):** A unique positive integer assigned by the OS at creation. Default range in Linux: **2 to 32768**.
- **Parent Process ID (PPID):** The PID of the creator (useful for tree structure).

### Block 2 — Process State

A process can be in one of the following canonical states:
- **New** → being created
- **Ready** → waiting to be assigned to a CPU
- **Running** → currently executing on the CPU
- **Blocked / Waiting** → waiting for some I/O or event to complete
- **Terminated** → finished execution

### Block 3 — Program Counter & CPU Registers

- **Program Counter (PC):** Address of the next instruction to be executed.
- **General Purpose Registers (GPRs):** Accumulator, base, index, stack pointer — all volatile state.

### Block 4 — CPU Scheduling Information

- **Process Priority:** Used by scheduler to decide execution order.
- **Scheduling Queue Pointers:** Pointers to the next/previous PCB in the ready queue.
- **Wait Time, Turnaround Time accumulators.**

### Block 5 — Memory Management Information

- **Base Register, Limit Register:** Define the process's logical address space.
- **Page Table Pointer / Segment Table Pointer.**
- **Memory Limits** (soft & hard limits).

### Block 6 — Accounting Information

- **CPU Time Used** (user mode and kernel mode separately).
- **Wall-Clock Time, Time Limits, Job/Process IDs for billing.**

### Block 7 — I/O Status Information

- List of I/O devices allocated to the process.
- List of open files, pending operations, and buffers.

### Block 8 — Open File & Resource Pointers

- **File Descriptor Table Pointer.**
- Pointers to socket descriptors, pipes, IPC queues.

### Block 9 — Inter-Process Communication (IPC) State

- Signals pending, blocked signal mask, message queue references.

### Block 10 — Process Privileges & Credentials

- Real User ID, Effective User ID, Group ID — used for access control.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Field Name** | **Data Type** | **Purpose / Utility** | **Update Frequency** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process ID (PID) | `int` | Unique process identifier | Once at creation |
| Process State | `enum` | Tracks execution state | Very high (every switch) |
| Program Counter | `register` | Address of next instruction | Every instruction |
| CPU Registers | `array[reg]` | Volatile execution context | Every context switch |
| Memory Base / Limit | `ptr` / `int` | Address space boundary | On memory ops |
| Page Table Pointer | `ptr` | Virtual → physical mapping | On page fault |
| Priority | `int` | Scheduling weight | Scheduler dependent |
| Accounting: CPU Time | `long` | Total CPU usage | Every tick |
| Open File List | `fd_set` | Active file descriptors | Per open/close |
| I/O Status | `bitmap` | Device allocation | Per I/O op |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Context Switch Overhead Formula:**
>
> $$T_{\text{total\_switch}} = T_{\text{save}} + T_{\text{load}} + T_{\text{scheduler}}$$
>
> where $T_{\text{save}}$ is the time to copy the outgoing process's CPU state into its PCB, $T_{\text{load}}$ is the time to restore the incoming process's state from its PCB, and $T_{\text{scheduler}}$ is the time for the dispatcher to select the next process. **All three operations involve direct PCB manipulation.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Process State Matrix

## 3.1 The Context Switch — Step-by-Step Logic

A *context switch* is the most PCB-intensive operation in any OS. Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step derivation** of how a context switch manipulates the PCB.

### Step 1 — Trigger Event

The OS kernel receives a *context switch request*. Common triggers:
- **Hardware interrupt** (timer tick, I/O completion).
- **System call** (e.g., `sleep()`, `wait()`).
- **Preemption** by a higher-priority process.

### Step 2 — Save the Incoming-Interrupt Context

When an interrupt fires, the CPU automatically pushes the **Program Counter** and **Processor Status Word (PSW)** onto the **kernel stack** of the currently running process. The hardware does this **before** the OS code runs.

### Step 3 — Locate the Running Process's PCB

The OS accesses a global register — usually called the **Current Process Pointer** (in Linux: `current` macro) — which stores the *base address* of the PCB of the running process. The kernel reads this address into a working register.

### Step 4 — Save CPU Registers into the PCB

The kernel pushes the contents of all **General Purpose Registers** (e.g., EAX, EBX, ECX, EDX, ESI, EDI, EBP, ESP) into a designated area inside the PCB. The *saved* PC and PSW from Step 2 are also moved into the PCB's *process state* field.

### Step 5 — Update the Outgoing Process's State

The state field in the running process's PCB is updated from `RUNNING` to either `READY` (if preempted) or `BLOCKED` (if waiting on I/O).

### Step 6 — Run the Scheduler

The scheduler examines the ready queue and selects the **next process** to run based on its algorithm (Round Robin, Priority, SJF, etc.). The decision is made by reading scheduling fields inside each candidate PCB.

### Step 7 — Update the Incoming Process's State

The selected PCB's state field is updated from `READY` to `RUNNING`. The global *Current Process Pointer* is now updated to point to this new PCB.

### Step 8 — Reload the Incoming Process's Context

The kernel copies the saved GPRs from the new PCB's register-save area back into the physical CPU registers. The PC and PSW fields are also restored.

### Step 9 — Return from Interrupt

A final `iret` (or equivalent) instruction atomically loads the saved PC and PSW into the CPU. The new process *resumes execution at the exact instruction* it was preempted at.

### Step 10 — Resume Execution

User-mode code of the new process continues. **No user code is aware that a switch occurred.**

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — PCB State Transitions

| **From State** | **Event** | **To State** | **PCB Field Updated** |
|---|---|---|---|
| NEW | Admission accepted | READY | `state = READY` |
| READY | Scheduler dispatches | RUNNING | `state = RUNNING`, `pc = saved_pc` |
| RUNNING | I/O request issued | BLOCKED | `state = BLOCKED`, `io_status` set |
| RUNNING | Time slice expired | READY | `state = READY`, registers saved |
| BLOCKED | I/O completion interrupt | READY | `state = READY`, queued at tail |
| RUNNING | Process calls `exit()` | TERMINATED | `state = TERMINATED`, cleanup begins |
| TERMINATED | OS reclaims resources | (Freed) | PCB deallocated |

## 3.3 Quantitative Context Switch Cost Derivation

Let us model the **average context switch cost** mathematically.

Let:
- $N$ = total number of context switches per second
- $T_{\text{switch}}$ = time taken for one full context switch
- $C_{\text{overhead}} = N \cdot T_{\text{switch}}$ = total CPU time wasted on switching

CPU utilization efficiency is:

$$\eta_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{T_{\text{useful}}}{T_{\text{useful}} + C_{\text{overhead}}}$$

If $N = 100$ switches/sec and $T_{\text{switch}} = 10 \, \mu s$:

$$C_{\text{overhead}} = 100 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-3} \, \text{seconds} = 1 \, \text{ms}$$

Thus, **0.1 %** of CPU time is lost to context switching for every 100 switches per second with a 10 μs switch cost. This is precisely why high-performance systems try to *minimize PCB size* and *reduce register count*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Process Control Block

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **internal structure of a PCB** as a hierarchical block. Each block represents one logical grouping of fields.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootPCB["PROCESS CONTROL BLOCK"]

    rootPCB --> blockA["PROCESS IDENTIFICATION"]
    blockA --> fA1["PID"]
    blockA --> fA2["PPID"]

    rootPCB --> blockB["PROCESS STATE"]
    blockB --> fB1["NEW"]
    blockB --> fB2["READY"]
    blockB --> fB3["RUNNING"]
    blockB --> fB4["BLOCKED"]
    blockB --> fB5["TERMINATED"]

    rootPCB --> blockC["CPU CONTEXT"]
    blockC --> fC1["Program Counter"]
    blockC --> fC2["General Purpose Registers"]
    blockC --> fC3["Stack Pointer"]
    blockC --> fC4["PSW Flags"]

    rootPCB --> blockD["MEMORY MANAGEMENT"]
    blockD --> fD1["Base Register"]
    blockD --> fD2["Limit Register"]
    blockD --> fD3["Page Table Pointer"]

    rootPCB --> blockE["SCHEDULING INFO"]
    blockE --> fE1["Priority"]
    blockE --> fE2["Queue Pointers"]
    blockE --> fE3["Wait Time"]

    rootPCB --> blockF["ACCOUNTING INFO"]
    blockF --> fF1["CPU Time Used"]
    blockF --> fF2["Time Limits"]
    blockF --> fF3["Job ID"]

    rootPCB --> blockG["I/O STATUS"]
    blockG --> fG1["Allocated Devices"]
    blockG --> fG2["Pending Operations"]

    rootPCB --> blockH["FILE & RESOURCE TABLE"]
    blockH --> fH1["File Descriptor Pointer"]
    blockH --> fH2["Socket Pointers"]
    blockH --> fH3["IPC References"]
```

## 4.2 Context Switch Flow — PCB Manipulation Sequence

This block-level functional architecture flow shows **how the kernel walks through PCB operations during a context switch**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1["INTERRUPT OR SYSCALL TRIGGERED"] --> step2["HARDWARE SAVES PC AND PSW ONTO KERNEL STACK"]
    step2 --> step3["KERNEL LOCATES CURRENT PCB VIA GLOBAL POINTER"]
    step3 --> step4["KERNEL PUSHES ALL CPU REGISTERS INTO PCB REGISTER AREA"]
    step4 --> step5["UPDATE OUTGOING PCB STATE TO READY OR BLOCKED"]
    step5 --> step6["INVOKE SCHEDULER TO PICK NEXT PROCESS"]
    step6 --> step7["LOCATE NEXT PCB FROM READY QUEUE"]
    step7 --> step8["UPDATE NEXT PCB STATE TO RUNNING"]
    step8 --> step9["RESTORE CPU REGISTERS FROM NEXT PCB"]
    step9 --> step10["RESTORE PC AND PSW FROM NEXT PCB"]
    step10 --> step11["RESUME USER MODE EXECUTION OF NEW PROCESS"]
```

## 4.3 Process Table — Linked List of PCBs

The OS maintains a **process table** — a linked list (or array) of all PCBs in the system. This is the master registry used by the scheduler.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    head["PROCESS TABLE HEAD POINTER"] --> pcb1["PCB 1 PID 1001 STATE READY"]
    pcb1 --> pcb2["PCB 2 PID 1002 STATE RUNNING"]
    pcb2 --> pcb3["PCB 3 PID 1003 STATE BLOCKED"]
    pcb3 --> pcb4["PCB 4 PID 1004 STATE READY"]
    pcb4 --> pcb5["PCB 5 PID 1005 STATE NEW"]
    pcb5 --> nullX["NULL END OF LIST"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Process Control Block. List any four fields stored in a PCB.**

**Model Answer:**

> A Process Control Block (PCB) is a kernel data structure that contains all the information about a particular process, including its execution state, CPU registers, memory map, scheduling parameters, accounting data, and I/O status. It is created when the process is admitted and destroyed when the process terminates.

**Four fields of a PCB:**

1. **Process ID (PID)** — Unique identifier assigned by the OS.
2. **Program Counter (PC)** — Address of the next instruction to execute.
3. **Process State** — Current state (New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated).
4. **CPU Registers** — Saved values of all general-purpose registers.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Marking Tip:** A 3-mark definition question requires *one* sentence of definition + a clean list. Do not exceed 4 fields; examiners expect crispness.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of PCB during a context switch?**

**Model Answer:**

> The PCB plays a **central role** in a context switch. When the currently running process is preempted, the OS saves the values of the Program Counter, Processor Status Word, and all CPU registers into the outgoing process's PCB. The scheduler then selects the next process, and the OS reloads the saved register and PC values from the incoming process's PCB, thereby restoring its exact execution state. **Without the PCB, the OS would have no mechanism to remember and restore process state.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **structure of a Process Control Block**. List all the major information categories stored in it. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **how the PCB is used during a context switch** with a step-by-step procedure. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

The Process Control Block is a kernel data structure containing all process-related information. The major categories are:

1. **Process Identification** — PID, PPID
2. **Process State** — New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated
3. **CPU Context** — Program Counter, Registers, Stack Pointer, PSW
4. **Memory Management Info** — Base, Limit, Page Table Pointer
5. **Scheduling Information** — Priority, Queue Pointers, Accounting
6. **I/O Status** — Allocated devices, pending operations
7. **Open File List** — File descriptor table pointer
8. **IPC Information** — Signal masks, message queues
9. **Credentials** — User ID, Group ID
10. **Pointer Field** — Links to the next PCB in the process table

> **Diagram Required:** A neat labeled block diagram of the PCB with all 10 fields (similar to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes). Students should hand-draw a clean rectangular box subdivided into 10 sections, with each section labeled.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

The PCB is used during a context switch as follows:

1. **Interrupt or system call** triggers the OS to consider switching.
2. The hardware **saves the PC and PSW** of the running process onto the kernel stack.
3. The OS **locates the PCB** of the running process using a global pointer.
4. The OS **saves all CPU registers** into the register-save area of the PCB.
5. The state of the outgoing PCB is updated to `READY` or `BLOCKED`.
6. The **scheduler** selects the next process from the ready queue.
7. The incoming PCB's state is updated to `RUNNING`.
8. The OS **restores CPU registers** from the incoming PCB.
9. The OS executes `iret` to load the saved PC and PSW into the CPU.
10. The new process **resumes execution** at the exact point it was suspended.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks on PCB context-switch questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "save" and "load"** — students often write the steps in wrong order. Marks are deducted for wrong sequence.
> 2. **Forgetting the hardware step** — Many students omit the initial hardware-saved PC/PSW. This is a **2-mark loss** in typical KTU valuation.
> 3. **Missing state-field update** — The PCB's `state` field changes during the switch. Forgetting this loses 1 mark.
> 4. **No mention of the global pointer (current PCB pointer)** — Examiners expect you to mention that the OS *finds* the PCB using a kernel-level pointer variable.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare the PCB fields used for **process scheduling** versus those used for **memory management**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A system performs **120 context switches per second**, with each switch taking **12 μs**. Compute the percentage of CPU time wasted on context switching. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

| **Scheduling Fields in PCB** | **Memory Management Fields in PCB** |
|---|---|
| Process Priority | Base Register |
| Scheduling Queue Pointers (next/prev) | Limit Register |
| CPU Time Used (so far) | Page Table Base Pointer |
| Wait Time, Turnaround Time accumulators | Segment Table Pointer |
| Pointer to scheduling algorithm-specific data | Virtual Address Space size |
| Recent CPU burst estimate | Memory locks held |
| Arrival Time in ready queue | Working set information |

**Comparison Summary:**
- **Scheduling fields** are *dynamic* and updated by the dispatcher on every quantum expiry.
- **Memory management fields** are *relatively stable*, only changing on memory allocation, page fault, or process creation.
- Scheduling fields determine **when** a process runs; memory management fields determine **what** the process can access.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given:**
- Number of context switches per second: $N = 120$
- Time per context switch: $T_{\text{switch}} = 12 \, \mu s = 12 \times 10^{-6} \, s$

**Step 1: Compute total time spent in context switching per second:**

$$T_{\text{overhead}} = N \times T_{\text{switch}}$$

$$T_{\text{overhead}} = 120 \times 12 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T_{\text{overhead}} = 1440 \times 10^{-6} \, s = 1.44 \times 10^{-3} \, s = 1.44 \, ms$$

**Step 2: Compute percentage of CPU time wasted:**

$$\% \, \text{Waste} = \frac{T_{\text{overhead}}}{1 \, \text{second}} \times 100$$

$$\% \, \text{Waste} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-3}}{1} \times 100 = 0.144 \, \%$$

**Final Answer:** The CPU wastes **0.144 %** of its time on context switching per second.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning — Numerical questions:**
> 1. **Unit conversion mistakes** are the #1 reason for losing marks. Always explicitly write $12 \mu s = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ s.
> 2. **Final percentage must be in %** — writing $0.00144$ without a percentage will lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Show all intermediate steps** — KTU examiners reward step-by-step working; skipping from formula to answer loses method marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Process Control Block**

- ✅ PCB = kernel data structure per process. **Also called Task Control Block.**
- ✅ Created at process admission; destroyed at process termination.
- ✅ **10 logical categories**: PID, State, PC, Registers, Memory info, Scheduling, Accounting, I/O status, Files, IPC.
- ✅ Process state can be **New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated** (5 canonical states).
- ✅ PCB is the **backbone of context switching** — both save and load operations rely on it.
- ✅ OS locates running PCB via a **global current-process pointer** (Linux: `current`).
- ✅ Standard PCB size: **1–4 KB** (Linux `task_struct` is larger: 8–16 KB).
- ✅ Context switch cost: $T_{\text{switch}} = T_{\text{save}} + T_{\text{load}} + T_{\text{scheduler}}$.
- ✅ PCB fields are partitioned into **volatile** (registers, PC) and **stable** (PID, credentials).
- ✅ A linked list (or hash table) of PCBs is called the **Process Table**.
- ✅ In a context switch, **state field updates are mandatory** — never omit them in answers.
- ✅ Hardware saves PC/PSW onto kernel stack **before** the OS runs — this is a frequently tested nuance.
- ✅ For 14-mark questions: always include a **neat labeled diagram** + a **step-by-step procedure**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Thread?

A **thread** is the smallest unit of CPU execution that exists within a **process**. A traditional (heavyweight) process has a single thread of control, but a multithreaded process can have multiple threads — each executing a different part of the program code, sharing the same code section, data section, and OS resources (open files, signals, etc.) while maintaining its own **thread ID**, **program counter (PC)**, **register set**, and **stack**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Silberschatz/Galvin/Gagne framework):**
> *A thread is a basic unit of CPU utilization; it comprises a thread ID, a program counter, a register set, and a stack. It shares with other threads belonging to the same process its code section, data section, and other operating-system resources such as open files and signals.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a large restaurant (the **process**). The restaurant has:
- One shared **kitchen** (code/data section)
- One shared **pantry** (heap, global variables)
- One shared **inventory** (open files, OS resources)

Now, a *traditional process* would be a restaurant with **one chef** doing everything — chopping, cooking, plating, and serving. The chef can do only one task at a time. If the chef pauses to boil water, nothing else moves forward.

A **multithreaded process** is a restaurant with **multiple chefs** working in the same kitchen, sharing the same pantry and inventory. Each chef (thread) has:
- Their **own knife and cutting board** (register set + stack)
- Their **own recipe card** (program counter)
- A common understanding of the **menu** (code section)

One chef can chop vegetables while another boils pasta, and a third plates the dish — all concurrently, all sharing the same resources. The kitchen itself is the process; the chefs are the threads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exams:** A process = **resource allocation unit**; a thread = **CPU execution / scheduling unit**. Multiple threads in one process = lighter, faster context switches than full process switches.

## 1.3 Motivation: Why Threads?

The two primary reasons for using threads are enumerated in the KTU syllabus:

1. **Responsiveness** — In a single-threaded application, if one part of the program blocks (e.g., I/O wait), the entire program freezes. With multiple threads, one thread can continue executing while another waits.
2. **Resource Sharing** — Threads share the memory and resources of the process by default, allowing automatic sharing without explicit inter-process communication (IPC) mechanisms.
3. **Economy** — Creating, switching between, and terminating threads is much cheaper than performing the same operations on processes. (Memory allocation, kernel bookkeeping is minimized.)
4. **Scalability (Multiprocessor Utilization)** — On multicore systems, threads can run in **true parallel** on different cores, achieving speedup that single-threaded code cannot.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love the **"Process vs. Thread"** comparison question. Memorize the table in Section 2.

## 1.4 The Single-Threaded vs. Multithreaded Process

| Component | Single-Threaded Process | Multithreaded Process |
|---|---|---|
| Registers | One set | One set **per thread** |
| Program Counter | One PC | One PC **per thread** |
| Stack | One stack | One stack **per thread** |
| Code Section | One (shared) | One (shared by all threads) |
| Data Section | One (shared) | One (shared by all threads) |
| OS Resources (Files, Sockets) | One set | One set (shared by all threads) |

This leads to the central principle: **Threads within a process share the process's resources; each thread has its own execution context.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process vs. Thread — The Core Distinction

| Feature | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | A program in execution; a unit of resource allocation | A unit of CPU scheduling within a process |
| Address Space | Each process has its **own** virtual address space | Threads **share** the address space of their parent process |
| Creation Cost | **Heavy** (memory allocation, PCB setup, kernel trap) | **Light** (often 10–100× cheaper) |
| Communication | IPC: pipes, sockets, shared memory, message queues | Direct via shared global memory (must synchronize) |
| Context Switch | **Expensive** (TLB flush, cache pollution, kernel mode) | **Cheaper** (mostly register + stack switch) |
| Termination | Frees all resources | Frees thread-local resources; process lives if one thread lives |
| Identifier | Process ID (PID) | Thread ID (TID) |
| Owned Resources | Open files, sockets, semaphores, memory, etc. | None of its own; borrows from process |

## 2.2 Types of Threads

### A. User-Level Threads (ULT)
- Thread management is done by a **user-level thread library** (e.g., `pthread` on older systems, GNU `pthreads`, `GNU Portable Threads`).
- The **kernel is unaware** of these threads — to the kernel, the process is single-threaded.
- **Pros:** Fast (no kernel mode switch), customizable scheduling, runs on any OS.
- **Cons:** A blocking system call (e.g., `read()`) blocks the **entire process** (all threads). Cannot exploit multiprocessors natively.

### B. Kernel-Level Threads (KLT)
- Thread management is done by the **kernel** (Windows, Linux, modern Solaris).
- The kernel schedules each thread independently.
- **Pros:** True parallelism on multicore. Blocking call affects only one thread.
- **Cons:** Slower thread creation/switch (kernel mode transition).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite Question:** *"Differentiate between ULT and KLT with examples."* The above table is your template.

## 2.3 Multithreading Models (The 3 Models)

These define how **user-level threads** are mapped to **kernel-level threads**.

### 2.3.1 Many-to-One Model
- Many ULTs → One KLT.
- Thread management done in user space.
- **Problem:** If one thread makes a blocking system call, the entire process blocks. No true parallelism on multiprocessors.
- **Examples:** Older Solaris Green Threads, GNU Portable Threads.

### 2.3.2 One-to-One Model
- Each ULT → One KLT.
- **Advantage:** True parallelism; one blocking thread does not block others.
- **Disadvantage:** Creating a user thread requires creating a corresponding kernel thread → overhead. The OS may limit the number of KLTs.
- **Examples:** Windows (Win32), Linux, modern Solaris.

### 2.3.3 Many-to-Many Model
- $m$ ULTs multiplexed onto $n$ KLTs, where typically $m \geq n$.
- Developers can create as many user threads as needed; the kernel can assign them to a smaller (or equal) pool of KLTs.
- Variations: **Two-level model** — allows a bound thread (1:1) or multiplexed thread (m:n).
- **Examples:** Solaris before 9, Tru64 UNIX, HP-UX, IBM AIX.

## 2.4 Thread Libraries

A **thread library** provides the API for creating and managing threads.

| Library | Type | Platform | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pthreads (POSIX)** | Standardized API; can be ULT or KLT depending on implementation | Linux, macOS, UNIX | `pthread_create()`, `pthread_join()`, `pthread_exit()` |
| **Win32 Thread API** | Kernel-level (1:1) | Windows | `CreateThread()`, `WaitForSingleObject()` |
| **Java Threads** | Built on top of OS native threads (1:1 on modern JVMs) | Cross-platform (JVM) | `Thread` class, `Runnable` interface, `start()`, `run()` |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Performance & Multicore Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Threads have no "physics equations," but KTU loves the following performance formulas, especially under the **Multicore Programming** subsection of Module 3.

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Amdahl's Law (Speedup)** | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | $S$ = speedup, $P$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = number of cores | Upper bound on speedup for $N$ cores |
| **Amdahl's Law (Alternative form)** | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{f_s + \dfrac{1-f_s}{N}}$ | $f_s$ = serial fraction | Same law, $P = 1-f_s$ |
| **Maximum Speedup ($N \to \infty$)** | $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1-P}$ | $P$ = parallel fraction | A program that is 95% parallel caps at speedup of $20\times$ |
| **CPU Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100\%$ | — | KTU 2024 syllabus context: scheduling effectiveness |
| **Thread Creation Overhead Ratio (qualitative)** | $\text{Cost}_{\text{thread}} \ll \text{Cost}_{\text{process}}$ | — | Often 10×–100× cheaper |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use the `|` character inside any table row.** In the Amdahl's law above, we used parentheses instead of absolute-value bars to remain markdown-table safe. The absolute-value form is $\vert x \vert$, but the table above is clean and unambiguous.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility — Where Multithreading Matters in Practice

- **Web servers** (e.g., Apache with worker MPM, NGINX) use thread pools to handle thousands of simultaneous client requests.
- **Database engines** (PostgreSQL, MySQL) assign one thread per client connection.
- **GUI applications** keep the UI thread free of long computations; background threads perform heavy work and post results back to the UI.
- **Real-time systems and embedded OS** (RTEMS, VxWorks) rely on deterministic thread scheduling.
- **Compilers** (gcc, clang) parallelize build steps across cores — directly relevant to **PERUT638 – Compiler Design and Operating Systems**!
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Thread Lifecycle (State Diagram Steps)

A thread, like a process, moves through several states. The standard five-state model is:

$$\text{New} \rightarrow \text{Ready} \rightarrow \text{Running} \rightarrow \text{Blocked} \rightarrow \text{Terminated}$$

Below is a step-by-step trace of the **transitions**:

| Step | From State | To State | Trigger Event | Code-Level Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (none) | New | `pthread_create()` invoked | `pthread_create(&tid, NULL, fn, arg);` |
| 2 | New | Ready | OS admits thread to ready queue | Scheduler places TCB in queue |
| 3 | Ready | Running | Dispatcher selects the thread on a CPU | Context switch occurs |
| 4 | Running | Ready | Time quantum expires (preemptive) | Timer interrupt |
| 5 | Running | Blocked | Thread issues blocking I/O / waits on mutex | `pthread_mutex_lock(&m);` on a held lock |
| 6 | Blocked | Ready | I/O completes / mutex released | `read()` returns |
| 7 | Running | Terminated | Thread function returns / `pthread_exit()` | `return NULL;` |

## 3.2 Amdahl's Law — Full Step-by-Step Derivation (Worked Example)

This is a guaranteed KTU problem. The setup: A program has a **parallel fraction $P = 0.90$** and a **serial fraction $1 - P = 0.10$**. We run it on $N = 4$ cores. What is the speedup?

### 3.2.1 Definition of Speedup

The **speedup** $S(N)$ is the ratio of the original (single-core) execution time to the new (multi-core) execution time:

$$S(N) = \frac{T(1)}{T(N)}$$

### 3.2.2 Decompose Execution Time

Let the total original work be $T(1) = 1$ (normalized). The parallel part takes time $P$ and the serial part takes time $(1-P)$. On $N$ cores, the parallel part can be split into $N$ sub-tasks running simultaneously, so it takes $\dfrac{P}{N}$ time. The serial part still takes $(1-P)$ time.

Therefore:

$$T(N) = (1 - P) + \frac{P}{N}$$

### 3.2.3 Substitute

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$$

### 3.2.4 Plug in Numbers ($P = 0.90$, $N = 4$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(4) &= (1 - 0.90) + \frac{0.90}{4} \\
&= 0.10 + 0.225 \\
&= 0.325
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(4) &= \frac{1}{0.325} \\
&\approx 3.076
\end{aligned}
$$

So a **90% parallel** program on **4 cores** achieves a speedup of approximately **3.08×** (not 4×).

### 3.2.5 Limit Case — Infinite Cores

Take the limit as $N \to \infty$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}} \\
&= \frac{1}{(1-P) + 0} \\
&= \frac{1}{1-P}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $P = 0.90$:

$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.90} = \frac{1}{0.10} = 10$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Even with infinite cores, the program runs at most **10× faster** because 10% of the work is serial. This is the **fundamental limit of parallelization** — the foundation of Amdahl's Law.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Pthread Program Walkthrough

Consider the canonical "two threads print hello" example:

```c
#include <pthread.h>
#include <stdio.h>

void *worker(void *arg) {
    printf("Hello from thread, arg=%d\n", *(int *)arg);
    return NULL;
}

int main(void) {
    pthread_t t1, t2;
    int a = 10, b = 20;
    pthread_create(&t1, NULL, worker, &a);   // (i)   spawn thread 1
    pthread_create(&t2, NULL, worker, &b);   // (ii)  spawn thread 2
    pthread_join(t1, NULL);                  // (iii) wait thread 1
    pthread_join(t2, NULL);                  // (iv)  wait thread 2
    return 0;
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace (Analytical Matrix)

| Instruction | Thread that runs | Action | State transition |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Main | `pthread_create(&t1, ...)` | T1: New → Ready; main continues |
| (ii) | Main | `pthread_create(&t2, ...)` | T2: New → Ready; main continues |
| Scheduler picks T1 | T1 | `worker(&a)` starts | T1: Ready → Running |
| Scheduler picks T2 | T2 | `worker(&b)` starts | T2: Ready → Running |
| T1 prints & returns | T1 | `return NULL;` → `pthread_exit` | T1: Running → Terminated |
| T2 prints & returns | T2 | `return NULL;` → `pthread_exit` | T2: Running → Terminated |
| (iii) | Main | `pthread_join(t1, NULL)` | Main: Running → Blocked → Running (after T1 done) |
| (iv) | Main | `pthread_join(t2, NULL)` | Main: Running → Blocked → Running (after T2 done) |
| `return 0;` | Main | Process exit | All threads (process) → Terminated |

> [!TIP]
> Output order is **non-deterministic** — the order in which "Hello from thread" lines appear depends on the scheduler. This is a classic KTU viva question.

## 3.4 Thread Cancellation — Two Approaches

| Aspect | Asynchronous Cancellation | Deferred (Cooperative) Cancellation |
|---|---|---|
| When it occurs | Immediately, at an arbitrary point | At defined **cancellation points** (e.g., `pthread_testcancel()`) |
| Risk | Can leave shared resources in inconsistent state (no cleanup of mutexes, memory) | Safe; thread checks for cancellation at safe points |
| Default in Pthreads | Not supported by default; must explicitly request | **Default** type is `PTHREAD_CANCEL_DEFERRED` |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Signal Handling in Multithreaded Programs

| Method | Behavior | Used When |
|---|---|---|
| **Deliver signal to the thread to which it applies** | Per-thread signal mask determines reception | Synchronous signals (e.g., `SIGSEGV` from a fault) |
| **Deliver signal to every thread in the process** | Each thread runs its own signal handler | Global process-level signals (e.g., `SIGINT` from Ctrl+C) |
| **Deliver signal to certain threads only** | Application specifies recipient via `pthread_kill()` | Custom inter-thread signaling |
| **Designate a dedicated thread to receive all signals** | One thread `sigwait`s on the signal set | Server architectures |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 scheme explicitly lists **signal handling in multithreaded programs** as a topic. The matrix above is your high-yield answer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single-Threaded vs. Multithreaded Process — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SP["Single-Threaded Process"]
        SP_REG["Registers"]
        SP_PC["Program Counter"]
        SP_STACK["Stack"]
        SP_CODE["Code Section"]
        SP_DATA["Data Section"]
        SP_OS["OS Resources: Files, Sockets"]
    end
    SP_REG --- SP_PC
    SP_PC --- SP_STACK
    SP_STACK --- SP_CODE
    SP_CODE --- SP_DATA
    SP_DATA --- SP_OS

    subgraph MP["Multithreaded Process"]
        direction TB
        MP_CODE["Code Section SHARED"]
        MP_DATA["Data Section SHARED"]
        MP_OS["OS Resources SHARED"]

        subgraph T1["Thread 1"]
            T1_REG["Registers"]
            T1_PC["Program Counter"]
            T1_STACK["Stack"]
        end

        subgraph T2["Thread 2"]
            T2_REG["Registers"]
            T2_PC["Program Counter"]
            T2_STACK["Stack"]
        end

        subgraph T3["Thread 3"]
            T3_REG["Registers"]
            T3_PC["Program Counter"]
            T3_STACK["Stack"]
        end
    end

    T1 --- MP_CODE
    T2 --- MP_CODE
    T3 --- MP_CODE
    T1 --- MP_DATA
    T2 --- MP_DATA
    T3 --- MP_DATA
    T1 --- MP_OS
    T2 --- MP_OS
    T3 --- MP_OS
```

## 4.2 The Three Multithreading Models — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph U["User Space"]
        direction TB
        U1["ULT 1"]
        U2["ULT 2"]
        U3["ULT 3"]
        U4["ULT 4"]
    end

    subgraph K["Kernel Space"]
        K1["KLT 1"]
        K2["KLT 2"]
        K3["KLT 3"]
    end

    U1 -.-> K1
    U2 -.-> K1
    U3 -.-> K1
    U4 -.-> K1

    style U fill:#eef
    style K fill:#fee
```

> **Figure (a): Many-to-One Model** — 4 ULTs multiplexed onto 1 KLT. The kernel sees only one execution flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph U2["User Space"]
        direction TB
        U1["ULT 1"]
        U2["ULT 2"]
        U3["ULT 3"]
    end

    subgraph K2["Kernel Space"]
        K1["KLT 1"]
        K2["KLT 2"]
        K3["KLT 3"]
    end

    U1 --> K1
    U2 --> K2
    U3 --> K3

    style U2 fill:#eef
    style K2 fill:#fee
```

> **Figure (b): One-to-One Model** — Each ULT is bound to exactly one KLT. True parallelism possible.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph U3["User Space"]
        direction TB
        U1["ULT 1"]
        U2["ULT 2"]
        U3["ULT 3"]
        U4["ULT 4"]
        U5["ULT 5"]
    end

    subgraph K3["Kernel Space"]
        K1["KLT 1"]
        K2["KLT 2"]
    end

    U1 --> K1
    U2 --> K2
    U3 --> K1
    U4 --> K2
    U5 --> K1

    style U3 fill:#eef
    style K3 fill:#fee
```

> **Figure (c): Many-to-Many Model** — 5 ULTs scheduled onto 2 KLTs by the thread library. The library may use LWP (lightweight processes) as intermediaries.

## 4.3 Thread Lifecycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New: pthread_create
    New --> Ready: Admitted by scheduler
    Ready --> Running: Dispatched on CPU
    Running --> Ready: Time quantum expires
    Running --> Blocked: I/O wait or mutex wait
    Blocked --> Ready: I/O complete or mutex released
    Running --> Terminated: Thread function returns
    Terminated --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow: Implicit Threading with a Thread Pool

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Client submits task"] --> B{"Pool has idle thread?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Dispatch task to idle thread"]
    B -- No --> D{"Pool full?"}
    D -- No --> E["Create new worker thread"]
    D -- Yes --> F["Enqueue task in buffer"]
    F --> G["Wait for thread to free up"]
    G --> C
    E --> C
    C --> H["Worker executes task"]
    H --> I["Worker returns to pool: idle"]
```

> **Figure: Thread Pool Architecture** — A common *implicit threading* technique where a fixed (or dynamic) set of worker threads services a queue of tasks. Used in web servers, GUI frameworks, and high-performance computing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a process and a thread.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Basic unit of | Resource allocation | CPU execution / scheduling |
| Address space | Each has its own | Shared with peer threads of same process |
| Context switch cost | High (TLB/cache flush, kernel mode) | Low (mostly register + stack switch) |
| Communication | Requires IPC (pipes, sockets, shared memory) | Direct via shared globals (synchronization needed) |
| Creation overhead | Heavy (PCB allocation) | Light (TCB allocation, ~10–100× cheaper) |
| Identifier | PID (Process ID) | TID (Thread ID) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (1 mark lost):** Students often write "process is a program, thread is a part of a program." The KTU key expects the *resource-allocation vs. scheduling* distinction.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is meant by implicit threading? Mention any two techniques.**

**Model Answer:**
Implicit threading refers to the delegation of thread creation and management to **compilers, run-time libraries, or language runtimes**, rather than the application programmer explicitly creating threads.

Two common techniques:

1. **Thread Pools** — A pre-created set of worker threads services a task queue; the application submits tasks to the pool.
2. **OpenMP** — A compiler-directed API using `#pragma omp parallel` directives to parallelize loops in C/C++/Fortran.

*(Other valid answers: Grand Central Dispatch — Apple platforms; Java Concurrency Framework `ExecutorService`.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer any **ONE** of the following. Each carries 14 marks (7 + 7).

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **three multithreading models** (many-to-one, one-to-one, many-to-many) with neat diagrams. Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **many-to-many multithreading model using thread libraries**. Differentiate between **user-level threads (ULT)** and **kernel-level threads (KLT)**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Q-A(a):

**Many-to-One Model:**
- Many user-level threads are mapped to a single kernel thread.
- Thread management is done in **user space** by a thread library.
- Only one thread can access the kernel at a time.
- **Examples:** Solaris Green Threads, GNU Portable Threads.

**One-to-One Model:**
- Each user thread is mapped to a separate kernel thread.
- Provides **more concurrency**; one thread's blocking system call does not block others.
- The drawback is that creating a user thread requires creating a corresponding kernel thread — overhead.
- **Examples:** Windows NT/XP/2000, Linux, Solaris 9+.

**Many-to-Many Model:**
- Multiplexes $m$ user-level threads onto $n$ kernel-level threads (where $m \geq n$).
- The number of kernel threads may be specific to either a particular application or the machine.
- Developers can create as many user threads as necessary, while the kernel threads can run in parallel on a multiprocessor.
- **Variants include the two-level model**, where a user thread may be bound to a single kernel thread.

**Diagrams** — Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the three models.

| Model | True Parallelism on Multiprocessor? | Blocking Syscall Impact |
|---|---|---|
| Many-to-One | No | Blocks entire process |
| One-to-One | Yes | Only blocks calling thread |
| Many-to-Many | Yes | Only blocks calling thread (via LWP) |

---

### Model Answer for Q-A(b):

**Thread Libraries — Definition:**
A thread library provides the programmer with an API for creating and managing threads. There are two primary ways of implementing a thread library:

1. **Library entirely in user space** — all code and data structures for the library exist in user space. Invoking a function in the library results in a local function call in user space, not a system call.
2. **Kernel-level library** — code and data structures for the library exist in kernel space. Invoking a function in the library API results in a system call to the kernel.

**Three main thread libraries in use today:**
- **POSIX Pthreads** — may be provided as either user-level or kernel-level.
- **Win32 threads** — provided as a kernel-level library on Windows systems.
- **Java threads** — typically implemented using the threads provided by the underlying OS (Pthreads on UNIX, Win32 on Windows).

**Pthreads** refers to the **POSIX standard** (IEEE 1003.1c) defining an API for thread creation and synchronization. This is the specification for thread behavior, not an implementation. The shared-memory, multithreaded programming model is:
- A program starts with one implicit thread (the main thread).
- Additional threads are created via `pthread_create()`.
- Each thread has its own stack and shared global data.
- Threads communicate via shared variables (must be synchronized).
- Thread terminates by calling `pthread_exit()` or returning from the thread function.

**Differentiating ULT and KLT** — Refer to the table in Section 2.2 of these notes (type, management, parallelism, blocking behavior, examples).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** What are **threads**? Discuss the **benefits of multithreading** in an operating system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various **threading issues** that arise in a multithreaded program. Explain **fork(), exec() system calls** in the context of multithreaded programs. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Q-B(a):

**Definition:**
A thread is a basic unit of CPU utilization. It comprises:
- A **thread ID**
- A **program counter**
- A **register set**
- A **stack space**

It shares with other threads belonging to the same process its **code section, data section, and other OS resources** (such as open files and signals). Traditionally, a process has a single thread of control. In a multithreaded system, a single process may have multiple threads, each capable of executing a different part of the code.

**Benefits of Multithreading:**

1. **Responsiveness** — Multithreading an interactive application may allow a program to continue running even if part of it is blocked or is performing a lengthy operation, thereby increasing responsiveness to the user.
2. **Resource Sharing** — By default, threads share the memory and other resources of the process; thus, multiple threads within a process can communicate with each other without invoking the kernel.
3. **Economy** — Allocating memory and resources for process creation is costly. Because threads share the resources of the process to which they belong, it is more economical to create threads than to create a brand new process. Thread switching also has lower overhead than context switching.
4. **Scalability** — A single-threaded process can run on only one CPU, no matter how many are available. Multithreading on a multi-CPU machine increases parallelism. (Amdahl's Law quantifies the limit.)

---

### Model Answer for Q-B(b):

**Threading Issues:**

1. **`fork()` and `exec()` system calls:**
   - If one thread in a program calls `fork()`, the **entire process** is duplicated — including all threads (or sometimes only the calling thread, depending on the implementation).
   - If a thread in a multithreaded program calls `exec()`, the program image specified by the parameters of `exec()` replaces the entire process — all threads except the calling thread are terminated.
   - On systems where `fork()` duplicates only the calling thread, any pending I/O from other threads in the original process is never processed because the new process is single-threaded.

2. **Signal Handling:**
   - Signals are used in UNIX systems to notify a process that a particular event has occurred.
   - In a single-threaded program, signal delivery is straightforward. In a multithreaded environment, however, the kernel must choose which thread to deliver the signal to.
   - Options: (a) deliver to the thread to which the signal applies, (b) deliver to every thread, (c) deliver to certain threads, (d) designate a specific thread to receive all signals.

3. **Thread Cancellation:**
   - A thread cancellation terminates a thread before it has completed. Two approaches:
     - **Asynchronous cancellation**: One thread immediately terminates the target thread.
     - **Deferred cancellation**: The target thread periodically checks whether it should terminate, allowing it an opportunity to clean up before exiting.

4. **Thread-Local Storage (TLS):**
   - Just as processes can have their own data, threads can have thread-specific data.
   - TLS allows each thread to have its own copy of certain data, useful for transactions and errno-style per-thread state.

5. **Scheduler Activations:**
   - A communication mechanism between the user-thread library and the kernel. The kernel provides a set of virtual processors (LWPs) to the application; the user-thread library schedules user threads onto LWPs. Upcalls notify the library of kernel events.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Threads Module:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse `fork()`'s behavior in multithreaded programs.** Many OS textbooks (and Linux behavior) differ — clarify in your answer whether the implementation duplicates all threads or only the calling thread.
> 2. **Amdahl's Law:** If asked to compute speedup, **always normalize** $T(1) = 1$ and clearly state the **parallel fraction $P$** vs. the **serial fraction $(1-P)$**.
> 3. **Multithreading Models:** Don't just list them — for each model, mention **true parallelism on multicore** and **blocking syscall behavior** (this is how KTU examiners differentiate between average and top answers).
> 4. **Pthread function arguments:** Be precise — `pthread_create(&tid, NULL, fn, arg)` — the *second* argument is the attribute object, not the argument to the thread function.
> 5. **TLS vs. Local Variables:** Local variables of a function are per-thread **only if the function is called by one thread at a time**; otherwise they are shared. TLS uses a key-based lookup (`pthread_key_create`).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Threads Module 3**

- **Definition:** Thread = `Thread ID + PC + Registers + Stack`, sharing process resources.
- **Why threads:** Responsiveness, Resource sharing, Economy, Scalability.
- **Process vs. Thread:** Process = resource owner; Thread = scheduled execution.
- **Two types:** ULT (user-managed) vs. KLT (kernel-managed).
- **Three multithreading models:**
  - **Many-to-One** → no parallel, blocks on syscall.
  - **One-to-One** → parallel, but creation overhead.
  - **Many-to-Many** → flexible, true parallelism.
- **Thread libraries:** Pthreads (POSIX), Win32, Java.
- **Thread states:** New → Ready → Running → Blocked → Terminated.
- **Threading issues:** `fork()`/`exec()`, signal handling, cancellation (async vs. deferred), TLS, scheduler activations.
- **Implicit threading:** Thread pools, OpenMP, `fork-join` parallelism.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$; max speedup at $N \to \infty$ is $\dfrac{1}{1-P}$.
- **Pthread essentials:** `pthread_create`, `pthread_join`, `pthread_exit`, `pthread_mutex_lock`, `pthread_cond_wait`.
- **Real-world uses:** Web servers, databases, GUI apps, compilers, embedded/real-time systems.

> **End of Module 3 – Threads Notes (PERUT638)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPU Scheduling** is the fundamental Operating System mechanism of selecting a process from the **Ready Queue** and allocating the CPU to it for execution. Since a uniprocessor system can execute only one process per CPU core at any instant, the OS scheduler (specifically the **Short-Term Scheduler / CPU Scheduler**) must multiplex the CPU among multiple competing processes to optimize system performance.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, scheduling is a core topic in Module 3 of Operating Systems. The scheduler is invoked whenever the CPU becomes idle or a process transitions from running to waiting/terminated state.

**Types of Schedulers (The Three-Tier Hierarchy):**

| Scheduler Type | Also Known As | Frequency of Invocation | KTU Weightage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Long-Term Scheduler** | Job / Admission Scheduler | Very low (seconds to minutes) | Low |
| **Short-Term Scheduler** | CPU Scheduler | Very high (every **10–100 ms**) | **High (Frequently Asked)** |
| **Medium-Term Scheduler** | Swapper | Moderate (for degree of multiprogramming control) | Moderate |

### 1.2 Real-World Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bank Teller Analogy:** Imagine a single bank teller (CPU) and 50 customers (processes) in the queue. The bank manager (OS) must decide *who goes next*. If the manager always picks the first person (FCFS), a customer withdrawing cash will hold up the entire queue. If the manager always picks the shortest task (SJF), throughput rises dramatically. If the manager assigns time-slots in rotation (Round Robin), every customer feels attended to fairly. This decision-making logic is **CPU Scheduling**.

### 1.3 The "Why" of Scheduling — The Core Motivation

The fundamental problem scheduling solves is the **Burst Nature of Process Execution**:
- Processes alternate between **CPU Bursts** (active computation) and **I/O Bursts** (waiting for disk/network).
- When one process is in I/O burst, the CPU is **idle** — a colossal waste of the **crores-of-rupees hardware**.
- Scheduling minimizes this idle time, thereby maximizing **CPU Utilization**, **Throughput**, and **Fairness**.

### 1.4 Preemptive vs Non-Preemptive Scheduling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preemptive Scheduling:** The OS can forcibly take (preempt) the CPU away from a running process. Examples: **Round Robin, SRTF, Priority (Preemptive).**
> 
> **Non-Preemptive Scheduling:** Once a process is allocated the CPU, it runs to completion of its CPU burst. Examples: **FCFS, SJF (Non-Preemptive), Priority (Non-Preemptive).**

### 1.5 Performance Metrics (KTU High-Priority Terminology)
