- **CPU Utilization:** Keep the CPU as busy as possible (**40%–90%** in real systems).
- **Throughput:** Number of processes completed per unit time.
- **Turnaround Time (TAT):** Time from process arrival to completion.
- **Waiting Time (WT):** Total time spent in the ready queue.
- **Response Time (RT):** Time from arrival to first CPU allocation.
- **Fairness:** Every process gets a fair share of the CPU.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Scheduling Criteria (The 5 Pillars of Evaluation)

The KTU 2024 examiner expects every scheduling algorithm to be evaluated against these five criteria:

1. **CPU Utilization** — Maximize
2. **Throughput** — Maximize
3. **Turnaround Time** — Minimize
4. **Waiting Time** — Minimize
5. **Response Time** — Minimize (critical for interactive systems)

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this table for **all numerical Gantt chart problems** in KTU exams. The vertical bar symbol is written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Metric | Formula | Units | Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$ | ms / seconds | $TAT_i \geq BT_i$ always |
| **Waiting Time (WT)** | $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$ | ms / seconds | Can be zero (if no contention) |
| **Response Time (RT)** | $RT_i = \text{First CPU Allocation} - AT_i$ | ms / seconds | $RT_i \geq 0$ |
| **Average Waiting Time** | $\overline{WT} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i$ | ms / seconds | Lower is better |
| **Average Turnaround Time** | $\overline{TAT} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} TAT_i$ | ms / seconds | Lower is better |
| **Throughput** | $Throughput = \frac{n}{T_{total}}$ | processes / unit time | Higher is better |
| **CPU Utilization (%)** | $CPU_{util} = \left(\frac{T_{busy}}{T_{total}}\right) \times 100$ | Percentage | Target: **40% – 90%** |
| **Convoy Effect (FCFS)** | Slow process blocks all behind it | Qualitative | FCFS weakness |
| **Starvation Risk** | Low priority process waits forever | Qualitative | SJF / Priority weakness |

Where:
- $AT_i$ = Arrival Time of process $i$
- $BT_i$ = Burst Time of process $i$
- $CT_i$ = Completion Time of process $i$
- $n$ = Total number of processes

### 2.3 Deep Dive into Each Scheduling Algorithm

#### 2.3.1 First-Come, First-Served (FCFS)
- **Logic:** Processes are executed in the order of their arrival. Implemented via a simple **FIFO queue**.
- **Pros:** Simplest algorithm, fair in the sense of "first come."
- **Cons:** Suffers from the **Convoy Effect** — one long process blocks all small processes behind it. Non-preemptive.
- **KTU Reality:** Easy to compute but rarely optimal; frequently appears in Part A (3-mark) questions.

#### 2.3.2 Shortest Job First (SJF) — Non-Preemptive
- **Logic:** When CPU is free, schedule the process with the **smallest burst time** among arrived processes.
- **Pros:** Provably **optimal** for minimizing average waiting time (non-preemptive case).
- **Cons:** Requires knowledge of future burst time (impossible in practice — estimated via exponential averaging). Suffers from **Starvation** of long processes.

#### 2.3.3 Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF) — Preemptive SJF
- **Logic:** If a new process arrives with a burst time **shorter** than the remaining time of the current process, preempt the current one.
- **Pros:** Optimal for minimizing average TAT (preemptive case).
- **Cons:** Starvation + expensive context switches.

#### 2.3.4 Priority Scheduling
- **Logic:** Each process is assigned a priority. The CPU is allocated to the process with the **highest priority**.
- **Convention:** Lower number = Higher priority (KTU standard) OR Higher number = Higher priority (always verify in problem).
- **Pros:** Flexibility to favor critical processes.
- **Cons:** **Starvation** of low-priority processes (solved via **Aging** — gradually increasing priority of waiting processes).

#### 2.3.5 Round Robin (RR) — The Time-Sharing Champion
- **Logic:** Each process gets a small unit of CPU time called a **Time Quantum (Q)**. After Q expires, the process is preempted and added to the end of the ready queue.
- **Pros:** Excellent **Response Time**, fair, no starvation.
- **Cons:** Performance heavily depends on quantum size:
  - If $Q \to \infty$: behaves like FCFS.
  - If $Q \to 0$: behaves like Processor Sharing (theoretical).
  - **Optimal Rule of Thumb:** $Q$ should be slightly greater than the time required to handle a typical **I/O burst** (typically **10–100 ms**).
- **KTU Hot Topic:** Always asked with Gantt chart and average WT/TAT computation.

#### 2.3.6 Multilevel Queue (MLQ) & Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ)
- **MLQ:** Ready queue is partitioned into multiple sub-queues (e.g., System, Interactive, Batch) each with its own scheduling algorithm.
- **MLFQ:** Processes can **move between queues** based on observed behavior. A process that uses too much CPU is demoted to a lower-priority queue; a process that waits too long is promoted (built-in **Aging**).
- **MLFQ is the algorithm used in modern OS like Linux (CFS) and Windows.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — The Master Gantt Chart Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Problem (Frequently Appears in KTU ESE):** Consider the following set of processes. Compute the **Average Waiting Time** and **Average Turnaround Time** for **FCFS, SJF (Non-Preemptive), SRTF, Priority (Non-Preemptive), and Round Robin (Q=4)**.

**Process Table:**

| Process | Arrival Time (AT) | Burst Time (BT) | Priority |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **P1** | 0 | 7 | 3 |
| **P2** | 1 | 4 | 1 |
| **P3** | 2 | 9 | 4 |
| **P4** | 3 | 5 | 2 |

*Convention: Lower priority number = Higher priority.*

---

### 3.2 Algorithm 1 — First-Come, First-Served (FCFS)

**Step 1 — Build the Gantt Chart (in order of arrival):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Gantt_{FCFS} &= \vert P1_{(0-7)} \vert P2_{(7-11)} \vert P3_{(11-20)} \vert P4_{(20-25)} \vert
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Completion Times (CT):**

| Process | CT (from Gantt) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **P1** | 7 |
| **P2** | 11 |
| **P3** | 20 |
| **P4** | 25 |

**Step 3 — Apply Formulas for TAT and WT:**

$$\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= CT_1 - AT_1 = 7 - 0 = 7 \\
TAT_{P2} &= 11 - 1 = 10 \\
TAT_{P3} &= 20 - 2 = 18 \\
TAT_{P4} &= 25 - 3 = 22
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
WT_{P1} &= TAT_{P1} - BT_1 = 7 - 7 = 0 \\
WT_{P2} &= 10 - 4 = 6 \\
WT_{P3} &= 18 - 9 = 9 \\
WT_{P4} &= 22 - 5 = 17
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Averages:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{TAT}_{FCFS} &= \frac{7 + 10 + 18 + 22}{4} = \frac{57}{4} = 14.25 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{WT}_{FCFS} &= \frac{0 + 6 + 9 + 17}{4} = \frac{32}{4} = 8.00 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Convoy Effect Observed:** P4 (short 5-unit job) waited 17 units because P1 (long 7-unit job) went first. This is a classic KTU pitfall to point out in the answer.

---

### 3.3 Algorithm 2 — Shortest Job First (SJF Non-Preemptive)

**Step 1 — Decision Points and Selection Logic:**

- At $t=0$: Only P1 has arrived. Select **P1** (mandatory).
- At $t=7$ (P1 done): Arrived processes = {P2(4), P3(9), P4(5)}. Select **P2** (BT=4 minimum).
- At $t=11$ (P2 done): Arrived = {P3(9), P4(5)}. Select **P4** (BT=5 minimum).
- At $t=16$ (P4 done): Only P3 remains. Select **P3**.

**Step 2 — Build the Gantt Chart:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Gantt_{SJF} &= \vert P1_{(0-7)} \vert P2_{(7-11)} \vert P4_{(11-16)} \vert P3_{(16-25)} \vert
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — CT Table:**

| Process | CT |
| :--- | :--- |
| P1 | 7 |
| P2 | 11 |
| P4 | 16 |
| P3 | 25 |

**Step 4 — Compute Metrics:**

$$\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 7, \quad TAT_{P2} = 10, \quad TAT_{P3} = 23, \quad TAT_{P4} = 13 \\
WT_{P1} &= 0, \quad WT_{P2} = 6, \quad WT_{P3} = 14, \quad WT_{P4} = 8
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{TAT}_{SJF} &= \frac{7 + 10 + 23 + 13}{4} = \frac{53}{4} = 13.25 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{WT}_{SJF} &= \frac{0 + 6 + 14 + 8}{4} = \frac{28}{4} = 7.00 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Observation:** SJF improved average WT by **12.5%** over FCFS. This is why SJF is theoretically optimal.

---

### 3.4 Algorithm 3 — Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF)

**Step 1 — Simulate with Preemption Check at Every Arrival:**

- $t=0$: P1 starts. Remaining: P1=7, P2=Not yet, P3=Not yet, P4=Not yet.
- $t=1$: **P2 arrives (BT=4)**. Compare with P1's remaining (7). 4 < 7, so **preempt P1**. P2 starts.
- $t=2$: **P3 arrives (BT=9)**. P2's remaining=3. 3 < 9, continue P2.
- $t=3$: **P4 arrives (BT=5)**. P2's remaining=2. 2 < 5, continue P2.
- $t=5$: P2 finishes. Compare: P1 remaining=6, P3=9, P4=5. Select **P4** (minimum).
- $t=10$: P4 finishes. Remaining: P1=6, P3=9. Select **P1**.
- $t=16$: P1 finishes. Remaining: P3=9. Select **P3**.
- $t=25$: P3 finishes.

**Step 2 — Build the Gantt Chart:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Gantt_{SRTF} &= \vert P1_{(0-1)} \vert P2_{(1-5)} \vert P4_{(5-10)} \vert P1_{(10-16)} \vert P3_{(16-25)} \vert
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — CT Table:**

| Process | CT |
| :--- | :--- |
| P1 | 16 |
| P2 | 5 |
| P3 | 25 |
| P4 | 10 |

**Step 4 — Compute Metrics:**

$$\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 16, \quad TAT_{P2} = 4, \quad TAT_{P3} = 23, \quad TAT_{P4} = 7 \\
WT_{P1} &= 9, \quad WT_{P2} = 0, \quad WT_{P3} = 14, \quad WT_{P4} = 2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{TAT}_{SRTF} &= \frac{16 + 4 + 23 + 7}{4} = \frac{50}{4} = 12.50 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{WT}_{SRTF} &= \frac{9 + 0 + 14 + 2}{4} = \frac{25}{4} = 6.25 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.5 Algorithm 4 — Priority Scheduling (Non-Preemptive)

*Priority Table (Lower number = Higher priority):* P1=3, P2=1, P3=4, P4=2

**Step 1 — Decision at Each Free CPU Moment:**

- $t=0$: Only P1 available. **Select P1** (forced).
- $t=7$: Available = {P2(1), P3(4), P4(2)}. Highest priority = **P2** (priority 1).
- $t=11$: Available = {P3(4), P4(2)}. Highest priority = **P4** (priority 2).
- $t=16$: Remaining = {P3}. Select **P3**.

**Step 2 — Gantt Chart:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Gantt_{Priority} &= \vert P1_{(0-7)} \vert P2_{(7-11)} \vert P4_{(11-16)} \vert P3_{(16-25)} \vert
\end{aligned}$$

*Note:* For this specific data set, Priority gives the same Gantt chart as SJF. This is coincidental and not a general rule.

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{TAT}_{Priority} &= 13.25 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{WT}_{Priority} &= 7.00 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.6 Algorithm 5 — Round Robin (Q = 4 ms)

**Step 1 — Maintain a Circular Ready Queue:**

- $t=0$ to $t=4$: P1 executes (4 ms of its 7 ms burst). P1 remaining = 3. Ready queue now contains arrivals in this window: P2(at t=1), P3(at t=2), P4(at t=3). Queue: [P2, P3, P4, P1] (P1 appended after its 4 ms).
- $t=4$ to $t=8$: P2 executes (completes its 4 ms burst). Queue: [P3, P4, P1].
- $t=8$ to $t=12$: P3 executes (4 ms of its 9 ms burst). P3 remaining = 5. Queue: [P4, P1, P3].
- $t=12$ to $t=15$: P4 executes (4 ms of its 5 ms burst). P4 remaining = 1. Queue: [P1, P3, P4].
- $t=15$ to $t=18$: P1 executes (completes its remaining 3 ms). Queue: [P3, P4].
- $t=18$ to $t=22$: P3 executes (4 ms of its 5 ms remaining). P3 remaining = 1. Queue: [P4, P3].
- $t=22$ to $t=23$: P4 executes (completes its 1 ms). Queue: [P3].
- $t=23$ to $t=24$: P3 executes (completes its 1 ms). Done!

**Step 2 — Build the Gantt Chart:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Gantt_{RR} &= \vert P1_{(0-4)} \vert P2_{(4-8)} \vert P3_{(8-12)} \vert P4_{(12-16)} \vert P1_{(16-19)} \vert \\
&\quad \vert P3_{(19-23)} \vert P4_{(23-24)} \vert P3_{(24-25)} \vert
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — CT Table:**

| Process | CT |
| :--- | :--- |
| P1 | 19 |
| P2 | 8 |
| P3 | 25 |
| P4 | 24 |

**Step 4 — Compute Metrics:**

$$\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 19, \quad TAT_{P2} = 7, \quad TAT_{P3} = 23, \quad TAT_{P4} = 21 \\
WT_{P1} &= 12, \quad WT_{P2} = 3, \quad WT_{P3} = 14, \quad WT_{P4} = 16
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{TAT}_{RR} &= \frac{19 + 7 + 23 + 21}{4} = \frac{70}{4} = 17.50 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{WT}_{RR} &= \frac{12 + 3 + 14 + 16}{4} = \frac{45}{4} = 11.25 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Response Time (Unique to RR):**

$$\begin{aligned}
RT_{P1} &= 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ ms (first served)} \\
RT_{P2} &= 4 - 1 = 3 \text{ ms} \\
RT_{P3} &= 8 - 2 = 6 \text{ ms} \\
RT_{P4} &= 12 - 3 = 9 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.7 Master Comparative Matrix (KTU Gold Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparative table is the **highest-yield content** for KTU 14-mark questions. Memorize the relationships below.

| Algorithm | Preemptive? | $\overline{WT}$ (this case) | $\overline{TAT}$ (this case) | Starvation? | Convoy? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **FCFS** | No | 8.00 ms | 14.25 ms | No | **Yes (High)** |
| **SJF** | No | 7.00 ms | 13.25 ms | **Yes** | No |
| **SRTF** | Yes | 6.25 ms | 12.50 ms | **Yes** | No |
| **Priority** | No | 7.00 ms | 13.25 ms | **Yes** | No |
| **Round Robin (Q=4)** | Yes (forced) | 11.25 ms | 17.50 ms | No | No |

**Verdict for this dataset:** **SRTF is the best** in terms of average WT and TAT, as expected from theory.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1 — Process States and Scheduling Trigger Points

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid state diagram shows exactly *when* the Short-Term Scheduler is invoked, and how process states transition during scheduling decisions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New : Process Created
    New --> Ready : Admitted by Long-Term Scheduler
    Ready --> Running : Short-Term Scheduler dispatches process
    Running --> Ready : Preemption / Time Quantum expiry / Interrupt
    Running --> Waiting : I/O request or Event Wait
    Waiting --> Ready : I/O Completion or Event Signal
    Running --> Terminated : Process Exit (Normal / Error)
    Ready --> [*] : Swapped out by Medium-Term Scheduler
    Terminated --> [*] : Resources released to OS
```

**Reading Guide:**
- **Long-Term Scheduler** controls the *New → Ready* transition (admission control).
- **Short-Term Scheduler** controls the *Ready → Running* transition (this is **CPU Scheduling**).
- **Medium-Term Scheduler** swaps processes in/out of memory (degree of multiprogramming control).

---

### 4.2 Figure 2 — Hierarchical Taxonomy of CPU Scheduling Algorithms

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CPU Scheduling Algorithms] --> B[Non-Preemptive]
    A --> C[Preemptive]
    B --> B1[FCFS]
    B --> B2[SJF Non-Preemptive]
    B --> B3[Priority Non-Preemptive]
    C --> C1[SRTF]
    C --> C2[Priority Preemptive]
    C --> C3[Round Robin]
    C --> C4[Multilevel Feedback Queue]
    A --> D[Hybrid Approaches]
    D --> D1[Multilevel Queue with Feedback]
    D --> D2[Linux CFS Completely Fair Scheduler]
    D --> D3[Windows Priority based Scheduler]
```

**Reading Guide:** Note the division between *Preemptive* and *Non-Preemptive*. The right side (modern hybrid) is what real-world operating systems use.

---

### 4.3 Figure 3 — Round Robin Time Quantum Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[CPU Scheduler invoked] --> Check{Is Ready Queue empty?}
    Check -- Yes --> Idle[CPU remains Idle]
    Check -- No --> Pick[Pick process at FRONT of Ready Queue]
    Pick --> Run[Allocate CPU for Q ms or until burst completes]
    Run --> Done{Burst completed?}
    Done -- Yes --> Remove[Remove from queue and Terminate]
    Done -- No --> ReQueue[Append process to REAR of Ready Queue]
    Remove --> Start
    ReQueue --> Start
    Idle --> Wait[I/O or new process arrival]
    Wait --> Start
```

**Reading Guide:** This is the complete decision loop for Round Robin. Notice the **circular ready queue** — this is the defining feature of RR.

---

### 4.4 Figure 4 — Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ) Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q0[Queue 0: Highest Priority, Q = 8 ms] --> Q1[Queue 1: Medium Priority, Q = 16 ms]
    Q1 --> Q2[Queue 2: Lowest Priority, FCFS]
    NewArr[New Process] --> Q0
    Q0 -- Quantum expired --> Q1
    Q1 -- Quantum expired --> Q2
    Q0 -- Burst completed --> Term[Process Terminated]
    Q1 -- Burst completed --> Term
    Q2 -- Burst completed --> Term
    Q0 -- I/O Wait --> Q0
    Q1 -- I/O Wait --> Q0
    Q2 -- Aging Promotion --> Q1
```

**Reading Guide:** This is the MLFQ algorithm used in classic Unix systems. A CPU-bound process (long bursts) gets demoted, while an I/O-bound process (short bursts) stays in the highest-priority queue, achieving the best of both worlds.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define CPU Scheduling. List any two differences between Preemptive and Non-Preemptive scheduling.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** CPU Scheduling is the process of selecting a process from the ready queue and allocating the CPU to it for execution, performed by the Short-Term Scheduler.
- **Difference 1 (1 Mark):** In Preemptive scheduling, the OS can forcibly take the CPU from a running process (e.g., Round Robin). In Non-Preemptive scheduling, once a process is allocated the CPU, it runs until completion of its CPU burst or voluntary I/O wait.
- **Difference 2 (1 Mark):** Preemptive scheduling incurs **overhead due to context switching**; Non-Preemptive has no such overhead but suffers from poor response time for short jobs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the Convoy Effect? In which scheduling algorithm does it occur and how can it be solved?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Convoy Effect (1 Mark):** The phenomenon where a single long CPU-bound process holding the CPU forces all other short processes behind it to wait, leading to low CPU and device utilization.
- **Algorithm (1 Mark):** Occurs in **FCFS (First-Come, First-Served)** scheduling.
- **Solution (1 Mark):** Solved by adopting **Preemptive algorithms like SJF or Round Robin**, where the long process can be preempted in favor of short jobs.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions of 14 marks have **internal choice**: a student answers **EITHER Question A OR Question B**. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]*

**(a) Consider the following processes with their Arrival Time (AT) and Burst Time (BT). Draw the Gantt Chart and calculate the Average Waiting Time and Average Turnaround Time using:**
**(i) FCFS scheduling (3 Marks)**
**(ii) SJF (Non-Preemptive) scheduling (4 Marks)**

| Process | AT | BT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| P1 | 0 | 5 |
| P2 | 1 | 3 |
| P3 | 2 | 8 |
| P4 | 3 | 6 |

**(i) FCFS Solution (3 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Gantt_{FCFS} &= \vert P1_{(0-5)} \vert P2_{(5-8)} \vert P3_{(8-16)} \vert P4_{(16-22)} \vert
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
CT &= [5, 8, 16, 22] \\
TAT &= CT - AT = [5, 7, 14, 19] \\
WT &= TAT - BT = [0, 4, 6, 13]
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{TAT}_{FCFS} &= \frac{5+7+14+19}{4} = 11.25 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{WT}_{FCFS} &= \frac{0+4+6+13}{4} = 5.75 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) SJF Non-Preemptive Solution (4 Marks):**

- $t=0$: P1 starts.
- $t=5$: Arrived = {P2(3), P3(8), P4(6)}. Select P2.
- $t=8$: Arrived = {P3(8), P4(6)}. Select P4.
- $t=14$: Remaining = P3.

$$\begin{aligned}
Gantt_{SJF} &= \vert P1_{(0-5)} \vert P2_{(5-8)} \vert P4_{(8-14)} \vert P3_{(14-22)} \vert
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
CT &= [5, 8, 22, 14] \\
TAT &= [5, 7, 20, 11] \\
WT &= [0, 4, 12, 5]
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{TAT}_{SJF} &= \frac{5+7+20+11}{4} = 10.75 \text{ ms} \\
\overline{WT}_{SJF} &= \frac{0+4+12+5}{4} = 5.25 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Explain the Round Robin scheduling algorithm with a suitable example. Discuss the effect of Time Quantum size on performance. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- **Definition (2 Marks):** Round Robin is a preemptive scheduling algorithm designed for time-sharing systems. Each process is assigned a fixed time unit called the **Time Quantum (Q)**. Processes are kept in a circular ready queue. When the quantum expires, the process is preempted and moved to the rear of the queue.

- **Example (3 Marks):** For 3 processes P1(AT=0, BT=5), P2(AT=1, BT=4), P3(AT=2, BT=2) with Q=3:

$$\begin{aligned}
Gantt_{RR} &= \vert P1_{(0-3)} \vert P2_{(3-6)} \vert P3_{(6-8)} \vert P1_{(8-10)} \vert P2_{(10-11)} \vert
\end{aligned}$$

CT = [10, 11, 8]; TAT = [10, 10, 6]; WT = [5, 6, 4].

- **Effect of Time Quantum (2 Marks):**
  - If $Q$ is **very large**, RR degenerates into **FCFS**.
  - If $Q$ is **very small**, RR degenerates into **Processor Sharing** (too many context switches, thrashing).
  - **Optimal Rule:** $Q$ should be slightly greater than the time of a typical **I/O burst**, typically **10–100 ms** in real systems.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern — Alternative Choice]*

**(a) Explain Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ) scheduling with a neat diagram. What problem does it solve compared to standard Multilevel Queue? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- **Standard MLQ Limitation (2 Marks):** In a standard Multilevel Queue, processes are **permanently assigned** to a queue based on their type (System, Interactive, Batch) at admission. A process that becomes interactive later cannot move to the interactive queue. This leads to **inflexibility** and **starvation**.

- **MLFQ Definition (3 Marks):** Multilevel Feedback Queue allows processes to **move between queues** based on their observed CPU and I/O behavior. A process that uses too much CPU is **demoted** to a lower-priority queue. A process that waits too long or issues many I/O requests stays in (or is **promoted** to) a higher-priority queue.

- **Diagram (1 Mark):** Refer to Figure 4 in Section 4 of these notes.

- **Problem Solved (1 Mark):** Eliminates the need for prior knowledge of process behavior (unlike SJF) and **prevents starvation** via the **Aging** mechanism built into promotion.

**(b) Consider the following processes and compute Average Waiting Time using SRTF (Preemptive SJF) scheduling. Also identify any case of starvation. (7 Marks)**

| Process | AT | BT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| P1 | 0 | 8 |
| P2 | 1 | 4 |
| P3 | 2 | 9 |
| P4 | 3 | 5 |

**Solution:**

- $t=0$: P1 starts (only arrival).
- $t=1$: P2 arrives (BT=4). P1 remaining=7. 4 < 7 → preempt P1. P2 starts.
- $t=2$: P3 arrives (BT=9). P2 remaining=3. 3 < 9 → continue P2.
- $t=3$: P4 arrives (BT=5). P2 remaining=2. 2 < 5 → continue P2.
- $t=5$: P2 done. Remaining: P1=7, P3=9, P4=5. Min = **P4**. P4 starts.
- $t=10$: P4 done. Remaining: P1=7, P3=9. Min = **P1**. P1 starts.
- $t=17$: P1 done. Remaining: P3=9. P3 starts.
- $t=26$: P3 done.

$$\begin{aligned}
Gantt_{SRTF} &= \vert P1_{(0-1)} \vert P2_{(1-5)} \vert P4_{(5-10)} \vert P1_{(10-17)} \vert P3_{(17-26)} \vert
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
CT &= [17, 5, 26, 10] \\
TAT &= CT - AT = [17, 4, 24, 7] \\
WT &= TAT - BT = [9, 0, 15, 2]
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{WT}_{SRTF} &= \frac{9 + 0 + 15 + 2}{4} = \frac{26}{4} = 6.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Starvation Case (1 Mark):** If a continuous stream of short processes (BT=1) keeps arriving, P3 (longest process) may **never get the CPU** — classic SRTF starvation.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Scheduling Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Wrong Turnaround Time Formula:** Students often write $TAT = BT + WT$ (true but circular). The examiner expects the foundational formula $TAT = CT - AT$ to be explicitly stated and applied. **Deduction: 1 mark** if formula is missing.
> 
> 2. **Missing Gantt Chart:** A Gantt chart is **mandatory** for any scheduling question. A solution without a Gantt chart loses **2–3 marks** outright.
> 
> 3. **Priority Convention Errors:** In KTU problems, "Priority 1 = highest" in 60% of cases, but "Priority 1 = lowest" in 40%. **Always re-read the problem statement** for the convention. Wrong convention = wrong Gantt = **full marks lost on sub-part**.
> 
> 4. **Round Robin Arithmetic Mistakes:** Forgetting to re-queue a process that completed *before* its quantum expires is a common error. If P2 has BT=2 and Q=4, then P2 finishes at the end of its quantum *but must NOT be re-queued*. Mistake here = **2 marks lost**.
> 
> 5. **Confusing SJF and SRTF:** In SJF (non-preemptive), once a process starts, it is NOT preempted by newly arriving short processes. In SRTF, it IS preempted. Wrong choice = entire Gantt chart wrong.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final-Day Revision — Master List for Scheduling Module:**

- **Three Schedulers:** Long-Term (admission), Short-Term (CPU), Medium-Term (swapping).
- **Two Scheduling Modes:** Preemptive (OS can force context switch) and Non-Preemptive (process holds CPU until I/O or completion).
- **Five Core Metrics:** CPU Utilization (↑), Throughput (↑), TAT (↓), WT (↓), RT (↓).
- **TAT Master Formula:** $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$.
- **WT Master Formula:** $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$.
- **FCFS:** Simple but suffers from **Convoy Effect**. Implemented via FIFO.
- **SJF (Non-Preemptive):** **Provably optimal** for average WT. Suffers **Starvation**.
- **SRTF (Preemptive SJF):** Optimal for average TAT in preemptive domain. Suffers Starvation + Context Switch overhead.
- **Priority Scheduling:** Two conventions (low number = high OR high number = high). Solved starvation via **Aging**.
- **Round Robin:** Designed for time-sharing. Quantum $Q \approx$ **10–100 ms** is ideal. Best for **Response Time**.
- **Multilevel Queue (MLQ):** Static partition, no process migration. Each queue has its own algorithm.
- **Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ):** Dynamic migration. Demotes CPU-bound, promotes I/O-bound. **Aging** built-in. Used in classic Unix and modern OS.
- **Convoy Effect = FCFS only.**
- **Starvation = SJF, SRTF, Priority only** (solved by Aging/MLFQ).
- **Optimal Quantum Rule:** $Q$ slightly greater than typical I/O burst time.
- **Linux CFS and Windows NT scheduler** are practical implementations of advanced MLFQ variants.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inter-Process Communication - Shared Memory Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Inter-Process Communication - Shared Memory Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Inter-Process Communication (IPC) — Formal Definition **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)** is a set of operating system mechanisms that allow independent processes to exchange data, share resources, and synchronize their execution. IPC is the kernel-supported mechanism for **cooperating processes** — processes that either explicitly affect each other's execution or work jointly on a shared task. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** taxonomy, IPC is grouped into two broad paradigms: | IPC Paradigm | Communication Style | Data Flow | |---|---|---| | **Shared Memory** | Direct memory sharing | Bidirectional, high-speed | | **Message Passing** | Kernel-mediated exchange | Typically unidirectional or RPC-style | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Definition (verbatim from syllabus):** "Shared Memory is an IPC mechanism in which two or more processes map a common region of physical memory into their respective virtual address spaces, enabling read/write access without kernel intervention on every access." ### 1.2 Shared Memory Systems — Intuitive Analogy Imagine two colleagues working in the same open-plan office: - **Colleague A (Process 1)** writes a project status on a **shared whiteboard**. - **Colleague B (Process 2)** reads the whiteboard at any time and updates it as needed. - The whiteboard is a **common physical resource** in the room (a **shared region of memory**). - They don't need to *pass a message* — they simply look at the same surface. The **kernel** acts like the **office manager** who initially *mounts the whiteboard* (creates and maps the shared segment). Once mounted, the colleagues interact with the board **directly**, without asking the manager for every read/write. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** In shared memory IPC, the kernel is involved **only at setup time** (to map the segment) and **at teardown time** (to unmap/remove the segment). All *during-execution* accesses bypass the kernel — this is what makes it the **fastest IPC mechanism**. ### 1.3 Why Shared Memory? — Motivation Processes run in **isolated virtual address spaces** (a fundamental OS protection guarantee). Without IPC, no process can directly access another process's memory. Shared memory provides a **controlled, mutually agreed exception** to this rule, allowing: - **Data-intensive** exchange (e.g., multimedia pipelines, databases). - **Fine-grained** communication at memory-write speeds. - **Lock-free** synchronization (when combined with atomic primitives). > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric (POSIX):** Typical shared memory segment sizes range from a few **bytes** to **`SHMMNI`** segments system-wide, with each segment often limited to **`SHMMAX`** (commonly 32 MiB on Linux by default). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Shared Memory Region A shared memory segment is established through a **three-phase lifecycle**: **Phase 1 — Creation (Kernel Allocates)** - One process (or a server) requests the OS to create a shared memory object. - The kernel reserves a **region of physical RAM** and returns a *handle/identifier*. **Phase 2 — Attachment (Process Maps)** - Each cooperating process invokes an attach system call, which maps the physical region into its **own virtual address space**. - The OS uses **page tables** to remap the same physical frames under different virtual addresses in each process. - After this, the processes can read/write the segment using ordinary pointer dereferences. **Phase 3 — Detachment & Destruction** - When done, each process detaches (unmaps) the segment. - The creator (or last user) explicitly removes it; otherwise, it persists in `/dev/shm` until system shutdown. ### 2.2 The Bounded-Buffer (Producer–Consumer) Problem The classical KTU problem framed on top of shared memory is the **Bounded-Buffer Problem**: - Two processes share a buffer of fixed size **N** in shared memory. - **Producer** writes items into the buffer (must wait if full). - **Consumer** reads items from the buffer (must wait if empty). - At any instant, only one process may modify a given slot. This forces students to combine **shared memory** (for the buffer) with **synchronization primitives** like semaphores or mutexes (to prevent race conditions). ### 2.3 Shared Memory API — POSIX View The KTU 2024 module typically teaches **System V** and/or **POSIX** shared memory. The POSIX API is cleaner: 1. `shm_open()` — create/open named segment → returns a file descriptor. 2. `ftruncate()` — set the segment size. 3. `mmap()` — map the segment into the virtual address space → returns a `void*` pointer. 4. *(use the pointer directly like any array)* 5. `munmap()` — unmap. 6. `shm_unlink()` — remove the segment. > [!TIP] > The `mmap()` call is the same one used for memory-mapped files — a key KTU point: the **MMU hardware** and **page-fault handler** are what actually implement the sharing transparently. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Parameter Sheet | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | **N** | Buffer capacity (slots) | Integer $\geq 1$ | Bounded buffer size | | **in** | Producer index (next write slot) | $0 \leq \text{in} < N$ | Wraps modulo N | | **out** | Consumer index (next read slot) | $0 \leq \text{out} < N$ | Wraps modulo N | | **count** | Number of items currently in buffer | $0 \leq \text{count} \leq N$ | Used in monitors | | **empty** | Empty-slot semaphore | Init = N | Signals free space | | **full** | Filled-slot semaphore | Init = 0 | Signals available items | | **mutex** | Mutual-exclusion lock | Init = 1 | Protects buffer indices | | **K** | Total items produced = consumed | Integer | For invariant checks | | **S** | Sum of items remaining after K ops | $\text{S} = \text{(produced)} - \text{(consumed)}$ | Invariant of system | ### 2.5 Real-World Utility Shared memory IPC is the **backbone of high-performance computing** and appears in: - **Database engines** (e.g., Oracle SGA, PostgreSQL shared buffers). - **GUI toolkits** (X11 server–client shared pixmaps). - **Multimedia pipelines** (GStreamer, FFmpeg frame buffers between decoder and renderer). - **Operating system kernels themselves** (kernel memory mapped into user space via `/dev/mem` carefully). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why it is fastest:** All inter-process data transfer occurs at **memory bus speed** (~tens of GB/s on modern hardware) rather than crossing kernel–user boundaries via `read()`/`write()` syscalls (which incur context-switch overhead, typically **1–10 μs per syscall**). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Index Wrap-Around in a Circular Bounded Buffer The producer and consumer use **modular arithmetic** so the buffer behaves as a ring. The KTU 2024 evaluation expects you to derive the wrap condition explicitly. We want a buffer of **N** slots indexed $0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$. After writing to slot $N-1$, the producer must return to slot $0$. **Step 1 — Express the next index naively.** If `in` currently points to slot $i$, the next write position is $i + 1$. **Step 2 — Apply the modular constraint.** We require the result to be in $\{0, 1, \ldots, N-1\}$. The standard operation is the **modulo**: $$ \text{next\_in} \;=\; (i + 1) \;\bmod\; N $$ **Step 3 — Verify the boundary cases.** - If $i = N - 1$ (last slot): $$ \text{next\_in} = (N - 1 + 1) \bmod N = N \bmod N = 0 \quad \checkmark $$ - If $i = 0$ (first slot): $$ \text{next\_in} = (0 + 1) \bmod N = 1 \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 4 — General counter increment (alternate style).** Some implementations use a free-running counter and only modulo on use: $$ \text{in} \leftarrow \text{in} + 1 $$ $$ \text{if } \text{in} \geq N \text{ then } \text{in} \leftarrow 0 $$ Both formulations are equivalent; the KTU board accepts either. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Buffer State Invariant A KTU favourite question is: *"After K producer operations and K consumer operations on an initially empty bounded buffer, what is the state?"* **Step 1 — Define the state variable.** Let $B$ be the number of items currently stored in the buffer. Initially $B_0 = 0$. **Step 2 — Track each operation.** - A producer write (when buffer not full) increments $B$. - A consumer read (when buffer not empty) decrements $B$. - A blocked write or blocked read does not change $B$. **Step 3 — Apply the conservation law.** After $K$ successful writes and $K$ successful reads: $$ B_{\text{final}} \;=\; B_0 \;+\; (\text{writes}) \;-\; (\text{reads}) $$ $$ B_{\text{final}} \;=\; 0 \;+\; K \;-\; K \;=\; 0 $$ **Step 4 — State the invariant.** > The number of items in the buffer satisfies: $0 \leq B \leq N$ at all times. This invariant must hold for the system to be **correct**. A KTU answer that does not state the invariant usually loses **1 mark**. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Semaphore Operations on the Bounded Buffer | Operation | `wait(empty)` | `wait(full)` | `wait(mutex)` | `signal(mutex)` | `signal(full)` | `signal(empty)` | Effect on $B$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Producer entry | Acquires (N→N−1) | — | Acquires (1→0) | — | — | — | $B$ about to $\uparrow$ | | Producer body | — | — | — | — | — | — | $B \leftarrow B+1$ | | Producer exit | — | — | — | Releases (0→1) | Releases (0→1) | Releases (N−1→N) | $B$ now $B+1$ | | Consumer entry | — | Acquires (0→1) | Acquires (1→0) | — | — | — | $B$ about to $\downarrow$ | | Consumer body | — | — | — | — | — | — | $B \leftarrow B-1$ | | Consumer exit | — | — | — | Releases (0→1) | — | — | $B$ now $B-1$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** The order of `wait` operations matters. A `wait(mutex)` *before* the count-semaphore `wait` can cause **deadlock** in some textbook variants. The KTU-accepted safe ordering is: `wait(empty/full) → wait(mutex) → critical section → signal(mutex) → signal(full/empty)`. --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Effective Bandwidth of Shared Memory vs. Pipe The KTU module often asks for a quantitative comparison. Let $T_{\text{syscall}}$ = context-switch + syscall overhead per `read()`/`write()` = $\sim 1 \mu\text{s}$. Let $T_{\text{mem}}$ = DRAM access latency = $\sim 100 \text{ ns} = 0.1 \mu\text{s}$. Let $D$ = bytes transferred per item. **Pipe (message passing):** $$ \text{Time per item} \;=\; T_{\text{syscall, write}} \;+\; T_{\text{syscall, read}} \;=\; 2 \, T_{\text{syscall}} $$ **Shared memory (after setup):** $$ \text{Time per item} \;=\; 2 \, T_{\text{mem}} \quad \text{(one write + one read)} $$ **Speedup factor:** $$ S \;=\; \frac{2 \, T_{\text{syscall}}}{2 \, T_{\text{mem}}} \;=\; \frac{T_{\text{syscall}}}{T_{\text{mem}}} \;=\; \frac{1 \,\mu\text{s}}{0.1 \,\mu\text{s}} \;=\; 10 $$ So shared memory is roughly **10× faster** for small items on typical hardware, and the gap grows with item size since pipes must copy through kernel buffers. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Shared Memory Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ProcA["Process A — Virtual Address Space"] VA_A["Virtual Address 0x7f00...1000"] CodeA["Code & Heap"] end subgraph ProcB["Process B — Virtual Address Space"] VA_B["Virtual Address 0x4ab0...2000"] CodeB["Code & Heap"] end subgraph Kernel["Kernel / OS — Page Table Manager"] PTa["Page Table A"] PTb["Page Table B"] ShmMgr["shmget / mmap Handler"] end subgraph PhysMem["Physical Memory (RAM)"] SharedFrames["Shared Frames
Buffer of N slots
Physical Addr 0x12345000"] end ShmMgr --> PTa ShmMgr --> PTb PTa -.maps to.-> SharedFrames PTb -.maps to.-> SharedFrames VA_A --> PTa VA_B --> PTb CodeA --> VA_A CodeB --> VA_B ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The two processes see **different virtual addresses**, but both page tables point to the **same physical frames**. Hence, when Process A writes a byte, Process B reads the updated value on its next read — without any kernel mediation in between. --- ### 4.2 Producer–Consumer Sequence on Shared Memory ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant P as Producer Process participant SM as Shared Buffer (N slots) participant C as Consumer Process participant K as Kernel (semaphores) P->>K: wait(empty) — free slot available P->>K: wait(mutex) — enter CS P->>SM: write item at index in P->>SM: in = (in + 1) mod N P->>K: signal(mutex) — exit CS P->>K: signal(full) — one more item C->>K: wait(full) — item available C->>K: wait(mutex) — enter CS C->>SM: read item at index out C->>SM: out = (out + 1) mod N C->>K: signal(mutex) — exit CS C->>K: signal(empty) — one more free slot ``` --- ### 4.3 Memory Mapping Mechanism (mmap Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["User process
calls mmap(NULL, size, ...)"] --> B["Kernel checks
valid segment"] B --> C["Allocate VMA
in process"] C --> D["Setup page table
entries pointing
to shared frames"] D --> E["Return void*
pointer to user"] E --> F["Process accesses
memory directly
(no syscall)"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]** *Define shared memory IPC. Mention any two advantages over message passing.* **Model Answer (expected length: ~5–6 lines):** > Shared memory IPC is a mechanism in which two or more processes map a common region of physical memory into their respective virtual address spaces, allowing them to communicate by directly reading and writing that region. > > **Advantages over message passing:** > 1. **Speed** — Once the segment is mapped, communication occurs at memory bus speed with no kernel intervention per access. > 2. **No kernel buffering overhead** — Data is not copied into kernel buffers; the processes share the actual physical frames. > *(Optional 3rd point: convenient for large data sets like shared buffers or memory-mapped files.)* **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]** *Differentiate between System V and POSIX shared memory APIs in Linux.* **Model Answer:** > **System V IPC** uses `shmget()`, `shmat()`, `shmdt()`, `shmctl()` and identifies segments by **integer keys** chosen by the user. The segments are **persistent** — they survive process termination until explicitly removed or the system reboots. > > **POSIX IPC** uses `shm_open()`, `mmap()`, `munmap()`, `shm_unlink()` and identifies segments by **name strings** (filesystem-like). It integrates with the virtual file system, supports `ftruncate()` for sizing, and is generally considered more portable and modern. > > Functionally, both provide the same underlying mechanism of mapping shared physical pages; they differ mainly in **API style and discoverability**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. In the bounded-buffer problem, **forgetting to state the invariant** ($0 \leq B \leq N$) costs **1 mark** even if the rest of the code is correct. > 2. **Wrong semaphore initialisation** (e.g., `full = 0` for a producer-only first run looks trivial but is the most common bug). > 3. Drawing the **producer and consumer code with identical semaphore order** — they should be **symmetric but mirrored**: producer does `wait(empty)`, consumer does `wait(full)`. > 4. Skipping the **`shmget` flags** explanation loses a mark in API-based questions. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]` **(a)** Explain the steps involved in establishing a shared memory segment between two cooperating processes using the **System V IPC** API. Include the role of the kernel. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write the **producer process** code skeleton for the **bounded-buffer problem** using shared memory and **three semaphores**: `empty`, `full`, and `mutex`. State clearly the initial values and the invariant maintained. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Steps to Establish Shared Memory (System V):** 1. **Creation by Process 1 (the server/producer):** - Process 1 invokes `shmget(key, size, IPC_CREAT | 0666)`. - The **kernel** checks permissions, allocates the requested number of physical pages, and assigns a unique **shared memory ID** (`shmid`). - Returns `shmid` to the caller. 2. **Attachment by Process 1:** - Process 1 calls `shmat(shmid, NULL, 0)`. - The kernel **adds a new virtual memory area (VMA)** to Process 1's address space and **updates its page table** so that the VMA points to the physical frames of the segment. - Returns a `void*` pointer to the start of the segment. 3. **Attachment by Process 2 (the client/consumer):** - Process 2 must know the `key` (e.g., via `ftok()`). - It calls `shmget(key, size, 0666)` (without `IPC_CREAT` if it expects the segment to exist) to obtain the same `shmid`. - It calls `shmat(shmid, NULL, 0)` to map the segment into **its own** virtual address space. - The kernel reuses the **same physical frames**, but the virtual address returned may differ from Process 1's pointer. 4. **Communication:** - Both processes now read/write the same physical memory using ordinary pointer arithmetic. The kernel is **not involved** during these accesses. 5. **Detachment and Removal:** - Each process calls `shmdt(ptr)` to remove the mapping from its address space. - The creator (or any process) calls `shmctl(shmid, IPC_RMID, NULL)` to mark the segment for destruction. The kernel frees the physical pages once **all** attached processes detach. > **Role of the kernel:** The kernel manages **physical frame allocation, page-table entries, permission checks, and lifecycle (attach counts)**. It is the trusted intermediary that enforces protection while enabling controlled sharing. --- **(b) Bounded-Buffer Producer Code (with Three Semaphores):** **Semaphore initialisation:** - `empty = N` (number of free slots, where N is buffer size) - `full = 0` (number of filled slots) - `mutex = 1` (binary lock for mutual exclusion) **Invariant maintained at all times:** $0 \leq \text{items in buffer} \leq N$ **Producer process pseudocode:** ``` do { /* 1. Produce the next item (local computation, outside critical section) */ next_item = produce_item(); /* 2. Decrement the count of empty slots; block if buffer is full */ wait(empty); /* 3. Acquire exclusive access to the buffer */ wait(mutex); /* ----- Critical Section begins ----- */ shared_buffer[in] = next_item; in = (in + 1) % N; /* ----- Critical Section ends ----- */ /* 4. Release exclusive access */ signal(mutex); /* 5. Increment the count of filled slots; wake up consumer */ signal(full); } while (TRUE); ``` **Why the ordering matters:** Placing `wait(empty)` **before** `wait(mutex)` prevents the classic deadlock where two producers each hold the mutex and wait for the other to release a slot. Likewise, `signal(mutex)` must come **before** `signal(full)` so that a waiting consumer is unblocked into a state where the buffer is consistent. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]` **(a)** Compare **shared memory** and **message passing** as IPC mechanisms. In which scenarios would you prefer each? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the role of `mmap()` in implementing POSIX shared memory. Describe how the page fault handler contributes to the sharing. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison Table (write this in the exam — easy marks):** | Aspect | Shared Memory | Message Passing | |---|---|---| | **Setup complexity** | Higher (must create, attach) | Lower (just open pipe/queue) | | **Speed** | Faster (memory-speed after setup) | Slower (syscall + kernel copy) | | **Synchronization** | Must be added explicitly (mutex/sem) | Often implicit in send/recv | | **Data size** | Best for large data | Best for small messages | | **Kernel involvement** | Only at attach/detach | On every send/recv | | **Portability** | OS-specific APIs | More standardised | | **Protection** | Harder (any process with pointer) | Easier (kernel checks) | **When to prefer shared memory:** - When large volumes of data must be transferred (e.g., video frames, scientific arrays). - When processes are tightly coupled and on the same machine. - When latency is critical. **When to prefer message passing:** - When processes are on different machines (sockets). - When natural synchronization (send blocks until receive) simplifies design. - When the data volume is small and infrequent. --- **(b) Role of `mmap()` in POSIX Shared Memory:** **What `mmap()` does:** - The function signature is `void *mmap(void *addr, size_t length, int prot, int flags, int fd, off_t offset)`. - For shared memory, the call is `mmap(NULL, length, PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE, MAP_SHARED, fd, 0)` where `fd` came from `shm_open()`. - `mmap()` creates a **virtual memory area (VMA)** in the calling process and records that it should be backed by the file/segment referred to by `fd`. - Critically, **no physical memory is yet allocated** — only the VMA descriptor and page-table placeholders exist. **Role of the page fault handler:** - The first time the process dereferences a pointer inside the mapped region, the MMU raises a **page fault** because no physical frame is yet mapped. - The **page fault handler** in the kernel: 1. Identifies which VMA owns the faulting address. 2. Allocates a **physical page frame** (zero-filled). 3. Sets up the **page table entry** to map the faulting virtual address to this physical frame. 4. **Marks the page as part of the shared region** — i.e., records the underlying shared-memory object so that any *other* process that faults on the *same logical page* of the same segment will be given a page-table entry pointing to **the same physical frame**. - This is the magic step: the sharing is **enforced lazily by the page fault handler**, and the kernel maintains an internal `struct file` / `struct shmem_file_operations` reference count to ensure the underlying object persists until all mappings are released. **Why it is elegant:** The user process perceives the shared region as just an array in memory. All the complexity of **COW (Copy-on-Write) suppression for shared pages**, **reference counting**, and **synchronization with disk-backed segments** is hidden inside the page fault path. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Shared Memory** is the **fastest IPC** because the kernel is bypassed during data transfer. - The kernel only intervenes at **create / attach / detach / destroy** stages. - A **bounded buffer** is the canonical shared-memory problem and **must** include a stated invariant ($0 \leq B \leq N$). - Always use **three semaphores** for the standard producer–consumer: `empty` (init = N), `full` (init = 0), `mutex` (init = 1). - The **safe ordering** is: count-semaphore `wait` → `mutex wait` → critical section → `mutex signal` → count-semaphore `signal`. - **System V** uses integer keys; **POSIX** uses name strings — both achieve the same physical memory sharing. - **`mmap()`** is the cornerstone: it sets up a VMA, and the **page fault handler** does the actual frame allocation and sharing at access time. - Shared memory **requires explicit synchronization** — without it, **race conditions** are guaranteed. - A KTU favourite: deriving the **wrap-around index** using modulo arithmetic on N. - The **speed advantage** over pipes is roughly an order of magnitude (~10×) for small messages. - Real-world uses: **database buffers, multimedia pipelines, X11, IPC in containers**.

topics.title: Message Passing Systems. Process Scheduling: Basic Concepts - Scheduling Criteria - Scheduling Algorithms - First Come First Served
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Message Passing Systems. Process Scheduling: Basic Concepts - Scheduling Criteria - Scheduling Algorithms - First Come First Served

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Message Passing Systems (MPS)

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)
> **Message Passing** is an inter-process communication (IPC) mechanism in which cooperating processes communicate **without sharing any address space**. Information is exchanged through the kernel via two primitive operations — `send(message)` and `receive(message)` — with a fixed message size (for direct/indirect naming) or variable size, transported through communication links.

In distributed, networked, or multi-core systems, processes execute in **separate address spaces** (different memory regions, often on different physical nodes). Since shared memory is impossible across these boundaries, the OS kernel provides a **message-passing facility** as the bridge.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Post-Office Model
Imagine two offices in two different buildings:
- **Office A** (Process P) writes a letter (the **message**).
- It hands the letter to the **Post Office** (the kernel / OS communication subsystem).
- The Post Office delivers the letter through a **postal route** (communication link — wired/wireless, logical/physical).
- **Office B** (Process Q) receives the letter from its mailbox.

The offices themselves never enter each other's premises (no shared address space), and the Post Office guarantees correct sequencing, addressing, and integrity.

### Key Properties
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Properties of Message Passing:**
> - **No shared memory** — hence suitable for distributed systems.
> - **Synchronization is built-in** — both parties need not run simultaneously if the kernel buffers messages.
> - **Implementation is in kernel space** (system calls `msgsnd`, `msgrcv` in System V, or `sendmsg`/`recvmsg` in POSIX).
> - **Slower than shared memory** due to context switches and copies.

---

## 1.2 Process Scheduling — Basic Concepts

### Formal Definition
> **Process Scheduling** is the activity of the **OS scheduler (short-term scheduler / CPU scheduler)** that selects one of the processes in the **ready queue** to be dispatched to the CPU for execution. It is the core mechanism that maximizes **CPU utilization**, **throughput**, and **fairness** while minimizing **waiting time**, **turnaround time**, and **response time**.

### The Life-Cycle Link
A process alternates between bursts of **CPU execution** and **I/O wait**. This gives rise to the famous observation by **Denning (1968)** and the **histogram of CPU-burst times**, which justifies *preemptive* and *priority* scheduling.

> [!NOTE]
> **CPU Burst vs. I/O Burst:**
> - **CPU Burst**: A continuous span during which a process uses the CPU.
> - **I/O Burst**: A continuous span during which a process waits for I/O.
> - Most processes alternate: short CPU burst → long I/O burst → short CPU burst … (I/O-bound) or the opposite (CPU-bound).

### Intuitive Analogy — The Bank Teller Queue
Picture a single bank teller (the **CPU**) serving customers (processes):
- A customer walks in, gets a token, and **joins the queue** (Ready Queue).
- The teller (scheduler) **picks the next customer** by some rule (algorithm).
- The customer is served for some time (CPU burst), then may go to another counter (I/O) and return later.
- The scheduler's job is to keep the teller **never idle** and customers **never unfairly delayed**.

### Types of Schedulers in KTU Scope
| Scheduler | Frequency | Module in KTU Focus |
|---|---|---|
| Long-Term (Admission) | Decides job mix | Not core in Module 3 |
| **Short-Term (CPU)** | Every CPU dispatch | **Primary focus of Module 3** |
| Medium-Term (Swapper) | Suspend/resume | Adjacent concept |

---

## 1.3 Scheduling Criteria

### Formal Definition
> **Scheduling Criteria** are the *quantitative performance metrics* the OS uses to compare two algorithms. They are derived from **user-oriented** and **system-oriented** goals.

### Intuition
When you grade two bank managers, you look at: *"How long did each customer wait? How many were served per hour? How fast did the first one get a response?"* — these are exactly the KTU scheduling criteria.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Seven Canonical KTU Scheduling Criteria:**
> 1. **CPU Utilization** — keep the CPU as busy as possible.
> 2. **Throughput** — number of processes completed per unit time.
> 3. **Turnaround Time (TAT)** — total time from submission to completion.
> 4. **Waiting Time (WT)** — total time spent in the ready queue.
> 5. **Response Time (RT)** — time from submission to first CPU response.
> 6. **Fairness** — every process gets a fair share of CPU.
> 7. **Predictability / Determinism** — same input → same output timing.

---

## 1.4 First-Come First-Served (FCFS) Scheduling

### Formal Definition
> **FCFS (First-Come, First-Served)** is the simplest non-preemptive CPU scheduling algorithm. Processes are dispatched in the exact order of their arrival in the ready queue, governed by a **FIFO (First-In, First-Out)** structure. The CPU is allocated to the head-of-queue process and is **never preempted** until it terminates or voluntarily releases the CPU (e.g., for I/O).

### Intuitive Analogy — The Cinema Ticket Counter
People stand in a **single line** at the ticket window. The person who arrived **first** buys first; the cashier does not stop mid-transaction to help a VIP. This is FCFS.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight Box:** FCFS is **non-preemptive**, **simple**, and suffers from the **Convoy Effect** — a long CPU-bound process at the head of the queue causes all short I/O-bound processes behind it to wait, leaving devices idle.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Message Passing — Operational Mechanics

### 2.1.1 Naming Schemes
To `send` a message, the sender and receiver must **identify each other** in some scheme:

| Scheme | Direct/Indirect | Visibility | KTU Focus |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct Symmetric** | Direct | Both name each other | High |
| **Direct Asymmetric** | Direct | Sender names receiver only | High |
| **Indirect (Port / Mailbox)** | Indirect | Via shared mailbox/port | High |

### 2.1.2 Communication Link Properties (KTU Standard List)
1. **Logical vs. Physical** — logical is a software abstraction over a physical link.
2. **Direct vs. Indirect** — based on naming scheme.
3. **Synchronous vs. Asynchronous** — blocking vs. non-blocking.
4. **Buffered vs. Unbuffered** — capacity of the link's queue.
5. **Unicast vs. Broadcast / Multicast** — one receiver vs. many.

### 2.1.3 Synchronization Modes
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blocking Primitives (Synchronous):**
> - `send` blocks the sender until the message is received.
> - `receive` blocks the receiver until a message arrives.
> - **Rendezvous** — both `send` and `receive` block until they meet.

> **Non-Blocking Primitives (Asynchronous):**
> - `send` returns immediately; kernel buffers the message.
> - `receive` returns either with a valid message or a "no message" flag.
> - Faster but requires bounded **buffer management** in the kernel.

### 2.1.4 Buffering Capacity
- **Zero Capacity** → rendezvous (sender waits always).
- **Bounded Capacity** → finite queue; sender blocks when full.
- **Unbounded Capacity** → sender never blocks; receiver may wait.

---

## 2.2 Process Scheduling — Conceptual Hierarchy

```
                ┌────────────────────────────┐
                │  Long-Term Scheduler       │  ← Job admission
                └─────────────┬──────────────┘
                              ▼
                ┌────────────────────────────┐
                │  Ready Queue (in Memory)   │
                └─────────────┬──────────────┘
                              ▼
                ┌────────────────────────────┐
                │  Short-Term / CPU Scheduler│  ← Module-3 focus
                └─────────────┬──────────────┘
                              ▼
                ┌────────────────────────────┐
                │       CPU executes         │
                └─────────────┬──────────────┘
                              ▼  (on I/O or event wait)
                ┌────────────────────────────┐
                │   Waiting Queue (I/O etc.) │
                └────────────────────────────┘
```

### Preemption — The Central Decision
- **Non-preemptive**: Once a process gets the CPU, it runs until it blocks or terminates. (FCFS, Non-Preemptive SJF)
- **Preemptive**: Scheduler may forcibly take the CPU away using a clock interrupt. (Round Robin, Preemptive SJF, Priority Preemptive)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Cue:** Whenever you see a "context switch" in the middle of a process's CPU burst, it is a **preemption**. FCFS never preempts.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following metrics must be **memorized** verbatim. They form 60% of numerical questions in Module 3.

| # | Metric | Formula | Unit / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$ | Time units (ms) |
| 2 | **Waiting Time (WT)** | $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$ | Time units (ms) |
| 3 | **Waiting Time (alternate)** | $WT_i = ST_i - AT_i$ | Time units (ms) |
| 4 | **Response Time (RT)** | $RT_i = \text{First CPU start} - AT_i$ | Time units (ms) |
| 5 | **Burst Time (BT)** | CPU time requested (given in table) | Time units (ms) |
| 6 | **Average TAT** | $\bar{TAT} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} TAT_i$ | Time units |
| 7 | **Average WT** | $\bar{WT} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i$ | Time units |
| 8 | **CPU Utilization** | $\%U_{CPU} = \dfrac{\text{Busy time}}{\text{Total time}}\times 100$ | Percentage 0–100 |
| 9 | **Throughput** | $Th = \dfrac{\text{Processes completed}}{\text{Time unit}}$ | Jobs / sec |
| 10 | **Convoy Effect Indicator** | $\text{Head process } BT \gg \text{tail processes } BT$ | Boolean |

Where:
- $AT_i$ = Arrival Time of process $i$
- $BT_i$ = Burst Time of process $i$
- $ST_i$ = Start Time of process $i$
- $CT_i$ = Completion Time of process $i$
- $n$ = number of processes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:**
> - **WT** is "**how long the process stood in the queue**" (FIFO-stood time).
> - **TAT** is "**from the moment it entered the system to the moment it left**" (full lifecycle time).
> - **RT** is "**how long until it was first touched by the CPU**" (first-touch latency).
> - In **non-preemptive** algorithms, $RT_i = WT_i$ because the first CPU start IS the queue exit.

---

## 2.4 FCFS — Algorithmic Properties (Why and How)

### Why It Works
- **Implementation**: A FIFO linked list of PCBs (Process Control Blocks). Head is dispatched; tail is where new arrivals are appended.
- **Selection Rule**: $\arg\min_i \{AT_i\}$ among ready processes (ties broken by PID or arrival order).
- **Completion**: Process holds CPU until $CT_i = ST_i + BT_i$.

### Why It Sometimes Fails (The Convoy Effect)
Consider a slow truck (long BT) followed by 5 fast cars (short BT). The cars cannot overtake. Result:
- **CPU-bound head process** → all **I/O-bound tail processes** wait.
- **I/O devices remain idle** because no process reaches the I/O burst phase.
- **Average waiting time balloons**, though CPU stays at 100% utilization.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "Why is FCFS *not* optimal?", the expected answer is: *"It can produce higher average waiting time and suffers from the convoy effect, although it is fair and starvation-free."*

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- Used in **batch systems** (legacy mainframes) where jobs run end-to-end.
- Forms the **default queue discipline** in TCP network stacks (FIFO packet queuing).
- Is the **baseline** against which all other algorithms (SJF, RR, Priority) are compared in academic literature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example for FCFS

> **Problem Statement (KTU-Standard):**
> Consider the following set of processes. Compute Average Waiting Time and Average Turnaround Time using **FCFS** (non-preemptive).
>
> | Process | Arrival Time (AT) | Burst Time (BT) |
> |---|---|---|
> | P1 | 0 | 7 |
> | P2 | 1 | 4 |
> | P3 | 2 | 1 |
> | P4 | 3 | 4 |

### Step 1 — Draw the Gantt Chart
Since FCFS is order-of-arrival, the Gantt chart is the natural timeline:

```
Time →  0       1       2       3               7           8      11
        ┌───────┬───────┬───┬───────────┬───────┐
        │  P1   │  P2   │P3 │    P2     │  P4   │ (note: P2 already in step 1? — recorrect below)
        └───────┴───────┴───┴───────────┴───────┘
```

**Correction (clean derivation):**

```
Time →  0           7   8          12  13    17
        ┌───────────┐ ┌─────────┐  ┌──┐ ┌─────────┐
        │    P1     │ │   P2    │  │P3│ │   P4    │
        └───────────┘ └─────────┘  └──┘ └─────────┘
        0           7   8         12  13  14       18
```

**Reading the chart:**
- **P1**: starts at 0, ends at $0 + 7 = 7$.
- **P2**: starts at 7, ends at $7 + 4 = 11$ (was queued since AT=1, waited 6 units).
- **P3**: starts at 11, ends at $11 + 1 = 12$ (was queued since AT=2, waited 9 units).
- **P4**: starts at 12, ends at $12 + 4 = 16$ (was queued since AT=3, waited 9 units).

> **Corrected final Gantt chart (one line):**
>
> | P1 | P2 | P3 | P4 |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 0 – 7 | 7 – 11 | 11 – 12 | 12 – 16 |

### Step 2 — Compute Completion Time (CT)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P1} &= 7 \\
CT_{P2} &= 11 \\
CT_{P3} &= 12 \\
CT_{P4} &= 16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** $CT_i$ is the rightmost edge of process $i$ in the Gantt chart.

### Step 3 — Compute Turnaround Time (TAT)

Apply the KTU formula $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 7 - 0 = 7 \\
TAT_{P2} &= 11 - 1 = 10 \\
TAT_{P3} &= 12 - 2 = 10 \\
TAT_{P4} &= 16 - 3 = 13
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** TAT measures the *whole* residence time of a process in the system, from arrival to completion.

### Step 4 — Compute Waiting Time (WT)

Apply the KTU formula $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P1} &= 7 - 7 = 0 \\
WT_{P2} &= 10 - 4 = 6 \\
WT_{P3} &= 10 - 1 = 9 \\
WT_{P4} &= 13 - 4 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** WT is the *excess* time the process spent in the queue but did NOT execute — TAT minus actual CPU time = idle-in-queue time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-verification using $WT_i = ST_i - AT_i$:**
> - $WT_{P1} = 0 - 0 = 0$ ✓
> - $WT_{P2} = 7 - 1 = 6$ ✓
> - $WT_{P3} = 11 - 2 = 9$ ✓
> - $WT_{P4} = 12 - 3 = 9$ ✓
>
> Both methods give identical results. **Always use the second formula as a built-in checker in KTU exams.**

### Step 5 — Compute Averages

$$
\bar{TAT} = \frac{7 + 10 + 10 + 13}{4} = \frac{40}{4} = 10 \text{ units}
$$

$$
\bar{WT} = \frac{0 + 6 + 9 + 9}{4} = \frac{24}{4} = 6 \text{ units}
$$

### Final Summary Table (Examiner-Friendly)

| Process | AT | BT | ST | CT | TAT = CT − AT | WT = TAT − BT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 7 | 0 | 7 | 7 | 0 |
| P2 | 1 | 4 | 7 | 11 | 10 | 6 |
| P3 | 2 | 1 | 11 | 12 | 10 | 9 |
| P4 | 3 | 4 | 12 | 16 | 13 | 9 |
| **Avg** | — | — | — | — | **10** | **6** |

### Step 6 — Verify the Convoy Effect

> [!WARNING]
> P1 has BT=7 (longest), arrived first, and is non-preemptively executed. P3 (BT=1) had to wait 9 units behind it. This is the textbook **convoy effect** in action. Examiners award bonus credit if you point this out.

---

## 3.2 Re-ordering to Show FCFS is Not Optimal

> **Same processes, different arrival order — to highlight non-optimality:**
> | Process | AT | BT |
> |---|---|---|
> | P1 | 0 | 7 |
> | P2 | 0 | 4 |
> | P3 | 0 | 1 |
> | P4 | 0 | 4 |

FCFS order (ties broken by PID): P1 → P2 → P3 → P4

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P1} &= 7,\; TAT_{P1} = 7,\; WT_{P1} = 0 \\
CT_{P2} &= 11,\; TAT_{P2} = 11,\; WT_{P2} = 7 \\
CT_{P3} &= 12,\; TAT_{P3} = 12,\; WT_{P3} = 11 \\
CT_{P4} &= 16,\; TAT_{P4} = 16,\; WT_{P4} = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\bar{WT} = \frac{0 + 7 + 11 + 12}{4} = \frac{30}{4} = 7.5 \text{ units}
$$

Now reorder to **P3 → P2 → P4 → P1** (an *admissible* ordering since all arrived at 0):

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P3} &= 1,\; TAT_{P3} = 1,\; WT_{P3} = 0 \\
CT_{P2} &= 5,\; TAT_{P2} = 5,\; WT_{P2} = 1 \\
CT_{P4} &= 9,\; TAT_{P4} = 9,\; WT_{P4} = 5 \\
CT_{P1} &= 16,\; TAT_{P1} = 16,\; WT_{P1} = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\bar{WT} = \frac{0 + 1 + 5 + 9}{4} = \frac{15}{4} = 3.75 \text{ units}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Same process set, two FCFS orderings, **average waiting time drops from 7.5 → 3.75**. This proves FCFS is **not optimal** — it is sensitive to arrival order, especially under tie-breaking.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Message Passing — End-to-End Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PA[Process P Sender]
    KB1[Kernel Buffer Out Queue]
    LNK[Communication Link]
    KB2[Kernel Buffer In Queue]
    PB[Process Q Receiver]

    PA -- send message --> KB1
    KB1 -- transmit --> LNK
    LNK -- deliver --> KB2
    KB2 -- receive message --> PB

    style PA fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style PB fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style KB1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style KB2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style LNK fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
```

**Block-Level Functional Reading:**
- `PA` and `PB` live in *different* address spaces — they never touch each other's memory.
- `KB1` and `KB2` are *kernel-resident* bounded queues (FIFO discipline).
- `LNK` may be a UNIX pipe, a TCP socket, a shared bus, or a network route.

---

## 4.2 FCFS — Scheduler Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Process Arrives]
    B[Append PCB to Tail of Ready Queue]
    C{Is CPU Idle?}
    D[Dispatch Head of Queue to CPU]
    E[CPU runs Process to Completion]
    F[Remove PCB from Queue]
    G[Mark CPU as Idle]
    H[Repeat for next process]

    A --> B --> C
    C -- Yes --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> D
    C -- No --> B

    style A fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style E fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style D fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
```

**Reading:** FCFS never preempts. A process is *only* considered for dispatch when the CPU becomes idle. This is the structural reason for the **convoy effect**.

---

## 4.3 Process State Machine Relevant to Scheduling

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> Ready : Admitted by Long-Term Scheduler
    Ready --> Running : Dispatched by Short-Term Scheduler
    Running --> Waiting : I/O or Event Wait
    Waiting --> Ready : I/O or Event Completion
    Running --> Ready : Preemption or Time Quantum Expiry
    Running --> Terminated : Exit or Error
    Terminated --> [*]
```

**KTU Context:** FCFS only operates on the `Ready → Running` and `Running → Terminated` transitions. The `Running → Ready` preemption arrow is *never* activated under FCFS.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Message Passing vs. Shared Memory

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MPS [Message Passing System]
        direction LR
        X1[Process P] -- send msg --> Y1[Kernel]
        Y1 -- deliver msg --> Z1[Process Q]
    end

    subgraph SMS [Shared Memory System]
        direction LR
        X2[Process P] <--> SHM[(Shared Memory Region)]
        Z2[Process Q] <--> SHM
    end

    style MPS fill:#e7f1ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style SMS fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#fd7e14
```

**Examiner Cue:** When asked *"Why use message passing in distributed systems?"* — answer: *"Because processes do not share a common memory; the kernel must mediate via messages."*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Differentiate between direct and indirect message passing in operating systems. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Direct Message Passing (1 mark):** Processes must explicitly name each other. `send(P, message)` and `receive(Q, message)` — both sender and receiver names are used. Tightly coupled, hard-coded references.
- **Indirect Message Passing (1 mark):** Processes communicate through a shared **mailbox** or **port**. `send(A, message)` and `receive(A, message)` — only the mailbox name matters. Loosely coupled, more flexible.
- **Example (0.5 mark each):** Direct — UNIX signal with explicit PID; Indirect — UNIX pipes (mailbox is the pipe buffer), email systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing *"Indirect uses email, Direct uses SMS"* without naming OS-level constructs. Always anchor to OS primitives — pipes, sockets, signals, mailboxes.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List and briefly explain any five CPU scheduling criteria used to evaluate scheduling algorithms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks = 0.6 per criterion):**
1. **CPU Utilization** — Percentage of time CPU is busy; aim for 40–90%.
2. **Throughput** — Number of processes completed per unit time; higher is better.
3. **Turnaround Time** — Total time from submission to completion; $TAT = CT - AT$; lower is better.
4. **Waiting Time** — Time spent in ready queue; $WT = TAT - BT$; lower is better.
5. **Response Time** — Time from submission to first CPU response; critical for interactive systems.
6. **Fairness** — Equal CPU share; no starvation.
7. **Predictability** — Same input yields same output timing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Confusing *Turnaround Time* with *Response Time*. TAT includes total execution; RT stops at first CPU allocation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

> Consider the following processes arriving at time 0 with the given CPU burst times. Compute the **Average Waiting Time** and **Average Turnaround Time** using **FCFS scheduling**. Also draw the Gantt chart and identify if the convoy effect is present.
>
> | Process | Burst Time (ms) |
> |---|---|
> | P1 | 10 |
> | P2 | 3 |
> | P3 | 5 |
> | P4 | 1 |

#### Part (a) — Draw the Gantt chart and compute Average Waiting Time. **[7 marks]**

**Step 1: Gantt Chart**

All processes arrive at $AT = 0$, so FCFS order is P1 → P2 → P3 → P4.

```
Time →  0               10  11    14  15        20
        ┌──────────────┐ ┌──┐ ┌───┐ ┌─┐ ┌────────┐
        │      P1      │ │P2│ │P3 │ │P4│        │
        └──────────────┘ └──┘ └───┘ └─┘ └────────┘
        0              10 11   14 15            19
```

| Process | ST | CT |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 | 10 |
| P2 | 10 | 13 |
| P3 | 13 | 18 |
| P4 | 18 | 19 |

**Step 2: Waiting Time via $WT_i = ST_i - AT_i$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P1} &= 0 - 0 = 0 \\
WT_{P2} &= 10 - 0 = 10 \\
WT_{P3} &= 13 - 0 = 13 \\
WT_{P4} &= 18 - 0 = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Average Waiting Time**

$$
\bar{WT} = \frac{0 + 10 + 13 + 18}{4} = \frac{41}{4} = 10.25 \text{ ms}
$$

#### Part (b) — Compute Average Turnaround Time and identify the Convoy Effect. **[7 marks]**

**Step 1: TAT via $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= 10 - 0 = 10 \\
TAT_{P2} &= 13 - 0 = 13 \\
TAT_{P3} &= 18 - 0 = 18 \\
TAT_{P4} &= 19 - 0 = 19
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Average TAT**

$$
\bar{TAT} = \frac{10 + 13 + 18 + 19}{4} = \frac{60}{4} = 15 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Convoy Effect Identification**

> [!NOTE]
> Yes, the **Convoy Effect** is present. P1 (BT=10) is the longest job and runs first, non-preemptively. P4 (BT=1) — the shortest — waits 18 ms. CPU utilization is 100%, but **device utilization and overall system throughput** are degraded. This is the classic symptom.

**Verification using alternate formula $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P1} &= 10 - 10 = 0 \quad ✓ \\
WT_{P2} &= 13 - 3 = 10 \quad ✓ \\
WT_{P3} &= 18 - 5 = 13 \quad ✓ \\
WT_{P4} &= 19 - 1 = 18 \quad ✓
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (Internal Choice) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

> With neat diagrams, explain the **two synchronization modes** of message passing in operating systems. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. Mention the role of the **kernel buffer** in each mode.

#### Part (a) — Explain Blocking (Synchronous) and Non-Blocking (Asynchronous) primitives with diagrams. **[7 marks]**

**Blocking Send / Receive (Synchronous) — 3.5 marks:**

The sender executes `send()` and **suspends** (blocks) until the message is delivered to and accepted by the receiver. Similarly, the receiver executes `receive()` and blocks until a message arrives.

```
Process P (Sender)                 Process Q (Receiver)
  ─────●────────                    ─────●────────
       │                                 │
   send(P,Q,msg)                    receive(Q,msg)
       │                                 │
       ▼  (BLOCKED here)             (BLOCKED here)
       │                                 │
       │◄─── rendezvous / message handoff ──►
       │                                 │
       ▼  (resumes)                    (resumes)
  ─────●────────                    ─────●────────
       Time passes — both processes wait for each other
```

**Non-Blocking Send / Receive (Asynchronous) — 3.5 marks:**

The sender hands the message to the kernel buffer and **continues immediately**; the receiver polls or uses a callback. Buffer must be finite (bounded) to prevent unbounded memory use.

```
Process P (Sender)                 Kernel Buffer
  ─────●────────                       ┌──────┐
       │                               │ msg  │
   send(P,Q,msg)                       └──────┘
       │  (returns immediately)            │
       ▼                                  ▼
  ─────●────────  (continues)        Process Q reads later
                                      when scheduled
```

#### Part (b) — Compare both modes and the role of the kernel buffer. **[7 marks]**

| Aspect | Blocking (Synchronous) | Non-Blocking (Asynchronous) |
|---|---|---|
| **Sender behavior** | Waits until received | Continues immediately |
| **Receiver behavior** | Waits until message arrives | Returns with flag or null |
| **Buffer need** | Minimal (or zero — rendezvous) | Mandatory (bounded/unbounded) |
| **Speed** | Slower (process stalls) | Faster (no stall) |
| **Risk** | Deadlock (circular wait) | Buffer overflow, lost messages |
| **Use case** | Tightly-coupled rendezvous | Distributed/loosely-coupled |
| **Implementation** | Easier | Harder (needs buffer mgmt) |

**Role of Kernel Buffer (3 marks for elaboration):**
- Acts as the **intermediary** storage in the asynchronous case.
- Implemented as a bounded queue of message descriptors (`struct msg`).
- Capacity choices: **zero (rendezvous)**, **bounded (most common)**, **unbounded (theoretical)**.
- When full, sender either **blocks** (degrades to synchronous) or **errors out** (returns `-1` with `EAGAIN`).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Saying "non-blocking send never waits" — this is wrong if the buffer is full. Many students lose 1 mark here. Always state the **bounded-buffer assumption** explicitly.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in Module 3 questions:**
> 1. **Confusing WT formula** — using $WT = CT - AT$ instead of $TAT - BT$. They are equal only for FCFS-by-arrival, NOT in general.
> 2. **Drawing Gantt charts without start-time labels** — examiners *require* the `ST` and `CT` of every process.
> 3. **Forgetting the Convoy Effect explanation** in FCFS answers — it is worth 1–2 marks as a "comment".
> 4. **Mixing up direct vs. indirect** — saying "Indirect = shared memory". Indirect is *still message passing*, just via a mailbox.
> 5. **Skipping the unit (ms)** in the final average — always write "ms" or "time units" in numerical answers.
> 6. **In Message Passing, omitting the kernel** — the kernel is the *active intermediary*; a diagram without it is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 (FCFS + Message Passing):**
> - Message Passing = `send()` + `receive()` primitives, no shared memory, kernel-mediated.
> - Naming schemes: **Direct** (P→Q) vs. **Indirect** (via mailbox/port).
> - Synchronization: **Blocking (sync / rendezvous)** vs. **Non-blocking (async)**.
> - Buffer capacity: **Zero / Bounded / Unbounded**.
> - Scheduling Criteria (must memorize all 7): CPU Utilization, Throughput, TAT, WT, RT, Fairness, Predictability.
> - $TAT = CT - AT$ ; $WT = TAT - BT$ ; $WT = ST - AT$ (cross-check) ; $RT = \text{First CPU start} - AT$.
> - FCFS = **FIFO**, **non-preemptive**, simple, fair, starvation-free.
> - FCFS drawback = **Convoy Effect** (long head process blocks all tail processes).
> - FCFS is **not optimal** — re-ordering same AT can lower $\bar{WT}$ (shown with $7.5 \to 3.75$ example).
> - In FCFS, since non-preemptive: $RT_i = WT_i$ for every process.
> - Always draw **Gantt chart** with start/end times — partial credit in exams depends on it.
> - **Examiner's favorite line**: "Compare FCFS and SJF on the basis of average waiting time" — be ready with a one-line contrast.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Round Robin Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Round Robin Scheduling ## 1.
