| **No Preemption** | Allow OS to preempt resources (save state, take resource, resume later) | Difficult to implement for arbitrary resources; only works for state-restorable resources like CPU, memory |
| **Circular Wait** | Impose a **total ordering** on resource acquisition (always acquire locks in order 1 → 2 → 3) | Hard to enforce globally in large systems; breaks encapsulation |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define a deadlock. List the four necessary conditions for a deadlock to occur. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **deadlock** is a state in an operating system in which two or more processes are permanently blocked, with each process waiting for a resource that is held by another process in the same set, forming an unresolvable circular wait.

The four **Coffman Conditions** (necessary conditions) for a deadlock are:

1. **Mutual Exclusion:** At least one resource is non-shareable.
2. **Hold and Wait:** A process holding a resource waits for additional resources held by others.
3. **No Preemption:** Resources cannot be forcibly taken; they are released only voluntarily.
4. **Circular Wait:** A closed chain of processes exists where each process waits for a resource held by the next process in the chain.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q2. State the Coffman conditions. Is mutual exclusion a sufficient condition for deadlock? Justify. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Coffman conditions are the four necessary conditions for a deadlock: **Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, and Circular Wait**.

**No, mutual exclusion alone is NOT sufficient for a deadlock.** Mutual exclusion is only one of the four necessary conditions. A deadlock requires **all four conditions to hold simultaneously**. For example, if hold-and-wait is absent (i.e., processes request all resources upfront), mutual exclusion alone will not lead to a deadlock, because processes will only start executing after acquiring all required resources, eliminating the circular wait scenario.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, Question A]**

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the four necessary conditions for a deadlock in an Operating System with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a system with three processes P1, P2, P3 and three resource types R1 (2 instances), R2 (2 instances), and R3 (2 instances). The current state of allocation is:
- P1 holds 1 instance of R1 and requests 1 instance of R2.
- P2 holds 1 instance of R2 and 1 instance of R3, and requests 1 instance of R1.
- P3 holds 1 instance of R1 and 1 instance of R3, and requests 1 instance of R2.

Draw the Resource Allocation Graph and determine whether the system is in a deadlock. Justify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

##### **Part (a) — Explanation of Four Necessary Conditions (7 Marks)**

**Definition:** A set of processes is in a deadlock state when every process in the set is waiting for an event that can be caused only by another process in the set. For a deadlock to occur, the following four **Coffman conditions** must hold simultaneously:

**1. Mutual Exclusion (2 marks):**
At least one resource must be held in a non-shareable mode. This means that if another process requests that resource, it must be delayed until the resource is released. For example, a printer cannot be simultaneously shared by two processes printing different documents; only one process can use it at a time.

**2. Hold and Wait (2 marks):**
A process must currently be holding at least one resource and simultaneously waiting to acquire additional resources that are currently being held by other processes. For example, Process P1 holds a printer and is waiting for a scanner held by Process P2.

**3. No Preemption (1.5 marks):**
Resources cannot be preempted. A resource is released only voluntarily by the process holding it, after that process has completed its task. The operating system has no authority to forcibly take a resource away from a process. For example, once a process acquires a CD-ROM drive for burning, the OS cannot take it back mid-burn.

**4. Circular Wait (1.5 marks):**
A set of processes $\{P_0, P_1, \ldots, P_n\}$ must exist such that $P_0$ is waiting for a resource held by $P_1$, $P_1$ is waiting for a resource held by $P_2$, ..., and $P_n$ is waiting for a resource held by $P_0$. This forms a closed chain of waiting processes. For example, P1 waits for P2, P2 waits for P3, and P3 waits for P1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conclusion:** All four conditions are **necessary** but **not sufficient** individually. They must hold **simultaneously** for a deadlock to occur. Breaking any one of them prevents deadlock.

---

##### **Part (b) — Resource Allocation Graph Analysis (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Construct the Resource Allocation Graph (3 marks)**

From the given data, we identify the edges:

- **Assignment Edges (P → R):**
  - $P_1 \rightarrow R_1$ (1 instance held)
  - $P_2 \rightarrow R_2$ (1 instance held)
  - $P_2 \rightarrow R_3$ (1 instance held)
  - $P_3 \rightarrow R_1$ (1 instance held)
  - $P_3 \rightarrow R_3$ (1 instance held)

- **Request Edges (R → P):**
  - $R_2 \rightarrow P_1$ (P1 requests R2)
  - $R_1 \rightarrow P_2$ (P2 requests R1)
  - $R_2 \rightarrow P_3$ (P3 requests R2)

**Step 2: Convert to a Wait-For Graph (2 marks)**

In a wait-for graph, an edge $P_i \rightarrow P_j$ means $P_i$ is waiting for a resource held by $P_j$.

- $P_1$ waits for $R_2$ held by $P_2$ and $P_3$. Let us consider any one — say $P_1$ waits for $P_2$.
- $P_2$ waits for $R_1$ held by $P_1$ and $P_3$. So $P_2$ waits for $P_1$ (or $P_3$).
- $P_3$ waits for $R_2$ held by $P_2$ (or $P_1$, if $P_1$'s request is also directed at the free instance).

Let us check the **resource instance counts** to see if there is a free instance:
- $R_1$: 2 total, 2 allocated (1 to $P_1$ + 1 to $P_3$). **0 free.**
- $R_2$: 2 total, 2 allocated (1 to $P_2$ + 0 to others from allocation data; $P_1$ and $P_3$ request it but don't hold). Wait — checking again: $P_1$ holds 0 of R2, $P_2$ holds 1 of R2, $P_3$ holds 0 of R2. Total allocated = 1. **1 free instance of R2.**
- $R_3$: 2 total, 2 allocated (1 to $P_2$ + 1 to $P_3$). **0 free.**

**Step 3: Determine Deadlock (2 marks)**

Since there is **1 free instance of $R_2$**, the OS can allocate it to **either $P_1$ or $P_3$** (whichever requests it). Suppose the OS grants it to $P_3$:
- $P_3$ now holds R1 and R2 and R3, can complete execution, and releases all resources.
- This frees an instance of $R_1$ and an instance of $R_3$.
- $P_2$ (which was waiting for $R_1$) can now acquire the free $R_1$, complete, and release everything.
- $P_1$ (which was waiting for $R_2$) can now acquire a free $R_2$, complete, and release everything.

**Conclusion:** There exists a **safe sequence**: $\langle P_3, P_2, P_1 \rangle$.

**The system is NOT in a deadlock. It is in a SAFE state.**

Even though there is a cycle in the wait-for graph ($P_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow P_1$ via R2, or a longer one), the existence of a free instance of R2 allows at least one process to make progress, breaking the cycle.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, Question B — Alternative Choice]**

### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the Resource Allocation Graph (RAG). How can it be used to detect a deadlock in a system with single-instance resources? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between the four necessary conditions for a deadlock. For each condition, suggest one practical strategy to break it. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

##### **Part (a) — Resource Allocation Graph and Deadlock Detection (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Definition and Components of RAG (2 marks)**

A **Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)** is a directed graph that captures the state of resource allocation in a system. It consists of:

- **Process vertices** ($P_i$): Represented by **circles**, denoting processes in the system.
- **Resource vertices** ($R_j$): Represented by **rectangles**. Each rectangle contains a number of **dots** equal to the number of identical instances of that resource type.

**Step 2: Types of Edges (2 marks)**

There are two types of directed edges in an RAG:

- **Assignment Edge $P_i \rightarrow R_j$:** Indicates that process $P_i$ is currently holding an instance of resource $R_j$.
- **Request Edge $R_j \rightarrow P_i$:** Indicates that process $P_i$ is currently waiting for (has requested) an instance of resource $R_j$.

**Step 3: Deadlock Detection Using RAG for Single-Instance Resources (3 marks)**

**Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** *In a system where each resource type has only a single instance, the system is in a deadlock state if and only if there is a cycle in the Resource Allocation Graph.*

**Detection Procedure:**
1. Construct the RAG from the current system state.
2. Check for the existence of a cycle in the RAG.
3. **If a cycle exists → DEADLOCK detected.**
4. **If no cycle exists → NO DEADLOCK.**

**Justification:** If there is no cycle, the wait-for graph is a DAG, and a topological ordering exists. At least one process has no outgoing request edge, so it can complete, release resources, and unblock others. By induction, all processes complete.

If there is a cycle, the processes in the cycle are each waiting for a resource held by another process in the cycle, and no process in the cycle can ever make progress — a deadlock.

---

##### **Part (b) — Differentiating the Four Conditions with Prevention Strategies (7 Marks)**

| **#** | **Necessary Condition** | **Description** | **Practical Strategy to Break It** |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Mutual Exclusion** | At least one resource is non-shareable; only one process can use it at a time. | **Spooling:** Use an intermediate storage (e.g., printer spooler, disk buffer) so that processes output to the spool, and a single dedicated daemon manages the actual device. This effectively makes the device shareable. |
| **2** | **Hold and Wait** | A process holds at least one resource while waiting for additional resources. | **All-or-Nothing Allocation:** Require each process to request and be allocated **all** its required resources before beginning execution. If it cannot get all, it gets none and must wait. |
| **3** | **No Preemption** | Resources are released only voluntarily by the holding process. | **Forced Preemption:** When a process holding certain critical resources requests another resource that cannot be immediately allocated, the OS preempts (takes back) the held resources. The preempted resources are added to a waiting list for the requesting process. |
| **4** | **Circular Wait** | A closed chain of processes exists where each waits for a resource held by the next. | **Total Ordering of Resources:** Assign a unique numeric ordering to all resource types. Require that every process request resources in strictly **increasing (or decreasing) numerical order**. This makes cycles impossible. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT say "the four conditions cause deadlock".** The correct phrasing is "the four conditions must hold **simultaneously** for a deadlock to occur." Each one is **necessary** but **not sufficient** alone.
> 2. **For Part (b) RAG questions, ALWAYS count resource instances carefully.** Many students miscount the dots inside the resource rectangles and incorrectly conclude "deadlock" when a free instance actually exists.
> 3. **For Banker's-style questions, ALWAYS produce a safe sequence** if you claim the system is safe. Merely stating "no cycle = safe" without showing the sequence loses 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Do not confuse the "cycle in RAG" rule.** It applies to **single-instance** resources. For **multi-instance** resources, a cycle only indicates **possibility** of deadlock, not certainty.
> 5. **In the prevention table, prevention strategies come with trade-offs** (e.g., spooling requires disk space, all-or-nothing reduces utilization, total ordering is hard to enforce globally). Mention this for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Deadlocks Necessary Conditions**

- **Definition:** A deadlock is a permanent block where each process in a set waits for a resource held by another in the same set.
- **Coffman Conditions (4):** **M**utual Exclusion, **H**old and Wait, **N**o Preemption, **C**ircular Wait.
- **Necessary but not Sufficient:** All four must hold *simultaneously*. Break *any one* → no deadlock.
- **Mutual Exclusion:** At least one non-shareable resource.
- **Hold and Wait:** Process holds some, waits for more.
- **No Preemption:** Resources released only voluntarily.
- **Circular Wait:** Closed chain of waiting processes $P_0 \rightarrow P_1 \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow P_0$.
- **Resource Allocation Graph (RAG):** Vertices are processes (circles) and resources (rectangles with dots = instance count). Edges: Assignment ($P \rightarrow R$) and Request ($R \rightarrow P$).
- **Single-instance resources:** Cycle in RAG ⟺ Deadlock (necessary AND sufficient).
- **Multi-instance resources:** Cycle in RAG ⟹ Possible deadlock, but use **Banker's Algorithm** to confirm.
- **Wait-For Graph:** Simplified RAG where edges are $P_i \rightarrow P_j$ (Pi waits for a resource held by Pj).
- **Prevention vs Avoidance:** Prevention breaks one of the 4 conditions at design time; Avoidance (Banker's) ensures the system never enters an unsafe state at runtime.
- **Practical Examples:** Printer (mutual exclusion), Database transaction holding lock + waiting for another (hold and wait), CD-R burn (no preemption), P1 holds L1 waits for L2 while P2 holds L2 waits for L1 (circular wait).
- **Spooling:** Solves mutual exclusion for printers.
- **Total Ordering:** Solves circular wait globally.
- **All-or-Nothing Request:** Solves hold and wait.
- **Preemption of resources:** Solves no preemption.
- **High-Yield Keywords for KTU Exam:** "Coffman conditions", "necessary conditions", "circular wait", "hold and wait", "Resource Allocation Graph", "single instance", "multiple instances", "Banker's algorithm".

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resource Allocation Graphs - Deadlock Avoidance - Banker's Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Resource Allocation Graphs - Deadlock Avoidance - Banker's Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
A *Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)* is the bipartite directed graph model used by an Operating System to represent the precise instantaneous state of system resources and process-resource interactions. It is the **canonical graphical tool** for visualizing *deadlock existence* in a system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A RAG contains a **set of vertices V** and a **set of edges E**. The vertices are partitioned into two disjoint sets:
> - **P = {P₁, P₂, ..., Pₙ}** — the set consisting of all active **processes** in the system.
> - **R = {R₁, R₂, ..., Rₘ}** — the set consisting of all **resource types** in the system.
>
> A directed edge from process Pᵢ to resource type Rⱼ (Pᵢ → Rⱼ) is a **request edge**. A directed edge from Rⱼ to Pᵢ (Rⱼ → Pᵢ) is an **assignment edge**.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Shared Workshop":**
Imagine a college engineering workshop with **4 lathes** and **3 students** working on projects.
- A *process* (student) sometimes requests a lathe → drawn as an arrow **from the student to the lathe** (request edge).
- When the student returns the lathe, the arrow reverses → becomes an **assignment edge** (lathe is currently *with* the student).
- If every lathe is being held by students, and every student is waiting for another lathe → **we have a circular wait, i.e., a deadlock** (the workshop is frozen!).

The RAG is simply the **"freeze-frame snapshot"** of who holds what and who is waiting for what at any moment.

> [!NOTE]
> **RAG with Single Instance vs. Multiple Instances:**
> - **Single-instance resources** → RAG can *definitively* detect deadlock (a cycle *implies* a deadlock).
> - **Multiple-instance resources** → RAG may show cycles that are **NOT deadlocks**. Here, the **Banker's Algorithm** must be used.

---

### 1.2 Deadlock Avoidance

**Formal Definition:**
*Deadlock Avoidance* requires that the operating system be given **advance information** about the *maximum* number of resources of each type that *every* process may ever request. Using this *a priori* knowledge, the OS dynamically decides whether the current resource allocation could **lead to a deadlock in the future**, and if so, refuses the request — even if the request is currently safe to grant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Famous Deadlock Avoidance Algorithm = Banker's Algorithm** (proposed by Edsger W. Dijkstra, 1965). The name comes from the analogy of a banker who never allocates cash in a way that prevents lending to all customers.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Cautious Banker":**
A banker has a fixed amount of cash. Customers declare the **maximum loan** they might ever need. The banker grants a smaller loan only if he can still satisfy the *maximum need* of *every other customer* with the remaining cash. If granting the loan would risk an unsafe state, the banker **makes the customer wait**, even though technically cash is free.

---

### 1.3 Banker's Algorithm — Core Idea

**Formal Definition:**
The Banker's Algorithm tests every hypothetical allocation against a set of *safety conditions*. A state is **safe** if there exists *some* sequence of process execution (a *safe sequence*) in which all processes can complete even when requesting their maximum resources. If no such sequence exists, the state is **unsafe**, and the corresponding request must be **denied**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Fundamental State Concepts (KTU must-know):**
>
> | State | Meaning |
> |-------|---------|
> | **Safe State** | A safe sequence exists; no deadlock guaranteed. |
> | **Unsafe State** | No guarantee of safe sequence; **possible** deadlock. |
> | **Deadlocked State** | Certain deadlock — at least one process is permanently blocked. |
>
> **Safe ⊂ Non-Deadlocked ⊆ All States.** Deadlock Avoidance = **avoidance of unsafe states**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the Resource Allocation Graph

The RAG has **two types of vertices** and **two types of edges**:

- **Vertices:** Circles (processes P₁, P₂, ...) and Rectangles (resource types R₁, R₂, ...).
- **Dots inside rectangles** indicate the **count (instances)** of each resource type.
- **Request Edge:** Pᵢ → Rⱼ (process is requesting an instance of Rⱼ).
- **Assignment Edge:** Rⱼ → Pᵢ (an instance of Rⱼ is allocated to process Pᵢ).

**Why and How the RAG Works:**
1. The kernel maintains this graph in memory in real time.
2. Whenever a process **requests** a resource, a request edge is inserted.
3. When the resource is **granted**, the request edge is *transformed* into an assignment edge.
4. When the process **releases** the resource, the assignment edge is deleted.
5. The cycle-detection algorithm runs periodically (e.g., DFS-based) to detect deadlocks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Theorem (Memorize This!):**
> *If a RAG contains **no cycles** → **No deadlock**.*
> *If a RAG contains a cycle →*
> → If every resource type has **only one instance** → **Deadlock exists**.
> → If some resource type has **multiple instances** → **Possibility of deadlock**, *not certainty* (use Banker's Algorithm).

---

### 2.2 Deadlock Avoidance — Operational Logic

The system models future behavior using:

- **Max[i]** = maximum instances of each resource that process Pᵢ may ever request.
- **Need[i]** = Max[i] − Allocation[i] = *remaining* resources Pᵢ may still need.
- **Available[j]** = free instances of resource Rⱼ right now.

**Three Sequential Steps (the master algorithm):**

1. **Initialize** — Compute Need matrix = Max − Allocation.
2. **Safety Algorithm** — Determine whether the current state is safe; find a safe sequence if it exists.
3. **Resource Request Algorithm** — Simulate the allocation; if the resulting state is safe, **grant**; otherwise, **make the process wait**.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Card — Print This!**

| # | Symbol | Meaning | Mathematical Relation |
|---|--------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Available** | Free instances of each resource | $Available[j] = Total[j] - \sum_{i=1}^{n} Allocation[i][j]$ |
| 2 | **Max** | Maximum demand matrix | Process-declared upper bound |
| 3 | **Allocation** | Currently held by each process | Runtime dynamic |
| 4 | **Need** | Remaining requirement | $Need[i][j] = Max[i][j] - Allocation[i][j]$ |
| 5 | **Work** | Working copy of Available during safety test | Initially $Work = Available$ |
| 6 | **Finish[i]** | Boolean — has process Pᵢ completed? | Initially $Finish[i] = \text{false}$ |
| 7 | **Request[i]** | Current pending request by Pᵢ | Must satisfy $Request[i] \le Need[i]$ (else error) |
| 8 | Safe State Condition | $\exists$ sequence such that all $Need[i]$ satisfied | $Work + Allocation[i] \le Available$ (after each step) |

**Critical Boundary Conditions:**

- If $Request[i][k] > Need[i][k]$ → **error** (process exceeded its declared maximum).
- If $Request[i] > Available$ → process $P_i$ must **wait** (resources not free).
- If simulated state is safe → **grant**; if unsafe → **roll back** to original state, process **waits**.

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- **Embedded systems** in aerospace/automotive use avoidance to guarantee real-time deadlines.
- **Database transaction managers** use a similar concept (Wait-Die / Wound-Wait) to prevent transaction deadlocks.
- **Network routers** use avoidance protocols (e.g., BGP route locking) to prevent routing-table deadlocks.

---

### 2.4 Logical Breakdown — Why Banker's Algorithm Works

The algorithm exploits the **principle of "least commitment"**:
- It grants requests *only* when a *safe sequence* is provable.
- Even if granting a request does not create an immediate cycle, the algorithm **looks ahead** to whether the worst-case remaining requests can still be satisfied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitations (KTU Examiner's favorite!):**
> 1. Requires processes to **declare maximum needs in advance** — unrealistic in many real systems.
> 2. **Fixed number of processes** must be known at all times (no dynamic process creation).
> 3. **Overly conservative** — may unnecessarily block requests that *would have been safe*.
> 4. Time complexity of Safety Algorithm = $O(m \times n^2)$ where $m$ = resource types, $n$ = processes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Resource Allocation Graph (Cycle Detection)

**Problem:** Consider 3 processes P₁, P₂, P₃ and 3 resource types R₁ (1 instance), R₂ (2 instances), R₃ (1 instance). Edges are:

- R₁ → P₂ (R₁ allocated to P₂)
- R₂ → P₁ (one instance of R₂ allocated to P₁)
- R₂ → P₃ (one instance of R₂ allocated to P₃)
- P₁ → R₁ (P₁ requests R₁)
- P₂ → R₃ (P₂ requests R₃)
- P₃ → R₂ (P₃ requests another instance of R₂)

**Step 1 — Identify vertices and edges.**

Vertices: P = {P₁, P₂, P₃}, R = {R₁, R₂, R₃}.

**Step 2 — Draw the graph.** (See SECTION 4.)

**Step 3 — Look for cycles.**

A candidate cycle:

$$P_1 \rightarrow R_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow R_3 \rightarrow ?$$

R₃ has no outgoing assignment edge → **no cycle through this path**.

Another attempt:

$$P_1 \rightarrow R_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow R_3$$
$$P_3 \rightarrow R_2 \rightarrow P_1 \rightarrow R_1 \rightarrow P_2 \rightarrow R_3$$

R₃ has no outgoing edge → cycle terminates.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** No cycle exists in the RAG. **Therefore, no deadlock.**

---

### 3.2 Banker's Algorithm — Detailed Worked Example

**Problem Setup (Classic KTU-style Question):**

Consider a system with **5 processes** P₀..P₄ and **3 resource types**:
- A = 10 instances, B = 5 instances, C = 7 instances.

**Snapshot at time T₀:**

| Process | Allocation (A, B, C) | Max (A, B, C) |
|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| P₀ | (0, 1, 0) | (7, 5, 3) |
| P₁ | (2, 0, 0) | (3, 2, 2) |
| P₂ | (3, 0, 2) | (9, 0, 2) |
| P₃ | (2, 1, 1) | (2, 2, 2) |
| P₄ | (0, 0, 2) | (4, 3, 3) |

**Step 1 — Compute the Need matrix.**

Using $Need[i] = Max[i] - Allocation[i]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Need[P_0] &= (7,5,3) - (0,1,0) = (7,4,3) \\
Need[P_1] &= (3,2,2) - (2,0,0) = (1,2,2) \\
Need[P_2] &= (9,0,2) - (3,0,2) = (6,0,0) \\
Need[P_3] &= (2,2,2) - (2,1,1) = (0,1,1) \\
Need[P_4] &= (4,3,3) - (0,0,2) = (4,3,1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Available vector.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Available &= Total - \sum_{i=0}^{4} Allocation[i] \\
&= (10,5,7) - (0+2+3+2+0,\; 1+0+0+1+0,\; 0+0+2+1+2) \\
&= (10,5,7) - (7, 2, 5) \\
&= (3, 3, 2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Execute the Safety Algorithm.**

Initialize:
$$
Work = Available = (3, 3, 2), \quad Finish = [\text{false, false, false, false, false}]
$$

**Iteration 1:** Find i such that $Finish[i] = false$ AND $Need[i] \le Work$.

- P₀: Need (7,4,3) > (3,3,2) ❌
- P₁: Need (1,2,2) ≤ (3,3,2) ✅

Select P₁:
$$
Work = Work + Allocation[P_1] = (3,3,2) + (2,0,0) = (5,3,2)
$$
$$Finish[P_1] = true$$

**Iteration 2:** Need ≤ Work?

- P₀: (7,4,3) > (5,3,2) ❌
- P₂: (6,0,0) > (5,3,2) ❌
- P₃: (0,1,1) ≤ (5,3,2) ✅

Select P₃:
$$
Work = (5,3,2) + (2,1,1) = (7,4,3)
$$
$$Finish[P_3] = true$$

**Iteration 3:** Need ≤ Work?

- P₀: (7,4,3) ≤ (7,4,3) ✅

Select P₀:
$$
Work = (7,4,3) + (0,1,0) = (7,5,3)
$$
$$Finish[P_0] = true$$

**Iteration 4:** Need ≤ Work?

- P₂: (6,0,0) ≤ (7,5,3) ✅

Select P₂:
$$
Work = (7,5,3) + (3,0,2) = (10,5,5)
$$
$$Finish[P_2] = true$$

**Iteration 5:** Need ≤ Work?

- P₄: (4,3,1) ≤ (10,5,5) ✅

Select P₄:
$$
Work = (10,5,5) + (0,0,2) = (10,5,7)
$$
$$Finish[P_4] = true$$

**Conclusion of Safety Algorithm:**

All Finish[i] = true → **State is SAFE**.

**Safe Sequence: ⟨ P₁, P₃, P₀, P₂, P₄ ⟩**

---

### 3.3 Resource Request Algorithm — Worked Example

**Continuation:** Process P₁ now requests **Request₁ = (1, 0, 2)**.

**Step 1 — Verify Request₁ ≤ Need[P₁].**

$Need[P_1] = (1,2,2)$. Request = (1,0,2). Yes, (1,0,2) ≤ (1,2,2) ✅.

**Step 2 — Verify Request₁ ≤ Available.**

$Available = (3,3,2)$. Request = (1,0,2). Yes, (1,0,2) ≤ (3,3,2) ✅.

**Step 3 — Pretend to allocate (simulate):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Available' &= (3,3,2) - (1,0,2) = (2,3,0) \\
Allocation'[P_1] &= (2,0,0) + (1,0,2) = (3,0,2) \\
Need'[P_1] &= (1,2,2) - (1,0,2) = (0,2,0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Run Safety Algorithm on the new state.**

New Available = (2,3,0).

- P₀: Need (7,4,3) > (2,3,0) ❌
- P₁: Need (0,2,0) ≤ (2,3,0) ✅ → Work = (2,3,0) + (3,0,2) = (5,3,2)
- P₃: Need (0,1,1) ≤ (5,3,2) ✅ → Work = (5,3,2) + (2,1,1) = (7,4,3)
- P₀: Need (7,4,3) ≤ (7,4,3) ✅ → Work = (7,4,3) + (0,1,0) = (7,5,3)
- P₂: Need (6,0,0) ≤ (7,5,3) ✅ → Work = (10,5,5)
- P₄: Need (4,3,1) ≤ (10,5,5) ✅ → Work = (10,5,7)

All Finish = true → **Simulated state is SAFE**.

**Step 5 — Grant the request.**

Since the resulting state is safe, the actual values are committed:
- $Available = (2,3,0)$
- $Allocation[P_1] = (3,0,2)$
- $Need[P_1] = (0,2,0)$

**New Safe Sequence: ⟨ P₁, P₃, P₀, P₂, P₄ ⟩**

---

### 3.4 Counter-Example — When a Request Must Be Denied

Suppose now P₄ requests **Request₄ = (3, 3, 0)**.

**Step 1:** Need[P₄] = (4,3,1) ≥ (3,3,0) ✅.
**Step 2:** Available = (2,3,0) — but 3 > 2 for resource A. **Request₄ > Available** → **P₄ must WAIT**.

If we *forced* it anyway: Available' = (-1, 0, 0), which is **invalid**.

---

### 3.5 Algorithmic Pseudocode (Banker's Safety Algorithm)

```
Safety_Algorithm():
    Work = Available
    Finish[i] = false  for all i = 0 .. n-1
    
    found = true
    while found:
        found = false
        for i = 0 to n-1:
            if Finish[i] == false AND Need[i] <= Work:
                Work = Work + Allocation[i]
                Finish[i] = true
                found = true
    
    if all Finish[i] == true:
        return SAFE with sequence
    else:
        return UNSAFE
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Resource Allocation Graph — Schematic (Cycle-Free Example)

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1["R1 1 inst"] -- assigned --> P2["P2"]
    R2["R2 2 inst"] -- assigned --> P1["P1"]
    R2["R2 2 inst"] -- assigned --> P3["P3"]
    P1["P1"] -- requests --> R1["R1"]
    P2["P2"] -- requests --> R3["R3"]
    P3["P3"] -- requests --> R2["R2"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** P₁ holds one instance of R₂ and waits for R₁; P₂ holds R₁ and waits for R₃; P₃ holds one instance of R₂ and waits for another instance of R₂. **No cycle → no deadlock.**

### 4.2 Resource Allocation Graph — Schematic (With Deadlock Cycle)

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1["R1 1 inst"] -- assigned --> P1["P1"]
    R2["R2 1 inst"] -- assigned --> P2["P2"]
    R3["R3 1 inst"] -- assigned --> P3["P3"]
    P1["P1"] -- requests --> R2["R2"]
    P2["P2"] -- requests --> R3["R3"]
    P3["P3"] -- requests --> R1["R1"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Cycle exists: P₁ → R₂ → P₂ → R₃ → P₃ → R₁ → P₁. With **single-instance resources**, this cycle implies **a definite deadlock**.

### 4.3 Banker's Algorithm — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Process Pi raises Request vector"] --> B{"Request <= Need?"}
    B -- No --> X1["ERROR: Exceeded declared Max"]
    B -- Yes --> C{"Request <= Available?"}
    C -- No --> X2["Pi must WAIT"]
    C -- Yes --> D["Pretend to allocate"]
    D --> E["Update Available, Allocation, Need"]
    E --> F["Run Safety Algorithm"]
    F --> G{"State SAFE?"}
    G -- No --> H["ROLLBACK: Pi must WAIT"]
    G -- Yes --> I["COMMIT allocation to Pi"]
    I --> J["Resources granted to Pi"]
```

### 4.4 Safety Algorithm — Internal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Initialize Work = Available, Finish = false"] --> S2["Scan processes i"]
    S2 --> S3{"Finish i is false AND Need i <= Work?"}
    S3 -- No --> S2
    S3 -- Yes --> S4["Work = Work + Allocation i"]
    S4 --> S5["Finish i = true"]
    S5 --> S2
    S2 --> S6{"All Finish true?"}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7["SAFE STATE; output sequence"]
    S6 -- No --> S8["UNSAFE STATE"]
```

### 4.5 Deadlock Handling Strategy Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Deadlock Problem"] --> B["1. Ignore / Ostrich Algorithm"]
    A --> C["2. Detection & Recovery"]
    A --> D["3. Dynamic Avoidance"]
    A --> E["4. Prevention"]
    D --> D1["Bankers Algorithm"]
    D --> D2["Resource Allocation Graph RAG"]
    E --> E1["Mutual Exclusion"]
    E --> E2["Hold and Wait"]
    E --> E3["No Preemption"]
    E --> E4["Circular Wait"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)? Explain with a neat diagram how it is used to detect deadlock.*

**Model Answer:**
A Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) is a directed bipartite graph that represents the state of resource allocation among processes. It contains two sets of vertices: processes (P₁, P₂, ..., Pₙ) drawn as circles, and resource types (R₁, R₂, ..., Rₘ) drawn as rectangles (with dots inside indicating the number of instances).

Two types of directed edges exist:
- **Request edge:** Pᵢ → Rⱼ — process Pᵢ is requesting an instance of Rⱼ.
- **Assignment edge:** Rⱼ → Pᵢ — an instance of Rⱼ is allocated to Pᵢ.

If the graph contains **no cycle**, the system is deadlock-free. If the graph contains a **cycle**, then:
- If all resource types are **single-instance**, a deadlock **definitely exists**.
- If at least one resource type is **multi-instance**, a cycle only indicates the **possibility** of deadlock.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define Safe State and Safe Sequence in the context of Banker's Algorithm. Is an unsafe state always a deadlock state? Justify.*

**Model Answer:**
A state is called a **Safe State** if there exists a sequence of process execution ⟨P₁, P₂, ..., Pₙ⟩, called a **Safe Sequence**, such that for every process Pᵢ in the sequence, the resources that Pᵢ can still request (its remaining Need) can be satisfied by the currently available resources plus the resources held by all preceding processes in the sequence.

**No**, an unsafe state is **not always** a deadlock state. An unsafe state simply means that *there exists no guaranteed safe sequence*; the system *may* eventually deadlock if all processes simultaneously request their maximum remaining resources, but it may also continue running safely if processes release resources early or request less than their declared maximum. Deadlock is only a **certainty** when processes are actually permanently blocked.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **Banker's Algorithm** in detail. Discuss the data structures used and the algorithm with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following snapshot of a system:

| Process | Allocation | Max |
|---------|-----------|-----|
| | A B C D | A B C D |
| P₀ | 0 0 1 2 | 0 0 1 2 |
| P₁ | 2 0 0 0 | 2 7 5 0 |
| P₂ | 0 0 3 4 | 6 6 5 6 |
| P₃ | 1 0 0 0 | 4 3 5 6 |
| P₄ | 0 0 2 4 | 0 6 5 2 |

Total resources: A=6, B=7, C=8, D=10.

Using Banker's Algorithm:
- (i) Compute the Need matrix. **(2 Marks)**
- (ii) Is the system in a safe state? If yes, find the safe sequence. **(5 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Data Structures Used:**

- **Available:** Vector of length $m$. Indicates the number of available instances of each resource type.
- **Max:** $n \times m$ matrix. Defines the maximum demand of each process.
- **Allocation:** $n \times m$ matrix. Defines the number of resources of each type currently allocated to each process.
- **Need:** $n \times m$ matrix. Indicates the remaining resource need of each process.

$$
Need[i][j] = Max[i][j] - Allocation[i][j]
$$

**Safety Algorithm (Step-by-Step):**

1. Let $Work = Available$ and $Finish[i] = false$ for $i = 0, 1, ..., n-1$.
2. Find an index $i$ such that:
   - $Finish[i] == false$, AND
   - $Need[i] \le Work$.
3. If such $i$ exists, update:
   $$Work = Work + Allocation[i]$$
   $$Finish[i] = true$$
   Repeat from Step 2.
4. If no such $i$ exists, go to Step 5.
5. If all $Finish[i] == true$, the system is in a **safe state**; otherwise, it is **unsafe**.

**Resource Request Algorithm:**

When process $P_i$ requests $Request_i$:
1. If $Request_i \le Need_i$, go to Step 2; else **error** (process exceeded its declared Max).
2. If $Request_i \le Available$, go to Step 3; else $P_i$ must **wait**.
3. Pretend to allocate:
   $$Available = Available - Request_i$$
   $$Allocation_i = Allocation_i + Request_i$$
   $$Need_i = Need_i - Request_i$$
4. Run the Safety Algorithm on the new state. If safe, **commit**; else, **rollback** and $P_i$ must **wait**.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Need Matrix:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Need[P_0] &= (0,0,1,2) - (0,0,1,2) = (0,0,0,0) \\
Need[P_1] &= (2,7,5,0) - (2,0,0,0) = (0,7,5,0) \\
Need[P_2] &= (6,6,5,6) - (0,0,3,4) = (6,6,2,2) \\
Need[P_3] &= (4,3,5,6) - (1,0,0,0) = (3,3,5,6) \\
Need[P_4] &= (0,6,5,2) - (0,0,2,4) = (0,6,3,-2)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** $Need[P_4][D] = -2$ indicates the system snapshot is **invalid** (over-allocated). In a real KTU paper, such a row would be corrected by the student, or the question would not include this anomaly.

**(ii) Compute Available:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Available &= Total - \sum Allocation \\
&= (6,7,8,10) - (0+2+0+1+0,\; 0+0+0+0+0,\; 1+0+3+0+2,\; 2+0+4+0+4) \\
&= (6,7,8,10) - (3, 0, 6, 10) \\
&= (3, 7, 2, 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Safety Algorithm Execution (assuming corrected Need):**

$Work = (3,7,2,0)$, all $Finish[i] = false$.

| Iteration | Selected Process | Need ≤ Work? | New Work | Finish[i] |
|-----------|------------------|--------------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | P₀ | (0,0,0,0) ≤ (3,7,2,0) ✅ | (3,7,3,2) | true |
| 2 | P₃ | (3,3,5,6) > (3,7,3,2) ❌ | — | false |
| 2 | P₂ | (6,6,2,2) > (3,7,2,0) ❌ | — | false |
| 3 | P₄ | (0,6,3,1) ≤ (3,7,3,2) ✅ | (3,7,5,6) | true |
| 4 | P₃ | (3,3,5,6) ≤ (3,7,5,6) ✅ | (4,7,5,6) | true |
| 5 | P₂ | (6,6,2,2) ≤ (4,7,5,6) ❌ | — | false |
| 5 | P₁ | (0,7,5,0) ≤ (4,7,5,6) ✅ | (6,7,5,6) | true |
| 6 | P₂ | (6,6,2,2) ≤ (6,7,5,6) ✅ | (6,7,8,10) | true |

All Finish = true → **SAFE State**.

**Safe Sequence: ⟨ P₀, P₄, P₃, P₁, P₂ ⟩**

---

#### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **deadlock avoidance** using the **Resource Allocation Graph** scheme for a system with single instances of each resource type. Why is this scheme insufficient when resources have multiple instances? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a system with 5 processes P₀..P₄ and 3 resource types A (10 instances), B (5 instances), C (7 instances). The current state is given below:

| Process | Allocation (A B C) | Max (A B C) |
|---------|---------------------|--------------|
| P₀ | 0 1 0 | 7 5 3 |
| P₁ | 2 0 0 | 3 2 2 |
| P₂ | 3 0 2 | 9 0 2 |
| P₃ | 2 1 1 | 2 2 2 |
| P₄ | 0 0 2 | 4 3 3 |

Now P₂ requests (0, 1, 0). Determine using Banker's Algorithm whether the request can be granted immediately. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

In a system with **single-instance resources**, every resource type has exactly one instance. The Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) can be extended with **claim edges** (also called **future request edges**) to support deadlock avoidance.

**Claim Edge:** A claim edge $P_i \dashrightarrow R_j$ indicates that process $P_i$ may request resource $R_j$ sometime in the future. When $P_i$ actually requests $R_j$, the claim edge is converted to a **request edge**. When $R_j$ is released by $P_i$, the assignment edge is converted back to a **claim edge**.

**Avoidance Logic:**

A request $P_i \to R_j$ can be granted **only if converting the request edge to an assignment edge does NOT create a cycle** in the resulting graph. If a cycle would result, the request must be denied and $P_i$ must wait, even if the resource is technically free. This cycle check can be performed using DFS in $O(V+E)$ time.

**Why Insufficient for Multiple Instances:**

When resources have multiple instances, the RAG cycle test gives a **necessary but not sufficient** condition. Consider this example:
- R₁ has 2 instances.
- P₁ holds one instance and waits for R₂.
- P₂ holds the other instance and waits for R₁.

The RAG contains a cycle P₁ → R₂ → P₂ → R₁ → P₁, **yet no deadlock exists** — the moment P₁ or P₂ finishes, the other can proceed.

Thus, the RAG scheme works *only* for single-instance systems; the **Banker's Algorithm** is required for multi-instance resources.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Need matrix.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Need[P_0] &= (7,5,3) - (0,1,0) = (7,4,3) \\
Need[P_1] &= (3,2,2) - (2,0,0) = (1,2,2) \\
Need[P_2] &= (9,0,2) - (3,0,2) = (6,0,0) \\
Need[P_3] &= (2,2,2) - (2,1,1) = (0,1,1) \\
Need[P_4] &= (4,3,3) - (0,0,2) = (4,3,1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Available.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Available &= (10,5,7) - (0+2+3+2+0,\; 1+0+0+1+0,\; 0+0+2+1+2) \\
&= (10,5,7) - (7,2,5) \\
&= (3,3,2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify Request from P₂ = (0,1,0).**

- $Request_2 \le Need[P_2]$? $(0,1,0) \le (6,0,0)$? **No** — request exceeds declared need for resource B.

Therefore, the request is **invalid (error)** — process P₂ has exceeded its declared maximum for resource type B. The system raises an error condition, and the request is **rejected**.

(If the question intends a valid request, a corrected Request would have B = 0. In that case, Request₂ = (0,0,0) is trivially grantable.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $Request \le Need$ first.** Many students directly check $Request \le Available$ and skip the boundary error. This loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Not showing the rollback step.** If the simulated state is unsafe, you MUST explicitly say "the system rolls back the pretended allocation and the process is made to wait." Just saying "denied" loses marks.
> 3. **Confusing "Unsafe" with "Deadlocked."** An unsafe state is *not necessarily* a deadlock. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 4. **Arithmetic errors in Need computation** — verify $Max - Allocation$ for *every* cell. Tabulate, don't guess.
> 5. **Misidentifying the safe sequence.** Even if your Work vector is right, if the sequence order is wrong, you lose the final 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Resource Allocation Graphs & Banker's Algorithm**

- [ ] **RAG Vertices:** Two disjoint sets — Processes (circles) and Resources (rectangles with instance dots).
- [ ] **Two Edge Types:** Request edge `Pi → Rj` and Assignment edge `Rj → Pi`.
- [ ] **Claim Edge (`Pi ⇢ Rj`):** Used in deadlock avoidance to denote a *future possible* request.
- [ ] **Cycle Rule (Single-instance):** Cycle ⇒ definite deadlock. No cycle ⇒ no deadlock.
- [ ] **Cycle Rule (Multi-instance):** Cycle ⇒ *possible* deadlock only; use Banker's Algorithm.
- [ ] **Four Data Structures of Banker's Algorithm:** Available, Max, Allocation, Need.
- [ ] **Need Formula:** $Need[i][j] = Max[i][j] - Allocation[i][j]$.
- [ ] **Available Formula:** $Available = Total - \sum Allocation[i]$.
- [ ] **Safe State:** A safe sequence exists where each process can complete in some order.
- [ ] **Unsafe ≠ Deadlocked:** Unsafe means *no guaranteed safe sequence*; deadlock is *certain blocking*.
- [ ] **Safety Algorithm:** Initialize Work = Available, find process whose Need ≤ Work, repeat.
- [ ] **Request Algorithm:** Check $Request \le Need$ (else error), then $Request \le Available$ (else wait), then simulate and test safety.
- [ ] **Grant Rule:** Grant the request **only if** the simulated state is safe; otherwise, rollback and make process wait.
- [ ] **Banker's Algorithm Limitations:** Requires advance max-declaration; assumes fixed process count; conservative.
- [ ] **Time Complexity:** Safety Algorithm is $O(m \cdot n^2)$.
- [ ] **Real-World Use Cases:** Aerospace embedded systems, DBMS transaction managers, network routing protocols.

---

**End of Notes — Module 4: Resource Allocation Graphs, Deadlock Avoidance & Banker's Algorithm**
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PERUT638 — Compiler Design and Operating Systems*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Management: Concept of Address Spaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Memory Management: Concept of Address Spaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Address Space** is the range of addresses that a process uses in its instructions, defining the set of all logical (virtual) addresses that a process can reference. Each process operates within its **own private logical address space**, isolated from other processes in main memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board-Examiner Favorite)**
> The **Logical Address Space** is the set of all logical addresses generated by a program (CPU). The **Physical Address Space** is the set of all physical addresses corresponding to locations in main memory (RAM). The mapping between them is performed by the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** at run-time.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Hotel Room Numbering System**
>
> Imagine a luxury hotel where every guest is given a room number starting from **101** (their "logical address"). The hotel's front desk has a master registry mapping **Room 101 (Logical)** to the actual physical room on the **4th floor, wing B (Physical)**. When a guest asks for "Room 101", the concierge silently translates it to the real physical location. Two different guests can both have "Room 101" in their reservation confirmation, yet sleep in entirely different physical rooms — because the logical address is *always relative to their own booking*. This is exactly how a process's logical address space works in modern operating systems!

### 1.3 Classification of Addresses

| Address Type | Alternate Name | Generated By | Visible To |
|--------------|----------------|---------------|------------|
| **Logical Address** | Virtual Address | CPU (during program execution) | User Process |
| **Physical Address** | Real Address / Absolute Address | Memory Management Unit (MMU) | Main Memory Hardware |
| **Relative Address** | Offset | Compiler | Loader |

### 1.4 Address Binding – The Three Critical Phases

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Address Binding (Address Mapping)** is the act of mapping instructions and data from a logical address to a physical memory address. This binding can occur at **three distinct stages** of program lifecycle:

1. **Compile-Time Binding** — If the process will reside at a known fixed location in memory at compile time, the compiler generates **absolute code** (e.g., starting at location `0x0000`). The binding is fixed permanently.
2. **Load-Time Binding** — If the location is unknown at compile time, the compiler generates **relocatable code**. The final binding is delayed until the program is loaded into memory.
3. **Execution-Time (Run-Time) Binding** — Binding is postponed until *run time*. Supports process movement during execution; requires hardware support (MMU + base/limit registers).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Run-time binding is the most flexible and is used by virtually all modern general-purpose operating systems (Windows, Linux, macOS) because it enables **swapping, paging, and dynamic memory allocation**.

### 1.5 Memory Management Unit (MMU) – The Hardware Translator

The **MMU** is a dedicated hardware chip (or a CPU-integrated circuit) that performs **on-the-fly translation** of every logical address into a physical address during program execution. The CPU never directly accesses physical memory; it always generates logical addresses, which the MMU resolves.

### 1.6 Base Register & Limit Register – The Address Guardians

- **Base Register (Relocation Register):** Stores the *starting physical address* of a process in memory.
- **Limit Register:** Stores the *size (length)* of the process's address space (boundary value).

Any logical address generated by the CPU is **validated against the limit register** before translation. If the address exceeds the limit, a **trap (memory protection fault)** is raised by the OS.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Address Translation Equation (Core Operation)

The MMU performs a single, lightning-fast operation for every memory reference made by the CPU:

$$\text{Physical Address} = \text{Base Register Value} + \text{Logical Address (Offset)}$$

This is a simple addition, but the MMU also performs a **bound check** before the addition:

$$\text{If } \text{Logical Address} \geq \text{Limit Register} \Rightarrow \text{Trap to OS (Segmentation Fault)}$$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of MMU Operation

- **Step 1:** CPU generates a logical address `L` for a memory reference.
- **Step 2:** MMU compares `L` against the value stored in the **Limit Register**.
- **Step 3:** If `L ≥ Limit`, the MMU sends an interrupt/trap to the OS (protection violation).
- **Step 4:** If `L < Limit`, the MMU performs **Base + Offset** addition.
- **Step 5:** The resulting **Physical Address** is sent to the memory bus for actual read/write.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Boundary Condition | Unit | Purpose |
|-------------------|------------------------|-------------------|------|---------|
| Physical Address Calculation | $\text{PA} = \text{Base} + \text{Logical Address}$ | $\text{LA} < \text{Limit}$ | Bytes / Words | Translate virtual to physical |
| Memory Protection Check | $\text{LA} < \text{Limit Register}$ | Trap if violated | — | Process Isolation |
| Relocation | $\text{Relocated PA} = \text{Old PA} + \Delta$ | where $\Delta = \text{New Base} - \text{Old Base}$ | Bytes | Move process in memory |
| Swapping Time | $T_{\text{swap}} = T_{\text{roll out}} + T_{\text{roll in}}$ | $T_{\text{roll out}} = T_{\text{roll in}}$ | Milliseconds | Total swap cost |
| Total Swap Time | $T_{\text{total}} = n \cdot T_{\text{swap}}$ | where $n$ = processes | Milliseconds | Multi-process context |
| Effective Access Time (EAT) | $\text{EAT} = (1-p) \cdot T_{\text{mem}} + p \cdot T_{\text{trap}}$ | $p$ = page fault probability | Nanoseconds | With paging overhead |
| Logical Address Range | $[0, \, 2^{n} - 1]$ | where $n$ = address bits | — | Address space size |

### 2.4 Why Run-Time Binding? (The 'Why' Behind It)

- **Flexibility:** A process can be moved anywhere in physical memory during execution.
- **Swapping Support:** Enables the OS to roll a process out to disk and roll it back into a *different* physical location.
- **Memory Sharing:** Multiple processes can share code (e.g., shared libraries) while maintaining separate logical views.
- **Memory Protection:** Each process's limit register prevents illegal access outside its own memory.

### 2.5 Swapping – Standard OS Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> **Swapping** is a memory management technique in which a process can be temporarily swapped **out** of main memory to a **backing store** (e.g., disk), and later swapped **back into memory** for continued execution.

- **Roll Out / Swap Out:** Moving a process from RAM to the backing store.
- **Roll In / Swap In:** Moving a process from the backing store back to RAM.
- **Standard Swapping:** Major time overhead — entire process must be moved.
- **Mobile OS Variant:** Modern Android/iOS do NOT swap entire processes (due to memory + flash wear concerns). They use **trimming** (reclaiming pages from background apps) instead.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Virtualization:** Hypervisors (VMware, VirtualBox) use address space abstraction to run multiple VMs on one physical machine.
- **Containerization:** Docker containers use Linux **namespaces** to provide isolated logical address spaces.
- **Java/JVM:** The JVM provides every Java application with a virtual address space, independent of underlying OS.
- **Embedded Systems:** Real-time OS (RTOS) use static address binding for predictable performance in automotive/aerospace systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Address Translation with MMU

**Problem Statement:**
A process is loaded into memory with **Base Register = 14000** and **Limit Register = 3000**. The CPU generates a logical address of **346**. Calculate the corresponding physical address. Is the access legal?

---

### Step 1: Identify the Given Parameters

$$\text{Base Register} = 14000$$

$$\text{Limit Register} = 3000$$

$$\text{Logical Address (Offset)} = 346$$

---

### Step 2: Apply the Memory Protection Check

The MMU must first verify that the logical address lies within the limit:

$$\text{Is } \text{Logical Address} < \text{Limit Register} ?$$

$$\text{Is } 346 < 3000 ?$$

$$\text{Result: } 346 < 3000 \rightarrow \text{TRUE (Access is Legal)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Explanation:** Since the offset (346) is less than the limit (3000), the requested memory location falls within the process's legal address space. The MMU proceeds to translation.

---

### Step 3: Apply the Address Translation Formula

Using the core MMU translation equation:

$$\text{Physical Address} = \text{Base Register} + \text{Logical Address}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\text{Physical Address} = 14000 + 346$$

$$\text{Physical Address} = 14346$$

---

### Step 4: Verify the Physical Address Falls in Legal Memory Range

$$\text{Range Check: } \text{Base} \leq \text{PA} < (\text{Base} + \text{Limit})$$

$$14000 \leq 14346 < (14000 + 3000)$$

$$14000 \leq 14346 < 17000 \rightarrow \text{TRUE}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The logical address `346` of the process corresponds to physical address `14346` in main memory.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: Illegal Access Detection

**Problem Statement:** Same process (Base = 14000, Limit = 3000). CPU generates logical address **4300**. What happens?

---

### Step 1: Apply Protection Check

$$\text{Is } 4300 < 3000 ?$$

$$\text{Result: } 4300 \geq 3000 \rightarrow \text{FALSE (Illegal Access)}$$

---

### Step 2: MMU Response

$$\text{MMU generates a TRAP} \rightarrow \text{OS handles as Segmentation Fault / Memory Protection Error}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Examiner Insight:** The protection check happens **BEFORE** the address translation. The CPU never even tries to access memory at physical address 18300. This prevents one process from corrupting another process's memory — the foundation of **process isolation** in modern OS.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example: Swapping Time Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A system's average swap-out time is **50 ms**, average swap-in time is **50 ms**, and the average memory transfer time is **40 ms**. Calculate total swap time per process.

---

### Step 1: Identify Given Parameters

$$T_{\text{roll out}} = 50 \, \text{ms}$$

$$T_{\text{roll in}} = 50 \, \text{ms}$$

$$T_{\text{transfer}} = 40 \, \text{ms}$$

---

### Step 2: Apply the Swap Time Formula

$$\text{Total Swap Time} = T_{\text{roll out}} + T_{\text{transfer}} + T_{\text{roll in}}$$

$$\text{Total Swap Time} = 50 + 40 + 50$$

$$\text{Total Swap Time} = 140 \, \text{ms}$$

---

### Step 3: Calculate Multi-Process Swap Time (if 5 processes are swapped)

$$\text{Total Time} = n \times T_{\text{swap}}$$

$$\text{Total Time} = 5 \times 140 = 700 \, \text{ms}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Address Binding Comparison

| Parameter | Compile-Time Binding | Load-Time Binding | Run-Time Binding |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **When binding occurs** | Before program execution | During loading | During execution |
| **Code type** | Absolute code | Relocatable code | Relocatable code |
| **Process movement allowed?** | ❌ No | ❌ No (before execution) | ✅ Yes |
| **Swapping supported?** | ❌ No | ❌ Limited | ✅ Full |
| **Hardware support (MMU)** | ❌ Not required | ❌ Not required | ✅ Required |
| **Used in modern OS?** | ❌ Rare | ⚠️ Some embedded systems | ✅ Universal |
| **Performance overhead** | None | Negligible | Small (MMU delay) |
| **Flexibility** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Effective Access Time (EAT)

**Problem Statement:** Memory access time is **200 ns**. With paging support, page service time including trap is **25 μs**. If the page fault rate is **p = 0.001**, calculate EAT.

---

### Step 1: Identify Parameters

$$T_{\text{mem}} = 200 \, \text{ns} = 0.2 \, \mu\text{s}$$

$$T_{\text{trap}} = 25 \, \mu\text{s}$$

$$p = 0.001$$

---

### Step 2: Apply EAT Formula

$$\text{EAT} = (1 - p) \cdot T_{\text{mem}} + p \cdot T_{\text{trap}}$$

$$\text{EAT} = (1 - 0.001)(0.2) + (0.001)(25)$$

$$\text{EAT} = (0.999)(0.2) + 0.025$$

$$\text{EAT} = 0.1998 + 0.025$$

$$\text{EAT} = 0.2248 \, \mu\text{s} = 224.8 \, \text{ns}$$

---

### Step 3: Performance Impact Analysis

$$\text{Slowdown Factor} = \frac{\text{EAT}}{T_{\text{mem}}} = \frac{224.8}{200} = 1.124$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A page fault rate of just **0.1%** slows down memory access by **~12.4%**. This is why optimizing page fault rate is critical in OS design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MMU Address Translation Process (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPU[CPU Generates Logical Address LA]
    L1{LA less than Limit Register}
    TRAP[Trap to OS: Memory Protection Violation]
    ADD[MMU Performs Addition Base + LA]
    PA[Physical Address Sent to Memory Bus]
    RAM[Main Memory Read or Write Operation]
    RES[Result Returned to CPU]
    CPU --> L1
    L1 -->|FALSE| TRAP
    L1 -->|TRUE| ADD
    ADD --> PA
    PA --> RAM
    RAM --> RES
```

---

### 4.2 Address Binding Lifecycle (Conceptual Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Source Code]
    S2[Compiler Generates Object Code]
    S3{Address Binding Type}
    S4A[Compile-Time Binding: Absolute Code]
    S4B[Load-Time Binding: Relocatable Code]
    S4C[Run-Time Binding: MMU Translation]
    S5[Process Executed in Memory]
    S6[Process Can Be Swapped Out and In]
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 -->|Stage 1| S4A
    S3 -->|Stage 2| S4B
    S3 -->|Stage 3| S4C
    S4A --> S5
    S4B --> S5
    S4C --> S5
    S4C --> S6
```

---

### 4.3 Logical vs Physical Address Space Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LA[Logical Address Space of Process]
        L0[0]
        L1[1]
        L2[2]
        L3[3]
        L4[4]
        L5[5]
    end
    subgraph PA[Physical Address Space of RAM]
        P14000[14000]
        P14001[14001]
        P14002[14002]
        P14003[14003]
        P14004[14004]
        P14005[14005]
    end
    MMU2[MMU Adds Base 14000]
    L0 -.-> MMU2
    L1 -.-> MMU2
    L2 -.-> MMU2
    L3 -.-> MMU2
    L4 -.-> MMU2
    L5 -.-> MMU2
    MMU2 --> P14000
    MMU2 --> P14001
    MMU2 --> P14002
    MMU2 --> P14003
    MMU2 --> P14004
    MMU2 --> P14005
```

---

### 4.4 Swapping Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process P1 in Memory at Address X]
    B[OS Decides to Roll Out P1]
    C[Copy Entire P1 to Backing Store Disk]
    D[Free Memory Frame for Other Process]
    E[OS Schedules P1 Again]
    F[Roll In P1 from Backing Store]
    G[P1 May Be Loaded at New Address Y]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
```

---

### 4.5 Base and Limit Register Protection Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Base Register: 14000]
    B[Process Limit Register: 3000]
    C[Valid Range: 14000 to 16999]
    D[CPU Request for Logical Address]
    E{Check: LA less than Limit}
    F[Allow Access: Translate and Send]
    G[Block Access: Generate Trap]
    D --> E
    E -->|TRUE| F
    E -->|FALSE| G
    A -.-> C
    B -.-> C
```

---

### 4.6 Three Address Binding Phases Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE1[Compile Time Binding]
        A1[Source Code]
        A2[Compiler Knows Absolute Location]
        A3[Generates Absolute Addresses]
    end
    subgraph PHASE2[Load Time Binding]
        B1[Relocatable Code]
        B2[Loader Resolves Addresses]
        B3[Fixed at Load Time]
    end
    subgraph PHASE3[Run Time Binding]
        C1[Relocatable Code]
        C2[MMU Translates Every Reference]
        C3[Process Can Move Freely]
    end
    PHASE1 --> PHASE2
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the terms: (i) Logical Address Space, (ii) Physical Address Space. How are they related?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **(i) Logical Address Space** is the set of all logical addresses generated by a program (CPU) during its execution. It is independent of the actual physical memory and typically starts from address 0. It is also called the virtual address space.
>
> **(ii) Physical Address Space** is the set of all physical addresses corresponding to locations in the main memory (RAM) of the computer system.
>
> **Relationship:** During execution, every logical address generated by the CPU is translated by the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** into a corresponding physical address using the formula:
>
> $$\text{Physical Address} = \text{Base Register Value} + \text{Logical Address}$$
>
> This binding occurs at **run-time** in modern OS using relocation registers.

**[Mark Distribution: Definition × 2 marks + Relationship explanation × 1 mark]**

---

#### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Explain the role of the base register and limit register in memory protection.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Base Register** (or Relocation Register) stores the starting physical address of a process in memory. The **Limit Register** stores the size (length) of the process's address space.
>
> When the CPU generates a logical address, the MMU first compares the offset against the limit register:
>
> $$\text{If } \text{Logical Address} \geq \text{Limit Register} \rightarrow \text{Trap (Memory Protection Violation)}$$
>
> If valid, the MMU computes **Physical Address = Base + Logical Address**. This mechanism ensures that one process cannot access memory outside its allocated region, providing **process isolation** and **memory protection**.

**[Mark Distribution: Base register role × 1 mark + Limit register role × 1 mark + Protection mechanism × 1 mark]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of address binding in operating systems. Discuss the different stages at which address binding can occur. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Memory Management Unit (MMU) with a suitable diagram. How does it translate logical addresses to physical addresses using base and limit registers? Solve: Base = 30000, Limit = 12000, Logical Address = 7500. Is the access valid? Find the physical address. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Address Binding:**

**Address Binding Definition:**
Address binding refers to the mapping of instructions and data in a process from logical addresses to physical addresses in main memory.

**Three Stages of Address Binding:**

**Stage 1 — Compile-Time Binding:**
- If the memory location is known at compile time, the compiler generates **absolute code**.
- The process will always be loaded at the same physical address.
- Example: Embedded systems with fixed memory layouts.
- **Limitation:** Cannot move the process after compilation.

**Stage 2 — Load-Time Binding:**
- The compiler generates **relocatable code** (uses relative addresses).
- The loader resolves actual physical addresses when the program is loaded into memory.
- **Limitation:** Once loaded, the process cannot move during execution.

**Stage 3 — Execution-Time (Run-Time) Binding:**
- Final binding is postponed until the program actually executes.
- Requires **hardware support** (MMU and relocation registers).
- Process can be moved during execution (enables swapping and paging).
- **Used in all modern OS** (Windows, Linux, Unix).

**Diagram:**

```
Source Code → Compiler → Object Code → Linker → Executable → Loader → Memory
   ↓              ↓             ↓             ↓              ↓
[Compile]   [Compile Time]   [Relocatable] [Link Time]   [Load Time]
                                                       [Execution Time = MMU]
```

**Conclusion:** Run-time binding is the most flexible and is preferred in multiprogramming environments.

**[Marks: Definition × 1 + 3 stages × 2 each = 7 marks]**

---

#### **Part (b) — MMU and Address Translation:**

**Memory Management Unit (MMU):**
The MMU is a hardware device that performs **run-time address translation**. The CPU generates logical addresses; the MMU translates them to physical addresses using the relocation register (base) and validates them using the limit register.

**Diagram (MMU Operation):**

```
   ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  CPU → Logical Address               │
   │          ↓                            │
   │   MMU: Compare with Limit Register    │
   │          ↓ (if valid)                 │
   │   Physical Address = Base + Offset    │
   │          ↓                            │
   │   Physical Memory Access              │
   └──────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Base Register = 30000
- Limit Register = 12000
- Logical Address = 7500

**Step 1 — Protection Check:**

$$\text{Is } 7500 < 12000 \, ? \rightarrow \text{YES (Access Valid)}$$

**Step 2 — Address Translation:**

$$\text{Physical Address} = \text{Base} + \text{Logical Address}$$

$$\text{Physical Address} = 30000 + 7500$$

$$\text{Physical Address} = 37500$$

**Step 3 — Range Verification:**

$$\text{Range: } 30000 \leq 37500 < (30000 + 12000) = 42000 \, \rightarrow \text{VALID}$$

> **Conclusion:** The logical address `7500` is valid and maps to physical address `37500`.

**[Marks: MMU diagram × 2 + Working × 2 + Numerical × 3 = 7 marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of swapping in operating systems. Discuss standard swapping and its variation in mobile operating systems. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define address space. Differentiate between logical and physical address space. A process has logical address space of 16 pages and page size is 4 KB. Calculate the logical address space size. If the physical memory has 32 frames, what is the size of physical address space? **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Swapping:**

**Definition:**
Swapping is a memory management technique in which a process can be temporarily moved from main memory to a backing store (secondary storage, usually a fast disk) and later brought back into memory for continued execution.

**Operations Involved:**
- **Roll Out / Swap Out:** Moving a process from RAM to disk.
- **Roll In / Swap In:** Moving a process from disk back to RAM.

**Standard Swapping:**
- Used in traditional time-sharing systems.
- Entire process is moved as a single unit.
- Requires run-time address binding (MMU).
- The OS maintains a **swap partition** on disk.
- Swap time depends on memory size and disk transfer rate.

**Mobile OS Swapping Variation:**
- **Android/iOS do NOT swap entire processes** between RAM and disk.
- Reasons:
  1. Flash memory has limited write cycles (wear).
  2. Swapping drains battery quickly.
  3. App launch time must be minimal.
- Instead, mobile OS uses **memory trimming** — reclaiming pages from background apps via `Application.onTrimMemory()` (Android) or memory warnings (iOS).

**Swapping Time Formula:**

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{roll out}} + T_{\text{transfer}} + T_{\text{roll in}}$$

**[Marks: Definition × 2 + Standard swap × 2 + Mobile variation × 2 + Formula × 1 = 7 marks]**

---

#### **Part (b) — Address Space Calculation:**

**Definition:**
Address Space is the range of all valid addresses that can be generated by a process (logical) or that exist in physical memory (physical).

**Difference Between Logical and Physical Address Space:**

| Aspect | Logical Address Space | Physical Address Space |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Generated by** | CPU | Memory hardware |
| **Visible to** | Process (user) | OS / Hardware |
| **Starts from** | 0 | 0 (RAM address) |
| **Size depends on** | CPU architecture (address bits) | Actual RAM installed |
| **Translated by** | — | MMU |

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Number of pages = 16
- Page size = 4 KB = $4 \times 1024$ bytes = 4096 bytes

**Step 1 — Calculate Logical Address Space Size:**

$$\text{Logical Address Space} = \text{Number of Pages} \times \text{Page Size}$$

$$\text{Logical Address Space} = 16 \times 4 \, \text{KB}$$

$$\text{Logical Address Space} = 64 \, \text{KB}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Physical Address Space Size:**

Given: Number of frames = 32

$$\text{Physical Address Space} = \text{Number of Frames} \times \text{Frame Size}$$

$$\text{Physical Address Space} = 32 \times 4 \, \text{KB}$$

$$\text{Physical Address Space} = 128 \, \text{KB}$$

> **Conclusion:** Logical address space is **64 KB** and physical address space is **128 KB** for the given system.

**[Marks: Definition × 1 + Difference × 2 + Logical calc × 2 + Physical calc × 2 = 7 marks]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the Protection Check:** Students often directly compute `PA = Base + LA` without first verifying that `LA < Limit`. This loses **1–2 marks** per question. **Always show the limit check first.**
>
> 2. **Confusing Logical with Physical Address:** Many students mix up the terms. Remember: **Logical = Virtual = CPU-generated**. **Physical = Real = RAM address**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Diagram in 7-Mark Questions:** A neat, labeled MMU diagram is **mandatory** for full marks in KTU valuation. Even a simple block diagram earns 1.5–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Wrong Unit Conversions:** When given page/frame sizes in KB, convert to bytes explicitly: **1 KB = 1024 bytes**. Forgetting this loses calculation marks.
>
> 5. **Not Stating Address Binding Stage:** When asked about address translation, always specify whether binding is compile-time, load-time, or run-time — examiners look for this keyword.
>
> 6. **Incomplete Mobile Swapping Answer:** Do NOT say mobile OS does NOT swap. The correct answer is that they **swap pages selectively via trimming**, not entire processes.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Concept of Address Spaces**
>
> - ⭐ **Logical Address** = Virtual address generated by CPU; starts from 0.
> - ⭐ **Physical Address** = Real RAM location; translated by MMU.
> - ⭐ **MMU** = Hardware device that performs run-time address translation.
> - ⭐ **Base Register** = Stores starting physical address of a process.
> - ⭐ **Limit Register** = Stores size of process's address space (boundary check).
> - ⭐ **Core Formula:** `Physical Address = Base Register + Logical Address`
> - ⭐ **Protection Check:** `Logical Address < Limit Register` (else TRAP).
> - ⭐ **3 Stages of Address Binding:** Compile-Time → Load-Time → Run-Time.
> - ⭐ **Run-Time Binding** is most flexible; used by all modern OS; requires MMU.
> - ⭐ **Swapping** = Roll Out + Roll In between RAM and Backing Store.
> - ⭐ **Mobile OS does NOT swap entire processes**; uses memory trimming instead.
> - ⭐ **Logical Address Space Size** = Pages × Page Size.
> - ⭐ **Physical Address Space Size** = Frames × Frame Size.
> - ⭐ **Effective Access Time (EAT)** = `(1-p)·Tmem + p·Ttrap`
> - ⭐ Modern OS uses **run-time binding** with MMU and relocation registers for maximum flexibility and protection.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Contiguous Memory Allocation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Contiguous Memory Allocation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Contiguous Memory Allocation (CMA)** is a classical memory management scheme in which **each process is allocated a single, continuous block of physical memory** (a contiguous range of addresses) during its execution. The Operating System maintains a table tracking which memory regions are currently allocated to processes and which are free (holes), and uses allocation strategies to assign these free blocks to incoming processes.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PERUT638 – Compiler Design and Operating Systems, Module 4), CMA is treated as the foundational model for **Main Memory Management**, preceding advanced schemes like Paging and Segmentation.

**Key Terminology (Board-Examiner Approved):**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Hole** | A contiguous block of free (unallocated) physical memory available for use. |
| **Base Register** | Hardware register holding the smallest physical address of a process's allocated block. |
| **Limit Register** | Hardware register holding the size (length) of the allocated block; used for protection. |
| **Logical (Relative) Address** | Address generated by the CPU, ranging from `0` to `max`. |
| **Physical Address** | Actual address in main memory: `Base + Logical Address`. |
| **Internal Fragmentation** | Wasted space **inside** an allocated partition (partition > process). |
| **External Fragmentation** | Total free memory exists, but no single hole is large enough for a request. |
| **Compaction** | OS technique to shuffle memory contents to merge scattered holes. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hotel Room Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — A Hotel with Variable-Sized Rooms**

Imagine a hotel where rooms are not pre-built. When a guest (process) arrives, the manager (OS) gives them a **continuous stretch of rooms** of *exactly* the size they need, somewhere along a corridor (RAM).

- A solo traveler asks for **1 room** → manager finds a **1-room gap** somewhere in the corridor.
- A family of four asks for **4 adjacent rooms** → manager finds a **contiguous 4-room gap**.
- Over time, guests leave, creating **scattered empty rooms (holes)**. The corridor now has gaps — but if a family of 4 arrives and no 4 adjacent rooms exist (even though total empty rooms = 6), they are **rejected**. This is **External Fragmentation**.

This is exactly how CMA works in RAM: the process must occupy *one continuous chunk* — a severe constraint that motivates Paging (Module 5).

### 1.3 Memory Protection — The Base/Limit Register Pair

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The CPU's **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** performs address translation:
> $$\text{Physical Address} = \text{Base Register} + \text{Logical Address}$$
> Protection is enforced by checking: `0 ≤ Logical Address < Limit Register`. Any violation triggers a **trap** to the OS (illegal memory access).

Standard metrics used in KTU problems:

- **Partition Size = 200 KB** (fixed unit used as standard KTU example size)
- **Page Size = 4 KB** (standard reference for later modules)
- **Effective Access Time (EAT)** = *(1 – p) × ma + p × (ma + mmu_time)*, where `p` is the page-fault probability and `ma` is the memory access time.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Major Schemes of Contiguous Allocation

### A) Fixed (Static) Partitioning

- Memory is divided into **N fixed-size partitions at system boot**.
- Each partition can hold **exactly one process**.
- **Degree of multiprogramming = number of partitions**.
- Suffers from **Internal Fragmentation** (unused space inside a partition).

### B) Variable (Dynamic) Partitioning

- Partitions are created **on-demand**, sized to match the process.
- No internal fragmentation.
- Suffers from **External Fragmentation** as processes come and go.

---

## 2.2 Allocation Strategies (Variable Partitioning)

These are the algorithms an OS uses to choose *which hole* to allocate to an incoming process. KTU board questions frequently test these.

### (i) First-Fit
- Scan memory from the **beginning**; allocate the **first hole** big enough.
- **Advantage:** Fast, simple.
- **Disadvantage:** Tends to fragment the front of memory.

### (ii) Best-Fit
- Search the **entire** list; allocate the **smallest hole** that is big enough.
- **Advantage:** Minimizes leftover wasted space per allocation.
- **Disadvantage:** Produces many tiny unusable holes ("splinters").

### (iii) Worst-Fit
- Allocate the **largest hole** available.
- **Advantage:** Leaves the largest possible remainder.
- **Disadvantage:** Quickly destroys large free blocks.

### (iv) Next-Fit
- Like First-Fit, but continues the search from **where the last search ended** (circular scan).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD — Memorize the following table for KTU University Exam.**

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Physical Address Translation | `PA = Base + Logical Address` | bytes |
| Memory Protection Check | `0 ≤ LA < Limit` | boolean; trap if violated |
| Internal Fragmentation | `IF = Partition Size – Process Size` | KB / MB |
| Total Internal Frag (Fixed) | `ΣIF_i` over all partitions | KB |
| External Fragmentation | `EF = Total Free Memory – Largest Single Free Block` | KB |
| Available Memory Ratio | `(Total Free) / (Total RAM) × 100%` | percentage |
| Memory Utilization | `Σ(Process Size) / Total RAM × 100%` | percentage |
| Degree of Multiprogramming | `min(⌊Total RAM / Avg Process Size⌋, N_partitions)` | integer |
| Compaction Time (approx.) | `T = Σ (Memory to Copy) / Disk Bandwidth` | ms / s |
| Effective Access Time (with MMU) | `EAT = (1 – p)·ma + p·(ma + mmu_time)` | ns |
| Hole Merging Condition | `Hole[i].end = Hole[i+1].start` | adjacency test |
| Compaction Necessity | `EF > 0 AND Largest_Hole < Incoming_Process` | trigger |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall:** In KTU valuation, the examiner awards partial marks **only** for explicitly stating the *strategy name* AND *showing the search/selection logic*. Writing "best fit is used" without showing the comparison earns **0/3 marks**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems (RTOS)** | Fixed CMA is used in real-time systems (e.g., automotive ECUs) for deterministic allocation. |
| **Early Mainframes (IBM 360)** | First-Fit was the original IBM OS strategy. |
| **Microcontroller Firmware** | Static partitions for bootloader / kernel / app regions. |
| **Kernel Memory Allocators** | Linux `SLAB` and `SLUB` are CMA-style contiguous region allocators. |
| **GPU Memory Pools** | CUDA unified memory uses contiguous buffers for kernels. |
| **Database Buffers** | InnoDB buffer pool uses contiguous chunks for index pages. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Galvin-Style KTU Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU University Exam Question Type** (frequently repeated, Dec 2021, June 2022, Dec 2023 patterns).

### Problem Statement

Given free memory holes of sizes: **100 KB, 500 KB, 200 KB, 300 KB, 600 KB** (in that order).
A new sequence of process requests arrives:

| Process | Size (KB) |
|---|---|
| P1 | 212 |
| P2 | 417 |
| P3 | 112 |
| P4 | 426 |

Show the memory layout after each allocation under **(a) First-Fit**, **(b) Best-Fit**, and **(c) Worst-Fit**. Compute fragmentation statistics.

---

### (a) First-Fit Allocation

**Rule:** Allocate the **first hole** (from start) that is large enough.

| Step | Process | Size | Hole Chosen | Hole Before | Hole After Allocation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1 | 212 | 500 KB (3rd hole) | 500 | **288** |
| 2 | P2 | 417 | 600 KB (5th hole) | 600 | **183** |
| 3 | P3 | 112 | 288 KB (remainder of 500) | 288 | **176** |
| 4 | P4 | 426 | None fits | — | **Rejected** |

**Memory State after First-Fit:**

$$
