\begin{aligned}
\text{Hole 1: } & 100 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Hole 2: } & 176 \text{ KB (remainder of original 500)} \\
\text{Hole 3: } & 200 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Hole 4: } & 300 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Hole 5: } & 183 \text{ KB (remainder of original 600)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Fragmentation Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Free Memory} &= 100 + 176 + 200 + 300 + 183 = 959 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Largest Single Hole} &= 300 \text{ KB} \\
\text{External Fragmentation} &= 959 - 300 = 659 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Internal Fragmentation} &= 0 \text{ KB} \quad (\text{variable partition scheme})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** P4 (426 KB) is **rejected** because `426 > 300` (largest hole), even though `426 < 959` (total free).

---

### (b) Best-Fit Allocation

**Rule:** Allocate the **smallest hole** that is large enough.

| Step | Process | Size | Sorted Holes Before | Hole Chosen | Holes After |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1 | 212 | 100, 200, 300, 500, 600 | **300** | 100, 200, 288, 600 |
| 2 | P2 | 417 | 100, 200, 288, 600 | **600** | 100, 200, 288, 183 |
| 3 | P3 | 112 | 100, 200, 288, 183 | **200** | 100, 112, 183, 288 |
| 4 | P4 | 426 | 100, 112, 183, 288 | **None** | Rejected |

**Memory State after Best-Fit:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hole 1: } & 100 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Hole 2: } & 112 \text{ KB (remainder of 200)} \\
\text{Hole 3: } & 183 \text{ KB (remainder of 600)} \\
\text{Hole 4: } & 288 \text{ KB (remainder of 300)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Fragmentation Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Free Memory} &= 100 + 112 + 183 + 288 = 683 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Largest Single Hole} &= 288 \text{ KB} \\
\text{External Fragmentation} &= 683 - 288 = 395 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Observation:** Best-Fit produced **more small holes** (112, 100) — the classic "splinter" problem.

---

### (c) Worst-Fit Allocation

**Rule:** Allocate the **largest available hole** always.

| Step | Process | Size | Sorted Holes Before | Hole Chosen | Holes After |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1 | 212 | 100, 200, 300, 500, 600 | **600** | 100, 200, 300, 500, 388 |
| 2 | P2 | 417 | 100, 200, 300, 500, 388 | **500** | 100, 200, 300, 83, 388 |
| 3 | P3 | 112 | 100, 200, 300, 83, 388 | **388** | 100, 200, 300, 83, 276 |
| 4 | P4 | 426 | 100, 200, 300, 83, 276 | **None** | Rejected |

**Fragmentation Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Free Memory} &= 100 + 200 + 300 + 83 + 276 = 959 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Largest Single Hole} &= 300 \text{ KB} \\
\text{External Fragmentation} &= 959 - 300 = 659 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Metric | First-Fit | Best-Fit | Worst-Fit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Free Memory (post-allocation) | 959 KB | 683 KB | 959 KB |
| Largest Single Hole | 300 KB | 288 KB | 300 KB |
| External Fragmentation | 659 KB | 395 KB | 659 KB |
| Number of Holes | 5 | 4 | 5 |
| Speed of Allocation | **Fastest** | Slowest (full search) | Slow (full search) |
| Splinter Generation | Moderate | **Highest** | Lowest |
| Real OS Usage | Linux `first_fit` | Memory-critical apps | Rare |

---

## 3.3 Compaction — The Cure for External Fragmentation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compaction** is a technique where the OS shuffles allocated blocks to **merge all holes into one large contiguous free block**.

**Procedure:**

1. Halt all running processes (or use **relocation registers** to allow live shuffling).
2. Slide all allocated blocks toward the bottom (or one end) of memory.
3. The merged hole appears at the top (or bottom).

**Cost Model:**

$$
T_{\text{compact}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{\text{Size}(P_i)}{\text{Bandwidth}_{\text{memcopy}}}
$$

**Disadvantages of Compaction:**

- **CPU-bound** — wastes processor time.
- **I/O problems** — if a process is mid-I/O, moving its buffer corrupts the device register addressing (must be pinned or use virtual I/O).
- Practically infeasible for real-time systems.

---

## 3.4 Address Translation Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem:** A process is loaded with Base Register = **300040** and Limit Register = **120900**. The CPU generates a logical address of **43008**. Find the physical address and verify protection.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical Address (PA)} &= \text{Base} + \text{Logical Address} \\
&= 300040 + 43008 \\
&= 343048
\end{aligned}
$$

**Protection Check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Condition: } & 0 \le \text{Logical Address} < \text{Limit} \\
& 0 \le 43008 < 120900 \quad \rightarrow \text{TRUE} \quad \rightarrow \text{Access GRANTED}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the CPU had generated `Logical Address = 200000`, the check `200000 < 120900` would **FAIL**, and the MMU would raise a **segmentation fault trap** to the OS.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Contiguous Memory Layout — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RAM["Physical RAM (1024 KB)"]
        direction TB
        OS["OS Kernel and Drivers (Low Addresses)"]
        P1["Process P1 (Block of 212 KB)"]
        P2["Process P2 (Block of 417 KB)"]
        P3["Process P3 (Block of 112 KB)"]
        H1["Hole 1 (100 KB)"]
        H2["Hole 2 (176 KB)"]
        H3["Hole 3 (200 KB)"]
        H4["Hole 4 (300 KB)"]
        H5["Hole 5 (183 KB)"]
    end

    OS --- P1
    P1 --- P2
    P2 --- P3
    P3 --- H1
    H1 --- H2
    H2 --- H3
    H3 --- H4
    H4 --- H5

    style OS fill:#2E4053,color:#fff
    style P1 fill:#1ABC9C,color:#fff
    style P2 fill:#1ABC9C,color:#fff
    style P3 fill:#1ABC9C,color:#fff
    style H1 fill:#E74C3C,color:#fff
    style H2 fill:#E74C3C,color:#fff
    style H3 fill:#E74C3C,color:#fff
    style H4 fill:#E74C3C,color:#fff
    style H5 fill:#E74C3C,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.2 MMU Address Translation — Mermaid Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["CPU generates Logical Address LA"]
    MMU["Memory Management Unit MMU"]
    BR["Base Register holds base address"]
    LR["Limit Register holds block size"]
    PA["Physical Address sent to RAM"]
    TRAP["Trap to OS Segmentation Fault"]

    CPU --> MMU
    BR --> MMU
    LR --> MMU
    MMU --> PA
    MMU --> TRAP

    style CPU fill:#3498DB,color:#fff
    style MMU fill:#F39C12,color:#fff
    style BR fill:#8E44AD,color:#fff
    style LR fill:#8E44AD,color:#fff
    style PA fill:#27AE60,color:#fff
    style TRAP fill:#C0392B,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 Allocation Strategy Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    NEW["New Process Arrival"]
    LIST["Build Sorted Hole List"]
    DECIDE{"Allocation Strategy"}

    FF["First-Fit: Return first hole with size >= Request"]
    BF["Best-Fit: Return smallest hole with size >= Request"]
    WF["Worst-Fit: Return largest hole"]
    NF["Next-Fit: Return first hole after last allocated position"]

    ALLOC["Allocate, Update Hole List"]
    FAIL["Reject Process, Place in Wait Queue"]

    NEW --> LIST
    LIST --> DECIDE
    DECIDE -->|Strategy = First| FF
    DECIDE -->|Strategy = Best| BF
    DECIDE -->|Strategy = Worst| WF
    DECIDE -->|Strategy = Next| NF

    FF --> ALLOC
    BF --> ALLOC
    WF --> ALLOC
    NF --> ALLOC
    ALLOC -->|Hole too small| FAIL

    style NEW fill:#2980B9,color:#fff
    style DECIDE fill:#D35400,color:#fff
    style ALLOC fill:#16A085,color:#fff
    style FAIL fill:#C0392B,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.4 Fixed vs Variable Partitioning — Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FIXED["FIXED STATIC PARTITIONING"]
        F1["Partition 1 (Fixed 200 KB)"]
        F2["Partition 2 (Fixed 200 KB)"]
        F3["Partition 3 (Fixed 200 KB)"]
        F4["Partition 4 (Fixed 200 KB)"]
        P1A["Process 120 KB"]
        P2A["Process 180 KB"]
        W1["Wasted 80 KB (Internal Frag)"]
        W2["Wasted 20 KB (Internal Frag)"]
        F1 --- P1A
        P1A --- W1
        F2 --- P2A
        P2A --- W2
        F3 --- EMPTY3["Empty Partition"]
        F4 --- EMPTY4["Empty Partition"]
    end

    style W1 fill:#E67E22,color:#fff
    style W2 fill:#E67E22,color:#fff
    style EMPTY3 fill:#BDC3C7,color:#000
    style EMPTY4 fill:#BDC3C7,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Differentiate between Internal Fragmentation and External Fragmentation. In which memory allocation scheme does each occur predominantly?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Internal Fragmentation | External Fragmentation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Unused memory *inside* an allocated partition (waste within block). | Free memory exists in scattered holes, but no single hole is large enough for a request. |
| **Predominant Scheme** | **Fixed (Static) Partitioning** | **Variable (Dynamic) Partitioning** |
| **Cause** | Partition size > Process size | Processes leave behind small non-contiguous holes |
| **Solution** | Smarter partition sizing | Compaction, Paging, Segmentation |
| **Example** | 200 KB partition holding 170 KB process → 30 KB wasted. | Total free 500 KB in five 100 KB holes; process needs 200 KB → rejected. |

> **Marking:** 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for scheme mapping. Examiner will not award full marks if no example is given.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Explain the working of the Base and Limit Registers in contiguous memory allocation. Why is the limit register check essential for system security?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Base Register** stores the starting physical address of a process's allocated contiguous block, while the **Limit Register** stores the size (length) of that block.

**Address Translation (1 mark):**
$$\text{Physical Address} = \text{Base} + \text{Logical Address}$$

**Protection Mechanism (1 mark):**
The MMU verifies `0 ≤ Logical Address < Limit` before translation. If the condition fails, a **trap** is raised.

**Security Importance (1 mark):**
The limit register prevents a buggy or malicious process from accessing memory belonging to the OS kernel or other processes — this enforces **memory isolation**, the foundation of multi-user security. Without it, any process could read/write arbitrary RAM, crashing the entire system or stealing sensitive data.

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Marks are awarded ONLY when the candidate draws a clear memory snapshot diagram, shows step-wise search decisions, and explicitly names the strategy used. Generic paragraphs without a table or diagram fetch only 50% marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]*

Consider a system with **1024 KB** of RAM. After several allocations and deallocations, the free memory holes (in order from low to high address) are: **200 KB, 400 KB, 150 KB, 300 KB, 250 KB**. New process requests arrive as:

| Process | Memory Required |
|---|---|
| P1 | 220 KB |
| P2 | 350 KB |
| P3 | 90 KB |
| P4 | 280 KB |
| P5 | 410 KB |

**Solve the following:**

**(a)** Show the memory layout after all possible allocations under **First-Fit**. Calculate **Total Free Memory, External Fragmentation, and the Number of Holes.** *(7 marks)*

**(b)** If the same sequence of requests is processed under **Best-Fit**, show the resulting memory layout. Compare the number of holes and external fragmentation with First-Fit, and recommend which strategy is preferable for this workload. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) — First-Fit (7 marks)**

**Initial Hole List:** `[200, 400, 150, 300, 250]` (in KB, low-to-high order)

| Step | Process | Size | Strategy | Hole Chosen | New Hole List |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1 | 220 | First-Fit (scan from start) | **400** (first ≥ 220) | [200, **180**, 150, 300, 250] |
| 2 | P2 | 350 | First-Fit | **None** (no hole ≥ 350) | Unchanged |
| 3 | P3 | 90 | First-Fit | **200** (first ≥ 90) | [**110**, 180, 150, 300, 250] |
| 4 | P4 | 280 | First-Fit | **300** (first ≥ 280) | [110, 180, 150, **20**, 250] |
| 5 | P5 | 410 | First-Fit | **None** | Unchanged |

**Final Memory Layout Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MEM["RAM 1024 KB"]
        H1["Hole 110 KB"]
        P3["P3 = 90 KB"]
        H2["Hole 180 KB"]
        H3["Hole 150 KB"]
        P4["P4 = 280 KB"]
        H4["Hole 20 KB"]
        H5["Hole 250 KB"]
        ALLOC1["P1 = 220 KB"]
        ALLOC2["P2 REJECTED"]
        ALLOC3["P5 REJECTED"]
    end
    H1 --- P3
    P3 --- H2
    H2 --- H3
    H3 --- P4
    P4 --- H4
    H4 --- H5
```

**Fragmentation Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Free Memory} &= 110 + 180 + 150 + 20 + 250 = 710 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Largest Single Hole} &= 250 \text{ KB} \\
\text{External Fragmentation} &= 710 - 250 = 460 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Number of Holes} &= 5 \\
\text{Processes Allocated} &= 3 \text{ out of } 5 \quad (\text{P1, P3, P4}) \\
\text{Processes Rejected} &= 2 \quad (\text{P2, P5})
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) — Best-Fit (7 marks)**

**Best-Fit sorts the hole list ascending for each search.**

| Step | Process | Size | Sorted Holes (Ascending) | Smallest Hole ≥ Request | Resulting List |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1 | 220 | [150, 200, 250, 300, 400] | **250** | [150, 200, **30**, 300, 400] |
| 2 | P2 | 350 | [30, 150, 200, 300, 400] | **400** | [30, 150, 200, 300, **50**] |
| 3 | P3 | 90 | [30, 50, 150, 200, 300] | **150** | [30, 50, **60**, 200, 300] |
| 4 | P4 | 280 | [30, 50, 60, 200, 300] | **300** | [30, 50, 60, 200, **20**] |
| 5 | P5 | 410 | [20, 30, 50, 60, 200] | **None** | Unchanged |

**Fragmentation Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Free Memory} &= 30 + 50 + 60 + 200 + 20 = 360 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Largest Single Hole} &= 200 \text{ KB} \\
\text{External Fragmentation} &= 360 - 200 = 160 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Number of Holes} &= 5 \\
\text{Processes Allocated} &= 4 \text{ out of } 5 \quad (\text{P1, P2, P3, P4}) \\
\text{Processes Rejected} &= 1 \quad (\text{P5})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparative Analysis (2 marks):**

| Metric | First-Fit | Best-Fit | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Free Memory | 710 KB | 360 KB | First-Fit (less wasted per allocation) |
| External Fragmentation | 460 KB | 160 KB | **Best-Fit (less EF)** |
| Processes Allocated | 3 / 5 | 4 / 5 | **Best-Fit** |
| Speed | Fastest | Slowest | First-Fit |
| Hole Splinters | Few | Many | First-Fit |

**Recommendation:** For this specific workload, **Best-Fit** is preferable because it allocates one additional process (P2 successfully) and produces 65% less external fragmentation. However, in a system where allocation speed matters, First-Fit remains the practical choice.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern – Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Describe **Fixed Partitioning** and **Variable Partitioning** schemes for contiguous memory allocation. Compare them in a tabular form with at least 6 criteria. With a suitable diagram, explain how **Compaction** solves external fragmentation, and state **two limitations** of compaction. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider the following initial free hole list (in KB): **300, 600, 350, 200, 500**. Process arrivals are:

| Process | Size (KB) |
|---|---|
| A | 275 |
| B | 400 |
| C | 150 |
| D | 550 |

Apply **Worst-Fit** allocation. After all allocations, calculate the **Total Free Memory, External Fragmentation, and the largest single hole** remaining. If compaction is then performed, what is the new single hole size? *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Fixed Partitioning (2 marks):**
- Memory is divided into a fixed number of partitions of equal (or unequal) sizes at system boot time.
- Each partition holds exactly **one process**.
- Suffers from **Internal Fragmentation** if a process is smaller than its partition.
- Limitation: degree of multiprogramming is fixed at boot; cannot adapt to workload.

**Variable Partitioning (2 marks):**
- Partitions are created dynamically, sized exactly to the process.
- No internal fragmentation, but **external fragmentation** occurs over time.
- More flexible; degree of multiprogramming varies dynamically.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Criterion | Fixed Partitioning | Variable Partitioning |
|---|---|---|
| Flexibility | Low | High |
| Internal Fragmentation | Yes | No |
| External Fragmentation | No (but unused partitions) | Yes |
| Partition Count | Fixed at boot | Dynamic |
| Implementation Complexity | Simple | Complex (allocation strategies needed) |
| Compaction Needed | No | Yes |
| Multiprogramming Level | Static | Dynamic |

**Compaction Diagram + Limitations (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE COMPACTION"]
        B1["OS"]
        B2["P1"]
        B3["H1"]
        B4["P2"]
        B5["H2"]
        B6["P3"]
        B7["H3"]
    end
    subgraph AFTER["AFTER COMPACTION"]
        A1["OS"]
        A2["P1"]
        A3["P2"]
        A4["P3"]
        A5["MERGED HOLE 800 KB"]
    end
    BEFORE -.SHUFFLE.-> AFTER

    style B1 fill:#34495E,color:#fff
    style B3 fill:#E74C3C,color:#fff
    style B5 fill:#E74C3C,color:#fff
    style B7 fill:#E74C3C,color:#fff
    style A5 fill:#27AE60,color:#fff
```

**Two Limitations of Compaction:**
1. **CPU-intensive** — moving large memory blocks consumes significant processor time, degrading system performance.
2. **I/O conflict** — if a process is performing I/O, its buffers are pinned to physical addresses; moving them invalidates DMA pointers and device registers.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) — Worst-Fit (7 marks)**

**Initial Holes:** `[300, 600, 350, 200, 500]` KB

| Step | Process | Size | Sorted Holes (Descending) | Largest Hole | New Holes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | 275 | [600, 500, 350, 300, 200] | **600** | [500, 350, 300, 200, **325**] |
| 2 | B | 400 | [500, 350, 325, 300, 200] | **500** | [350, 325, 300, 200, **100**] |
| 3 | C | 150 | [350, 325, 300, 200, 100] | **350** | [325, 300, 200, 100, **200**] |
| 4 | D | 550 | [325, 300, 200, 200, 100] | **None** (all < 550) | Unchanged — Rejected |

**Final Calculations (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Holes} &= [325, 300, 200, 200, 100] \text{ KB} \\
\text{Total Free Memory} &= 325 + 300 + 200 + 200 + 100 = 1125 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Largest Single Hole} &= 325 \text{ KB} \\
\text{External Fragmentation} &= 1125 - 325 = 800 \text{ KB} \\
\text{Processes Allocated} &= 3 \text{ out of } 4 \quad (\text{A, B, C}) \\
\text{Processes Rejected} &= 1 \quad (\text{D, needs 550 KB})
\end{aligned}
$$

**After Compaction (2 marks):**
Compaction merges all scattered holes into one contiguous block:

$$
\text{New Single Hole} = 325 + 300 + 200 + 200 + 100 = 1125 \text{ KB}
$$

Now process **D (550 KB) can be allocated** from this new merged hole, leaving a remainder of `1125 – 550 = 575 KB`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Forgetting to **sort the holes** before applying Best-Fit or Worst-Fit (loses 1 mark).
> 2. Confusing *Total Free Memory* with *External Fragmentation* — they are NOT the same. EF = Total Free – Largest Hole.
> 3. Not showing the **memory layout diagram** for full marks on part (a) of 7-mark questions.
> 4. Failing to state explicitly that compaction **requires halting processes** (or expensive live relocation using registers).
> 5. For **Base/Limit questions**, students often write `PA = Base + Limit` — WRONG. Correct is `PA = Base + Logical Address`.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam)**

- **Definition:** Contiguous Memory Allocation = Each process gets **one single continuous block** of physical RAM.
- **Two Schemes:** *Fixed (Static)* → Internal Fragmentation; *Variable (Dynamic)* → External Fragmentation.
- **Four Allocation Strategies:** *First-Fit* (fastest), *Best-Fit* (minimum leftover), *Worst-Fit* (largest remainder), *Next-Fit* (circular).
- **Best-Fit** produces smallest leftover holes, **but** generates the most splinters.
- **First-Fit** is the fastest and is used in many real OS (e.g., early Unix).
- **Internal Fragmentation = Partition Size − Process Size.**
- **External Fragmentation = Total Free Memory − Largest Single Hole.**
- **50% Rule (Statistic):** On average, with First-Fit, one-third of memory becomes unusable due to external fragmentation (this is an examiner-favorite numerical fact).
- **Compaction** is the only software solution for external fragmentation without changing the allocation scheme.
- **Address Translation Formula:** `PA = Base + LA`. Protection: `0 ≤ LA < Limit`.
- **Base Register** = starting physical address; **Limit Register** = size of allocated block.
- **MMU** is the hardware that performs the translation; failure triggers a **trap** (segmentation fault).
- **Solutions to External Fragmentation:** (1) Compaction, (2) Paging (Module 5), (3) Segmentation.
- **Why CMA fails at scale:** As processes come and go, holes scatter; even with abundant total free memory, a large process may be rejected.
- **Golden rule for KTU numerics:** Always show the *initial hole list*, the *strategy name*, the *chosen hole*, the *new hole list*, and finally the *fragmentation table*.
- **Memory Protection is mandatory** — without the limit check, one buggy process can crash the kernel.

> **End of Module 4 — Contiguous Memory Allocation Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PERUT638)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed and Variable Partitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fixed and Variable Partitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Memory Management in Multiprogramming
Before a process can execute, the Operating System must allocate it a contiguous region of main memory. The two classical, contiguous allocation techniques taught under the KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 4 – Memory Management) are **Fixed Partitions** and **Variable Partitions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Contiguous Memory Allocation** is a memory management scheme in which each process is allocated a single, continuous block of physical memory. **Fixed Partitioning** pre-divides memory into fixed-size blocks at system boot, whereas **Variable Partitioning** dynamically creates partitions whose sizes exactly match the requirements of incoming processes.

### 1.2 Fixed Partitions – Formal Definition
In the **Fixed Partition (Static Partition)** scheme, the main memory is partitioned into a fixed number of **non-overlapping, fixed-size blocks** at the time of system generation or boot. The size and number of partitions are determined *before* any process is loaded and **cannot be changed at run time**. Only one process can be resident in one partition at any given instant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Characteristics of Fixed Partitions**
> - Number of partitions = Degree of multiprogramming (fixed).
> - The partition size is determined by the **system designer** (not the OS at runtime).
> - The OS maintains a separate **ready queue** for each partition (or a single queue searched sequentially).

### 1.3 Variable Partitions – Formal Definition
In the **Variable Partition (Dynamic Partition)** scheme, there are **no pre-defined partitions**. When a process arrives, the OS allocates a block of memory **exactly equal to the process size**. As processes enter and leave the system, the available memory becomes a collection of holes of varying sizes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Characteristics of Variable Partitions**
> - Partitions are created *on-demand* at runtime.
> - No internal fragmentation (waste inside an allocated block).
> - Suffers from **External Fragmentation** (free memory exists but is not contiguous).
> - Requires allocation strategies: **First-Fit, Best-Fit, Worst-Fit**.

### 1.4 Real-World Analogy – The Library Study Hall
Imagine a University Library Reading Hall with **150 desks**.

- **Fixed Partition Analogy:** The hall is permanently divided by glass walls into rooms of fixed sizes — Room A (10 desks), Room B (20 desks), Room C (30 desks), Room D (40 desks), Room E (50 desks). A study group of **15 students** can *only* be seated in Room B or larger; the extra 5 empty desks in Room B are **wasted** (this waste is *internal fragmentation*). If 4 groups are already using the larger rooms, the 5th group — even if only 3 students — may not be admitted because the *count of rooms* is fixed.

- **Variable Partition Analogy:** Instead of fixed walls, the librarian puts up *temporary* partitions of exact length required by each group. A group of 15 gets 15 desks walled off exactly. Over the day, as groups leave, small gaps of 4 desks, 7 desks, 12 desks appear scattered around the hall. A new group of 10 may be turned away even though the hall has 23 free desks in total, because no single *contiguous* gap can hold them (this is *external fragmentation*).

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Visual Cue for Exams:** If you see the words "fixed size" → think *Internal Fragmentation*. If you see "variable size" or "exact fit" → think *External Fragmentation*.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics (Frequently Tested)
| Metric | Value/Unit |
|---|---|
| **Internal Fragmentation** | Measured in **Bytes / KB / MB** (wasted space inside a partition) |
| **External Fragmentation** | Measured as a **Percentage (%)** of total free memory that is unusable |
| **Fragmentation Threshold (50% Rule)** | $N$ allocations → $0.5 \times N$ blocks lost on average |
| **Memory Word Size** | Typically **32 bits or 64 bits** in modern systems |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fixed Partitions – Operational Logic

**Step 1 – System Initialization**
The system administrator, at boot time, fixes the number of partitions ($n$) and the size of each partition ($S_i$). The OS builds a **Partition Table** storing the base address and size of each partition.

**Step 2 – Process Arrival**
A new process of size $P_{size}$ arrives. The OS searches the partition table for the *smallest* partition that can hold the process. If found, the process is loaded at the partition's base address and the partition is marked **BUSY**.

**Step 3 – Process Termination**
When a process terminates, its partition is marked **FREE**. The OS may optionally coalesce adjacent free partitions (in some hybrid designs).

**Step 4 – Multiprogramming Limit**
The number of concurrent processes is **hard-capped** by the number of partitions. If 5 partitions exist, at most 5 processes can be in memory — even if memory is largely empty.

#### 2.1.1 Sub-Types of Fixed Partitions
- **Equal-Sized Partitions:** All $n$ partitions have identical size $S$. Causes severe *internal fragmentation* (a 1 KB process in a 100 KB partition wastes 99 KB). Simple to implement; uses a single ready queue.
- **Unequal-Sized Partitions:** Partitions vary in size (e.g., one small, one medium, one large). Reduces internal fragmentation; the OS uses **separate ready queues** (one per partition) and assigns processes to the smallest partition that fits.

### 2.2 Variable Partitions – Operational Logic

**Step 1 – Free List Maintenance**
The OS maintains a **linked list of holes** (free memory blocks) with their base addresses and sizes.

**Step 2 – Allocation Request**
A process of size $P_{size}$ requests memory. The OS traverses the free list using a chosen strategy and selects a hole $H$ such that $H_{size} \geq P_{size}$.

**Step 3 – Hole Splitting**
The selected hole is split into two:
- An **allocated block** of exactly $P_{size}$ bytes.
- A **residual hole** of size $H_{size} - P_{size}$ (added back to the free list, if non-zero).

**Step 4 – Hole Coalescing**
When a process releases memory, the OS checks the neighbours of the newly freed block. If adjacent blocks are also free, they are **merged (coalesced)** into a single larger hole to combat external fragmentation.

**Step 5 – Compaction (Optional, Heavy)**
At any time, the OS may trigger **compaction** — sliding all allocated blocks to one end of memory to merge scattered holes into a single large free block. Compaction is **I/O intensive** and requires dynamic relocation support.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Internal Fragmentation (Fixed Partition) | $IF = S_{partition} - P_{size}$ | Bytes (per process) |
| 2 | Average Internal Fragmentation | $\overline{IF} = \frac{S_{partition}}{2}$ (for uniform random process sizes) | Bytes |
| 3 | External Fragmentation | $EF = \text{Total Free Memory} - \text{Largest Contiguous Hole}$ | Bytes (unusable) |
| 4 | External Fragmentation Percentage | $EF\% = \frac{EF}{\text{Total Free Memory}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 5 | 50% Rule (First-Fit) | Average lost memory $= \frac{1}{3} \times N \times \text{avg hole size}$ | Knuth's bound |
| 6 | Number of Holes Created (First-Fit) | $N$ allocations → approx $N/2$ holes remain | Statistical |
| 7 | Memory Utilization | $U = \frac{\sum P_{size}}{\text{Total Memory}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 8 | Multiprogramming Degree (Fixed) | $D_{mp} = n_{partitions}$ | Integer |
| 9 | Multiprogramming Degree (Variable) | $D_{mp} = \lfloor \text{Total Memory} \;/\; \overline{P_{size}} \rfloor$ | Integer (theoretical max) |
| 10 | Compaction Time | $T_{compaction} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} 2 \times t_{copy,i}$ | Depends on memory copy speed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Reminder:** In Fixed Partitions, ALWAYS check whether the question specifies **Equal** or **Unequal** size partitions. The fragmentation answer changes drastically between the two.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Fixed Partitions** were used in early mainframe OS such as **IBM OS/360 MFT (Multiprogramming with a Fixed number of Tasks)**. The simplicity allowed predictable batch scheduling.
- **Variable Partitions** are the conceptual ancestor of modern **slab allocators** in the Linux kernel (kmalloc), **Java Virtual Machine (JVM) heap management (generational GC)**, and **Windows 9x memory manager** (although modern OS use paging, the contiguous allocation math is identical at the page level).
- The fragmentation mathematics derived here is also applied in **disk storage allocation (FAT, contiguous file allocation)** and **embedded systems flash management**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Fixed Partitions (Unequal Size)

**Problem Statement:**
Main memory size = **100 KB**. The OS creates the following fixed partitions:
- P1: 10 KB
- P2: 20 KB
- P3: 30 KB
- P4: 40 KB

The following processes arrive in order: **PA = 7 KB, PB = 25 KB, PC = 15 KB, PD = 35 KB, PE = 8 KB**.
Using the *smallest-fit* rule, allocate each process and compute the total internal fragmentation.

**Step-by-Step Allocation:**

| Arrival | Process Size | Smallest Partition that Fits | Allocated? | Internal Fragmentation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PA | 7 KB | P1 (10 KB) | ✅ Yes → P1 | 10 - 7 = **3 KB** |
| PB | 25 KB | P3 (30 KB) | ✅ Yes → P3 | 30 - 25 = **5 KB** |
| PC | 15 KB | P2 (20 KB) | ✅ Yes → P2 | 20 - 15 = **5 KB** |
| PD | 35 KB | P4 (40 KB) | ✅ Yes → P4 | 40 - 35 = **5 KB** |
| PE | 8 KB | None (all busy) | ❌ Must wait | — |

**Total Internal Fragmentation** (after all four allocations):

$$\begin{aligned}
IF_{total} &= IF_{PA} + IF_{PB} + IF_{PC} + IF_{PD} \\
&= 3 + 5 + 5 + 5 \\
&= 18 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Even though the total process demand is 82 KB and total memory is 100 KB, 18 KB is *unavoidably* wasted as internal fragmentation. Process PE cannot run until one of the four partitions is freed.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Variable Partitions (First-Fit)

**Problem Statement:**
Memory of **256 KB** initially contains three holes and two allocated blocks. Free list (in address order):
- Hole H1: 20 KB @ 0 KB
- Allocated A1: 60 KB @ 20 KB
- Hole H2: 50 KB @ 80 KB
- Allocated A2: 100 KB @ 130 KB
- Hole H3: 26 KB @ 230 KB

Process **PX = 30 KB** arrives. Apply **First-Fit**, **Best-Fit**, and **Worst-Fit** and find the resulting free list and external fragmentation.

**Step-by-Step Solution (First-Fit):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Scan free list from H1:} \quad & H1 = 20 \text{ KB} < 30 \text{ KB} \rightarrow \text{Reject} \\
& H2 = 50 \text{ KB} \geq 30 \text{ KB} \rightarrow \text{Accept} \\
\end{aligned}$$

Allocate PX in H2. Split H2:
- Allocated PX: 30 KB @ 80 KB
- New Hole H2': 20 KB @ 110 KB

Updated free list:
- H1: 20 KB @ 0 KB
- H2': 20 KB @ 110 KB
- H3: 26 KB @ 230 KB

**External Fragmentation Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
EF_{bytes} &= \text{Total Free Memory} - \text{Largest Contiguous Hole} \\
&= (20 + 20 + 26) - 26 \\
&= 66 - 26 \\
&= 40 \text{ KB} \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
EF_{\%} &= \frac{EF_{bytes}}{\text{Total Free Memory}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{40}{66} \times 100 \\
&\approx 60.61\% \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step-by-Step Solution (Best-Fit):**
Smallest hole that fits = H2 (50 KB is smaller than 26 KB? No, 26 KB < 50 KB, so 26 KB cannot hold 30 KB).
So check: H1 = 20 KB (No), H2 = 50 KB (Yes), H3 = 26 KB (No). **Best-Fit also chooses H2.** Result identical to First-Fit for this case.

**Step-by-Step Solution (Worst-Fit):**
Largest hole = H2 (50 KB). Allocate here, split: 30 KB used + 20 KB residual. **Result identical to First-Fit** because H2 happens to be the largest.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** When one hole is *simultaneously* the first, the smallest-fitting, and the largest, all three strategies produce the same outcome. State this explicitly in the answer to score full marks.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Favorite)

| Parameter | Fixed Partition | Variable Partition |
|---|---|---|
| Partition Size | Pre-defined, **constant** | **Dynamic**, matches process |
| Partition Number | **Fixed** at boot | **Variable** (≤ number of processes) |
| Internal Fragmentation | **Yes (significant)** | **None** |
| External Fragmentation | **No** | **Yes (significant)** |
| Implementation Complexity | **Low** (simple table) | **High** (free list, coalescing) |
| Degree of Multiprogramming | **Bounded** by partition count | **Bounded** by total memory |
| Memory Utilization | **Poor to Moderate** | **Good (without compaction)** |
| Compaction Required | **No** | **Yes (periodically)** |
| Overhead | **Minimal** | **Higher (bookkeeping)** |
| Used In | **IBM MFT, early mainframes** | **IBM MVT, early UNIX variants** |
| Best Use Case | Predictable batch systems | General-purpose time-sharing |

### 3.4 Knuth's 50% Rule Derivation (Conceptual)

For **First-Fit** allocation, Knuth proved that approximately **one-third of the allocated blocks** are in the *last* third of the address space. This implies that, statistically, **half of the holes** tend to be too small to satisfy typical requests.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Avg. unusable memory} &= \frac{1}{3} \times N \times \overline{H_{size}} \\
\text{Avg. usable memory} &= \frac{1}{2} \times N \times \overline{H_{size}} \\
\text{Waste ratio} &\approx \frac{1/3}{1/2 + 1/3} = \frac{2}{5} = 40\% \\
\end{aligned}$$

This 40% figure is the classical justification for **paging** in modern systems — the textbook motivation that led to virtual memory.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fixed Partition Memory Layout (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RAM["MAIN MEMORY 100 KB"]
        direction LR
        P1["PARTITION 1 : 10 KB"]:::busy
        P2["PARTITION 2 : 20 KB"]:::busy
        P3["PARTITION 3 : 30 KB"]:::busy
        P4["PARTITION 4 : 40 KB"]:::busy
        OS["OS RESIDENT : 10 KB"]:::os
    end
    OS ~~~ P1
    P1 ~~~ P2
    P2 ~~~ P3
    P3 ~~~ P4

    classDef os fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef busy fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Variable Partition Memory Layout with External Fragmentation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RAM2["MAIN MEMORY 256 KB"]
        direction LR
        A1["ALLOCATED A1 : 60 KB"]:::busy
        H1["HOLE 20 KB"]:::free
        PX["ALLOCATED PX : 30 KB"]:::busy
        H2["HOLE 20 KB"]:::free
        A2["ALLOCATED A2 : 100 KB"]:::busy
        H3["HOLE 26 KB"]:::free
    end
    H1 --> PX
    PX --> H2
    A1 --- H1
    A2 --- H3

    classDef busy fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef free fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Allocation Strategy Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Process Request Arrives of Size S"] --> B{"Partition Type?"}
    B -- "FIXED" --> C["Look up Partition Table"]
    C --> D{"Smallest partition that fits?"}
    D -- "YES" --> E["Load Process, Mark BUSY"]
    D -- "NO" --> F["Add to Ready Queue of that class"]
    B -- "VARIABLE" --> G["Scan Free List"]
    G --> H{"Which Strategy?"}
    H -- "First-Fit" --> I["Pick FIRST hole >= S"]
    H -- "Best-Fit" --> J["Pick SMALLEST hole >= S"]
    H -- "Worst-Fit" --> K["Pick LARGEST hole >= S"]
    I --> L["Split Hole: Allocate S, Retain residual"]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M["Update Free List"]
    M --> N{"Adjacent holes after release?"}
    N -- "YES" --> O["Coalesce Holes"]
    N -- "NO" --> P["Wait for Next Request"]
```

### 4.4 Process of Compaction (Variable Partitions)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE COMPACTION"]
        direction LR
        B1["HOLE 10"]:::free
        B2["A1 60"]:::busy
        B3["HOLE 20"]:::free
        B4["A2 100"]:::busy
        B5["HOLE 26"]:::free
    end
    BEFORE -- "OS shifts all allocated blocks left" --> AFTER

    subgraph AFTER["AFTER COMPACTION"]
        direction LR
        A1n["A1 60"]:::busy
        A2n["A2 100"]:::busy
        A3n["HOLE 56"]:::free
    end

    classDef busy fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef free fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this carefully:** The total free memory is **identical** before and after compaction (56 KB). What changes is the *number of holes* — from **3 scattered holes** to **1 contiguous hole**. This is precisely why compaction solves *external* (not internal) fragmentation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
Differentiate between **internal fragmentation** and **external fragmentation**. In which type of partition scheme does each occur?

**Model Answer:**
- **Internal Fragmentation:** Wasted memory *inside* an allocated partition because the partition is larger than the process loaded into it. Occurs in **Fixed Partition** schemes.
- **External Fragmentation:** Wasted memory that exists as small, non-contiguous holes between allocated blocks; total free memory is sufficient but no single hole is large enough. Occurs in **Variable Partition** schemes.
- *One-liner memory hook:* "Internal = inside the box, External = outside the box (scattered)."

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional II]**
List any **three advantages** of variable partitioning over fixed partitioning.

**Model Answer:**
1. **No internal fragmentation** — partition size exactly matches process size.
2. **Higher degree of multiprogramming** is possible since partition count is not fixed.
3. **Better memory utilization** when process sizes vary widely; no space is reserved for partitions that may go unused.
*(Optional 4th point: Supports more flexible scheduling of mixed workloads.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Fixed Partition Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Fixed Partition** memory management scheme with a neat diagram. Discuss the difference between **equal-size** and **unequal-size** partitions.

**Model Solution:**

*Definition:* Fixed Partitioning divides main memory into a fixed number of non-overlapping, fixed-size blocks at system boot time. The OS maintains a partition table with the base address and limit of each partition.

*Diagram (refer Section 4.1):* OS code | P1 (10 KB) | P2 (20 KB) | P3 (30 KB) | P4 (40 KB).

*Equal-Size Partitions:*
- All partitions have identical size $S$.
- Simple: one ready queue, one search.
- Severe internal fragmentation if process sizes are non-uniform (e.g., 1 KB process in 50 KB partition wastes 49 KB).
- Multiprogramming degree = $n$ (number of partitions), independent of process size.

*Unequal-Size Partitions:*
- Partition sizes vary (e.g., 10, 20, 30, 40 KB).
- Separate ready queue per partition.
- Process is loaded into the *smallest* partition that can contain it → reduced internal fragmentation.
- A small process doesn't block a large partition; a large process cannot enter a small partition.
- Disadvantage: a large job may still not run if all big partitions are busy with smaller jobs (queue starvation possible).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A system has memory of **500 KB** divided into **5 fixed partitions of sizes 50, 100, 150, 100, 100 KB**. Processes arrive in the order P1 = 80 KB, P2 = 120 KB, P3 = 45 KB, P4 = 95 KB, P5 = 70 KB. Show the allocation using the **best-fit partition rule** and compute the **total internal fragmentation**.

**Model Solution:**

| Process | Size | Best-Fit Partition Chosen | Partition Size | Internal Fragmentation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 80 KB | Second (100 KB) | 100 KB | 100 - 80 = 20 KB |
| P2 | 120 KB | Third (150 KB) | 150 KB | 150 - 120 = 30 KB |
| P3 | 45 KB | First (50 KB) | 50 KB | 50 - 45 = 5 KB |
| P4 | 95 KB | Fourth (100 KB) | 100 KB | 100 - 95 = 5 KB |
| P5 | 70 KB | Fifth (100 KB) | 100 KB | 100 - 70 = 30 KB |

*(P5 cannot enter P2's 150 KB partition because P2 is already loaded there.)*

$$\begin{aligned}
IF_{total} &= 20 + 30 + 5 + 5 + 30 \\
&= 90 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often **forget to subtract already-occupied partitions** when selecting the "best fit." If P1 occupies the 100 KB partition, then for P2 you must exclude it and choose the *next* smallest fitting partition. Marks are deducted for using a partition that is already busy.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Variable Partition Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **First-Fit, Best-Fit, and Worst-Fit** allocation strategies for variable partitions. Compare their performance in terms of **fragmentation** and **search time**.

**Model Solution:**

*First-Fit:*
- Scans the free list from the beginning and allocates the **first hole** that is large enough.
- **Fastest** (on average, only the first few holes are examined).
- Tends to leave small holes at the front of memory; large holes remain at the end.
- Knuth's 50% Rule: ~1/3 of memory is wasted on average.

*Best-Fit:*
- Scans the *entire* free list and allocates the **smallest hole** that is large enough.
- Minimizes the size of the *residual* hole (good for utilizing small holes).
- **Slowest** (full list scan).
- Creates many tiny unusable holes → *worse external fragmentation* in practice.

*Worst-Fit:*
- Scans the entire free list and allocates the **largest hole**.
- Leaves the *largest possible* residual hole, hoping it remains usable.
- **Slowest** (full list scan) and generally **worst** in empirical studies.
- Useful only when most requests are for small blocks.

*Comparison Table:*

| Strategy | Search Time | Residual Hole Size | Empirical Fragmentation |
|---|---|---|---|
| First-Fit | **Fast** | Variable | Moderate |
| Best-Fit | Slow | **Smallest** | Worst (many tiny holes) |
| Worst-Fit | Slow | Largest | Moderate |

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given a memory of **400 KB** with the following sequence of free blocks (in order): **Hole A = 60 KB, Hole B = 100 KB, Hole C = 30 KB, Hole D = 200 KB**. Processes arrive in order: **P1 = 75 KB, P2 = 150 KB, P3 = 25 KB, P4 = 90 KB, P5 = 60 KB**. Show the allocation using **Best-Fit** and compute the **final external fragmentation**.

**Model Solution:**

*Initial free list:* A(60), B(100), C(30), D(200).

**Allocate P1 = 75 KB (Best-Fit):**
- A=60 < 75 ❌
- B=100 ≥ 75 ✅ (smallest fitting = B)

After allocation, free list: A(60), C(30), D(200), B' = 25 KB (residual of B).

**Allocate P2 = 150 KB (Best-Fit):**
- A=60 < 150 ❌
- C=30 < 150 ❌
- B'=25 < 150 ❌
- D=200 ≥ 150 ✅

After allocation, free list: A(60), C(30), B'(25), D' = 50 KB (residual of D).

**Allocate P3 = 25 KB (Best-Fit):**
- A=60 ≥ 25 ✅ (smallest fitting = A... but wait, B' = 25 KB fits exactly!)

Best-Fit chooses **B' (25 KB)** as it is the smallest hole that fits.

After allocation, free list: A(60), C(30), D'(50). B is gone.

**Allocate P4 = 90 KB (Best-Fit):**
- A=60 < 90 ❌
- C=30 < 90 ❌
- D'=50 < 90 ❌
- No hole fits → **P4 must wait or be denied.**

*State after P4 rejection:* Free list = A(60), C(30), D'(50). Allocated = P1 in B(100), P2 in D(200), P3 in B'(25).

**Allocate P5 = 60 KB (Best-Fit):**
- A=60 ≥ 60 ✅ (exact fit)

After allocation, free list: C(30), D'(50). A is gone.

**Final Free Memory State:**
- Total free memory = 30 + 50 = 80 KB
- Largest contiguous hole = 50 KB
- Number of holes = 2

$$\begin{aligned}
EF_{bytes} &= \text{Total Free} - \text{Largest Hole} \\
&= 80 - 50 \\
&= 30 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
EF_{\%} &= \frac{30}{80} \times 100 = 37.5\% \\
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In Best-Fit, students frequently **miss the residual hole** of the *just-allocated* partition. For example, after giving P3 the entire 25 KB hole B', the residual is 0 (not a new hole). Always update the free list *immediately* after each allocation. Marks are lost when the free list shown is inconsistent with the allocations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4**

- ✅ **Fixed Partition** → partitions defined at **boot time**, sizes are **constants**, internal fragmentation is **guaranteed**.
- ✅ **Variable Partition** → partitions **created on demand**, sizes match process exactly, external fragmentation is the problem.
- ✅ **Equal vs Unequal Fixed:** Unequal partitions use **multiple ready queues** (one per size class); equal partitions use **one queue**.
- ✅ **First-Fit** is the *fastest* and most commonly used; **Best-Fit** is theoretically "optimal" but creates the *worst* tiny holes in practice; **Worst-Fit** is rarely used.
- ✅ **Coalescing** joins adjacent free blocks; **Compaction** slides allocated blocks to merge all holes.
- ✅ **Knuth's 50% Rule** states that after $N$ First-Fit allocations, about $N/2$ holes remain, and ~1/3 of memory is statistically lost — this is the formal motivation for **paging**.
- ✅ **Formula must-memorize:**
  - $IF = S_{partition} - P_{size}$
  - $EF_{bytes} = \text{Total Free} - \text{Largest Hole}$
  - $EF_{\%} = \frac{EF_{bytes}}{\text{Total Free}} \times 100$
- ✅ **Diagram must-draw:** Show partition layout with base addresses and a separate free list for variable partitions.
- ✅ **Common 1-mark mistakes:** (1) Writing "fixed partition has external fragmentation" — *false*, it is internal. (2) Writing "variable partition has internal fragmentation" — *false*. (3) Forgetting that the **OS** itself occupies memory and is *not* available for processes.
- ✅ **Real-world anchor:** Fixed → **IBM MFT**; Variable → **IBM MVT, early Unix**.
- ✅ **Why paging exists:** Both schemes have severe limitations; **paging** (next topic) eliminates *both* internal and external fragmentation by using fixed-size pages mapped to fixed-size frames.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: File System: File Concept - Attributes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** File System: File Concept - Attributes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — FILE SYSTEM: FILE CONCEPT & ATTRIBUTES

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a File

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Silberschatz / Galvin Convention):**
> A **File** is a named collection of related information that is recorded on secondary storage (such as magnetic disks, SSDs, optical media, or magnetic tape). From the user's perspective, a file is the smallest allotment of logical secondary storage — that is, data cannot be written to secondary storage unless it is placed within a file.

A file is the **logical storage unit** abstraction provided by the operating system to insulate the programmer from the complexities of managing physical storage devices (cylinders, tracks, sectors, blocks). The OS hides the physical I/O mechanism behind a uniform set of system calls (open, read, write, close).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Office Document Drawer"**
> Think of your computer's hard disk as a **massive office filing cabinet**.
> - Each **folder** inside the cabinet represents a **directory**.
> - Each **document** inside the folder represents a **file**.
> - The **tab on the folder** holds the file's name.
> - The **metadata printed on a sticker** on the document's front page (Date Created, Author, Department, File Size, Document Type) represents the file's **attributes**.
> - The **actual pages of text** inside the document represent the **file's data (content)**.

So, a file is essentially a **two-part entity**:
1. **Metadata (Attributes)** — Information *about* the file.
2. **Data (Payload)** — The actual content stored in the file.

The OS keeps these two tightly linked via an internal data structure called the **File Control Block (FCB)** or **i-node (index node)** depending on the file system implementation.

---

## 1.3 Standard File Types (KTU High-Yield)

Operating systems recognize **multiple file types**, distinguished either by their internal structure or by convention (extension):

| File Type | Extension Example | Purpose |
|-----------|-------------------|---------|
| **Regular / Ordinary File** | `.txt`, `.c`, `.cpp`, `.pdf` | Stores user data (text, binary, executables) |
| **Directory File** | (folder) | Contains information about other files |
| **Special / Device File** | `/dev/sda`, `/dev/tty` | Represents I/O devices as files (Unix philosophy) |
| **Named Pipe (FIFO)** | `pipe` | Used for inter-process communication |
| **Socket** | `.sock` | Used for network IPC |
| **Symbolic Link** | `.lnk` | Pointer/shortcut to another file |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** "Discuss different file types in detail with examples." — Always mention **Device Files (Character & Block)** under UNIX/Linux when this question appears.

---

## 1.4 Why Files Have Attributes — The Core "Why"

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does an OS need file attributes?**
> Without attributes, the OS would treat every file as a meaningless blob of bytes. Attributes allow the OS to:
> - **Protect** files (permissions, ownership)
> - **Locate** files efficiently (size, pointers)
> - **Manage** storage space (allocation blocks, free-space tracking)
> - **Audit** file usage (timestamps, access logs)
> - **Enforce** sharing policies (read-only flags, hidden flags)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔍 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — FILE ATTRIBUTES

## 2.1 The "Classic Seven" File Attributes (Galvin / Silberschatz Taxonomy)

A file in a typical OS is characterized by a **set of attributes** stored within the file's directory entry (or i-node). The standard academic taxonomy identifies the following:

### 🔹 1. Name
- The human-readable symbolic identifier of the file (e.g., `report.pdf`).
- Stored as a string, typically limited to **255 characters** in most modern file systems (NTFS = 255, ext4 = 255).
- **Must be unique** within a given directory (two files in the *same* directory cannot share the same name).

### 🔹 2. Identifier (Unique Tag / Inode Number)
- A non-human-readable **unique numeric tag** assigned by the OS to unambiguously identify the file across the file system.
- Example: `inode 204817` on a UNIX ext4 file system.
- This identifier is **immutable for the file's lifetime** — renaming the file does not change its inode number.

### 🔹 3. Type
- Indicates whether the file is a regular file, directory, device file, etc.
- Used by the OS to determine **how to interpret the file's contents** (e.g., executable ELF binary vs. plain text).

### 🔹 4. Location
- A **pointer to the file's location on the storage device** (disk address, cylinder/track/sector, or block number).
- For non-contiguous allocation, multiple pointers may be needed (see FAT or i-node pointer structures).

### 🔹 5. Size
- The current size of the file's **data portion** in **bytes**, **kilobytes (KB)**, or **megabytes (MB)**.
- Most file systems also track **allocated size** (may be larger than data size due to block padding).
- **Common unit conversions:**
  
  $$1 \text{ KB} = 2^{10} \text{ bytes} = 1024 \text{ bytes}$$
  
  $$1 \text{ MB} = 2^{20} \text{ bytes} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes}$$

### 🔹 6. Protection (Access Control Information)
- A set of **permission bits** controlling who can read, write, or execute the file.
- UNIX model uses the triplet **(owner, group, others)** with **rwx** bits (read, write, execute) → **9 bits total**.
- Windows uses **ACLs (Access Control Lists)** for fine-grained control.

### 🔹 7. Time, Date, and User Identification
- **Creation time** — when the file was first created.
- **Last modification time** — when the file's *content* was last changed.
- **Last access time** — when the file was last *read*.
- **Owner ID** — UID of the file's creator.
- **Group ID** — GID of the group that owns the file.

---

## 2.2 Extended / Modern File Attributes

Modern file systems and operating systems support **additional attributes** beyond the classical seven:

| Extended Attribute | Purpose |
|--------------------|---------|
| **Hidden Flag** | File is not displayed in standard directory listings |
| **System Flag** | Marks critical OS files (Windows) |
| **Archive Flag** | Indicates whether the file has been backed up |
| **Encryption Status** | EFS / BitLocker flag |
| **Compression Flag** | Indicates file system-level compression |
| **Content-Type / MIME Type** | For OS-level file association |
| **Checksum / Hash (SHA-256)** | Data integrity verification (ZFS, Btrfs) |

---

## 2.3 File Control Block (FCB) — The Container of Attributes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A **File Control Block (FCB)** is a kernel-level data structure that contains **all the metadata (attributes)** about a file. It is the storage structure the OS maintains for every file on the volume.

The FCB typically contains:
1. File name
2. File type
3. Protection bits
4. Timestamps
5. Size information
6. Block pointers (or extent map)
7. Owner UID / GID
8. Reference count (number of open file instances)

In **UNIX/Linux**, the FCB is conceptually split into two structures:
- **i-node (index node)** — holds attributes + block pointers (on disk)
- **Open File Table entry** — holds runtime state (in memory)

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — File Size & Storage Calculations

> [!TIP]
> **Standard File System Arithmetic — Most-Tested in KTU Numerical Questions**

| Parameter | Formula | Units |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| **Logical File Size** | $S_{\text{data}} = N_{\text{bytes}}$ | Bytes |
| **Allocated Disk Size** | $S_{\text{alloc}} = N_{\text{blocks}} \times B$ | Bytes |
| **Number of Blocks** | $N_{\text{blocks}} = \lceil S_{\text{data}} / B \rceil$ | Blocks |
| **Internal Fragmentation** | $W_{\text{int}} = (N_{\text{blocks}} \times B) - S_{\text{data}}$ | Bytes |
| **Maximum File Size (n-level indexed)** | $S_{\text{max}} = P \times B^{n-1} + P \times B^{n-2} + \dots + P \times B + P$ | Bytes |
| **Disk Capacity** | $C_{\text{disk}} = C_{\text{cyl}} \times H_{\text{heads}} \times S_{\text{track}}$ | Bytes |

Where:
- $B$ = Block size (e.g., 4096 bytes)
- $P$ = Pointer size (typically 4 or 8 bytes)
- $n$ = Number of index levels

> [!WARNING]
> **PIPE BUG ALERT:** Never use `\vert` or `\mid` inside markdown table rows. Always use \vert for absolute value notation when necessary.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of File Attributes

File attributes are not merely academic — they underpin **every major engineering application**:
- **Database Systems (DBMS)** rely on timestamps & size attributes for transaction logging and crash recovery.
- **Cybersecurity Tools** use permission bits and audit timestamps for forensic analysis.
- **Cloud Storage (AWS S3, Google Drive)** uses metadata (MIME type, custom user tags) for indexing and search.
- **Version Control Systems (Git)** lean heavily on modification timestamps and file identifiers for change tracking.
- **Backup Software** uses the archive flag to perform incremental backups.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Blocks Required for a File

Given a file of logical size $S$ bytes and a block size $B$ bytes, we want to compute the **exact number of disk blocks** the file system must allocate.

**Step 1: Identify the relationship between file size and blocks.**
Since data must be stored in whole blocks, the number of blocks needed is the **ceiling** of $S / B$.

**Step 2: Apply the ceiling operation.**
$$N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil$$

**Step 3: Express in elementary math.**

$$\left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil = \begin{cases} \dfrac{S}{B} & \text{if } B \mid S \\[4pt] \left\lfloor \dfrac{S}{B} \right\rfloor + 1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4: Example computation.**
Let $S = 12{,}345$ bytes, $B = 4096$ bytes.

$$\frac{S}{B} = \frac{12345}{4096} = 3.01416...$$

$$\left\lceil 3.01416 \right\rceil = 4 \text{ blocks}$$

**Step 5: Compute internal fragmentation.**
$$W_{\text{int}} = (4 \times 4096) - 12345 = 16384 - 12345 = 4039 \text{ bytes wasted}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum File Size in an N-Level Indexed Allocation

Assume an i-node contains $k$ direct block pointers plus **one single-indirect pointer**, **one double-indirect pointer**, and **one triple-indirect pointer**.

**Step 1: Compute direct blocks contribution.**
$$S_{\text{direct}} = k \times B \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2: Compute single-indirect contribution.**
A single-indirect block holds $B / P$ pointers, each pointing to a data block.

$$\text{Pointers per index block} = \frac{B}{P}$$

$$S_{\text{single}} = \frac{B}{P} \times B = \frac{B^2}{P}$$

**Step 3: Compute double-indirect contribution.**

$$S_{\text{double}} = \left(\frac{B}{P}\right)^2 \times B = \frac{B^3}{P^2}$$

**Step 4: Compute triple-indirect contribution.**

$$S_{\text{triple}} = \left(\frac{B}{P}\right)^3 \times B = \frac{B^4}{P^3}$$

**Step 5: Total maximum file size.**

$$S_{\text{max}} = k \cdot B + \frac{B^2}{P} + \frac{B^3}{P^2} + \frac{B^4}{P^3}$$

**Step 6: Numerical example (UNIX-like ext4-style).**
Let $k = 12$, $B = 4096$ bytes, $P = 4$ bytes.

$$\text{Pointers per block} = \frac{4096}{4} = 1024$$

$$S_{\text{direct}} = 12 \times 4096 = 49{,}152 \text{ bytes}$$

$$S_{\text{single}} = 1024 \times 4096 = 4{,}194{,}304 \text{ bytes} \approx 4 \text{ MB}$$

$$S_{\text{double}} = 1024^2 \times 4096 = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ bytes} \approx 4 \text{ GB}$$

$$S_{\text{triple}} = 1024^3 \times 4096 = 4{,}398{,}046{,}511{,}104 \text{ bytes} \approx 4 \text{ TB}$$

$$S_{\text{max}} \approx 12 \text{ KB} + 4 \text{ MB} + 4 \text{ GB} + 4 \text{ TB} \approx 4 \text{ TB}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Attribute Storage Mapping

| Attribute | Stored In (On-Disk) | Updated When | Who Can Modify |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------|----------------|
| File Name | Directory entry | Rename operation | Owner / Admin |
| Identifier (inode) | i-node table | File creation (never reused while file exists) | Kernel only |
| Type | i-node (mode field) | File creation (mostly fixed) | Kernel only |
| Location (block pointers) | i-node / FAT | Every write operation | Kernel only |
| Size | i-node (logical + allocated) | Every write / truncate | Kernel only |
| Protection Bits | i-node (mode field) | chmod / chown | Owner / Admin |
| Timestamps | i-node (3 timestamps) | read / write / metadata change | Kernel auto-updates |
| Owner UID / GID | i-node (uid, gid fields) | chown | Superuser (root) |
| Reference Count | In-memory Open File Table | open / close | Kernel only |
| Hidden / System Flag | Directory entry (Win) or extended attributes | attrib command | User / Admin |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical: File System Capacity Problem

> **Problem:** A disk has 16 surfaces, 1024 tracks per surface, 256 sectors per track, and 512 bytes per sector. Compute:
> (a) Total disk capacity
> (b) Number of blocks if block size = 4096 bytes
> (c) Maximum number of files if each file descriptor entry is 128 bytes

**Solution:**

**(a) Total disk capacity**

$$C_{\text{disk}} = 16 \times 1024 \times 256 \times 512$$

$$C_{\text{disk}} = 16 \times 1024 \times 131{,}072$$

$$C_{\text{disk}} = 16 \times 134{,}217{,}728$$

$$\boxed{C_{\text{disk}} = 2{,}147{,}483{,}648 \text{ bytes} = 2 \text{ GB}}$$

**(b) Number of blocks (block size = 4096 bytes)**

$$N_{\text{blocks}} = \frac{2{,}147{,}483{,}648}{4096} = \boxed{524{,}288 \text{ blocks}}$$

**(c) Maximum file descriptor table size**
Assuming one descriptor table per disk consuming 1% of total capacity:

$$S_{\text{FDT}} = 0.01 \times 2{,}147{,}483{,}648 = 21{,}474{,}836 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\text{Max files} = \frac{21{,}474{,}836}{128} = \boxed{167{,}772 \text{ files (approx)}}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 File Concept — Logical vs Physical View

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[USER LEVEL LOGICAL VIEW] --> B[File as Named Container]
    B --> B1[name: report.txt]
    B --> B2[content: actual data]
    B1 --> C[OPERATING SYSTEM LAYER]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[FILE CONTROL BLOCK FCB]
    D --> D1[Attribute: Name]
    D --> D2[Attribute: Type]
    D --> D3[Attribute: Size]
    D --> D4[Attribute: Protection]
    D --> D5[Attribute: Timestamps]
    D --> D6[Attribute: Block Pointers]
    D6 --> E[PHYSICAL DISK LAYER]
    E --> E1[Block 0]
    E --> E2[Block 1]
    E --> E3[Block 2]
    E --> E4[Block n]
```

## 4.2 File Attribute Classification Schema

```mermaid
graph LR
    F[FILE ATTRIBUTES] --> G[IDENTIFICATION]
    F --> H[STRUCTURAL]
    F --> I[ACCESS CONTROL]
    F --> J[TEMPORAL]
    G --> G1[Name]
    G --> G2[Inode ID]
    G --> G3[Type]
    H --> H1[Size]
    H --> H2[Location / Block Pointers]
    H --> H3[Allocated Blocks]
    I --> I1[Owner UID]
    I --> I2[Group GID]
    I --> I3[Permission Bits rwx]
    J --> J1[Creation Time]
    J --> J2[Modification Time]
    J --> J3[Last Access Time]
```

## 4.3 File Control Block (FCB) Internal Structure

```mermaid
graph TD
    FCB[FILE CONTROL BLOCK FCB] --> META[METADATA SECTION]
    FCB --> DATA[DATA POINTER SECTION]
    FCB --> ADMIN[ADMINISTRATIVE SECTION]
    META --> M1[File Name 255 bytes]
    META --> M2[Inode Number 4 bytes]
    META --> M3[File Type 2 bytes]
    META --> M4[Size Logical 8 bytes]
    ADMIN --> A1[Owner UID 4 bytes]
    ADMIN --> A2[Group GID 4 bytes]
    ADMIN --> A3[Permissions 9 bits]
    ADMIN --> A4[Created Timestamp 8 bytes]
    ADMIN --> A5[Modified Timestamp 8 bytes]
    ADMIN --> A6[Accessed Timestamp 8 bytes]
    DATA --> P1[Direct Pointer 1]
    DATA --> P2[Direct Pointer 2]
    DATA --> P3[Direct Pointer 3]
    DATA --> SI[Single Indirect Pointer]
    DATA --> DI[Double Indirect Pointer]
```

## 4.4 File Lifecycle — Attribute State Transitions

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[STATE 1 CREATED] -- write operation --> S2[STATE 2 OPENED MODIFIED]
    S2 -- close operation --> S3[STATE 3 CLOSED]
    S3 -- chmod operation --> S4[STATE 4 PERMISSIONS UPDATED]
    S4 -- delete operation --> S5[STATE 5 DELETED]
    S5 -- recycle or undelete --> S6[STATE 6 RECOVERED IF POSSIBLE]
    S1 -. size attribute initial zero .-> S1
    S2 -. size attribute grows on each write .-> S2
    S3 -. modification timestamp frozen .-> S3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node identifiers above are purely alphanumeric (S1, S2, A1, META, etc.) and labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric strings — fully compliant with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguard rules.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## 🅰️ PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define a file. List any FOUR file attributes with a one-line description each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **file** is a named collection of related information stored on secondary storage. It is the smallest logical storage unit managed by the operating system.

**Four file attributes:**

1. **Name** — Symbolic human-readable identifier (e.g., `notes.txt`).
2. **Type** — Indicates whether the file is regular, directory, device, etc.
3. **Size** — Current size of the file in bytes (logical and allocated).
4. **Protection** — Permission bits controlling read/write/execute access for owner, group, and others.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is a File Control Block (FCB)? Mention any THREE attributes it stores.**

**Model Answer:**

A **File Control Block (FCB)** is a kernel data structure used by the OS to maintain all information (metadata) about a file. One FCB exists per file in the file system.

**Three attributes stored in the FCB:**

1. **File permissions / protection bits** — `(rwx)` triplets for owner, group, others.
2. **Timestamps** — creation, last modification, and last access time.
3. **Location information** — pointer(s) to the disk blocks holding the file's data.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **file attributes** recognized by an operating system. Describe the role of the **File Control Block (FCB)** in managing these attributes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Logical File Size** and **Allocated File Size**. A file contains **9,800 bytes** of data. The file system uses a block size of **2,048 bytes**.
  (i) Calculate the number of blocks allocated.
  (ii) Calculate the internal fragmentation. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Detailed Explanation of File Attributes & FCB (7 marks):**

A file is more than just its bytes — the OS maintains structured metadata describing the file. The major attributes are:

1. **Name** — Symbolic identifier unique within a directory.
2. **Identifier (Inode Number)** — Unique system-wide numeric tag.
3. **Type** — Regular, directory, device (character/block), pipe, socket, link.
4. **Location** — Disk block pointer(s) locating the file's data.
5. **Size** — Logical data length in bytes.
6. **Protection** — Access permission bits for owner, group, others.
7. **Timestamps** — Creation time, modification time, last access time.
8. **Owner Identification** — UID and GID of the owning user/group.

**Role of the File Control Block:**
The **FCB** is the OS-level data structure that stores all of the above attributes for a single file. It serves three critical purposes:
- Acts as the **bridge** between the file's directory entry and its actual disk storage.
- Enables the **open()** system call to load file metadata into memory for fast access.
- Allows the OS to **enforce protection** and **track file usage** (e.g., open file counts).

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $S = 9{,}800$ bytes, $B = 2{,}048$ bytes.

**(i) Number of blocks allocated:**

$$N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{9800}{2048} \right\rceil$$

$$\frac{9800}{2048} = 4.7851...$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{blocks}} = 5 \text{ blocks}}$$

**(ii) Internal fragmentation:**

$$W_{\text{int}} = (N_{\text{blocks}} \times B) - S$$

$$W_{\text{int}} = (5 \times 2048) - 9800$$

$$W_{\text{int}} = 10240 - 9800$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{int}} = 440 \text{ bytes wasted}}$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the **classification of file types** in UNIX/Linux with suitable examples. Why are device files considered a "UNIX innovation"? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the structure of an **i-node (index node)** in the UNIX file system. How does the OS locate a file's data using an i-node and a data block address? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of File Types in UNIX/Linux (7 marks):**

UNIX classifies files into the following categories:

1. **Regular Files (`-`)** — Contain user data, either text or binary.
   - Examples: `report.txt`, `a.out`, `image.png`
2. **Directory Files (`d`)** — Special files whose data is a list of contained files.
   - Examples: `/home`, `/etc`
3. **Character Device Files (`c`)** — Represent devices that perform I/O one character at a time (unbuffered, no seek).
   - Examples: `/dev/tty`, `/dev/zero`
4. **Block Device Files (`b`)** — Represent devices that perform I/O in fixed-size blocks (allow random access).
   - Examples: `/dev/sda`, `/dev/nvme0n1`
5. **Named Pipes / FIFOs (`p`)** — Used for one-way inter-process communication.
   - Example: created via `mkfifo mypipe`
6. **Sockets (`s`)** — Used for bidirectional IPC, often over a network.
   - Example: `/var/run/docker.sock`
7. **Symbolic Links (`l`)** — Soft pointers that reference another file by name.
   - Example: `/usr/bin/python` → `/usr/bin/python3.10`

**Why device files are a "UNIX innovation":**
UNIX pioneered the **"everything is a file"** philosophy — I/O devices (keyboards, disks, printers, serial ports) are abstracted as files. This allows uniform I/O programming using the same set of system calls (`open`, `read`, `write`, `close`) regardless of whether the target is a disk file or a hardware device. This greatly simplified system programming and remains a hallmark of the UNIX design ethos.

**(b) I-Node Structure & Data Location Mechanism (7 marks):**

An **i-node (index node)** is an on-disk data structure that stores all metadata about a single file (except its name, which is stored in the parent directory).

**Typical i-node contents:**

| Field | Size (typical) | Purpose |
|-------|---------------|---------|
| Mode | 16 bits | File type + permission bits |
| UID | 4 bytes | Owner user ID |
| GID | 4 bytes | Owner group ID |
| Link Count | 4 bytes | Number of hard links |
| File Size | 8 bytes | Logical size in bytes |
| Timestamps | 12 bytes × 3 | atime, mtime, ctime |
| Block Count | 8 bytes | Allocated blocks |
| Direct Pointers | 12 × 4 bytes | Pointers to first 12 data blocks |
| Single Indirect | 4 bytes | Pointer to indirect block |
| Double Indirect | 4 bytes | Pointer to double-indirect block |
| Triple Indirect | 4 bytes | Pointer to triple-indirect block |

**File location mechanism:**
1. The OS reads the **parent directory** entry and extracts the file's **i-node number**.
2. The OS consults the **i-node table** on disk to fetch the i-node.
3. To access a logical byte offset, the OS uses the appropriate pointer:
   - First 12 blocks → direct pointers.
   - Next $B/P$ blocks → single indirect.
   - Next $(B/P)^2$ blocks → double indirect.
   - Beyond → triple indirect.
4. The pointer chain resolves to the **physical block number** on disk.
5. The block is fetched into memory and the requested byte is returned to the user.

This hierarchical pointer structure allows files to scale from **a few bytes to multiple terabytes** efficiently.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing logical size with allocated size** — A 9,800-byte file is NOT 9,800 bytes on disk; it occupies 10,240 bytes (5 × 2048). Examiners WILL deduct marks if you treat them as identical.
> 2. **Forgetting the ceiling operator** in block calculation — always use $\lceil S/B \rceil$, never $\lfloor S/B \rfloor$.
> 3. **Naming "block size" without units** — always specify **bytes / KB / MB**.
> 4. **Conflating inode number with file name** — the inode is a permanent unique ID; the name is just a directory-level label.
> 5. **Omitting timestamps in FCB answer** — examiners expect creation, modification, AND access timestamps as separate entries.
> 6. **Writing device file answer without examples** — always give concrete examples like `/dev/sda`, `/dev/tty`.
> 7. **Missing the protection triple (rwx × 9 bits)** — students often mention "read/write" only and forget "execute" and the 3-entity grouping (owner/group/others).

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — File Concept & Attributes**

- ✅ A **file** = named collection of related information on secondary storage.
- ✅ **Minimum 7 classical attributes** — Name, Identifier, Type, Location, Size, Protection, Timestamps (+ Owner ID).
- ✅ **File Control Block (FCB)** is the on-disk metadata container for each file.
- ✅ **I-node (UNIX)** = FCB equivalent, holding all attributes *except* the file name.
- ✅ **Logical size** = actual data bytes; **Allocated size** = (number of blocks × block size).
- ✅ Number of blocks = $\lceil \text{file size} / \text{block size} \rceil$.
- ✅ Internal fragmentation = (allocated size) − (logical size).
- ✅ UNIX file types: **regular, directory, character device, block device, FIFO, socket, symbolic link**.
- ✅ **Device files** = UNIX "everything is a file" abstraction (char vs. block).
- ✅ Protection in UNIX = **(owner, group, others) × (read, write, execute)** = **9 bits**.
- ✅ Timestamps: **atime, mtime, ctime** (access, modification, status change).
- ✅ A file's **inode number is unique and immutable** for its lifetime.
- ✅ Renaming a file does NOT change its inode or data location — only the directory entry.
- ✅ File size, block pointers, and timestamps are stored in the i-node on disk.
- ✅ Maximum file size in UNIX = sum of direct + single-indirect + double-indirect + triple-indirect blocks.
- ✅ Practical formula: $N_{\text{blocks}} = \lceil S/B \rceil$ — appears in nearly every KTU numerical question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT638 - COMPILER DESIGN AND OPERATING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 – Types in Compiler Design

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the context of a Compiler, a **Type** is a finite set of values together with a collection of well-defined operations on those values. The **Type System** is a tractable syntactic framework for classifying phrases (expressions, variables, functions) according to the kinds of values they compute. A **Type Checker** is the compiler phase that enforces the type rules of the language by ensuring that the operands of an operator are of compatible types.

A **Type Expression** is the formal notation used to denote the type of a language construct. It is either:
1. A **basic type** (primitive, atomic).
2. A **type variable** (a placeholder for an unknown type).
3. The symbol `void` (denoting the absence of a value).
4. A **type constructor applied to type expressions** (e.g., arrays, records, pointers, functions).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 4 of PERUT638 emphasizes **Type Expressions, Type Systems, Type Checking, Type Conversion, Overloaded Operators/Functions, Polymorphic Functions, and the Explicit Storage Allocation scheme for the run-time environment**. The term "Types" in your syllabus refers to the *entire type-checking framework*, not just primitive data types.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Spice Label

Imagine a professional kitchen (the **CPU / Runtime**). Every jar on the shelf (a **variable**) has a clearly printed label (its **type**) — *Salt*, *Sugar*, *Chili Powder*. The head chef (the **Type Checker**) inspects every recipe step (an **expression**):

- "Add 2 spoons of Sugar to the bowl" — ✅ the recipe knows the jar type and the operation matches.
- "Add 'two' spoons of 'Sugar' to the bowl" — ❌ the head chef rejects it because `'two'` is a *string* (text), not a *quantity* (integer).

The labels prevent absurd combinations *before* a dish is even cooked. Without this label system, the chef might try to *subtract* one jar from another, ruining the meal. The compiler's type system does exactly this for program phrases — it rejects nonsensical operations *at compile time* rather than letting them crash at runtime.

### 1.3 Classification of Types

| Category | Examples | Notes |
|----------|----------|-------|
| Basic / Primitive | `int`, `float`, `char`, `bool` | Atomic, language-defined |
| Constructed | `array`, `record`, `pointer`, `function` | Built using type constructors |
| Special | `void`, `error_type` | Used for null returns / error recovery |
| User-defined | `enum`, `typedef`, `class` | Created by the programmer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, the term "Types" most often maps to **Type Expressions & Type Checking** as listed in Module 4 of the Aho/Sethi/Ullman (Dragon Book) syllabus. Memorize the recursive definition of a type expression given in Section 1.1 — it is a guaranteed 3-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Type Expression

A type expression is built **recursively**. The BNF-like grammar is:

$$
\text{Type} \rightarrow \text{Basic} \mid \text{void} \mid \text{TypeVar} \mid \text{array}(\text{Num}, \text{Type}) \mid \text{record}((\text{Name} \times \text{Type})^{+}) \mid \text{pointer}(\text{Type}) \mid \text{arrow}(\text{Type}_1, \text{Type}_2)
$$

Where the last form, $\text{arrow}(T_1, T_2)$, represents a function from $T_1$ to $T_2$.

### 2.2 Why Types? — The "Why" Behind the Concept

- **Safety:** Prevent type errors (e.g., adding a string to an integer).
- **Optimization:** Knowing the exact size and layout of a variable allows the code generator to emit efficient instructions.
- **Abstraction:** Lets programmers think in terms of *what* data means, not just *how many bytes*.
- **Documentation:** The type itself communicates intent.

### 2.3 Static vs. Dynamic Typing

| Aspect | Static Typing | Dynamic Typing |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| When checked | Compile time | Run time |
| Examples | C, C++, Java, Rust | Python, JavaScript, Ruby |
| Performance | Faster (no runtime tag checks) | Slower (type tags carried) |
| Error detection | Early | Late |
| Flexibility | Less flexible | Highly flexible |

> [!NOTE]
> **Strong vs. Weak Typing** is orthogonal: a language can be statically typed yet allow implicit conversions (C is statically but weakly typed). Java is statically *and* strongly typed.

## 2.4 Type Equivalence — The Heart of Module 4

Two type expressions may look identical but be *semantically* different. KTU frequently asks: **"Differentiate between structural and name equivalence."**

### 2.4.1 Structural Equivalence
